catalogs.overview: Claude Code's interactive terminal provides extensive keyboard shortcuts, input modes, and controls for efficient prompting, editing, navigation, and workflow management. These span Ctrl key combinations, Esc behavior, multiline input methods, command history search, file/memory mentions, shell mode, vim keybindings, and customizable keybindings through ~/.claude/keybindings.json configuration.

items.name: Ctrl+C
items.summary: Interrupt running operation or clear input
items.details: First press interrupts a running operation. If nothing is running, first press clears prompt input; second press exits Claude Code entirely. Cannot be rebound.
items.example: While Claude is responding: press Ctrl+C to stop mid-turn and redirect. Press again to exit.
items.platformNotes: Same across macOS, Windows, Linux/WSL

items.name: Ctrl+D
items.summary: Exit Claude Code session
items.details: Sends EOF (end-of-file) signal to terminate the session cleanly. Cannot be rebound.
items.example: Type Ctrl+D to close the Claude Code terminal
items.platformNotes: Same across macOS, Windows, Linux/WSL

items.name: Ctrl+R (Reverse History Search)
items.summary: Interactively search through command history
items.details: Opens reverse history search mode. Type to filter prompts, press Ctrl+R again to cycle through matches, Ctrl+S to change scope (session/project/all), Tab/Esc to accept and edit, Enter to execute, Ctrl+C to cancel. Searches 100 most recent unique prompts.
items.syntax: Press Ctrl+R, type query, press Ctrl+R to cycle, Ctrl+S to change scope
items.example: Ctrl+R → type 'build' → shows matching commands → Ctrl+R again to see older matches → Enter to run
items.platformNotes: Same across platforms; scope cycling with Ctrl+S available everywhere

items.name: Ctrl+V / Alt+V
items.summary: Paste image from clipboard
items.details: Inserts [Image #N] chip at cursor for positional reference in prompt. On WSL, both Ctrl+V and Alt+V work; use Alt+V if terminal intercepts Ctrl+V. On iTerm2, use Cmd+V instead.
items.syntax: Press Ctrl+V (Windows/Linux/WSL) or Alt+V (WSL alternative) or Cmd+V (iTerm2)
items.example: Copy screenshot → in prompt press Ctrl+V → [Image #1] inserted → type 'fix the bug shown in [Image #1]'
items.platformNotes: Windows/Linux/WSL: Ctrl+V; iTerm2: Cmd+V; WSL: both Ctrl+V and Alt+V work

items.name: Ctrl+B
items.summary: Background running bash commands or agents
items.details: Moves a running bash command or agent to background. Tmux users must press twice due to tmux prefix key conflict. Background tasks run asynchronously; output saved to file; automatically cleaned up on exit; auto-terminated if output exceeds 5GB.
items.syntax: Press Ctrl+B (or Ctrl+B twice in tmux)
items.example: npm install running → press Ctrl+B → task backgrounded, you can keep working
items.platformNotes: Tmux: requires Ctrl+B press twice; regular terminals: single press
items.newcomerTip: Useful for long builds/tests - background them and ask Claude questions with /btw while they run

items.name: Ctrl+T
items.summary: Toggle task list visibility
items.details: Shows or hides the task list tracking progress on multi-step work. Displays up to 5 tasks with status indicators (pending/in-progress/complete). Ask Claude to 'show all tasks' or 'clear tasks' for full control.
items.example: Working on feature: press Ctrl+T to see task progress
items.platformNotes: Same across platforms

items.name: Ctrl+X Ctrl+K (chord)
items.summary: Stop all background subagents in session
items.details: Kills all running background subagents. Press twice within 3 seconds to confirm. Equivalent to Ctrl+B Ctrl+X in some contexts but this is the dedicated subagent-kill chord.
items.syntax: Press Ctrl+X, release, press Ctrl+K (requires v2.1.169+)
items.example: Subagents running in background → Ctrl+X Ctrl+K → Ctrl+X Ctrl+K again to confirm
items.platformNotes: Same across platforms; Ctrl+X Ctrl+B is tmux-friendly alternative if Ctrl+X Ctrl+K conflicts

items.name: Ctrl+G / Ctrl+X Ctrl+E
items.summary: Open prompt in external text editor
items.details: Opens default editor ($VISUAL/$EDITOR) to compose or edit prompt. Ctrl+X Ctrl+E is readline-native binding. If 'Show last response in external editor' enabled in /config, Claude's previous reply prepended as #-commented context above prompt; comments stripped on save.
items.example: Complex prompt: press Ctrl+G → compose in vim/nano → save → submitted to Claude
items.platformNotes: Same across platforms; uses $VISUAL or $EDITOR environment variable

items.name: Esc (single press)
items.summary: Interrupt Claude's response
items.details: Stops the current response or tool call mid-turn. Claude keeps work done so far, allowing you to redirect. Distinct from Ctrl+C which may exit entirely.
items.example: Claude running slow response → press Esc → 'let's try a different approach'
items.platformNotes: Same across platforms

items.name: Esc Esc (double press / chord)
items.summary: Clear input draft or open rewind menu
items.details: When input has text: clears it and saves draft to history (recoverable with Up). When input empty: opens rewind menu to restore or summarize code/conversation from previous checkpoint.
items.syntax: Press Esc, release, press Esc again
items.example: Long prompt typed → Esc Esc → clears and saves. Later: Up → recalled. Or empty prompt: Esc Esc → rewind menu opens
items.platformNotes: Same across platforms

items.name: Alt+M
items.summary: Cycle permission modes (alternative binding)
items.details: Alternative to Shift+Tab for cycling modes on terminals with VT mode limitations. May be default on some Windows or older configurations.
items.example: Shift+Tab not working? Try Alt+M to cycle modes
items.platformNotes: Windows (some configs), macOS if Option as Meta not set

items.name: Option+P / Alt+P
items.summary: Switch model without clearing prompt
items.details: Opens model picker to change LLM (Sonnet → Opus, etc.) while preserving input text. On macOS v2.1.132+, works without configuring Option as Meta.
items.example: In prompt: type question → Option+P → select opus → prompt still there, now uses Opus
items.platformNotes: macOS: Option+P (requires Option as Meta on older versions); Windows/Linux: Alt+P
items.newcomerTip: Great for switching to a stronger model mid-conversation without retyping

items.name: Option+T / Alt+T
items.summary: Toggle extended thinking mode
items.details: Enable or disable extended thinking (deep reasoning mode). No effect on Fable 5 which always uses extended thinking. Works on macOS v2.1.132+ without Option as Meta config.
items.example: Complex problem: Alt+T → extended thinking enabled → submit
items.platformNotes: macOS: Option+T; Windows/Linux: Alt+T

items.name: Option+O / Alt+O
items.summary: Toggle fast mode
items.details: Enable or disable fast mode for quicker responses with lower latency. Shows in status indicators.
items.example: Need quick feedback: Alt+O to enable fast mode, then submit
items.platformNotes: macOS: Option+O; Windows/Linux: Alt+O

items.name: Up / Down arrows
items.summary: Navigate command history or move cursor
items.details: When input spans multiple visual rows (wrapped or multiline): first moves cursor within prompt. Once cursor on first/last visual row, presses navigate history. As of v2.1.169, wrapped single-line input behaves like multiline.
items.example: Multiline prompt: Up moves cursor up within text; at first line, Up again → previous command in history
items.platformNotes: Same across platforms

items.name: Ctrl+P / Ctrl+N
items.summary: Navigate command history (alternative to arrows)
items.details: Ctrl+P = previous, Ctrl+N = next. Readline-standard bindings for history navigation.
items.example: Ctrl+P → older command, Ctrl+P again → even older
items.platformNotes: Same across platforms

items.name: Left / Right arrows (in dialogs)
items.summary: Cycle through dialog tabs
items.details: In permission dialogs, confirmation menus, and tab-based UI: Left/Right navigate between tabs.
items.example: Permission dialog with multiple tabs: Right → next tab
items.platformNotes: Same across platforms

items.name: Ctrl+A
items.summary: Move cursor to start of line
items.details: In multiline input, moves to start of current logical line (not entire input).
items.example: Cursor mid-line: Ctrl+A → cursor at line start
items.platformNotes: Same across platforms

items.name: Ctrl+E
items.summary: Move cursor to end of line
items.details: In multiline input, moves to end of current logical line.
items.example: Cursor at line start: Ctrl+E → cursor at line end
items.platformNotes: Same across platforms

items.name: Ctrl+K
items.summary: Delete from cursor to end of line
items.details: Deletes text from cursor to line end; stores deleted text for pasting with Ctrl+Y.
items.example: Text: 'hello world' with cursor after 'hello': Ctrl+K → 'hello' remains, ' world' stored for Ctrl+Y paste
items.platformNotes: Same across platforms

items.name: Ctrl+U
items.summary: Delete from cursor to line start
items.details: Deletes text from line start to cursor; stores for Ctrl+Y pasting. On macOS, Cmd+Backspace maps to this. Repeat to clear across lines in multiline input.
items.example: Cursor at end of line: Ctrl+U → entire line cleared
items.platformNotes: macOS Terminal/iTerm2: also use Cmd+Backspace

items.name: Ctrl+W
items.summary: Delete previous word
items.details: Deletes word before cursor; stores for Ctrl+Y pasting. On Windows, Ctrl+Backspace also works.
items.example: Text: 'hello world' with cursor at end: Ctrl+W → 'hello ' remains
items.platformNotes: Windows: Ctrl+Backspace also deletes previous word; macOS/Linux: Ctrl+W

items.name: Ctrl+Y
items.summary: Paste deleted text
items.details: Pastes text deleted with Ctrl+K, Ctrl+U, or Ctrl+W at cursor position.
items.example: Delete with Ctrl+U, then Ctrl+Y → text restored
items.platformNotes: Same across platforms

items.name: Alt+Y
items.summary: Cycle paste history
items.details: After pressing Ctrl+Y, repeatedly press Alt+Y to cycle through previously deleted text. Requires Option as Meta on macOS.
items.syntax: Press Ctrl+Y, then Alt+Y multiple times
items.example: Delete 'text1', delete 'text2', Ctrl+Y pastes 'text2', Alt+Y → 'text1'
items.platformNotes: macOS: requires Option as Meta config; Windows/Linux: Alt+Y directly

items.name: Alt+B
items.summary: Move cursor back one word
items.details: Moves cursor to start of previous word. Requires Option as Meta on macOS.
items.example: Cursor at end: 'hello world' → Alt+B → cursor at 'w' of 'world'
items.platformNotes: Requires Option as Meta on macOS; Windows/Linux: Alt+B by default

items.name: Alt+F
items.summary: Move cursor forward one word
items.details: Moves cursor to end of next word. Requires Option as Meta on macOS.
items.example: Cursor at line start: 'hello world' → Alt+F → cursor after 'hello'
items.platformNotes: Requires Option as Meta on macOS; Windows/Linux: Alt+F by default

items.name: Ctrl+J
items.summary: Insert newline without submitting
items.details: Works in all terminals without configuration. Inserts line break in multiline input.
items.example: Two-line prompt: type first line → Ctrl+J → type second line → Enter to submit
items.platformNotes: Same across all platforms, no setup needed

items.name: Shift+Enter
items.summary: Insert newline (terminal-specific)
items.details: Works natively in Ghostty, Kitty, iTerm2, WezTerm, Warp, Apple Terminal, Windows Terminal. VS Code/Cursor/Alacritty/Zed: run /terminal-setup once. gnome-terminal/JetBrains: use Ctrl+J or backslash+Enter instead.
items.example: Supported terminal: two-line prompt: type first line → Shift+Enter → type second line
items.platformNotes: Ghostty/Kitty/iTerm2/WezTerm: works without setup; VS Code/Cursor: run /terminal-setup; gnome-terminal: use Ctrl+J

items.name: Backslash + Enter (escape sequence)
items.summary: Insert newline without terminal setup
items.details: Quick escape method for multiline input. Type backslash, then press Enter. Works everywhere.
items.example: Unsupported terminal: type 'line1' → type \ → press Enter → type 'line2'
items.platformNotes: Works in all terminals, no configuration required

items.name: Option+Enter / Alt+Enter
items.summary: Insert newline (macOS option key method)
items.details: After enabling Option as Meta in terminal settings, Option+Enter inserts newline. Specific to macOS.
items.syntax: Press Option+Enter (requires Option as Meta config)
items.example: macOS with Option as Meta enabled: Option+Enter → newline
items.platformNotes: macOS only (iTerm2/Terminal.app); configure via terminal settings

items.name: Space (voice input)
items.summary: Hold or tap for voice dictation
items.details: When voice dictation enabled: hold Space to record, or run /voice tap for tap-to-toggle. Rebindable via /voice-dictation commands.
items.syntax: Hold Space or tap Space (depending on /voice mode)
items.example: /voice → hold Space, speak prompt, release Space → text transcribed
items.platformNotes: Works across macOS, Windows, Linux with voice dictation enabled

items.summary: Command or skill launcher
items.details: Type / to open command menu showing all available commands, bundled skills, plugin contributions, and MCP server commands. Filter by typing.
items.example: Type / → menu shows /clear, /config, /review-pr, custom skills, etc. → type 'review' → /review-pr highlighted
items.newcomerTip: If you forget a command name, just type / and browse—everything is discoverable

items.summary: Shell mode - run bash commands directly
items.details: Prefix input with ! to run shell command directly without Claude interpretation. Command and output added to context; Claude responds automatically (v2.1.186+). Supports history autocomplete: type partial command, Tab completes. Exit with Escape/Backspace/Ctrl+U on empty. Supports Ctrl+B backgrounding.
items.syntax: Type ! followed by command at prompt start
items.example: ! npm test → command runs, output shown, Claude explains failures. ! git sta[Tab] → completes to 'git status'
items.platformNotes: Same across platforms
items.newcomerTip: Use ! for quick shell operations while staying in conversation—faster than /btw which has no tool access

items.summary: File path mention / autocomplete
items.details: Type @ in prompt to trigger file/directory path autocomplete. Useful for @-mentioning files you want Claude to read.
items.example: Type '@src/' → autocomplete shows all files in src/ → select app.tsx → [File: src/app.tsx] inserted
items.newcomerTip: Reference files in your prompt without needing to copy/paste the full path

items.summary: Memory / CLAUDE.md mention shortcut
items.details: Type # to reference project memory (CLAUDE.md or auto-memory learnings). Inserts memory context into current prompt.
items.example: Type '#' → shows available memories → select 'build-commands' → memory content inserted
items.newcomerTip: Quick way to re-invoke project notes without hunting through CLAUDE.md

items.name: Tab (suggestion accept)
items.summary: Accept prompt suggestion
items.details: Prompt suggestions appear grayed-out after first turn. Press Tab or Right arrow to place in input, then Enter to submit.
items.example: Grayed-out suggestion appears: 'run tests' → Tab → suggestion moves to input → Enter to run
items.platformNotes: Same across platforms

items.name: /btw
items.summary: Side question without adding to history
items.details: Ask quick question about current work without cluttering context. Answers from existing context only (no file reads, no commands). Runs even while Claude working. Answer appears in dismissible overlay. Press Space/Enter/Esc to dismiss; Left/Right to step between earlier /btw answers; c to copy as markdown; f to fork into new session; x to clear prior questions.
items.syntax: Type /btw at prompt start, then question
items.example: /btw what was that config filename? → overlay shows answer → press Space → back to main session
items.newcomerTip: Use for quick context lookups while a long task runs, without derailing the main work

items.name: /recap
items.summary: Generate session summary on demand
items.details: Manually generate a one-line recap of session progress. Normally appears automatically after 3+ turns and 3+ minutes away from terminal.
items.example: /recap → 'Fixed bug in auth, added tests, ready to merge'
items.platformNotes: Same across platforms

items.name: /vim
items.summary: Enable vim editor mode (alternative to /config)
items.details: Shortcut to enable vim keybindings in prompt input. Also configurable via /config → Editor mode or settings.json editorMode: 'vim'.
items.example: /vim → INSERT mode active → vim hjkl navigation, v/V selection, etc. now work
items.platformNotes: Same across platforms

items.name: /doctor
items.summary: Run diagnostics and fix issues
items.details: Shows diagnostic report of potential setup issues (missing git, terminal config, keybinding conflicts). Press F to auto-fix when issues found.
items.example: /doctor → found Shift+Enter not configured → press F → /terminal-setup auto-runs
items.platformNotes: Same across platforms

items.name: Vim NORMAL mode
items.summary: Vi-style text navigation and editing
items.details: Enable via /config → Editor mode. h/j/k/l navigate left/down/up/right. w/e/b word navigation. 0/$/^ line start/end/first non-blank. gg/G input start/end. f/F/t/T jump to char. dd/x/D delete. dw/de/db delete word. cc/C/cw change. yy/yw yank (copy). p/P paste. u undo. . repeat. Text objects: iw/aw (word), i'/a' (quotes), i(/a( (parens), i{/a{ (braces). Esc enters NORMAL mode from INSERT. i/I/a/A/o/O enter INSERT.
items.syntax: Enable via /config, then use vim keybindings in NORMAL mode
items.example: NORMAL mode: 2dw → delete next 2 words. v then motions → visual selection. : commands not supported.
items.platformNotes: Same across platforms
items.newcomerTip: Vim mode is optional and independent of keybindings—you can use custom keybindings + vim editing together

items.name: Vim mode switching (INSERT/NORMAL/VISUAL)
items.summary: Switch between vim modes
items.details: Esc → NORMAL (from INSERT/VISUAL). i → INSERT before cursor. I → INSERT at line start. a → INSERT after cursor. A → INSERT at line end. o → INSERT on line below. O → INSERT on line above. v → character-wise selection (VISUAL). V → line-wise selection. In VISUAL, motions extend selection; operators (d/c/y) act on selection. Press v/V again or Esc to exit VISUAL.
items.example: INSERT: type text → Esc → NORMAL: vw → select word → d → delete word
items.platformNotes: Same across platforms

items.name: Vim motion 'h/j/k/l'
items.summary: Arrow key alternatives in vim NORMAL mode
items.details: h = left, j = down, k = up, l = right. Works in NORMAL and VISUAL modes. If at beginning/end of input, j/k/Up/Down navigate history instead.
items.example: NORMAL mode at line end: l → blocked, j → history next command
items.platformNotes: Same across platforms

items.name: Vim word motions 'w/e/b'
items.summary: Move by word in vim NORMAL mode
items.details: w = next word start, e = next word end, b = previous word start.
items.example: NORMAL: 3w → jump 3 words forward
items.platformNotes: Same across platforms

items.name: Vim text objects 'iw/aw', 'i"/a"', etc.
items.summary: Select text regions with operators
items.details: Work with d/c/y operators. iw/aw = inner/around word. iW/aW = inner/around WORD (whitespace-delim). i'/a' = single quotes. i"/a" = double quotes. i(/a( = parens. i[/a[ = brackets. i{/a{ = braces. 'inner' excludes delimiters; 'around' includes them.
items.syntax: In NORMAL: d+textobject, e.g., diw → delete inner word
items.example: NORMAL: cursor in word → diw → word deleted
items.platformNotes: Same across platforms

items.name: Vim f/F/t/T char jump
items.summary: Jump to character in vim NORMAL mode
items.details: f{char} = jump to next char. F{char} = jump to prev char. t{char} = jump to just before next char. T{char} = jump to just after prev char. ; = repeat last f/F/t/T. , = repeat in reverse.
items.syntax: In NORMAL: press f/F/t/T then character
items.example: NORMAL: fx → jumps to next 'x'. ; → repeat to next 'x'. , → previous 'x'
items.platformNotes: Same across platforms

items.name: Vim delete operators 'x/dd/D'
items.summary: Delete in vim NORMAL mode
items.details: x = delete char at cursor. dd = delete line. D = delete to end of line. Can combine with motions: dw = delete word, de = delete to word end, db = delete word back, dw = delete word.
items.syntax: In NORMAL: press x/dd/D or d+motion
items.example: NORMAL: dd → entire line deleted. dw → next word deleted
items.platformNotes: Same across platforms

items.name: Vim change operators 'c/C/cw'
items.summary: Change text in vim NORMAL mode
items.details: cc = change (delete + insert) line. C = change to end of line. cw/ce/cb = change word/to end/back. Deletes region and enters INSERT mode.
items.syntax: In NORMAL: press c+motion or cc/C
items.example: NORMAL: cw → word deleted, INSERT mode entered to type replacement
items.platformNotes: Same across platforms

items.name: Vim yank 'yy/Y/yw'
items.summary: Copy text in vim NORMAL mode
items.details: yy/Y = copy line. yw/ye/yb = copy word/to end/back. p = paste after cursor. P = paste before cursor.
items.syntax: In NORMAL: press y+motion or yy/Y, then p/P
items.example: NORMAL: yy → line copied. jp → pasted after next line
items.platformNotes: Same across platforms

items.name: Vim indent '>>/<<'
items.summary: Indent/dedent lines in vim
items.details: >> = indent line. << = dedent line. Works in NORMAL and VISUAL.
items.example: NORMAL: >> → line indented
items.platformNotes: Same across platforms

items.name: Vim J (join lines)
items.summary: Join lines in vim
items.details: J = join current line with next line. Works in NORMAL and VISUAL.
items.syntax: In NORMAL or VISUAL: press J
items.example: NORMAL: J → current and next lines joined
items.platformNotes: Same across platforms

items.name: Vim u (undo)
items.summary: Undo last change in vim
items.details: u = undo. . = repeat last change.
items.example: NORMAL: made change → u → change undone
items.platformNotes: Same across platforms

items.name: Vim / (search history)
items.summary: Open reverse history search in vim NORMAL mode
items.details: / in NORMAL mode opens reverse history search (same as Ctrl+R). As of v2.1.191, empty search prompt shows hint to press Esc then i then / for command menu.
items.example: NORMAL: / → search prompt → type 'build' → Esc to accept
items.platformNotes: Same across platforms

items.name: Vim VISUAL mode selection
items.summary: Select and operate on text regions in vim
items.details: v = start character-wise selection. V = start line-wise selection. Motions extend selection. d/x = delete selection. y = yank. c/s = change. p = replace with register. r{char} = replace all selected chars. ~/ u/U = toggle/lowercase/uppercase. >/< = indent/dedent. J = join. o = swap cursor and anchor.
items.syntax: In NORMAL: press v/V, then motions, then operator
items.example: NORMAL: vw → select to word end → d → delete selection
items.platformNotes: Block-wise Ctrl+V not supported

items.name: Keybindings customization (contexts)
items.summary: Define keybindings by UI context
items.details: ~/.claude/keybindings.json has 'bindings' array with context blocks. Contexts: Global, Chat, Autocomplete, Settings, Confirmation, Tabs, Help, Transcript, HistorySearch, Task, ThemePicker, Attachments, Footer, MessageSelector, DiffDialog, ModelPicker, Select, Plugin, Scroll, Doctor.
items.example: { "bindings": [{ "context": "Chat", "bindings": { "ctrl+e": "chat:externalEditor" } }] }
items.platformNotes: Same across platforms

items.name: Keybindings chord syntax 'ctrl+k ctrl+s'
items.summary: Define key chord sequences
items.details: Separate keystrokes with spaces. Press first chord, release, press second. Chords can share prefixes—unbinding all chords on prefix frees it for single-key.
items.example: "ctrl+x ctrl+e": "chat:externalEditor" (Emacs-style)
items.platformNotes: Same across platforms

items.name: Keybindings modifier syntax 'ctrl/shift/alt/cmd'
items.summary: Use modifiers in keybinding syntax
items.details: ctrl, shift, alt (or opt/option/meta), cmd (or command/super/win). Join with +. Uppercase letters imply Shift (e.g., 'K' = 'shift+k'). On macOS, cmd is detected by Kitty keyboard protocol; most terminals don't send it, so use ctrl/meta for cross-platform.
items.example: "meta+p": "chat:modelPicker" (Option+P on Mac, Alt+P elsewhere)
items.platformNotes: cmd/super detected only on Kitty protocol or xterm modifyOtherKeys; use ctrl/meta for portability

items.name: Keybindings unbind with null
items.summary: Disable default keybindings
items.details: Set action to null to unbind default. Also works for chord prefixes—unbinding all chords on prefix frees it for new single-key binding.
items.example: { "bindings": [{ "context": "Chat", "bindings": { "ctrl+s": null } }] } disables stash
items.platformNotes: Same across platforms

items.name: Keybindings reserved shortcuts
items.summary: Shortcuts that cannot be rebound
items.details: Ctrl+C (hardcoded interrupt), Ctrl+D (hardcoded exit), Ctrl+M (identical to Enter in terminals), Caps Lock (not delivered to apps).
items.example: Trying to rebind Ctrl+C has no effect; it remains interrupt
items.platformNotes: Same across platforms

items.name: Transcript viewer shortcuts (?/{{ }}/Ctrl+E/[/v/q)
items.summary: View and search transcript in fullscreen mode
items.details: Toggle with Ctrl+O. In fullscreen: ? = help panel. { } = jump prev/next user prompt (vim paragraph motion). Ctrl+E = toggle show all. [ = write to scrollback for native search. v = open in $VISUAL/$EDITOR. q/Ctrl+C/Esc = exit.
items.syntax: Press Ctrl+O to toggle, then use shortcuts
items.example: Ctrl+O → transcript opens → { → jump to previous user prompt → [ → write to scrollback
items.platformNotes: Requires fullscreen rendering mode

items.name: Terminal configuration: Shift+Enter newline
items.summary: Configure terminal for Shift+Enter to insert newline
items.details: Works natively in Ghostty, Kitty, iTerm2, WezTerm, Warp, Apple Terminal, Windows Terminal. VS Code/Cursor/Alacritty/Zed: run /terminal-setup. gnome-terminal/JetBrains: use Ctrl+J or backslash+Enter instead.
items.example: VS Code: /terminal-setup → Shift+Enter now inserts newline
items.platformNotes: Native support varies by terminal; /terminal-setup fixes VS Code/Cursor/Alacritty/Zed

items.name: Terminal configuration: Option as Meta (macOS)
items.summary: Enable Option key as Alt/Meta modifier on macOS
items.details: Required for Option+P, Option+T, Option+O, Option+Enter, Alt+B/F shortcuts. Apple Terminal: Settings → Profiles → Keyboard → 'Use Option as Meta Key'. iTerm2: Settings → Profiles → Keys → General → set Option keys to 'Esc+'. VS Code: add 'terminal.integrated.macOptionIsMeta': true to settings.
items.syntax: Configure in terminal application settings or VS Code settings.json
items.example: iTerm2: Settings → Profiles → Keys → Left Option: 'Esc+' → Option+P now works
items.platformNotes: macOS only; /terminal-setup configures for some terminals automatically

items.name: Terminal configuration: tmux extended keys
items.summary: Configure tmux for Shift+Enter and notifications
items.details: Add to ~/.tmux.conf: 'set -g allow-passthrough on', 'set -s extended-keys on', 'set -as terminal-features "xterm*:extkeys"'. Then run 'tmux source-file ~/.tmux.conf'. Fixes Shift+Enter submitting and notifications being swallowed.
items.syntax: Add 3 lines to ~/.tmux.conf, run tmux source-file
items.example: ~/.tmux.conf: set -g allow-passthrough on; set -s extended-keys on; set -as terminal-features 'xterm*:extkeys'
items.platformNotes: tmux on any platform

items.name: Terminal configuration: Desktop notifications
items.summary: Configure notifications when Claude finishes
items.details: Default: desktop notifications in Ghostty/Kitty/iTerm2. Other terminals: set preferredNotifChannel to 'terminal_bell' in settings.json, or use Notification hook for custom sound/command. iTerm2: Settings → Profiles → Terminal → 'Notification Center Alerts' + 'Filter Alerts' → 'Send escape sequence-generated alerts'.
items.syntax: Set 'preferredNotifChannel': 'terminal_bell' in settings.json, or configure Notification hook
items.example: { "preferredNotifChannel": "terminal_bell" } rings terminal bell when done
items.platformNotes: iTerm2 requires manual notification forwarding; other terminals vary

items.name: Task list status
items.summary: Track multi-step task progress
items.details: Claude creates task list for complex work. Ctrl+T toggles visibility (shows 5 tasks). Ask Claude 'show all tasks' or 'clear tasks'. Task indicators: pending/in-progress/complete. Persist across context compactions. Env var CLAUDE_CODE_TASK_LIST_ID=name-dir uses ~/.claude/tasks/name-dir for cross-session task tracking.
items.syntax: Press Ctrl+T to toggle; ask Claude for full control
items.example: Complex build: Ctrl+T → shows task list → [in progress] build, [pending] tests, [pending] deploy
items.platformNotes: Same across platforms

items.name: Background task management
items.summary: Run commands asynchronously in background
items.details: Press Ctrl+B while command running to background it (Ctrl+B twice in tmux). Output saved to file. Task ID for tracking. Auto-cleanup on exit. Auto-terminate if output >5GB. Disable with CLAUDE_CODE_DISABLE_BACKGROUND_TASKS=1.
items.example: npm install running → Ctrl+B → backgrounded, you work on other things, Claude can read output later
items.platformNotes: Tmux users: press Ctrl+B twice

items.name: Session recap auto-generation
items.summary: Auto-generate session summary after time away
items.details: After 3+ minutes away from terminal and 3+ turns in session, recap generates in background. Shows one-line summary when you return. Run /recap manually anytime. Toggle in /config → Session recap. Always skipped in non-interactive mode.
items.example: Away 5 minutes → return → recap shows: 'Fixed auth bug, added tests, ready for review'
items.platformNotes: Same across platforms

items.name: PR review status indicator
items.summary: Show PR approval status in footer
items.details: On branch with open PR: footer shows PR link (e.g., 'PR #446') with colored underline: green = approved, yellow = pending, red = changes requested, gray = draft. Cmd+click (Mac) or Ctrl+click (Win/Linux) opens PR in browser. Status refreshes every 60s or after gh pr/git push.
items.syntax: Auto-displayed when on PR branch; Cmd/Ctrl+click to open
items.example: Footer shows 'PR #123' with yellow underline (pending review) → Ctrl+click → opens GitHub PR
items.platformNotes: Requires gh CLI installed and authenticated (gh auth login); Win/Linux: Ctrl+click, macOS: Cmd+click

items.name: Prompt suggestions
items.summary: See suggested follow-up prompts
items.details: After first response, grayed-out suggestion appears (from git history or conversation). Tab/Right → place in input. Skipped after first turn, in plan mode, or with disabled prompt cache. Disable via env CLAUDE_CODE_ENABLE_PROMPT_SUGGESTION=false.
items.syntax: Press Tab or Right arrow to accept suggestion
items.example: Claude responds → suggestion 'run the tests' appears grayed → Tab → placed in input → Enter to submit
items.platformNotes: Same across platforms

catalogs.overview: Slash commands are the primary interface for controlling Claude Code—they provide quick ways to switch models, manage permissions, clear context, run workflows, and configure your environment. Commands are recognized at the start of a message, followed by optional arguments. Type `/` to see all available commands or `/search` to filter. Built-in commands are coded into the CLI, while bundled skills and custom commands are prompts Claude executes.

items.name: /config
items.summary: Open Settings interface or set configuration directly
items.example:
/config
/config thinking=false
/config theme=dark model=sonnet
items.details: Open Settings UI to adjust theme, model, output style, and other preferences. From v2.1.181, pass `key=value` pairs to set settings directly without opening the UI. Works in non-interactive mode. Alias: `/settings`. Run `/config --help` to see all settable keys
items.newcomerTip: Use the UI to explore options. Use `key=value` form in scripts or when you know what you want to change

items.name: /clear
items.summary: Start a new conversation with empty context
items.example:
/clear
/clear fixing-auth-bug
items.details: Starts a new session with empty conversation history. The previous conversation stays in `/resume` with an optional label you provide. Use this to reset context between different tasks without losing project memory (CLAUDE.md stays)
items.newcomerTip: Different from `/rewind`—this completely clears the conversation history. Use when switching from one task to a totally different one
items.platformNotes: Aliases: `/reset`, `/new`

items.name: /voice
items.summary: Toggle voice dictation on or off
items.example:
/voice hold
/voice off
items.details: Enable voice input in hold or tap mode. Requires a Claude.ai account. `hold` = hold spacebar to talk, `tap` = tap spacebar to toggle
items.newcomerTip: Use for hands-free input when you need to keep your hands free or when typing is uncomfortable

items.name: /help
items.summary: Show help and available commands
items.example: Type `/help` to see the full command list
items.details: Displays all available commands in the current session. Use `/` alone to see an interactive command list with filtering
items.newcomerTip: Start here when you're unsure what commands are available. Commands that aren't available for your setup (e.g., `/desktop` on Linux) won't appear

items.name: /plan
items.summary: Enter plan mode before a large change
items.example:
/plan refactor the auth module
/plan
items.details: Switches into plan mode where Claude presents a structured plan before making changes. Pass an optional description to start planning immediately. Useful for understanding the approach before committing to edits
items.newcomerTip: Use this for any task bigger than a single file edit. Claude will show the plan, you can review and revise it before execution

items.name: /diff
items.summary: Open an interactive diff viewer
items.example: /diff
items.details: Show uncommitted changes and per-turn diffs in an interactive viewer. Use left/right arrows to switch between git diff and individual Claude turns, up/down to browse files
items.newcomerTip: Much better than scrolling—see exactly what changed across your conversation

items.name: /code-review
items.summary: Review the current diff for bugs and cleanups
items.example:
/code-review
/code-review high --fix
/code-review ultra
items.details: Review uncommitted changes for correctness bugs and cleanup opportunities. Pass `--fix` to apply fixes, `--comment` to post as GitHub PR comments, or `ultra` for a deep cloud-based review. Effort levels range from `low` (fewer findings) to `max` (comprehensive). Marked as **Skill** in the commands reference—it's a prompt-based bundled skill
items.newcomerTip: Run before committing or before asking for review. `high` or `xhigh` finds real issues; `ultra` is a slow but thorough multi-agent review

items.name: /simplify
items.summary: Cleanup code without hunting for bugs
items.example:
/simplify
/simplify src/auth.ts
items.details: Four review agents run in parallel to simplify code: reuse of existing helpers, simplification, efficiency, and abstraction level. Unlike `/code-review`, this does not look for correctness bugs. Available from v2.1.154+. Pass a path or PR reference to review a specific target
items.newcomerTip: Use after `/code-review` to polish code without noise about potential bugs

items.name: /review
items.summary: Review a GitHub pull request
items.example:
/review
/review 42
/review https://github.com/owner/repo/pull/42
items.details: Review a pull request using the same engine as `/code-review`. With no arguments, lists open PRs to pick from. For cloud-based reviews, use `/code-review ultra` instead
items.newcomerTip: Use this when reviewing PRs from other developers. The read-only review doesn't edit files, just comments on the PR

items.name: /security-review
items.summary: Analyze pending changes for security vulnerabilities
items.example: /security-review
items.details: Review the git diff on the current branch for security vulnerabilities, injection attacks, auth issues, and data exposure. Deeper than `/code-review`
items.newcomerTip: Run this before merging any changes that touch auth, secrets, or sensitive data handling

items.name: /rewind
items.summary: Roll code and conversation back to a checkpoint
items.example: /rewind
items.details: Rewind to a previous point in the conversation or summarize part of the conversation. See [checkpointing](/en/checkpointing) for details. Aliases: `/checkpoint`, `/undo`
items.newcomerTip: Use when you went down the wrong path. Unlike `/clear`, this keeps everything but lets you go back in time

items.name: /fork
items.summary: Spawn a forked subagent to work on a directive
items.example: /fork investigate the database query performance issue
items.details: Spawn a background subagent that inherits the full conversation and works on the directive while you keep going. Results return when the subagent finishes. Available from v2.1.161+. To switch into a copy yourself, use `/branch` instead
items.newcomerTip: Use when you want Claude to investigate something in parallel while you continue on the main task

items.name: /init
items.summary: Initialize project with a CLAUDE.md guide
items.example: /init
items.details: Generates a starter CLAUDE.md file for your project. Set `CLAUDE_CODE_NEW_INIT=1` for an interactive flow that also walks through skills, hooks, and personal memory files
items.newcomerTip: Run this in a new project. It creates a template you customize with your coding standards, preferred libraries, and architecture decisions

items.name: /memory
items.summary: Edit CLAUDE.md memory files and manage auto-memory
items.example: /memory
items.details: Opens an editor to create or update CLAUDE.md memory files, enable/disable auto-memory, and view auto-memory entries. Auto-memory is persistent learning across sessions—Claude remembers build commands, debugging patterns, etc
items.newcomerTip: Use to update CLAUDE.md as you learn about the project. Auto-memory handles common patterns automatically

items.name: /permissions
items.summary: Manage allow, ask, and deny rules for tool permissions
items.example: /permissions
items.details: Opens an interactive dialog to view permission rules by scope, add/remove rules, manage working directories, and review auto mode denials. Controls which tools Claude can use without prompting. Alias: `/allowed-tools`
items.newcomerTip: Use to approve tools Claude uses frequently (like `git`) so you stop seeing permission prompts. Or deny dangerous tools for autonomous tasks

items.name: /agents
items.summary: Manage agent (subagent) configurations
items.example: /agents
items.details: Opens the agent manager to configure and delegate work to subagents. Subagents are specialized Claude instances that can work on side tasks in parallel
items.newcomerTip: Use when you need Claude to investigate something in parallel or run multiple independent tasks at once

items.name: /tasks
items.summary: View and manage everything running in the background
items.example: /tasks
items.details: Shows background subagents, bash commands, and async work running in the current session. Monitor and control parallel work. Alias: `/bashes`
items.newcomerTip: Use to check what's running while you continue with other work. See progress and manage long-running tasks

items.name: /background
items.summary: Detach the current session to run as a background agent
items.example:
/background run the test suite
/background
items.details: Detaches the current session to keep running as a background agent, freeing this terminal. Optionally pass one more instruction. Monitor with `claude agents`. Alias: `/bg`
items.newcomerTip: Use when you have a long-running task (tests, compilation, deployment) and want to free up your terminal

items.name: /batch
items.summary: Orchestrate large-scale changes in parallel
items.example: /batch migrate src/ from Solid to React
items.details: Research the codebase, decompose work into 5-30 independent units, present a plan, then spawn background subagents in git worktrees. Each subagent implements, tests, and opens a PR. A **Skill** (bundled prompt-based command). Requires git
items.newcomerTip: Use for refactoring across the codebase. Claude handles the decomposition and parallelization

items.name: /add-dir
items.summary: Add a working directory for file access during the session
items.example:
/add-dir /home/user/other-project
/add-dir ../sibling-repo
items.details: Grant file access to another directory without moving the session. Most `.claude/` configuration (skills, hooks, CLAUDE.md) is NOT discovered from the added directory, only file read/write access is granted
items.newcomerTip: Use when you need to reference or edit files in another project without switching directories

items.name: /cd
items.summary: Move the session to a new working directory
items.example:
/cd ../other-project
/cd ~/projects/myapp
items.details: Move to a new working directory while preserving the prompt cache. The new directory's CLAUDE.md is appended instead of rebuilding the system prompt. Available from v2.1.169+. Restrict targets with `Cd` permission rules
items.newcomerTip: Faster than starting a new session in another directory because the prompt cache isn't invalidated

items.name: /hooks
items.summary: View hook configurations for tool events
items.example: /hooks
items.details: Show all hooks configured for the current session. Hooks automate workflows around tool events—run prettier after file edits, auto-format before commits, etc
items.newcomerTip: Use to see what automation is set up. Edit with `/config` or in settings.json files in .claude/

items.name: /focus
items.summary: Toggle focus view (fullscreen mode only)
items.example: /focus
items.details: Show only your last prompt, a one-line tool summary with edit diffs, and the final response. Reduces noise in fullscreen mode. Selection persists across sessions
items.newcomerTip: Use in fullscreen rendering to reduce visual clutter and focus on the most recent turn

items.name: /copy
items.summary: Copy the last assistant response to clipboard
items.example:
/copy
/copy 2
items.details: Copy the last response (or the Nth-latest with `/copy N`). When code blocks are present, pick individual blocks or the full response. Press `w` to write to file instead of clipboard (useful over SSH)
items.newcomerTip: Use to quickly grab code snippets. Over SSH, press `w` to save directly to a file

items.name: /debug
items.summary: Enable debug logging and troubleshoot issues
items.example:
/debug
/debug slow performance when editing large files
items.details: Enable debug logging for the current session and read the debug log to troubleshoot issues. A **Skill** (bundled). Off by default unless started with `claude --debug`
items.newcomerTip: Use when something doesn't work as expected. Claude analyzes the logs and suggests fixes

items.name: /feedback
items.summary: Submit feedback, report a bug, or share your conversation
items.example:
/feedback
/feedback this command doesn't work
items.details: Submit feedback or bug reports with session context attached. Aliases: `/bug`, `/share`
items.newcomerTip: Use to report issues directly to Anthropic. Include as much detail as possible

items.name: /usage
items.summary: Show session cost, plan usage limits, and activity stats
items.example: /usage
items.details: View token usage, cost, plan limits, and activity stats. On Pro/Max/Team/Enterprise, includes breakdown by skill, subagent, plugin, and MCP server. See [cost tracking guide](/en/costs#using-the-%2Fusage-command). Aliases: `/cost`, `/stats`
items.newcomerTip: Check this regularly to understand token consumption and manage costs. Some features (skills, subagents) have per-tool breakdowns

items.name: /status
items.summary: Show version, model, account, and connectivity
items.example: /status
items.details: Open the Settings interface (Status tab) to check Claude Code version, current model, account status, and API connectivity. Works while Claude is responding
items.newcomerTip: Use to verify you're logged in and using the right model before starting work

items.name: /login
items.summary: Sign in to your Anthropic account
items.example: /login
items.details: Authenticate with Claude.ai or Anthropic Console. Prompts you to log in through your browser

items.name: /logout
items.summary: Sign out from your Anthropic account
items.example: /logout
items.details: Sign out and clear local credentials

items.name: /desktop
items.summary: Continue the current session in the Claude Code Desktop app
items.example: /desktop
items.details: Move this session to the Desktop app with visual diff review. Requires macOS or Windows and a Claude subscription. Alias: `/app`
items.newcomerTip: Use when you want visual diff review. The Desktop app shows side-by-side comparisons better than the terminal

items.name: /teleport
items.summary: Pull a Claude Code on the web session into this terminal
items.example: /teleport
items.details: Move a web session (from claude.ai/code) into this terminal. Opens a picker, fetches the branch and conversation. Also `/tp`. Requires a claude.ai subscription
items.newcomerTip: Use when you started a task on the web but want to continue locally with full terminal access

items.name: /remote-control
items.summary: Make this session available for remote control from claude.ai
items.example: /remote-control
items.details: Enable this terminal session to be controlled from your phone or browser. Alias: `/rc`. Requires a claude.ai subscription and Remote Control enabled on your account
items.newcomerTip: Use to keep working from your phone when away from your desk

items.name: /tui
items.summary: Set the terminal UI renderer
items.example:
/tui fullscreen
/tui default
items.details: Switch between default line-by-line rendering and fullscreen alt-screen mode. Fullscreen is flicker-free and shows more context at once. With no argument, prints the active renderer
items.newcomerTip: Try `fullscreen` if you want a cleaner interface. Some terminal emulators render better with `default`

items.name: /color
items.summary: Set the prompt bar color for the current session
items.example:
/color red
/color blue
items.details: Available colors: `red`, `blue`, `green`, `yellow`, `purple`, `orange`, `pink`, `cyan`. Use `default` to reset, or run with no argument for a random color. Syncs to claude.ai/code when Remote Control is connected
items.newcomerTip: Use different colors for different types of work (bug fixes in red, features in blue) for quick visual scanning

items.name: /loop
items.summary: Run a prompt repeatedly while the session stays open
items.example:
/loop 5m check if the deploy finished
/loop
items.details: Run a prompt on a fixed interval or let Claude self-pace. Omit the prompt to run an autonomous maintenance check or `.claude/loop.md`. A **Skill**. See [Run prompts on a schedule](/en/scheduled-tasks)
items.newcomerTip: Use for polling (deployment status, test results) or autonomous background maintenance

items.name: /schedule
items.summary: Create, update, list, or run routines on a schedule
items.example:
/schedule nightly dependency audit
/schedule
items.details: Create routines that execute on Anthropic-managed cloud infrastructure on a cron schedule, API trigger, or GitHub event. Walks you through setup. Alias: `/routines`. See [routines](/en/routines)
items.newcomerTip: Use for recurring tasks (PR reviews, dependency updates, backups) that should run even when your computer is off

items.name: /goal
items.summary: Set a goal for Claude to work toward across turns
items.example:
/goal all tests pass
/goal clear
items.details: Set a condition like 'all tests pass' and Claude keeps working across turns until it's met. With no argument, shows the current goal. Clear with `/goal clear`, `/goal stop`, `/goal off`, etc
items.newcomerTip: Use for open-ended tasks. Claude will iterate autonomously until the goal is satisfied

items.name: /run
items.summary: Launch and drive your app to verify changes work
items.example: /run
items.details: Build, launch, and interact with your project's app to verify changes in the running app (not just tests). Requires v2.1.145+. A **Skill**. See [Run and verify your app](/en/skills#run-and-verify-your-app)
items.newcomerTip: Use after making UI or integration changes. Claude controls the app and confirms the change actually works

items.name: /verify
items.summary: Confirm a code change works in the running app
items.example: /verify
items.details: Build and run your app to confirm a change does what it should, without falling back to tests. Requires v2.1.145+. A **Skill**. See [Run and verify your app](/en/skills#run-and-verify-your-app)
items.newcomerTip: Use `/run` to explore the app, `/verify` to confirm a specific change is correct

items.name: /run-skill-generator
items.summary: Teach /run and /verify how to build and launch your project
items.example: /run-skill-generator
items.details: Record the build/launch recipe by getting your app running from a clean environment. Commits it as `.claude/skills/run-<name>/`. Requires v2.1.145+. A **Skill**. After running this, `/run` and `/verify` use the recorded recipe
items.newcomerTip: Run once per project when `/run` can't figure out how to build. Re-run if the build process changes

items.name: /claude-api
items.summary: Load Claude API reference material and SDK docs
items.example:
/claude-api
/claude-api migrate
/claude-api managed-agents-onboard
items.details: Load reference material for Claude API in your project's language (Python, TypeScript, Java, Go, Ruby, C#, PHP, cURL). Covers tool use, streaming, batches, structured outputs. Pass `migrate` to upgrade code between Claude versions. Pass `managed-agents-onboard` for Managed Agents walkthrough. A **Skill**. Activates automatically when code imports `anthropic` or `@anthropic-ai/sdk`
items.newcomerTip: Use when building with the Claude API or SDK. The migration subcommand handles version upgrades

items.name: /code-review ultra
items.summary: Run a deep multi-agent code review in the cloud
items.example:
/code-review ultra
/code-review ultra 42
items.details: Spawn a cloud-based review using multiple specialized agents for thorough analysis. Includes 3 free runs on Pro/Max, then requires usage credits. See [ultrareview](/en/ultrareview). Also available as `/ultrareview`
items.newcomerTip: Use for critical changes before shipping. More thorough than local `/code-review` but slower and uses credits

items.name: /deep-research
items.summary: Fan out web searches and synthesize a cited report
items.example: /deep-research what are the latest authentication best practices in 2026?
items.details: Research a question by fanning out web searches, fetching and cross-checking sources, and synthesizing a cited report. A **Workflow** (bundled dynamic workflow)
items.newcomerTip: Use for research tasks. Claude automatically finds multiple sources and verifies information

items.name: /autofix-pr
items.summary: Watch a PR and automatically push fixes
items.example:
/autofix-pr
/autofix-pr only fix lint and type errors
items.details: Spawn a cloud session that watches the current branch's PR and pushes fixes when CI fails or reviewers leave comments. Detects the PR from your checked-out branch with `gh pr view`. By default fixes all failures; pass a prompt for custom instructions. Requires `gh` CLI and Claude Code on the web access
items.newcomerTip: Use for automated PR maintenance. Claude responds to feedback and CI failures automatically

items.name: /web-setup
items.summary: Connect GitHub to Claude Code on the web
items.example: /web-setup
items.details: Connect your GitHub account to Claude Code on the web using your local `gh` CLI credentials. `/schedule` prompts for this automatically if GitHub isn't connected
items.newcomerTip: Run once to set up cloud sessions. After this, you can use `/teleport` and `/schedule`

items.name: /ide
items.summary: Manage IDE integrations and show status
items.example: /ide
items.details: View status of VS Code, JetBrains, and other IDE integrations. Manage connections and settings
items.newcomerTip: Use to verify your IDE is properly integrated with Claude Code

items.name: /chrome
items.summary: Configure Claude in Chrome browser integration
items.example: /chrome
items.details: Configure settings for Claude Code's Chrome extension, which lets Claude test local web applications and automate browser tasks
items.newcomerTip: Use when testing web apps locally. Claude can fill forms, click buttons, and verify UI

items.name: /advisor
items.summary: Enable or disable the advisor tool for second opinions
items.example:
/advisor opus
/advisor off
items.details: Enable a second Claude model to consult for guidance at key moments. Accepts `opus`, `sonnet`, `fable`, or a full model ID. Requires v2.1.98+
items.newcomerTip: Use when you want a second opinion on architectural decisions or complex implementations

items.name: /exit
items.summary: Exit the CLI or detach from a background session
items.example: /exit
items.details: Exit Claude Code. In an attached background session, this detaches (the session keeps running). Alias: `/quit`
items.newcomerTip: In background sessions, `/exit` detaches and returns to your shell. The session keeps working

items.name: /powerup
items.summary: Discover features through interactive lessons with demos
items.example: /powerup
items.details: Walk through quick interactive lessons on Claude Code features with animated demonstrations
items.newcomerTip: Great for onboarding. Shows visual demos of features like diffs, permissions, and workflows

items.name: /insights
items.summary: Generate a report analyzing your Claude Code sessions
items.example: /insights
items.details: Analyze your Claude Code usage over time—project areas, interaction patterns, friction points—to identify where you're spending time
items.newcomerTip: Use to understand your work patterns and find opportunities for automation

items.name: /team-onboarding
items.summary: Generate a team onboarding guide from your usage
items.example: /team-onboarding
items.details: Create a markdown onboarding guide from your Claude Code usage (past 30 days). Analyzes sessions, commands, MCP servers. For claude.ai subscribers, also returns a share link teammates can open in Claude Code
items.newcomerTip: Use before onboarding a teammate. This captures your actual workflow, not theory

items.name: /release-notes
items.summary: View the changelog in an interactive version picker
items.example: /release-notes
items.details: Browse release notes across Claude Code versions. Select a specific version to see changes, or choose to show all versions
items.newcomerTip: Use to check what changed in a new version or to see the features added since you last updated

items.name: /skills
items.summary: List available skills and toggle their visibility
items.example: /skills
items.details: Show all available skills. Press `t` to sort by token count. Press `Space` to hide a skill from Claude, then `Enter` to save to settings
items.newcomerTip: Use to see what skills are available and disable ones you don't want Claude to use

items.name: /plugin
items.summary: Manage Claude Code plugins and marketplaces
items.example:
/plugin
/plugin install skillcreator
/plugin disable myskill
items.details: Manage plugins and marketplaces. Open the plugin menu with no argument, or pass a subcommand to act directly. Plugins package skills, hooks, and integrations
items.newcomerTip: Use to add new features to Claude Code. Plugins are like extensions in VS Code

items.name: /reload-plugins
items.summary: Reload all active plugins to apply pending changes
items.example: /reload-plugins
items.details: Re-scan plugins to apply changes without restarting. Reports loaded components. Pass `--force` to reload even when it would invalidate the prompt cache
items.newcomerTip: Use after editing plugin files or skills in plugins. Faster than restarting

items.name: /reload-skills
items.summary: Re-scan skill directories for new/changed skills
items.example: /reload-skills
items.details: Pick up skills added or changed on disk during the session without restarting. Added in v2.1.152
items.newcomerTip: Use after creating a new skill in `.claude/skills/`. Faster than restarting

items.name: /install-github-app
items.summary: Install the Claude GitHub App for a repository
items.example: /install-github-app
items.details: Walk through installing the Claude GitHub App and optionally setting up GitHub Actions workflows and secrets
items.newcomerTip: Use once per repository to enable automatic PR reviews and CI integration

items.name: /install-slack-app
items.summary: Install the Claude Slack app
items.example: /install-slack-app
items.details: Opens browser to complete OAuth flow for Claude Slack integration. Let Claude respond to Slack messages
items.newcomerTip: Use to route bug reports from Slack directly to Claude Code sessions

items.name: /stickers
items.summary: Order Claude Code stickers
items.example: /stickers
items.details: Opens a form to order physical Claude Code stickers for your laptop
items.newcomerTip: For fun! Show your support for Claude Code

items.name: /radio
items.summary: Open Claude FM lo-fi radio in your browser
items.example: /radio
items.details: Stream lo-fi music while you code. Opens in browser; prints URL when no browser is available. Not available on Bedrock, Vertex, or Foundry
items.newcomerTip: For ambiance while coding. Use when you need background music

items.name: /mobile
items.summary: Show QR code to download the Claude mobile app
items.example: /mobile
items.details: Display QR code to download the Claude app on iOS or Android. Aliases: `/ios`, `/android`
items.newcomerTip: Useful for quickly getting the app on your phone without searching

items.name: /passes
items.summary: Share a free week of Claude Code with friends
items.example: /passes
items.details: Generate referral codes to share a free week of Claude Code. Only visible if your account is eligible
items.newcomerTip: Use to invite friends to try Claude Code. Everyone wins!

items.name: /upgrade
items.summary: Open upgrade page to switch to a higher plan
items.example: /upgrade
items.details: Open the upgrade page in your browser. Only visible on Pro and Max plans
items.newcomerTip: Use when you've hit usage limits and need more capacity

items.name: /privacy-settings
items.summary: View and update your privacy settings
items.example: /privacy-settings
items.details: Manage data sharing preferences. Only available for Pro and Max subscribers
items.newcomerTip: Review this to understand what data is collected and shared

items.name: /workflow
items.summary: Watch and manage running workflows
items.example: /workflows
items.details: Open the workflow progress view to watch, pause, resume, or save running and completed workflows (like `/batch`)
items.newcomerTip: Use to monitor large parallel tasks that are running in the background

items.name: /sandbox
items.summary: Toggle sandbox mode for restricted execution
items.example: /sandbox
items.details: Enable or disable sandbox mode, which restricts Claude's file access and command execution. Available on supported platforms only
items.newcomerTip: Use when working with untrusted code. Sandbox prevents accidental data loss

items.name: /heapdump
items.summary: Write a JavaScript heap snapshot for diagnosing memory usage
items.example: /heapdump
items.details: Write heap snapshot and memory breakdown to ~/Desktop (or home directory on Linux). For diagnosing high memory usage. See [troubleshooting](/en/troubleshooting#high-cpu-or-memory-usage)
items.newcomerTip: Use only when diagnosing memory issues. Share the output with Anthropic support if needed

items.name: Custom slash commands
items.summary: Create your own commands using skills and commands directory
items.details: You can create custom commands in two ways: (1) Skills: `.claude/skills/<name>/SKILL.md` or `.claude/commands/<name>.md`. Both create `/name` command. (2) Frontmatter: YAML frontmatter at the top of `SKILL.md` configures behavior with fields like `description`, `disable-model-invocation`, `allowed-tools`, `arguments`, etc. (3) Dynamic context: Use `` !`command` `` syntax to run shell commands and inject output into the skill before Claude sees it. (4) Arguments: Access with `$ARGUMENTS`, `$0`, `$1`, or named arguments like `$issue`
items.example:
Create `~/.claude/skills/commit/SKILL.md` with:
---
name: commit
description: Stage and commit changes
disable-model-invocation: true
---
Commit with message: $ARGUMENTS
items.newcomerTip: Skills are more powerful than commands. Start with a skill if you need supporting files, dynamic context, or auto-invocation control. Commands are simpler for one-off instructions

items.name: Skill frontmatter reference
items.summary: YAML configuration options for skills at the top of SKILL.md
items.details: Configure skill behavior with YAML frontmatter fields between `---` markers. Key fields: `name` (display name), `description` (when to invoke it), `disable-model-invocation: true` (only manual invocation), `user-invocable: false` (only Claude invocation), `allowed-tools` (tools available without prompts), `model` (override model), `effort` (override effort level), `context: fork` (run in subagent), `agent` (which subagent type), `arguments` (named positional args), `when_to_use` (additional context), `paths` (glob patterns to limit when activated), `shell` (bash or powershell)
items.example:
---
name: deploy
description: Deploy to production
disable-model-invocation: true
allowed-tools: Bash(git *) Bash(npm run deploy)
arguments: [environment, version]
---
items.newcomerTip: Most important fields: `description` (so Claude knows when to use it), `disable-model-invocation: true` (for destructive actions like deploy), `allowed-tools` (to avoid permission prompts)

items.name: Skill arguments and substitutions
items.summary: Pass and access arguments in skills using placeholders
items.details: Access arguments with `$ARGUMENTS` (all args), `$ARGUMENTS[0]` (first arg), `$0` (shorthand), or named args like `$issue` (if declared in `arguments` frontmatter). Other substitutions: `${CLAUDE_SESSION_ID}` (session ID), `${CLAUDE_EFFORT}` (current effort level), `${CLAUDE_SKILL_DIR}` (skill directory). Multi-word arguments need quotes: `/my-skill "hello world" second`. Escape literal `$` with backslash: `\$1.00`
items.example: Skill with `arguments: [issue, branch]` can use `$issue` and `$branch`. Or invoke with `/my-skill fix-123 main` and use `$0` and `$1`
items.newcomerTip: Named arguments are clearer than positional. Declare them in frontmatter and reference by name in skill content

items.name: Dynamic context injection in skills
items.summary: Run commands and inject output before Claude sees the skill
items.details: Use `` !`command` `` to run a shell command immediately and replace the placeholder with its output. The output is injected into the skill before Claude reads it, so Claude sees actual data, not the command. For multi-line commands, use ` ```! ` fence. Substitution runs once; command output is not re-scanned for more placeholders. To disable this for non-bundled skills, set `disableSkillShellExecution: true` in settings
items.example:
!`git diff HEAD` gets replaced with the actual diff before Claude sees it. Or a multi-line version:
```!
node --version
npm --version
git status
```
items.newcomerTip: Use this to inject live data into static skill instructions. Perfect for PR summaries, log analysis, or codebase snapshots that change between invocations

items.name: MCP prompts as commands
items.summary: MCP servers can expose prompts that work like commands
items.details: MCP servers can expose prompts which appear as commands with format `/mcp__<server>__<prompt>`. These are dynamically discovered from connected MCP servers. See [MCP prompts](/en/mcp#use-mcp-prompts-as-commands) for details
items.example:
/mcp__github__summarize-pr
/mcp__notion__create-page
items.newcomerTip: These appear alongside your regular commands when the MCP server is connected. Useful for exposing server-specific workflows

items.name: Command namespacing
items.summary: How commands are named in nested projects and plugins
items.details: Commands from different locations use different naming: Project `.claude/skills/deploy/` → `/deploy`. Nested `.claude/skills/` when clashing → `/path:deploy` (directory-qualified). Plugin `skills/deploy/` → `/plugin-name:deploy`. Personal `~/.claude/skills/deploy/` → `/deploy`. When nested and project commands share a name, Claude picks the one matching current files; use qualified name to run a specific variant
items.example: In a monorepo with `apps/web/.claude/skills/deploy/SKILL.md` and root deploy skill: `/deploy` runs the project root version, `/apps/web:deploy` runs the nested version
items.newcomerTip: Nested skills are great for monorepos. Each package can have its own deploy, test, build commands

items.name: Arguments and CLI flags
items.summary: Pass arguments to CLI and commands at startup
items.example:
claude -p 'write tests for the auth module'
claude --debug
claude --continue SESSION_ID
items.details: CLI flags control Claude Code startup behavior: `-p` for non-interactive prompt, `--debug` for debug logging, `--continue SESSION_ID` to resume, `--add-dir PATH` for extra file access, etc. See [CLI reference](/en/cli-reference) for complete list
items.newcomerTip: Most common: `-p` for scripting, `--debug` for troubleshooting, `--continue` to resume sessions

catalogs.overview: The Claude Code CLI (`claude` command) is the terminal interface for Claude Code, supporting interactive sessions, headless execution, session management, configuration, and MCP server administration. It provides comprehensive command-line flags for customizing behavior, permissions, system prompts, and model selection, plus subcommands for authentication, installation, and tool configuration.

items.name: claude
items.summary: Start an interactive Claude Code session
items.details: Launches an interactive session in the current directory. Claude reads your codebase, CLAUDE.md files, hooks, skills, plugins, and MCP servers. The session stays open for multi-turn conversation.
items.example: cd my-project && claude
items.newcomerTip: This is your main entry point. Run it from your project root and ask Claude Code natural language questions like 'fix this bug' or 'add a feature'.

items.name: claude <prompt>
items.summary: Start an interactive session with an initial prompt
items.details: Launches a session and immediately sends your prompt to Claude, skipping the initial blank prompt.
items.example: claude "write tests for the auth module"
items.newcomerTip:
Combine with backslash to create multiline prompts: claude "write tests for
the auth module".

items.name: -p / --print
items.summary: Headless mode: run a task and exit without interaction
items.details: Executes a prompt non-interactively, prints the response, and exits. Useful for scripting, CI/CD, piping, and batch operations. Does not save a session transcript by default (use in combination with other flags to persist).
items.example: claude -p "check for security issues in the auth module"
items.newcomerTip: Use -p when you want Claude to complete a task and get back to your script without an interactive session.

items.name: -c / --continue
items.summary: Resume the most recent session in the current directory
items.details: Loads the last conversation you had in this project and reopens it. Includes sessions that added this directory with `/add-dir`. The conversation history and context are preserved.
items.example: claude --continue
items.newcomerTip: No need to remember session IDs—just use -c to pick up where you left off in the current project.

items.name: -r / --resume <session>
items.summary: Resume a specific session by ID, name, or interactive picker
items.details: Resume a conversation by session ID (UUID), session name (string), or open an interactive picker to choose. Background sessions appear marked with 'bg' in the picker (v2.1.144+).
items.example: claude --resume auth-refactor
items.newcomerTip: Name your sessions with `--name` (e.g., `claude -n "auth-refactor"`) so they're easy to resume later.

items.name: -n / --name
items.summary: Give the session a display name shown in /resume and the terminal title
items.details: Names are displayed in the session picker and terminal title bar. You can resume a named session later with `claude --resume <name>`. The `/rename` command changes the name mid-session.
items.example: claude -n "my-feature-work"
items.newcomerTip: Use descriptive names like 'fix-login-bug' or 'feature-dark-mode' so you can find sessions later.

items.name: -w / --worktree <name>
items.summary: Start Claude in an isolated git worktree for feature work
items.details: Creates or uses an existing git worktree at `.claude/worktrees/<name>` with isolated context, file history, and checkpoints. If no name is given, one is auto-generated. Pass `#<number>` or a GitHub PR URL to fetch that PR from `origin` and branch from it. Combine with `--tmux` to create a tmux session alongside the worktree.
items.example: claude -w feature-auth
items.newcomerTip: Use worktrees for large features that span multiple turns. Each worktree has its own checkpoint history, so you can experiment without losing your main branch.

items.name: --model <model>
items.summary: Set the AI model for this session only (overrides settings)
items.details: Use a full model ID like `claude-sonnet-4-6` or an alias (`sonnet`, `opus`, `haiku`, `fable`). Takes precedence over the `model` setting and `ANTHROPIC_MODEL` env var. Does not persist; next session uses the default.
items.example: claude --model opus
items.newcomerTip: Switch models mid-session with `/model`. Use Opus for complex reasoning, Sonnet for speed, Haiku for simple tasks.

items.name: --effort <level>
items.summary: Set reasoning effort: low, medium, high, xhigh, max, or ultracode
items.details: Controls how much compute the model spends on your task. Available levels depend on the model. `max` and `ultracode` are session-only and do not persist. `ultracode` combines xhigh reasoning with automatic workflow orchestration.
items.example: claude --effort high
items.newcomerTip: Use high effort for complex tasks like architecture design. Use low for quick questions to save tokens.

items.name: --permission-mode <mode>
items.summary: Begin in a specific permission mode: default, acceptEdits, plan, auto, dontAsk, or bypassPermissions
items.details: `default` = ask for permission, `acceptEdits` = auto-accept file edits, `plan` = show plan before executing, `auto` = ML classifier, `dontAsk` = auto-approve common operations, `bypassPermissions` = skip all checks. Overrides `defaultMode` from settings.
items.example: claude --permission-mode plan
items.newcomerTip: Start in plan mode for large changes to see what Claude plans before it runs. Use auto mode for faster workflows with trained auto-approval rules.

items.name: --allowedTools / --allowed-tools
items.summary: Tools that execute without permission prompts (space-separated list)
items.details: Supports glob patterns like `Bash(git log *)` or `Bash(npm run test *)`. Use `Read` to allow all file reads. Restrict entire tool families with patterns. Example: `--allowedTools "Bash(npm run lint)" "Bash(npm run test *)"`
items.example: claude --allowedTools "Bash(git log *)" "Read" "Edit"
items.newcomerTip: Use permission rules to reduce prompts in scripted CI tasks. Start narrow and expand as needed.

items.name: --disallowedTools / --disallowed-tools
items.summary: Deny rules: block specific tool calls or remove tools from Claude's context
items.details: A bare name like `Edit` removes the tool entirely. A scoped rule like `Bash(rm *)` leaves the tool available but denies matching patterns. Use `"*"` to deny all tools, `"mcp__*"` to deny all MCP tools.
items.example: claude --disallowedTools "Bash(rm *)" "Edit"
items.newcomerTip: Use deny rules to prevent dangerous operations like `rm -rf` in sensitive repos.

items.name: --append-system-prompt
items.summary: Append custom text to the default system prompt
items.details: Preserves Claude Code's default identity, tool guidance, and safety instructions while adding your extra rules. Use for per-invocation instructions, output formatting, or domain context.
items.example: claude --append-system-prompt "Always use TypeScript and prefer async/await"
items.newcomerTip: Use append when you want Claude to stay a coding assistant but follow extra rules. Use `--system-prompt` to replace the entire prompt instead.

items.name: --append-system-prompt-file
items.summary: Load additional system prompt text from a file and append
items.details: Reads a text file and appends its contents to the default system prompt, similar to `--append-system-prompt` but from a file. Useful for long or shared prompt rules.
items.example: claude --append-system-prompt-file ./company-standards.txt
items.newcomerTip: Store team coding standards in a file and reference it with this flag.

items.name: --system-prompt
items.summary: Replace the entire system prompt with custom text
items.details: Replaces the default prompt completely. Use when Claude's identity, tools, or safety instructions need to change (e.g., for a non-coding agent in a pipeline). You take responsibility for tool guidance and safety.
items.example: claude --system-prompt "You are a security auditor. Analyze code for vulnerabilities."
items.newcomerTip: Use append flags instead unless you specifically need a different identity. Replacing drops important tool guidance.

items.name: --system-prompt-file
items.summary: Load system prompt from a file, replacing the default
items.details: Reads a file and uses it as the entire system prompt, replacing Claude Code's defaults. Mutually exclusive with `--system-prompt`.
items.example: claude --system-prompt-file ./custom-prompt.txt
items.newcomerTip: Store custom prompts in files for reuse across multiple invocations.

items.name: --add-dir <path>
items.summary: Add additional working directories for file access during the session
items.details: Grants file read/write access to extra directories outside the project. Configuration files (CLAUDE.md, settings.json, .mcp.json) are NOT discovered from added directories, only from the main project. Resume from added directories with `--continue` or `--resume`.
items.example: claude --add-dir ../apps ../lib
items.newcomerTip: Use when your task involves multiple directories. To make this permanent, set `permissions.additionalDirectories` in settings.

items.name: --max-turns <n>
items.summary: Limit agentic turns in print mode (headless only)
items.details: Exits with an error when the turn limit is reached. No limit by default. Use in CI/CD to prevent runaway operations.
items.example: claude -p --max-turns 3 "check the build"
items.newcomerTip: Add a turn limit in automation to prevent infinite loops or runaway API usage.

items.name: --output-format <format>
items.summary: Output format in print mode: text (default), json, or stream-json
items.details: `text` = human-readable, `json` = full session payload at end, `stream-json` = newline-delimited JSON events as they occur. `stream-json` enables advanced flags like `--include-partial-messages` and `--include-hook-events`.
items.example: claude -p --output-format json "analyze this code"
items.newcomerTip: Use json/stream-json when scripting to parse Claude's structured responses programmatically.

items.name: --input-format <format>
items.summary: Input format for print mode: text or stream-json
items.details: Specifies how input is encoded when piping into Claude. `stream-json` expects newline-delimited JSON events.
items.example: cat input.json | claude -p --input-format stream-json "process this"
items.newcomerTip: Use stream-json when piping structured data from other tools.

items.name: --verbose
items.summary: Enable verbose logging showing full turn-by-turn output
items.details: Shows detailed tool calls, reasoning, and intermediate steps. Overrides the `viewMode` setting for this session only.
items.example: claude --verbose
items.newcomerTip: Turn on verbose to see Claude's full reasoning when debugging or learning how it works.

items.name: --debug [category]
items.summary: Enable debug mode with optional category filtering (e.g., 'api,hooks' or '!statsig,!file')
items.details: Logs internal events for troubleshooting. Categories can be included (comma-separated) or excluded (with ! prefix). Useful for diagnosing hook, MCP, or API issues.
items.example: claude --debug "api,mcp"
items.newcomerTip: Use debug when something isn't working. Include the debug output when reporting issues to Anthropic.

items.name: --debug-file <path>
items.summary: Write debug logs to a specific file path
items.details: Implicitly enables debug mode. Takes precedence over `CLAUDE_CODE_DEBUG_LOGS_DIR` env var. Useful for long-running sessions or CI logs.
items.example: claude --debug-file /tmp/claude-debug.log
items.newcomerTip: Use this in CI to capture logs to a file for post-mortem analysis.

items.name: --bare
items.summary: Minimal mode: skip auto-discovery of hooks, skills, plugins, MCP servers, auto-memory, and CLAUDE.md
items.details: Starts faster for scripted calls. Claude has access to Bash, Read, and Edit only. Sets `CLAUDE_CODE_SIMPLE` env var. Used internally for performance.
items.example: claude --bare -p "quick syntax check"
items.newcomerTip: Use --bare in CI when you don't need customizations and want to start immediately.

items.name: --safe-mode
items.summary: Start with all customizations disabled to troubleshoot broken configs
items.details: Disables CLAUDE.md, skills, plugins, hooks, MCP servers, custom commands, agents, themes, keybindings, and auto-memory. Built-in tools, auth, and permissions work normally. Managed settings still apply.
items.example: claude --safe-mode
items.newcomerTip: Use safe mode if Claude Code is crashing or behaving weirdly. If it works in safe mode, a plugin or hook is the culprit.

items.name: --dangerously-skip-permissions
items.summary: Skip permission prompts (equivalent to --permission-mode bypassPermissions)
items.details: Does not skip certain safety checks like syntax validation or dangerous operations. Only bypasses user permission dialogs. Use with caution.
items.example: claude --dangerously-skip-permissions -p "auto-fix the linter errors"
items.newcomerTip: Reserve this for trusted CI tasks. It removes the safety confirmation but not all safety checks.

items.name: --allow-dangerously-skip-permissions
items.summary: Add bypassPermissions to the Shift+Tab mode cycle without starting in it
items.details: Lets you begin in a safer mode like `plan`, then switch to `bypassPermissions` later using Shift+Tab. Useful when you want an escape hatch but don't start permissionless.
