items.example: claude --permission-mode plan --allow-dangerously-skip-permissions
items.newcomerTip: Use this to have an escape hatch for stuck sessions without skipping safety by default.

items.name: --bg / --background
items.summary: Start as a background agent and return immediately
items.details: Prints the session ID and management commands. The session keeps running on Anthropic infrastructure. Monitor with `claude agents` or `claude logs <id>`. Combine with `--exec` to run a shell command as a background job instead.
items.example: claude --bg "investigate the flaky test"
items.newcomerTip: Use background mode for long-running tasks you don't want to block your terminal.

items.name: --remote
items.summary: Create a new web session on claude.ai with the provided task
items.details: Starts a Claude Code on the Web session (cloud-hosted) instead of local. Requires a Claude.ai subscription. Useful for long-running tasks or when you don't have your local environment.
items.example: claude --remote "Fix the login bug"
items.newcomerTip: Use --remote for large tasks that might take hours, so your local machine stays free.

items.name: --remote-control / --rc
items.summary: Enable Remote Control so you can also control the session from claude.ai
items.details: Makes the local session available for remote control from the Claude web app. Optionally pass a name for the session. Use `/remote-control` inside a session to enable it mid-stream.
items.example: claude --remote-control "My Project"
items.newcomerTip: Enable this when you might need to continue work on your phone or another device.

items.name: --teleport
items.summary: Resume a web session in your local terminal
items.details: Loads a Claude Code on the Web session into your terminal. Opens a picker to choose which session. Requires a Claude.ai subscription.
items.example: claude --teleport
items.newcomerTip: Use this to pull a cloud session back to your laptop for full IDE and tool access.

items.name: --mcp-config <path-or-json>
items.summary: Load MCP servers from JSON files or inline JSON (space-separated)
items.details: Pass paths to .json files or inline JSON strings. Accepts multiple values. Works with `--strict-mcp-config` to use ONLY these servers.
items.example: claude --mcp-config ./mcp.json ./mcp-local.json
items.newcomerTip: Use this to test MCP configurations without committing them to .mcp.json.

items.name: --strict-mcp-config
items.summary: Only use MCP servers from --mcp-config, ignore all other MCP configurations
items.details: Disables .mcp.json, ~/.claude.json, and claude.ai connectors. Must be paired with `--mcp-config`.
items.example: claude --strict-mcp-config --mcp-config ./mcp-test.json
items.newcomerTip: Use this for isolated testing of a single MCP server configuration.

items.name: --plugin-dir <path>
items.summary: Load a plugin from a directory or .zip for this session only
items.details: Each flag takes one path. Repeat the flag for multiple plugins: `--plugin-dir A --plugin-dir B.zip`. Does not persist to settings.
items.example: claude --plugin-dir ./my-plugin --plugin-dir ./another.zip
items.newcomerTip: Use this to test a plugin you're developing before installing it globally.

items.name: --plugin-url <url>
items.summary: Fetch a plugin .zip from a URL for this session only
items.details: Repeat for multiple plugins or pass space-separated URLs in a single quoted value.
items.example: claude --plugin-url https://example.com/plugin.zip
items.newcomerTip: Use this to quickly test a plugin hosted on the web without downloading it.

items.name: --agents <json>
items.summary: Define custom subagents dynamically via JSON
items.details: Uses frontmatter field names plus a `prompt` field for instructions. Allows inline agent definitions without creating skill files.
items.example: claude --agents '{"reviewer":{"description":"Reviews code","prompt":"You are a code reviewer"}}'
items.newcomerTip: Use this for quick one-off agent definitions in scripts.

items.name: --agent <name>
items.summary: Run as a named subagent (overrides the `agent` setting)
items.details: Specifies which agent configuration to use for this session. Useful in CI or scripts that delegate to specific agents.
items.example: claude --agent code-reviewer
items.newcomerTip: Use this when you have different agents for different tasks, like a code-reviewer vs. feature-builder.

items.name: --advisor <model>
items.summary: Enable the advisor tool with a specific model (opus, sonnet, fable, or full ID)
items.details: The advisor consults a second model for guidance at key moments. Takes precedence over the `advisorModel` setting. Requires Claude Code v2.1.98+.
items.example: claude --advisor opus
items.newcomerTip: Enable an advisor for complex tasks where a second opinion helps. Opus advisors are most thorough but cost more.

items.name: --version / -v
items.summary: Print the Claude Code version and exit
items.details: Shows the installed version number. Useful for checking if you're on the latest release.
items.example: claude --version
items.newcomerTip: Use this to confirm you have the latest version, especially after updating.

items.name: claude update
items.summary: Update Claude Code to the latest version
items.details: Checks for updates and installs the newest release. Native installations auto-update in the background; Homebrew and WinGet require manual updates.
items.example: claude update
items.newcomerTip: Run this regularly to get the latest features and security fixes.

items.name: claude install [version]
items.summary: Install or reinstall the native binary at a specific version
items.details: Accepts `stable`, `latest`, or a version like `2.1.118`. Useful for pinning a version or recovering from a broken install.
items.example: claude install stable
items.newcomerTip: Use `stable` for production; `latest` gets features faster but may have regressions.

items.name: claude auth login
items.summary: Sign in to your Anthropic account
items.details: Supports `--email` to pre-fill, `--sso` to force SSO, and `--console` to use Anthropic Console (API key billing) instead of Claude subscription.
items.example: claude auth login --console
items.newcomerTip: Run this on first install. Use --console if you have an API key instead of a subscription.

items.name: claude auth logout
items.summary: Sign out from your Anthropic account
items.details: Clears stored authentication. You'll be prompted to log in again on next use.
items.example: claude auth logout
items.newcomerTip: Use this when switching accounts or on a shared machine.

items.name: claude auth status
items.summary: Show authentication status as JSON (or human-readable with --text)
items.details: Exits code 0 if logged in, 1 if not. Useful for scripts that check auth state.
items.example: claude auth status --text
items.newcomerTip: Use this in CI scripts to verify you're authenticated before running Claude Code.

items.name: claude mcp get <name>
items.summary: Get details for a specific MCP server
items.details: Shows type, URL, scope, OAuth status, and other configuration details.
items.example: claude mcp get github
items.newcomerTip: Use this to verify a server's config before using it.

items.name: claude mcp remove <name>
items.summary: Remove an MCP server
items.details: Deletes the configuration. The server is no longer available in new sessions.
items.example: claude mcp remove github
items.newcomerTip: Use this to clean up servers you no longer need.

items.name: claude mcp login <name>
items.summary: Run OAuth flow from the command line (v2.1.186+)
items.details: Authenticate an MCP server without opening a Claude Code session. Pass `--no-browser` for SSH or headless environments. Requires interactive terminal.
items.example: claude mcp login sentry --no-browser
items.newcomerTip: Use this before running CI tasks that need authenticated MCP servers.

items.name: claude mcp logout <name>
items.summary: Clear stored OAuth credentials for an MCP server (v2.1.186+)
items.details: Revokes access without removing the server configuration.
items.example: claude mcp logout sentry
items.newcomerTip: Use this to re-authenticate if a token expires or you want to revoke access.

items.name: claude mcp serve
items.summary: Run Claude Code itself as an MCP server
items.details: Starts Claude Code in stdio MCP mode so other applications (e.g., Claude Desktop) can connect to it and use Claude's tools (Read, Edit, Bash, etc.).
items.example: claude mcp serve
items.newcomerTip: Use this to integrate Claude Code's tools into Claude Desktop or other MCP clients.

items.name: claude config [key=value ...]
items.summary: Open Settings interface or set individual options via command line
items.details: Without arguments, opens interactive tabs for theme, model, output style, etc. With key=value pairs (v2.1.181+), sets options directly. Run `claude config --help` to list all settable keys.
items.example: claude config theme=dark model=sonnet
items.newcomerTip: Use the interactive interface for first-time setup. Use key=value in scripts.

items.name: claude doctor
items.summary: Diagnose your Claude Code installation and configuration
items.details: Checks installation health, settings validity, MCP server status, context usage, and other diagnostics. Shows status icons and suggestions. Press 'f' to auto-fix issues if found.
items.example: claude doctor
items.newcomerTip: Run this if something isn't working. It usually spots the problem quickly.

items.name: claude agents
items.summary: Open agent view to monitor and dispatch background sessions
items.details: Shows active and completed background sessions. Use `--cwd` to filter by directory, `--json` to output JSON for scripting, `--json --all` to include completed sessions. Accepts `--permission-mode`, `--model`, `--effort`, `--agent` to set defaults.
items.example: claude agents --json
items.newcomerTip: Use this to manage long-running background tasks and see their status without attaching.

items.name: claude attach <id>
items.summary: Attach to a background session in this terminal
items.details: Takes over control of a running background session. Exit with Ctrl+C to detach (session keeps running).
items.example: claude attach 7c5dcf5d
items.newcomerTip: Use this to watch a background task in real-time or intervene if needed.

items.name: claude stop / claude kill <id>
items.summary: Stop a background session
items.details: Terminates the session gracefully. The transcript is saved and can be resumed with `claude --resume`.
items.example: claude stop 7c5dcf5d
items.newcomerTip: Use this to clean up background sessions you no longer need.

items.name: claude logs <id>
items.summary: Print recent output from a background session
items.details: Shows the last output without attaching. Useful for quick checks.
items.example: claude logs 7c5dcf5d
items.newcomerTip: Use this to peek at what a background session is doing without fully attaching.

items.name: claude respawn <id>
items.summary: Restart a background session with its conversation intact
items.details: Useful after updating Claude Code. Use `--all` to restart every running session.
items.example: claude respawn 7c5dcf5d
items.newcomerTip: Use this when you update Claude Code and background sessions need to pick up the new binary.

items.name: claude rm <id>
items.summary: Remove a background session from the list
items.details: Cleans up the session entry. The transcript is still saved and can be resumed with `claude --resume`.
items.example: claude rm 7c5dcf5d
items.newcomerTip: Use this to clean up the background session list without losing the transcript.

items.name: claude daemon status
items.summary: Print background-session supervisor status (version, socket, workers)
items.details: Exits 1 if supervisor isn't running. Useful for diagnosing multi-session issues.
items.example: claude daemon status
items.newcomerTip: Run this if background sessions aren't working.

items.name: claude daemon stop --any
items.summary: Stop the background-session supervisor
items.details: Stops the supervisor and all hosted sessions. Use `--keep-workers` to leave sessions running (supervisor reconnects on next use).
items.example: claude daemon stop --any --keep-workers
items.newcomerTip: Use this to recover from an unresponsive supervisor.

items.name: claude setup-token
items.summary: Generate a long-lived OAuth token for CI and scripts
items.details: Prints the token to stdout without saving it. Requires Claude subscription. Use with `ANTHROPIC_AUTH_TOKEN` env var in scripts.
items.example: claude setup-token
items.newcomerTip: Use this to create a CI token. Store it securely (GitHub Secrets, etc.).

items.name: claude project purge [path]
items.summary: Delete all local Claude Code state for a project
items.details: Removes transcripts, task lists, debug logs, file history, prompt history, and the project entry in ~/.claude.json. Flags: `--dry-run` to preview, `-y`/`--yes` to skip confirmation, `-i`/`--interactive` to confirm each item, `--all` for every project.
items.example: claude project purge ~/work/repo --dry-run
items.newcomerTip: Use this to start fresh in a project without losing code.

items.name: claude remote-control
items.summary: Start a Remote Control server to control Claude Code from claude.ai or the Claude app
items.details: Runs in server mode (no local interactive session). Supports server-mode flags like `--name`, `--model`, `--permission-mode`.
items.example: claude remote-control --name "My Project"
items.newcomerTip: Use this when you want to control Claude Code from your phone or another device.

items.name: claude ultrareview [target]
items.summary: Run a deep multi-agent code review in the cloud (non-interactive)
items.details: Prints findings to stdout, exits 0 on success or 1 on failure. Use `--json` for raw payload, `--timeout <minutes>` to override 30-minute default. Alias: `claude /code-review ultra`
items.example: claude ultrareview 1234 --json
items.newcomerTip: Use this in CI for automated code reviews without needing an interactive session.

items.name: --init
items.summary: Run Setup hooks before the session (print mode only)
items.details: Executes hooks with `init` matcher before the conversation starts, allowing one-time setup per invocation.
items.example: claude -p --init "run tests"
items.newcomerTip: Use this in CI to ensure fresh environment setup before each run.

items.name: --init-only
items.summary: Run Setup and SessionStart hooks, then exit without conversation
items.details: Useful for one-time initialization before starting interactive work.
items.example: claude --init-only
items.newcomerTip: Use this to set up a project environment once before interactive sessions.

items.name: --exclude-dynamic-system-prompt-sections
items.summary: Move per-machine system prompt sections into first user message (improves prompt cache reuse)
items.details: Helps multi-user/multi-machine tasks reuse cached prompts. Use with `-p` for scripted workloads.
items.example: claude -p --exclude-dynamic-system-prompt-sections "query"
items.newcomerTip: Use this in shared CI environments to improve cache hit rates and reduce costs.

items.name: --fork-session
items.summary: Create a new session ID instead of reusing the original when resuming
items.details: Use with `--resume` or `--continue` to branch off a copy of an old session.
items.example: claude --resume abc123 --fork-session
items.newcomerTip: Use this when you want to try a different approach without overwriting the original session.

items.name: --from-pr
items.summary: Resume sessions linked to a specific PR
items.details: Accepts PR number, GitHub URL, GitLab URL, or Bitbucket URL. Sessions are linked automatically when Claude creates PRs.
items.example: claude --from-pr 123
items.newcomerTip: Use this to continue work on a PR that Claude Code previously opened.

items.name: --ide
items.summary: Auto-connect to IDE on startup if exactly one is available
items.details: Connects to VS Code, Cursor, JetBrains IDE, etc., if only one is running.
items.example: claude --ide
items.newcomerTip: Use this to automatically set up IDE integration on startup.

items.name: --session-id
items.summary: Use a specific session ID (UUID format)
items.details: Allows manual session ID specification instead of auto-generation.
items.newcomerTip: Use this in CI when you need reproducible session IDs.

items.name: --setting-sources
items.summary: Comma-separated list of setting sources to load (user, project, local)
items.details: Allows selective loading of configuration sources. Example: `--setting-sources user,project` skips local settings.
items.example: claude --setting-sources user,project
items.newcomerTip: Use this to ignore local settings in CI while keeping shared project/user settings.

items.name: --settings
items.summary: Path to settings JSON file or inline JSON string (overrides settings files for session)
items.details: Values set here override the same keys in settings files. Omitted keys keep their file-based values. Does not persist.
items.example: claude --settings ./settings.json
items.newcomerTip: Use this to test settings changes without editing files.

items.name: --betas
items.summary: Beta headers to include in API requests (API key users only)
items.details: Allows opting into experimental API features. Example: `--betas interleaved-thinking`
items.example: claude --betas interleaved-thinking
items.newcomerTip: Use this when Anthropic announces new beta features you want to test.

items.name: --fallback-model
items.summary: Enable automatic fallback to specified model(s) when primary is overloaded or retired
items.details: Accepts comma-separated list tried in order. Takes precedence over the `fallbackModel` setting.
items.example: claude --fallback-model sonnet,haiku
items.newcomerTip: Use this in production to gracefully handle model unavailability.

items.name: --json-schema
items.summary: Get validated JSON output matching a JSON Schema after agent completes (print mode only)
items.details: Uses structured outputs feature. Claude formats response to match the schema you provide.
items.example: claude -p --json-schema '{"type":"object","properties":{...}}' "query"
items.newcomerTip: Use this to ensure Claude's output is machine-readable JSON that matches your schema.

items.name: --max-budget-usd
items.summary: Maximum dollar amount to spend on API calls before stopping (print mode only)
items.details: Exits when budget is exceeded.
items.example: claude -p --max-budget-usd 5.00 "expensive task"
items.newcomerTip: Use this in CI to prevent runaway costs on expensive operations.

items.name: --no-session-persistence
items.summary: Disable session saving (print mode only)
items.details: Sessions are not saved to disk and cannot be resumed. Sets `CLAUDE_CODE_SKIP_PROMPT_HISTORY` env var.
items.example: claude -p --no-session-persistence "one-time query"
items.newcomerTip: Use this for sensitive one-off queries that shouldn't be saved.

items.name: --permission-prompt-tool
items.summary: Specify an MCP tool to handle permission prompts in non-interactive mode
items.details: Allows custom permission handling via MCP tool in headless mode.
items.example: claude -p --permission-prompt-tool mcp_auth_tool "query"
items.newcomerTip: Use this to integrate custom permission logic in CI workflows.

items.name: --prompt-suggestions
items.summary: Emit predicted next user prompt after each turn (requires stream-json output)
items.details: Shows `prompt_suggestion` messages in stream-json mode.
items.example: claude -p --prompt-suggestions --output-format stream-json --verbose "query"
items.newcomerTip: Use this in advanced automation to anticipate what Claude suggests next.

items.name: --include-hook-events
items.summary: Include all hook lifecycle events in output (requires stream-json)
items.details: Shows hook execution details in stream-json output for monitoring.
items.example: claude -p --output-format stream-json --include-hook-events --verbose "query"
items.newcomerTip: Use this to debug hooks in CI workflows.

items.name: --include-partial-messages
items.summary: Include partial streaming events in output (requires stream-json and --print)
items.details: Shows intermediate token-by-token events for real-time monitoring.
items.example: claude -p --output-format stream-json --include-partial-messages --verbose "query"
items.newcomerTip: Use this for real-time progress monitoring in CI or custom UIs.

items.name: --chrome
items.summary: Enable Chrome browser integration for web automation and testing
items.details: Gives Claude access to a Chrome browser for scraping, testing, and automation.
items.example: claude --chrome
items.newcomerTip: Use this when Claude needs to test or interact with web applications.

items.name: --no-chrome
items.summary: Disable Chrome integration for this session
items.details: Overrides Chrome settings for the current session only.
items.example: claude --no-chrome
items.newcomerTip: Use this if Chrome integration is causing issues.

items.name: --channels
items.summary: MCP servers whose channel notifications Claude should listen for (research preview)
items.details: Space-separated list of `plugin:<name>@<marketplace>` entries. Requires Claude.ai authentication.
items.example: claude --channels plugin:my-notifier@my-marketplace
items.newcomerTip: Use this to connect webhook-based MCP servers that push events into sessions.

items.name: --dangerously-load-development-channels
items.summary: Enable channels not on the approved allowlist for local development
items.details: Accepts `plugin:<name>@<marketplace>` and `server:<name>`. Prompts for confirmation.
items.example: claude --dangerously-load-development-channels server:webhook
items.newcomerTip: Use this when testing a custom channel you're developing.

items.name: --tmux
items.summary: Create a tmux session for the worktree (requires --worktree)
items.details: Uses iTerm2 native panes when available; pass `--tmux=classic` for traditional tmux.
items.example: claude -w feature-auth --tmux
items.newcomerTip: Use this to get a split-pane setup when working with worktrees.

items.name: --teammate-mode
items.summary: Set how agent team teammates display: in-process (default), auto, tmux, or iterm2 (v2.1.186+)
items.details: Controls visual display mode for subagent teams. Overrides `teammateMode` setting for this session.
items.example: claude --teammate-mode auto
items.newcomerTip: Use auto or tmux when working with multiple subagents simultaneously.

items.name: --exec
items.summary: Run a shell command as a PTY-backed background job instead of a Claude session
items.details: Use with `--bg` to launch from shell. Example: `claude --bg --exec 'pytest -x'` runs pytest in background.
items.example: claude --bg --exec 'pytest -x'
items.newcomerTip: Use this to run long-running shell tasks as background jobs managed by Claude Code.

items.name: --tools
items.summary: Restrict which built-in tools Claude can use: "" (none), "default" (all), or tool names
items.details: Example: `--tools "Bash,Edit,Read"`. MCP tools are not affected; use `--disallowedTools "mcp__*"` to deny those.
items.example: claude --tools "Bash,Edit,Read"
items.newcomerTip: Use this to sandbo Claude in sensitive repos by removing dangerous tools.

items.name: --ax-screen-reader
items.summary: Render screen-reader friendly output (flat text, no borders) v2.1.181+
items.details: Forces classic renderer, ignores `tui` setting, takes precedence over `CLAUDE_AX_SCREEN_READER` env var and settings.
items.example: claude --ax-screen-reader
items.newcomerTip: Use this if you're using a screen reader to access Claude Code.

items.name: --replay-user-messages
items.summary: Re-emit user messages from stdin back on stdout (requires stream-json i/o)
items.details: Requires `--input-format stream-json` and `--output-format stream-json`.
items.example: claude -p --input-format stream-json --output-format stream-json --replay-user-messages
items.newcomerTip: Use this in advanced scripting to echo back your inputs for logging.

items.name: --maintenance
items.summary: Run Setup hooks with maintenance matcher before session (print mode only)
items.details: Executes hooks configured with `maintenance` matcher.
items.example: claude -p --maintenance "query"
items.newcomerTip: Use this in scheduled CI tasks to run maintenance hooks automatically.

items.name: --remote-control-session-name-prefix
items.summary: Prefix for auto-generated Remote Control session names (defaults to hostname)
items.details: Set via flag or `CLAUDE_REMOTE_CONTROL_SESSION_NAME_PREFIX` env var.
items.example: claude remote-control --remote-control-session-name-prefix dev-box
items.newcomerTip: Use this to customize session naming in multi-machine setups.

items.name: ANTHROPIC_MODEL
items.summary: Environment variable to set default model (overridden by --model flag and settings)
items.details: Example: `export ANTHROPIC_MODEL=claude-opus-4-6`
items.example: ANTHROPIC_MODEL=claude-sonnet-4-6 claude
items.newcomerTip: Set this globally if you prefer a specific model, or per-command with the --model flag.

items.name: CLAUDE_CODE_SIMPLE
items.summary: Environment variable set by --bare flag; disables customizations for speed
items.details: Automatically set when using --bare; can also be set manually.
items.example: CLAUDE_CODE_SIMPLE=1 claude -p "query"
items.newcomerTip: Set this when scripting to skip slow startup in trusted, simple tasks.

items.name: CLAUDE_CODE_DEBUG_LOGS_DIR
items.summary: Directory for debug logs (overridden by --debug-file flag)
items.details: Example: `export CLAUDE_CODE_DEBUG_LOGS_DIR=/tmp/claude-logs`
items.example: CLAUDE_CODE_DEBUG_LOGS_DIR=/tmp/claude-logs claude
items.newcomerTip: Set this to collect debug logs in a specific location for troubleshooting.

items.name: CLAUDE_CODE_SAFE_MODE
items.summary: Environment variable set by --safe-mode flag
items.details: Automatically set when using --safe-mode.
items.example: CLAUDE_CODE_SAFE_MODE=1 claude
items.newcomerTip: Set this if you suspect a customization is causing issues.

items.name: ENABLE_TOOL_SEARCH
items.summary: Control MCP tool deferral: true (deferred), auto (threshold), or false (upfront)
items.details: Values: unset=deferred, true=deferred everywhere, auto=threshold mode, false=upfront.
items.example: ENABLE_TOOL_SEARCH=auto:5 claude
items.newcomerTip: Use auto mode to balance context usage and tool availability with many MCP servers.

items.name: MCP_TIMEOUT
items.summary: Global MCP server startup timeout in milliseconds
items.details: Example: `MCP_TIMEOUT=10000` = 10 seconds.
items.example: MCP_TIMEOUT=10000 claude
items.newcomerTip: Increase this if slow MCP servers are timing out on startup.

items.name: MCP_TOOL_TIMEOUT
items.summary: Default MCP tool execution timeout in milliseconds (can be overridden per-server)
items.details: Per-server timeout in .mcp.json overrides this value.
items.example: MCP_TOOL_TIMEOUT=60000 claude
items.newcomerTip: Increase this if MCP tools are timing out on slow operations.

items.name: MAX_MCP_OUTPUT_TOKENS
items.summary: Maximum MCP tool output tokens (default 25,000)
items.details: Tools declaring `anthropic/maxResultSizeChars` use that value instead.
items.example: MAX_MCP_OUTPUT_TOKENS=50000 claude
items.newcomerTip: Increase this if MCP tools produce large outputs that are being truncated.

items.name: CLAUDE_CODE_MCP_TOOL_IDLE_TIMEOUT
items.summary: Idle timeout for remote MCP tool calls in milliseconds (v2.1.187+, default 5 min)
items.details: Tool calls that send no response/progress for this period abort. Set to 0 to disable.
items.example: CLAUDE_CODE_MCP_TOOL_IDLE_TIMEOUT=600000 claude
items.newcomerTip: Increase this if remote MCP tools are timing out on long operations.

items.name: USE_BUILTIN_RIPGREP
items.summary: Use built-in ripgrep (default) or system-installed ripgrep (set to 0)
items.details: Set to 0 to use system ripgrep if bundled version doesn't work.
items.example: USE_BUILTIN_RIPGREP=0 claude
items.newcomerTip: Set to 0 if search isn't finding files on your system.

items.name: ENABLE_CLAUDEAI_MCP_SERVERS
items.summary: Enable/disable claude.ai MCP connectors (default enabled)
items.details: Set to false to disable cloud connectors in local sessions.
items.example: ENABLE_CLAUDEAI_MCP_SERVERS=false claude
items.newcomerTip: Set to false if you want to use only local MCP servers.

items.name: CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR
items.summary: Set by Claude Code for MCP servers and hooks; identifies the project root
items.details: Available in stdio MCP server environment. Example: `process.env.CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR` in Node.
items.example: # Automatically set; don't set manually
items.newcomerTip: MCP servers can use this to resolve relative paths without depending on working directory.

items.name: Piping stdin into -p
items.summary: Pipe data into Claude Code for processing
items.details: Data from stdin is included as context. Claude can analyze or process it. Example: `cat logs.txt | claude -p "find errors"`
items.example: tail -200 app.log | claude -p "Summarize any anomalies"
items.newcomerTip: Use piping for log analysis, code review of diffs, or processing file contents without including them explicitly.

items.name: Scripting and automation patterns
items.summary: Use Claude Code in bash/shell scripts, CI/CD pipelines, and cron jobs
items.details: Combine -p (headless), --output-format json for parsing, permission flags for automation, and env vars for configuration. Example: `git diff | claude -p --output-format json "review for security"` pipes and parses JSON.
items.example: #!/bin/bash\nif claude -p --dangerously-skip-permissions "lint check passed"; then\n  echo "Linting OK"; else echo "Linting failed"; fi
items.syntax: # Various combinations of flags and piping
items.newcomerTip: Use Claude Code in your CI/CD by combining -p, output-format, and permission flags. Store tokens in env vars for CI runners.

items.name: Exit behavior and codes
items.summary: Claude Code exits with 0 on success, 1 on error or when limits are reached
items.details: In print mode, exits 0 on completion, 1 if --max-turns limit hit or other errors. Interactive mode doesn't exit unless you quit. Use exit codes in scripts: `claude -p ... && echo OK || echo failed`
items.example: claude -p --max-turns 5 "task" && echo "Success" || echo "Hit turn limit"
items.newcomerTip: Always check exit codes ($?) in scripts and CI/CD to handle failures gracefully.

catalogs.overview: Claude Code uses a four-tier settings hierarchy (Managed > Local Project > Shared Project > User > Default) to control behavior across the CLI, VS Code, JetBrains, Desktop, and Web. Configuration lives in settings.json files at different scopes, with precedence rules determining which takes effect, and enterprise-deployed policies at the top level. This domain covers file locations, complete settings keys, permission rules, hooks, auto mode configuration, and real-world examples for all setup scenarios.

items.name: Managed Settings (Enterprise Policy)
items.summary: Centrally configured settings deployed via Anthropic servers, MDM, OS-level policies, or managed files; cannot be overridden by users or projects
items.details: Managed settings are the highest precedence tier and include three delivery mechanisms: (1) Server-managed settings fetched from Anthropic API for Claude for Teams/Enterprise orgs at authentication time, cached locally, polled hourly; (2) OS-level delivery via macOS plist at com.anthropic.claudecode, Windows registry HKLM\SOFTWARE\Policies\ClaudeCode, or Linux /etc/claude-code/; (3) File-based delivery via /Library/Application Support/ClaudeCode/ (macOS), /etc/claude-code/ (Linux/WSL), or C:\Program Files\ClaudeCode\ (Windows), with support for managed-settings.d/ directory with alphabetically-merged fragments. Fail-closed enforcement available with forceRemoteSettingsRefresh: true. Validation is tolerant: invalid entries are stripped with warnings, security fields handled per-field. Use for organization-wide policies like permission.deny lists, hook definitions, and infrastructure trust context.
items.example: Server-managed via claude.ai console > Admin Settings > Claude Code > Managed settings. File-based example: C:\Program Files\ClaudeCode\managed-settings.json with {"permissions": {"deny": ["Bash(curl *)"]}, "allowManagedPermissionRulesOnly": true}
items.platformNotes: Server-managed available for Claude for Teams/Enterprise with direct org OAuth/API key auth, not third-party providers (Bedrock/Vertex/Foundry). macOS: com.anthropic.claudecode domain. Windows: HKLM or HKCU registry + WSL inheritance with wslInheritsWindowsSettings. Linux: /etc/claude-code/ for system-wide, per-user at ~/.claude/settings.json.

items.name: User Settings (~/.claude/settings.json)
items.summary: Developer's personal configuration across all projects, committed to user home directory, applies to every session
items.details: User-level settings file located at ~/.claude/settings.json (resolved to %USERPROFILE%\.claude\settings.json on Windows) defines default behavior across all projects a user works on. Examples: preferred model, personal auto mode trusted infrastructure, user-level hooks, editor mode (vim/normal), language preference, environment variables, and personal subagent/skill definitions. User settings cannot override managed settings (org policy) or explicit denies from project scope; a project-level deny blocks regardless of user-level allow. Hot-reloads on change (no restart needed). Use /permissions command to view effective rules from all scopes.
items.example: {"model": "claude-opus-4-6", "editorMode": "vim", "autoMemoryEnabled": true, "env": {"NODE_ENV": "development"}}
items.platformNotes: Windows: ~/.claude resolves to %USERPROFILE%\.claude. Supports home-relative paths with ~ prefix. MCP servers at ~/.claude.json (global) or per-project .mcp.json. CLAUDE.md at ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md (user-level global instructions).

items.name: Project Settings (.claude/settings.json)
items.summary: Team-shared configuration checked into .claude/settings.json in the project repository, applies to all collaborators on that project
items.details: Project-level settings in .claude/settings.json define conventions, standards, and team preferences for a specific repository. Committed to git so all team members inherit the same configuration. Common uses: enforce permission modes (dontAsk, plan, acceptEdits), define project-specific hooks (pre-commit linting, security checks), block dangerous operations (deny Bash(rm -rf *)), enable/disable features, and configure shared MCP servers. Project settings cannot override managed policy. Team hooks (security audits, lint checks) go here; personal/local variations go in .local.json. Enable .claude directory creation on first use with claude init.
items.example: .claude/settings.json with {"permissions": {"deny": ["Bash(rm -rf /)"]}, "hooks": {"PostToolUse": [{"matcher": "Edit|Write", "hooks": [{"type": "command", "command": "npm run lint"}]}]}, "model": "claude-sonnet-4-6"}
items.platformNotes: Located at <project-root>/.claude/settings.json. Committed to version control. Respects gitignore for local overrides. Loaded at session start and reloaded on /clear or file-watch ConfigChange event.

items.name: Local Project Settings (.claude/settings.local.json)
items.summary: Developer's local-only project configuration, gitignored, overrides project settings for individual developer
items.details: .claude/settings.local.json applies only to one developer in one project, is not committed to git (automatically added to .gitignore by claude init), and sits between project-shared and user-level settings in precedence. Use for personal tweaks: different model preference in this repo, temporary permission overrides, developer-specific hooks, or test configurations. Cannot override managed settings or project-level denies. Hot-reloads on change. Useful for experimenting with settings (e.g., test-drive auto mode in a single project) before committing to project-level settings.
items.example: .claude/settings.local.json with {"model": "claude-haiku-4-5", "permissions": {"defaultMode": "plan"}} to use Haiku and plan mode locally without affecting teammates
items.platformNotes: Windows: .claude\settings.local.json. Auto-gitignored. Only loaded from current project, not from parent directories or additional directories.

items.name: Settings Precedence Order
items.summary: Deterministic evaluation order for settings conflicts: Managed > Command-line > Local > Project > User > Default
items.details: When the same setting appears in multiple scopes, Claude Code evaluates them in strict order and uses the first match: (1) Managed settings (enterprise policy, unoverrideable); (2) Command-line arguments (one-off session overrides with --model, --allowedTools, --disallowedTools); (3) Local project settings (.claude/settings.local.json); (4) Project settings (.claude/settings.json); (5) User settings (~/.claude/settings.json); (6) Built-in defaults. For permissions specifically, deny rules at ANY scope block outright before allow rules are checked. Example: if managed settings deny Bash(curl *) and project settings allow Bash(curl github.com), the deny wins—denies are not bypassable from lower scopes. Some settings like model auto-reload without restart; others like outputStyle require /clear or restart.
items.example: User has model: claude-haiku in ~/.claude/settings.json, project has model: claude-sonnet in .claude/settings.json, run `claude --model claude-opus-4-6`. Result: Opus is used this session (command-line wins). Next session reverts to Sonnet (project tier). To check effective settings: /permissions shows permission hierarchy; claude doctor shows full config.
items.newcomerTip: Think of settings like CSS cascade: lower-level (user) is overridden by higher-level (project) unless even higher (managed) forbids it. Deny rules always win. Use /permissions or claude doctor to debug which scope is winning.

items.name: model
items.summary: Specifies the default Claude model for sessions: uses model aliases (opus, sonnet, haiku, fable, best) or full model IDs
items.details: The model setting controls which Claude model answers prompts in Claude Code sessions. Supports model aliases for convenience (best, fable, opus, sonnet, haiku, opus-plan, sonnet-plan) or full model names from Anthropic API, AWS Bedrock ARN, Google Vertex version name, or Microsoft Foundry deployment name. Current recommended models: Claude 3.5 Sonnet (default for most users), Fable 5 (longest/hardest tasks), Opus 4 (complex reasoning). Aliases auto-update when new versions release, so alias-based configs stay evergreen. Model change requires /model command in session or new session start (does not hot-reload). Use claude --model <name> to override one session.
items.example: ~/.claude/settings.json: {"model": "best"} for auto-recommended model, or {"model": "claude-opus-4-6"} for explicit version. .claude/settings.json: {"model": "sonnet-plan"} for team to use Sonnet with planning by default.
items.platformNotes: Windows/macOS/Linux: same syntax. Third-party providers override with CLAUDE_CODE_USE_BEDROCK, CLAUDE_CODE_USE_VERTEX, CLAUDE_CODE_USE_FOUNDRY environment variables. Bedrock: use inference profile ARN. Vertex: version name. Foundry: deployment name.

items.name: permissions.allow
items.summary: Array of permission rules that pre-approve tool use without prompting (Bash commands, file reads, file edits, domain fetches, MCP tools)
items.details: permissions.allow is an array of rule strings that grant automatic access to specified tools. Rules use format Tool or Tool(specifier): Bash(npm run test), Bash(npm run *) with wildcards, Read(./src/**), Edit(.env), WebFetch(domain:github.com), mcp__<server>__<tool>. Read-only tools (Read, Grep, WebFetch(domain:), Bash read-only commands) require no permission. Write tools (Edit, Write, Bash mutations) prompt by default unless allow-listed. Allow rules cannot contain exceptions: Bash(safe *) doesn't whitelist Bash(safe && rm -rf). Use with deny rules: both active means deny is checked first (denies block even if allow matches). Setting just Bash (bare tool) is equivalent to Bash(*). Precedence: deny > ask > allow.
items.example: {"permissions": {"allow": ["Bash(npm run build)", "Bash(npm run test *)", "Read(~/.zshrc)", "Edit(/src/**/*.ts)", "WebFetch(domain:github.com)", "mcp__github__*"]}}
items.newcomerTip: Start with no allows and use permission dialog "Yes, don't ask again" to build your allow list naturally. Don't pre-allow risky patterns like Bash(*) unless you're in a VM. Use deny rules to block dangerous operations (rm -rf, curl external servers).

items.name: permissions.deny
items.summary: Array of rules that block tool use unconditionally, checked before allow rules; bare tool names remove the tool from Claude's context entirely
items.details: permissions.deny blocks tool calls by matching pattern. Deny rules are evaluated first in the permission system, before ask and allow, so a deny always wins. Bare tool names like Bash or Read remove the tool from Claude's context entirely (Claude never sees the tool), while scoped rules like Bash(rm *) let Claude see the tool but block matching calls at execution. Syntax: Bash(rm *), Bash(curl *), Bash(git push *), Read(.env), Read(.env.*), Read(./secrets/**), Edit(//tmp/**), WebFetch(domain:badsite.com), mcp__*. Compound commands: each subcommand matched independently (Bash(git push origin main && npm publish) requires matching rules for both subcommands). Denies in managed settings cannot be overridden by project or user allows.
items.example: {"permissions": {"deny": ["Bash(curl *)", "Bash(git push *)", "Read(.env)", "Read(.env.*)", "Read(./secrets/**)", "mcp__external__*"]}}
items.newcomerTip: Use deny for things Claude should never attempt: destructive operations (rm -rf), credential exposure (curl to external APIs), sensitive files. Use ask for things you want to review first. Combine both in org managed settings: deny the truly dangerous, ask for medium-risk.

items.name: permissions.defaultMode
items.summary: Sets default permission mode for session: default, acceptEdits, plan, auto, dontAsk, or bypassPermissions
items.details: defaultMode determines how Claude Code handles permission prompts for tool use. Six modes: (1) default: standard behavior, prompt on first use of each tool; (2) acceptEdits: auto-approve file edits and common fs commands (mkdir, touch, mv, cp) in working dir or additionalDirectories; (3) plan: Claude reads files and runs read-only shell commands to explore, but cannot edit source files; (4) auto: experimental, auto-approves with background safety checks that verify actions align with request (soft denials recorded in /permissions, can be marked for retry); (5) dontAsk: auto-denies tools unless pre-approved via permissions.allow or /permissions dialog; (6) bypassPermissions: skips prompts except explicit ask rules and circuit-breaker root/home rm commands (use only in isolated containers/VMs). Override per-session with /permissions mode command or --mode CLI flag. Admins can disable bypassPermissions and auto modes with permissions.disableBypassPermissionsMode or permissions.disableAutoMode.
items.example: {"permissions": {"defaultMode": "acceptEdits"}} for projects where Claude should auto-accept edits. Or {"permissions": {"defaultMode": "plan"}} for read-only exploration mode.
items.platformNotes: bypassPermissions and auto modes can be disabled org-wide by admin with managed settings. Auto mode requires Claude Code v2.1+ and is research preview on Anthropic API; requires explicit enablement on Bedrock/Vertex/Foundry (set CLAUDE_CODE_ENABLE_AUTO_MODE=1).

items.name: permissions.additionalDirectories
items.summary: Array of directory paths Claude can read and edit files in, in addition to the working directory
items.details: additionalDirectories is an array of absolute file paths that grant Claude file access beyond the current working directory. Paths follow Read/Edit gitignore semantics: absolute (//path), home (~/, relative to project (/src), or relative to cwd (./src). When used in settings files, these directories grant file access only and do NOT load configuration from those directories' .claude/ folders (unlike --add-dir CLI flag). Use case: allow Claude to access a shared config repo, templates, or documentation directory without changing the working directory. Paths are merged across all setting scopes (user, project, managed). Example: a team might set /Company/shared-templates in project settings and ~/.local/scripts in user settings, giving Claude access to both.
items.example: {"permissions": {"additionalDirectories": ["/home/user/templates", "/opt/company/shared"]}}
items.newcomerTip: Use this to grant file access without moving the working directory. For configuration (CLAUDE.md, skills, subagents) in another directory, use --add-dir CLI flag instead, which loads those config types.

items.name: env
items.summary: Object of environment variables set for all Claude Code sessions and subprocesses
items.details: The env setting is an object where keys are environment variable names and values are their values. These variables are injected into every Bash/PowerShell invocation and available to hooks and subprocesses. Use for build settings (NODE_ENV, RUST_LOG), credentials (passed via helper command apiKeyHelper), feature flags, or tool configuration. Do NOT commit secrets directly; use apiKeyHelper to fetch secrets at runtime. Variables hot-reload on settings change. System environment variables are inherited; env settings merge on top and can override them. Order of application: system env > user ~/.claude/settings.json > project .claude/settings.json > local .claude/settings.local.json > managed settings.
items.example: {"env": {"NODE_ENV": "development", "RUST_LOG": "debug", "DEBUG": "true"}}
items.newcomerTip: For secrets, don't store them in settings.json. Instead, use apiKeyHelper to run a script that fetches credentials (from 1Password, AWS Secrets Manager, etc.) at session start.

items.name: hooks
items.summary: Object mapping lifecycle events (SessionStart, PreToolUse, PostToolUse, etc.) to arrays of hook handlers that execute shell commands, HTTP calls, or MCP tool invocations
items.details: The hooks object lets you automate workflows or extend permission evaluation by running custom logic at Claude Code lifecycle events. Structure: {"hooks": {"EventName": [{"matcher": "pattern", "hooks": [{"type": "command|http|mcp_tool|prompt|agent", ...}]}]}}. Five hook types: (1) command: runs shell scripts, receives JSON input, returns JSON output with permission decisions; (2) http: POSTs JSON to a URL, parses response; (3) mcp_tool: invokes a tool on connected MCP server; (4) prompt: asks Claude a yes/no question; (5) agent: spawns subagent for verification (experimental). 31 events: SessionStart/End, PreToolUse/PostToolUse/PostToolUseFailure, FileChanged, ConfigChange, InstructionsLoaded, etc. Exit code 0 = success (parse stdout JSON), 2 = block/deny (read stderr as reason), other = continue. JSON output: {"continue": true|false, "stopReason": "...", "hookSpecificOutput": {"permissionDecision": "allow|deny|ask"}}. Hooks in managed settings trigger security approval dialogs before application.
items.example: PostToolUse hook to lint after edits: {"hooks": {"PostToolUse": [{"matcher": "Edit|Write", "hooks": [{"type": "command", "command": "npm run lint", "statusMessage": "Linting..."}]}]}}
items.platformNotes: Hooks in managed settings require user approval on startup (security dialogs). Non-interactive mode (-p flag) skips approval. Command hooks support shell: "bash" or "powershell". HTTP hooks can use allowedEnvVars to pass secrets. MCP tool hooks use ${tool_input.paramName} substitution.

items.name: autoUpdatesChannel
items.summary: Release channel for automatic updates: stable (conservative, ~1 week behind) or latest (new versions as shipped)
items.details: autoUpdatesChannel controls which release channel Claude Code pulls updates from. Two options: (1) stable: tracks the stable release channel, typically 1 week behind main, skips releases with major regressions, recommended for production/team environments; (2) latest: receives new versions immediately upon release, may have bleeding-edge features or minor issues. Default for native installations is stable and auto-updates in background. Homebrew/WinGet require manual update (brew upgrade claude-code or winget upgrade). Change channel with --update-channel CLI flag or set in settings.json. Use stable for team projects, latest for early feature access.
items.example: {"autoUpdatesChannel": "stable"} for conservative updates, or {"autoUpdatesChannel": "latest"} for cutting edge
items.platformNotes: Native installations auto-update; Homebrew/WinGet do not. Minimum version enforcement: requiredMinimumVersion (managed only) sets floor; minimumVersion (any scope) suggests floor; requiredMaximumVersion (managed only) sets ceiling.

items.name: effortLevel
items.summary: Persist effort level across sessions: low (speed), medium (balanced), high (thorough), xhigh (multi-agent, ultra-deep analysis)
items.details: effortLevel controls how hard Claude works on tasks: low for quick iterations, medium for balanced speed/quality, high for thorough analysis, xhigh for multi-agent subagent dispatch and ultra-deep review (slowest, most context/cost). Set in settings.json to make it the session default, or use /effort command to change per-session. Low is faster and cheaper; high/xhigh use extended thinking, subagent loops, and broader context. For team projects, set in .claude/settings.json to establish team standard effort. Command-line override: claude --effort xhigh.
items.example: {"effortLevel": "xhigh"} to default to maximum effort, good for critical code reviews or complex tasks
items.newcomerTip: Start with medium. Use low for fast iterations during development. Use high/xhigh for production code review, security audits, or when stuck. xhigh spawns subagents and does multi-layer analysis, so it's slowest and most expensive.

items.name: editorMode
items.summary: Keyboard binding mode: normal (Emacs-like) or vim (Vi/Vim keybindings in terminal editor)
items.details: editorMode sets the terminal UI key binding style when Claude Code runs in fullscreen/TUI mode. Two options: normal (Ctrl+K to kill line, Ctrl+A to move to start, standard Emacs-like), vim (j/k for movement, : commands, vim motions). Only affects terminal UI text input and editing. Does not affect IDE integrations (VS Code, JetBrains use their own keybindings). Set in any scope; changes take effect on /clear or next session.
items.example: {"editorMode": "vim"} for Vim users in terminal, {"editorMode": "normal"} for Emacs/standard users
items.platformNotes: Terminal-specific setting. VS Code extension and JetBrains plugin use their native keybindings, not this setting. Desktop app respects this for embedded input.

items.name: tui
items.summary: Terminal UI rendering mode: fullscreen (immersive TUI), inline (single-line mode), or plain (no UI, raw text)
items.details: The tui setting controls how Claude Code renders the terminal interface. Three modes: (1) fullscreen: rich interactive terminal UI with syntax highlighting, diffs, scrolling (default in most contexts); (2) inline: single-line summary mode for piped input/output; (3) plain: no formatting, raw text output for logs/CI. Set in settings.json or use --tui CLI flag to override. Some environments (piped stdin, CI) auto-detect and use inline/plain. fullscreen=immersive, colorful, best for interactive work. inline/plain=good for logs, CI, automation.
items.example: {"tui": "fullscreen"} for interactive work, {"tui": "plain"} for CI logs
items.platformNotes: Windows PowerShell may use inline by default depending on terminal. Piped input auto-selects inline/plain.

items.name: statusLine
items.summary: Customize the bottom status bar in terminal UI: show model, cost, token count, working directory, time, effort level, or custom text
items.details: The statusLine setting lets you customize what appears in the bottom status bar of the terminal UI. Default shows model and effort level. Tokens, keys, and functions: {model}, {cost}, {tokens}, {cwd}, {time}, {effort}. Can build custom format like "{model} | {tokens} tokens | {time}" or include literal text. Useful for tracking costs in long sessions, monitoring token usage, or displaying project context. Set as string with {key} substitution.
items.example: {"statusLine": "{model} | {tokens} | {effort} | {cwd}"}
items.newcomerTip: Use {cost} to track spending during long sessions, {tokens} to watch context usage, {model} to confirm which model is active.

items.name: outputStyle
items.summary: Adjust system prompt style for responses: concise, detailed, narrative, or technical
items.details: outputStyle shapes how Claude phrases responses to you. Options: concise (short answers, get to the point), detailed (comprehensive explanations), narrative (story-like flow), technical (precise, jargon). Default is balanced. Set in any scope. Requires /clear or restart to take effect (does not hot-reload). Does not affect code output or file edits, only how Claude explains itself to you.
items.example: {"outputStyle": "technical"} for engineers who like jargon and precision, {"outputStyle": "concise"} for busy folks
items.newcomerTip: Experiment to find your style. Rebuild the prompt with /clear to see the effect.

items.name: language
items.summary: Set Claude's response language: english, japanese, french, spanish, german, portuguese, chinese, korean, etc.
items.details: The language setting tells Claude to respond in your preferred language throughout the session. Supported languages: english, japanese, spanish, french, german, portuguese, chinese (simplified and traditional), korean, and others. Applied to explanations and responses; does not affect code output or filenames. Hot-reloads on change.
items.example: {"language": "japanese"} for responses in Japanese, {"language": "spanish"} for Spanish
items.platformNotes: Code and technical output remain in English (comments in code may use language setting). Works across all surfaces.

items.name: apiKeyHelper
items.summary: Command that returns an API key for authentication, called at session start; use for 1Password, AWS Secrets Manager, or similar
items.details: The apiKeyHelper setting specifies a shell command that runs at session start and returns an API key for Claude Code authentication. Use this to fetch secrets from secure vaults (1Password, AWS Secrets Manager, Azure Key Vault) instead of storing credentials in plain text. Command should print the key to stdout. Example: "op read op://my-vault/claude-api-key" for 1Password, or a custom script that calls AWS or HashiCorp Vault. API key helper output does not trigger settings fetch for server-managed settings; only org OAuth login or directly-configured API keys do.
items.example: {"apiKeyHelper": "op read op://my-vault/claude-api-key"} for 1Password, or custom script at ~/.claude/get-api-key.sh
items.newcomerTip: Better than storing ANTHROPIC_API_KEY in env. Fetch fresh credentials each session from a secure vault.

items.name: forceLoginMethod
items.summary: Restrict authentication method: claudeai (browser login) or console (Anthropic Console API key)
items.details: forceLoginMethod restricts which auth method Claude Code accepts. Two options: claudeai (browser-based OAuth login to claude.ai), console (API key from Anthropic Console). If set to claudeai, Anthropic Console API keys are rejected. If set to console, browser login is disabled. Useful for orgs that want to enforce API key auth (audit trail, token rotation) or browser login (SSO, org management). Set in managed settings for org-wide enforcement.
items.example: {"forceLoginMethod": "console"} to enforce Anthropic Console API key auth for audit trail
items.platformNotes: Managed-only setting (no effect in user/project). Organizations use to enforce compliance-required auth methods.

items.name: autoMemoryEnabled
items.summary: Enable/disable auto memory: automatically capture learnings (build commands, debug patterns, architecture insights) across sessions
items.details: When autoMemoryEnabled is true (default), Claude Code automatically captures learnings from sessions—build commands, debugging patterns, architectural decisions, common errors—and stores them in .claude/auto-memory/ as markdown files. These memories load at session start to provide context without you writing anything. Memories include: discovered build commands (npm run test), debugging insights ("this error means database offline"), architecture notes, library patterns, performance tips. They are merged into context alongside CLAUDE.md. Disable with autoMemoryEnabled: false if you prefer to manage CLAUDE.md manually or want to keep secrets out of auto-memory. Auto-memory files are gitignored by default.
items.example: {"autoMemoryEnabled": true} (default) to auto-capture learnings, or {"autoMemoryEnabled": false} to use only checked-in CLAUDE.md
items.newcomerTip: Auto-memory is a huge time-saver. It learns your build commands, common errors, and best practices without you documenting anything. Leave it on unless you have security concerns about auto-capturing content.

items.name: autoCompactEnabled
items.summary: Enable/disable automatic context compaction: when context window fills, Claude Code summarizes old turns to free space for new work
items.details: When autoCompactEnabled is true (default), Claude Code automatically summarizes and compacts old conversation turns when the context window fills, preserving key facts while freeing token budget for new work. Summaries are stored in .claude/compactions/ and loaded on resume. Disable with autoCompactEnabled: false if you want manual compaction control with /compact command. Auto-compaction enables long-running sessions without context exhaustion.
items.example: {"autoCompactEnabled": true} (default) for automatic context management
items.newcomerTip: Leave auto-compact on. It's invisible and prevents your session from dying when context fills up.

items.name: fileCheckpointingEnabled
items.summary: Enable/disable automatic file snapshots for /rewind command: saves file state for undo without git history
items.details: When fileCheckpointingEnabled is true (default), Claude Code takes snapshots of files before edits so you can /rewind to a previous state without git commits. Snapshots are stored in .claude/checkpoints/ and are independent of git history. Useful for undoing a series of edits that you haven't committed yet. Disable with fileCheckpointingEnabled: false if you want to manage undo through git only.
items.example: {"fileCheckpointingEnabled": true} (default) to enable /rewind snapshots
items.newcomerTip: Snapshots let you undo without git commit/revert. Useful during rapid iteration.

items.name: cleanupPeriodDays
items.summary: Auto-delete old session files: default 30 days, set higher to retain more history
items.details: Claude Code automatically deletes session files (logs, transcripts, auto-memory, snapshots) older than cleanupPeriodDays. Default is 30 days. Increase to 60 or 90 for longer retention, set to 0 to never auto-delete. Session files are stored in ~/.claude/sessions/. Old auto-memory entries are merged into CLAUDE.md before deletion, so learnings are preserved.
items.example: {"cleanupPeriodDays": 90} to keep 3 months of session history
items.newcomerTip: Default 30 days is reasonable. Increase if you need to search old session transcripts or recover auto-memory entries.

items.name: includeCoAuthoredBy
items.summary: Add Co-Authored-By trailers to git commits created by Claude, for attribution
items.details: When includeCoAuthoredBy is true, Claude Code adds a Co-Authored-By: Claude <claude@anthropic.com> trailer to commits it creates. Useful for tracking AI-assisted work in git blame and commit history. Disabled by default. Set to true to enable.
items.example: {"includeCoAuthoredBy": true} to tag Claude as co-author on commits
items.platformNotes: Works with GitHub, GitLab, and all git platforms. Appears in commit trailers and is recognized by code review tools.

items.name: autoMode.environment
items.summary: Prose description of your organization's trusted infrastructure (repos, domains, cloud buckets, services) for auto mode classifier
items.details: autoMode.environment is an array of prose descriptions that tell the auto mode classifier what infrastructure your org trusts, so it knows what's "external" and risky. Include: company name, source control orgs, cloud providers/buckets, internal domains, CI/CD services, key APIs. Use literal string "$defaults" to inherit defaults (local repo + configured remotes) and extend with custom entries. Example: ["$defaults", "GitHub org github.example.com/acme-corp and all repos under it", "Cloud buckets: s3://acme-build, s3://acme-datasets", "Internal domains: *.internal.example.com, api.example.com"]. Classifier reads these as natural language; be thorough so it understands your environment.
items.example: {"autoMode": {"environment": ["$defaults", "Source control: github.example.com/acme-corp", "Cloud: s3://acme-build, s3://acme-datasets", "Internal: *.internal.example.com, CI at ci.example.com"]}}
items.newcomerTip: Start with $defaults. Run claude auto-mode config to see effective environment. Add most-accessed external services first (source control, CI), then cloud buckets, then internal domains.

items.name: autoMode.allow
items.summary: Prose allow list for auto mode classifier: exceptions to soft block rules (e.g., staging deploys allowed, scratch buckets allowed)
items.details: autoMode.allow is an array of prose statements that tell the classifier to allow specific actions that would otherwise be soft-blocked (e.g., destructive but reversible). Examples: "Deploying to staging is allowed: staging is isolated and resets nightly", "Writing to s3://scratch/ is allowed: ephemeral with 7-day lifecycle". Include "$defaults" to inherit built-in exceptions and add your own. Entries are evaluated as natural language. Allows override matching soft_deny rules when user intent is unclear; explicit intent (e.g., user says "force-push this branch") overrides soft_deny without allow rule.
items.example: {"autoMode": {"allow": ["$defaults", "Deploying to staging-*.example.com is allowed: non-prod namespace"]}}
items.newcomerTip: Use allow to reduce false positives. If auto mode blocks a routine action, add an allow entry for it. Review with claude auto-mode critique.

items.name: autoMode.soft_deny
items.summary: Prose soft block rules for auto mode classifier: destructive/risky actions blocked unless user explicitly requests them
items.details: autoMode.soft_deny is an array of prose statements for actions that are destructive or risky and should be blocked unless the user explicitly requests them by name. Examples: "Don't force-push unless explicitly requested", "Don't delete databases without explicit confirmation", "Don't modify production terraform files without explicit intent". Include "$defaults" to inherit built-in rules (force push, curl | bash, prod deploys) and add org-specific rules. User explicit intent (e.g., "force-push this branch") overrides soft blocks; vague requests ("clean up the repo") do not.
items.example: {"autoMode": {"soft_deny": ["$defaults", "Never modify files under infra/terraform/prod without explicit intent"]}}
items.newcomerTip: Don't omit "$defaults" unless you intentionally want to replace the entire list. Soft blocks are user-overrideable; hard blocks are not.

items.name: autoMode.hard_deny
items.summary: Prose unconditional block rules for auto mode classifier: absolute security boundaries that user intent cannot override
items.details: autoMode.hard_deny is an array of prose statements for security boundaries that must never be crossed, even if the user explicitly requests them. Examples: "Never send repository contents to third-party APIs", "Never exfiltrate customer data", "Never bypass MFA on production access". Include "$defaults" to inherit built-in hard blocks (data exfiltration, auto-mode bypass loops) and add org-specific boundaries. Unlike soft_deny, hard_deny rules cannot be overridden by user intent. User cannot say "send this code to ChatGPT" if that's in hard_deny.
items.example: {"autoMode": {"hard_deny": ["$defaults", "Never send repository contents or credentials to external APIs"]}}
items.newcomerTip: Hard denies are for compliance, data protection, and security requirements. Don't use for preferences; use soft_deny for those. Always include $defaults unless you're intentionally rewriting the list.

items.name: enabledMcpServers
items.summary: Enable/disable specific MCP servers by name: {"mcp-name": true|false} to control which servers Claude can use
items.details: enabledMcpServers is an object where keys are MCP server names (as configured in .mcp.json or ~/.claude.json) and values are boolean. Set to false to disable a server without deleting its config. Merges across scopes: user level enables defaults, project level can override. Useful for disabling third-party servers in sensitive projects or enabling experimental servers for testing. Requires /clear to take effect.
items.example: {"enabledMcpServers": {"puppeteer": true, "external-api": false}} to enable puppeteer but disable external-api
items.newcomerTip: Use to manage MCP server access without deleting config. Helps with gradual rollout: set to false for testing, flip to true when ready.

items.name: allowManagedPermissionRulesOnly
items.summary: Managed-only setting: when true, prevents user/project settings from defining custom permission rules; only managed rules apply
items.details: When allowManagedPermissionRulesOnly is true (set in managed settings only), user and project settings cannot define their own allow/ask/deny rules—only managed settings rules are respected. Forces all permission decisions through org policy. Example: org policy denies Bash(curl *) and allows Bash(npm *); even if a developer adds Bash(*) to their project .claude/settings.json, only the managed rules apply. Useful for strict compliance environments where every permission decision must be auditable and org-controlled.
items.example: Managed settings: {"allowManagedPermissionRulesOnly": true, "permissions": {"deny": ["Bash(curl *)"]}} locks all permission rules to managed scope
items.platformNotes: Managed-only setting. Has no effect in user or project settings. Prevents permission rule override at all levels below managed.

items.name: allowManagedHooksOnly
items.summary: Managed-only setting: when true, disables user and project hooks; only managed hooks and force-enabled plugin hooks run
items.details: When allowManagedHooksOnly is true (managed settings only), user hooks and project hooks are disabled. Only hooks from managed settings, the Agent SDK, and plugins that are force-enabled in managed settings' enabledPlugins run. Example: org wants to enforce security audit hook on every edit but prevent developers from adding backdoor hooks. Set allowManagedHooksOnly: true in managed settings to lock down hook execution.
items.example: Managed settings: {"allowManagedHooksOnly": true, "hooks": {"PostToolUse": [{"matcher": "Edit", "hooks": [{"type": "command", "command": "/security/audit.sh"}]}]}} enforces audit without allowing user overrides
items.platformNotes: Managed-only setting. Force-enable plugins in managed settings' enabledPlugins to include their hooks.

items.name: strictPluginOnlyCustomization
items.summary: Managed-only setting: restrict skills, agents, hooks, and MCP servers to only come from plugins; block user/project definitions
items.details: When strictPluginOnlyCustomization is true (all structures can be customized via plugins only, not user/project files) or an array of specific types ["skills", "hooks"], prevents user and project directories from providing those customizations. Example: org mandates that all skills and hooks come from officially packaged plugins for audit and approval. Set strictPluginOnlyCustomization: true to require everything through plugins, or ["skills", "hooks"] to allow user agents/MCP servers but force skills/hooks through plugins. Useful for compliance, security, and controlled rollout environments.
items.example: {"strictPluginOnlyCustomization": ["skills", "hooks"]} allows user agents/MCP servers but requires skills/hooks from plugins only
items.platformNotes: Managed-only setting. Values: true (all customization through plugins), false (allow all), or array ["skills", "agents", "hooks", "mcp"].

items.name: disableAutoMode
items.summary: Prevent auto mode activation: set to disable to block /permissions auto-mode toggle and claude --mode auto CLI flag
items.details: When disableAutoMode is set to "disable", users cannot activate auto mode via /permissions or CLI flag --mode auto. Useful for orgs that want to enforce stricter permission modes. Can be set at any scope; managed setting cannot be overridden.
items.example: {"permissions": {"disableAutoMode": "disable"}} to prevent auto mode across organization
items.platformNotes: Works at any scope; managed setting enforcement is strongest. Set to "disable" to prevent; omit or leave blank to allow.

items.name: disableBypassPermissionsMode
items.summary: Prevent bypassPermissions mode activation: set to disable to block /permissions bypassPermissions toggle
items.details: When disableBypassPermissionsMode is set to "disable", users cannot activate bypassPermissions mode via /permissions or CLI flag. bypassPermissions is dangerous (skips permission prompts) and should only be used in isolated containers/VMs; disabling it prevents accidental use on unmanaged machines. Admins use this in managed settings for org-wide enforcement.
items.example: {"permissions": {"disableBypassPermissionsMode": "disable"}} to prevent bypassPermissions mode across organization
items.platformNotes: Works at any scope; managed setting enforcement is strongest. Set to "disable" to prevent; omit or leave blank to allow.

items.name: .mcp.json (Project MCP Servers)
items.summary: Project-level configuration file that defines MCP servers available in this repository
items.details: .mcp.json (or .mcp.yaml, .mcp.toml, .mcp.yml) in the project root configures MCP servers that Claude can use in this repository. Syntax: {"mcpServers": {"server-name": {"command": "...", "args": [...], ...}}}. Example: puppeteer, github, memory, slack. Project .mcp.json is checked into git so all team members have the same servers. User-level ~/.claude.json configures global servers (overrideable per-project). Managed MCP via managed-mcp.json blocks user/project servers unless allowAllClaudeAiMcps is true.
items.example: .mcp.json with {"mcpServers": {"github": {"command": "npx", "args": ["@modelcontextprotocol/server-github"]}, "memory": {"command": "node", "args": ["/path/to/memory-server.js"]}}}
items.platformNotes: Supports JSON, YAML, TOML formats. Project .mcp.json committed to git. User ~/.claude.json per-user (not committed). Managed-mcp.json deployed by org, overrides project/user when set.

items.name: CLAUDE.md (Project Instructions)
items.summary: Markdown file at project root or .claude/CLAUDE.md loaded at session start; contains team conventions, architecture, libraries, and instructions
items.details: CLAUDE.md is a markdown file (or multiple files: CLAUDE.md at root, .claude/CLAUDE.md, .claude/rules/) that Claude reads at the start of every session. Use it to document: coding standards, project architecture, preferred libraries, debugging patterns, team conventions, common errors, deployment procedures. Auto-memory supplements CLAUDE.md with discovered learnings (build commands, errors) captured across sessions. Project CLAUDE.md is committed to git; user-level ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md provides personal instructions across all projects; .claude/CLAUDE.local.md is local-only per-project (gitignored). Use /memory command to manage learnings.
items.example:
CLAUDE.md at project root with: # Project Setup

Build: npm run build
Test: npm run test

# Architecture

Use React with Next.js, TypeScript, Tailwind CSS.

# Common Errors

Port 3000 in use: kill $(lsof -t -i :3000)
items.newcomerTip: CLAUDE.md is your way of training Claude about your project without editing settings. Add build commands, architecture decisions, conventions, common mistakes. It's loaded every session and grows over time with auto-memory.

items.name: .claude/skills/ (Custom Workflows)
items.summary: Directory of custom skill files (.md or .sh) that define reusable workflows invoked with /skill-name command
items.details: .claude/skills/ is a directory of markdown or shell script files that define reusable workflows. Each file becomes a /skill-name command. Example: .claude/skills/lint-check.md defines /lint-check, .claude/skills/deploy.sh defines /deploy. Skills have YAML frontmatter for description, icon, inputs, outputs. Can be committed to .claude/skills/ (team-shared) or ~/.claude/skills/ (personal). Skills chain together tools, run Bash, read/write files, and call other skills. Bundled with projects, marketplaces, or distributed as plugins.
items.example:
.claude/skills/lint-check.md with: ---
name: Lint Check
description: Run linter and fix issues
---
```bash
npm run lint -- --fix
```
Then invoke with /lint-check in session
items.newcomerTip: Skills are your custom commands. Create one with .claude/skills/my-workflow.md and invoke with /my-workflow. Share team workflows in .claude/skills; personal ones go in ~/.claude/skills/.

items.name: .claude/agents/ (Subagents)
items.summary: Directory of subagent YAML files that define custom AI agents spawned for specific tasks (experimental)
items.details: .claude/agents/ contains YAML files that define custom subagents Claude can spawn to work on independent subtasks. Each file is a subagent definition with name, instructions, tools, capabilities. Subagents run in parallel, managed by a lead agent. Useful for multi-team projects, parallel code review, or specialized task agents. Stored in .claude/agents/ (project) or ~/.claude/agents/ (user). Experimental feature; see /sub-agents docs for details.
items.example: .claude/agents/code-reviewer.yaml with: name: Code Reviewer\ninstructions: Review code for bugs and best practices\ntools: [Read, Grep, WebFetch]
items.newcomerTip: Subagents are experimental. Start with simple skills and hooks. Use subagents when you have clearly independent tasks (parallel code review, distributed feature work).

items.name: .claude/rules/ (Project Rules Directory)
items.summary: Directory of rule files (.md) that load alongside CLAUDE.md; organize large instruction sets into multiple files
items.details: .claude/rules/ is a directory of markdown files that supplement CLAUDE.md. Instead of one large CLAUDE.md, split instructions into .claude/rules/architecture.md, .claude/rules/testing.md, etc. All are loaded at session start and merged into context. Useful for large teams with distinct domains. Files are alphabetically sorted and loaded in order.
items.example: .claude/rules/database.md with database patterns, .claude/rules/testing.md with test conventions, all loaded alongside CLAUDE.md
items.newcomerTip: Use when CLAUDE.md gets too large (>10KB). Split by domain: architecture, testing, API conventions, deployment. Easier to maintain.

items.name: .claude/auto-memory/ (Learned Patterns)
items.summary: Directory auto-populated by Claude Code with discovered learnings: build commands, errors, architecture patterns, optimization tips
items.details: .claude/auto-memory/ is auto-generated (do not edit directly). Claude Code captures learnings from sessions and stores them as markdown files: discovered build commands (npm run test), error meanings, architecture insights, library patterns, performance tips. These load at session start to provide context without manual documentation. Disable with autoMemoryEnabled: false. Entries are pruned/merged into CLAUDE.md as needed. Gitignored by default.
items.example: .claude/auto-memory/build.md with: "npm run test: runs Jest tests in watch mode", "npm run lint: checks TypeScript and ESLint"
items.newcomerTip: Don't edit auto-memory; it's auto-generated. It learns build commands, errors, and patterns without you documenting anything. Review /memory command periodically to prune stale entries.

items.name: .claude/checkpoints/ (File Snapshots)
items.summary: Directory of file snapshots for /rewind command; auto-populated when fileCheckpointingEnabled is true
items.details: .claude/checkpoints/ stores snapshots of files before edits (when fileCheckpointingEnabled: true). Use /rewind to restore a file to a previous checkpoint without git. Snapshots are timestamped and independent of git history. Useful during rapid iteration before commit. Gitignored.
items.example: .claude/checkpoints/main.ts.20240101T120000Z.backup with pre-edit copy of main.ts. Use /rewind main.ts to restore.
items.newcomerTip: Checkpoints let you undo without git. Useful during rapid iteration. /rewind picks most recent checkpoint.

items.name: .claude/compactions/ (Context Summaries)
items.summary: Directory of compressed conversation summaries; auto-generated when autoCompactEnabled is true to free context space
items.details: .claude/compactions/ stores summarized versions of old conversation turns when context fills up (with autoCompactEnabled: true). Claude Code summarizes key facts, retains important decisions, and prunes verbose chat. On resume, compactions are loaded to provide context without bloating token budget. Gitignored.
items.example: .claude/compactions/summary-001.md with compressed facts from turns 1-50 of a long session
items.newcomerTip: Auto-compaction is transparent. Long sessions don't die from context exhaustion; old turns are summarized. Don't edit these files.

items.name: Command-line override (--model, --mode, --effort)
items.summary: Temporary session overrides for model, permission mode, and effort level via CLI flags
items.details: CLI flags override settings at session start and take precedence over all setting scopes except managed settings. Use --model <name> to pick model for one session, --mode <mode> for permission mode, --effort <level> for effort. Examples: claude --model claude-haiku --mode plan --effort xhigh. Flags persist only for the session; next session reverts to settings-defined defaults. Useful for one-off overrides without changing settings files.
items.example: claude --model claude-opus-4-6 --mode acceptEdits --effort high "build the feature"
items.platformNotes: All surfaces support these flags: Terminal, VS Code, Desktop, Web. Managed settings cannot be overridden by CLI flags.

items.name: /permissions command
items.summary: Interactive UI to view, manage, and toggle permission modes; add allow/ask/deny rules; check effective permissions from all scopes
items.details: /permissions opens an interactive dialog showing all permission rules from all scopes (managed, project, local, user) merged and effective. Use to: (1) toggle permission mode (default, acceptEdits, plan, auto, dontAsk, bypassPermissions); (2) add allow/ask/deny rules (saved to local .claude/settings.local.json); (3) review recently denied tool calls and mark for retry; (4) view effective rule hierarchy.
