Rules added via /permissions are always saved to .claude/settings.local.json, not project or user scopes, so they're temporary per-developer per-project.
items.example: Type /permissions, toggle to "plan" mode, add Bash(npm run *) to allow list, see all active rules and their source files
items.newcomerTip: Use /permissions to build your allow list interactively. Approve actions with "Yes, don't ask again" and they're saved to local settings.

items.name: /model command
items.summary: Change the model for the current session without restarting; select from available models or aliases
items.details: /model opens a picker to choose the model for the current session. List available models, their descriptions, current selection. Selected model applies for the rest of the session. Does not change settings files; next session reverts to settings-defined default. Use when you want to switch models mid-session (e.g., use Haiku for quick iteration, then Opus for review).
items.example: Type /model, select "Claude Opus 4" from the list, continue session with Opus
items.newcomerTip: /model doesn't change your settings—only this session. Use -- --model flag on CLI to change session without /model command.

items.name: /effort command
items.summary: Toggle effort level (low, medium, high, xhigh) for the current session
items.details: /effort opens a menu to select effort level for the current session: low (fast), medium (balanced), high (thorough), xhigh (ultra-deep with subagents). Selection applies only to this session; next session uses effortLevel from settings. Useful for upgrading to xhigh when stuck on a hard problem.
items.example: Type /effort, select "xhigh" to spawn multi-agent deep analysis
items.newcomerTip: Start with medium. Use low for fast iteration, high for careful review, xhigh when stuck.

items.name: /memory command
items.summary: View, prune, and manage auto-memory learnings; export to CLAUDE.md or delete stale entries
items.details: /memory opens UI to review auto-captured learnings from .claude/auto-memory/. Useful to: (1) see what Claude has learned (build commands, error meanings); (2) prune outdated or sensitive entries; (3) export important learnings to CLAUDE.md; (4) understand what's being loaded each session. Auto-memory is great but sometimes captures noise; /memory lets you clean it up.
items.example: Type /memory, review captured build commands and error patterns, delete stale entries, export key patterns to CLAUDE.md
items.newcomerTip: Auto-memory is automatic, but review it periodically with /memory to remove outdated or sensitive patterns.

items.name: claude doctor command
items.summary: Diagnose Claude Code configuration: validate settings.json, check auth, verify MCP servers, report effective config
items.details: claude doctor runs diagnostics on Claude Code setup: (1) validates settings.json files from all scopes (JSON syntax, schema); (2) checks authentication status; (3) verifies MCP server connectivity; (4) shows effective settings (all scopes merged); (5) reports errors and warnings. Useful for debugging config issues, validating managed settings payloads, or verifying setup.
items.example: claude doctor shows: ✓ User settings valid, ✗ Project settings has typo in 'permissions', MCP servers: github (✓ connected), puppeteer (✗ failed), Effective model: claude-opus-4-6
items.newcomerTip: Run claude doctor when settings seem broken or MCP servers don't load. It shows the source of each setting and catches JSON/schema errors.

items.name: claude auto-mode defaults command
items.summary: Print built-in auto mode block and allow rules (hard_deny, soft_deny, allow, environment) for reference
items.details: claude auto-mode defaults prints the default environment, hard_deny, soft_deny, and allow rules used by the auto mode classifier. Use this to understand what auto mode blocks by default, then decide which rules to override or extend in your settings. Output is JSON.
items.example: claude auto-mode defaults | head -20 shows default rules, then add custom rules to autoMode.environment in settings.json and run claude auto-mode config to verify
items.newcomerTip: Run this to see what auto mode blocks. Then set autoMode.environment in settings.json to describe your trusted infra, and run claude auto-mode config to check what's effective.

items.name: claude auto-mode config command
items.summary: Print the effective auto mode configuration: merged environment, allow, soft_deny, hard_deny rules from all scopes
items.details: claude auto-mode config shows the complete merged auto mode configuration the classifier actually uses, with "$defaults" expanded and all scopes merged. Use after saving autoMode settings to verify the effective rules are correct. Output is JSON.
items.example: After setting autoMode.environment in .claude/settings.json, run claude auto-mode config to see merged rules and confirm your infrastructure is trusted
items.newcomerTip: Always run claude auto-mode config after modifying autoMode settings to verify. Shows which rules are active and where they came from.

items.name: claude auto-mode critique command
items.summary: AI feedback on custom allow, soft_deny, hard_deny rules: flags ambiguous, redundant, or high-false-positive entries
items.details: claude auto-mode critique analyzes your custom auto mode rules (allow, soft_deny, hard_deny) and gives AI feedback: flags rules that are too vague, conflict, or likely cause false positives. Useful after writing custom rules to catch issues before deployment.
items.example: After adding custom soft_deny rules, run claude auto-mode critique to get feedback on whether the rules are clear and unambiguous
items.newcomerTip: Use this before pushing auto mode config to your team. It catches ambiguous or conflicting rules.

catalogs.overview: Claude Code hooks are user-defined shell commands, HTTP endpoints, LLM prompts, or MCP tools that execute automatically at specific points in Claude Code's lifecycle. They enable deterministic automation, validation, and integration with external systems—format code after edits, block dangerous commands before execution, send notifications, inject context, audit changes, and more. Hooks fire at session-level (SessionStart, SessionEnd), per-turn (UserPromptSubmit, Stop), and per-tool-call (PreToolUse, PostToolUse) cadences, plus async events (FileChanged, ConfigChange). Configuration is JSON-based in settings.json files scoped globally (~/.claude/settings.json), per-project (.claude/settings.json), or per-component.

items.name: Hook Events: Session-Level
items.summary: SessionStart, SessionEnd, Setup – fires when session begins/resumes/ends or during initialization
items.details: SessionStart fires when a session begins, resumes from a checkpoint, or clears/compacts context. Can inject context or watch files. SessionEnd fires when the session terminates. Setup fires when you run `--init-only`, or with `--init`/`--maintenance` in `-p` mode for one-time CI preparation. SessionStart can match on source: 'startup' (new), 'resume' (resumed), 'clear' (/clear), or 'compact' (compaction). SessionEnd can match on reason: 'clear', 'resume', 'logout', 'prompt_input_exit', 'bypass_permissions_disabled', 'other'. Setup can match on trigger: 'init' or 'maintenance'.
items.example: Re-inject context after compaction: {"hooks": {"SessionStart": [{"matcher": "compact", "hooks": [{"type": "command", "command": "echo 'Use Bun, not npm. Run tests before commit.'"}]}]}} — outputs from exit 0 go into Claude's context.
items.newcomerTip: Use SessionStart with 'compact' matcher to remind Claude of project conventions when context fills up. Use Setup with 'init' to run one-time scripts in CI (e.g., brew install dependencies). SessionEnd hooks run after the session closes, so use them only for logging or cleanup, not for blocking.

items.name: Hook Events: Per-Turn
items.summary: UserPromptSubmit, UserPromptExpansion, Stop, StopFailure, PostToolBatch – fires during prompt submission, command expansion, or turn completion
items.details: UserPromptSubmit fires when you submit a prompt, before Claude processes it. Default timeout 30s. Can block the prompt or inject context. UserPromptExpansion fires when a user-typed command (like a skill invocation) expands into a prompt before reaching Claude. Can block the expansion. Stop fires when Claude finishes responding (not on user interrupts). Can block and tell Claude to keep working. StopFailure fires when the turn ends due to API error; output/exit code are ignored. PostToolBatch fires after a full batch of parallel tool calls completes, before the next model call. These events do not use matchers (fire on every occurrence).
items.example: Block a prompt until a condition is met: {"hooks": {"UserPromptSubmit": [{"hooks": [{"type": "command", "command": "bash -c 'if [ -z \"$API_KEY\" ]; then echo \"Set API_KEY\" >&2; exit 2; fi; exit 0'"}]}]}}
items.newcomerTip: UserPromptSubmit has a 30s timeout by default—keep hook logic fast. Stop hooks fire often (after every Claude response), so use them for critical checks like 'are tests passing?' Use `stop_hook_active` field to detect when a Stop hook has already fired 8+ times in a row (Claude overrides it then). PostToolBatch fires after parallel tool batches, useful for cross-tool validation.

items.name: Hook Events: Per-Tool-Call
items.summary: PreToolUse, PostToolUse, PostToolUseFailure, PermissionRequest, PermissionDenied – fires before, during, or after individual tool executions
items.details: PreToolUse fires before a tool call executes. Can block the tool (exit 2). Receives tool_name and tool_input. PostToolUse fires after a tool succeeds; cannot block (tool already ran). Can replace the tool's output. PostToolUseFailure fires after a tool fails. PermissionRequest fires when a permission dialog appears; can block/allow/ask. PermissionDenied fires when auto mode classifier denies a tool; can return {"retry": true}. All support matchers on tool name (e.g., 'Bash', 'Edit|Write', 'mcp__.*').
items.example: Block destructive Bash: {"hooks": {"PreToolUse": [{"matcher": "Bash", "hooks": [{"type": "command", "if": "Bash(rm -rf *)", "command": "jq -n '{hookSpecificOutput: {hookEventName: \"PreToolUse\", permissionDecision: \"deny\", permissionDecisionReason: \"rm -rf blocked\"}}'" }]}]}}
items.newcomerTip: PreToolUse fires BEFORE permission checks, so it can enforce policy users cannot bypass. PostToolUse cannot block (tool already ran), but it can transform output or run side effects like formatting. Use matchers to narrow: only run formatters on 'Edit|Write', not on every tool call. PermissionRequest hooks do NOT fire in non-interactive mode (-p); use PreToolUse instead.

items.name: Hook Events: Async & Notifications
items.summary: FileChanged, ConfigChange, CwdChanged, Notification, InstructionsLoaded, Elicitation – fires on filesystem/config changes or notifications
items.details: FileChanged fires when a watched file changes on disk. Matcher is literal filenames separated by |, not regex. CwdChanged fires when working directory changes (e.g., Claude runs cd). ConfigChange fires when settings or skills files change (external editor or process). Notification fires when Claude sends a notification (permission_prompt, idle_prompt, auth_success, elicitation_dialog, elicitation_complete, elicitation_response). InstructionsLoaded fires when CLAUDE.md or rules/*.md loads. Elicitation fires when MCP server requests user input during tool call. All support optional matchers to filter by type.
items.syntax: FileChanged matcher is literal: {"hooks": {"FileChanged": [{"matcher": ".envrc|.env", "hooks": [...]}]}}. ConfigChange matches source: {"matcher": "user_settings|project_settings|local_settings|policy_settings|skills"}. Notification matcher: {"matcher": "permission_prompt|idle_prompt|auth_success|..."}.
items.example: Reload environment on .envrc change: {"hooks": {"FileChanged": [{"matcher": ".envrc|.env", "hooks": [{"type": "command", "command": "direnv export bash > \"$CLAUDE_ENV_FILE\""}]}]}}. Desktop notify on idle: {"hooks": {"Notification": [{"matcher": "idle_prompt", "hooks": [{"type": "command", "command": "osascript -e 'display notification \"Claude waiting\" with title \"Claude Code\"'"}]}]}}
items.newcomerTip: FileChanged matcher is NOT regex—list literal filenames like '.env|.envrc'. Pair SessionStart + CwdChanged to sync direnv across directories. Notification matcher is case-sensitive ('permission_prompt', not 'PermissionPrompt'). InstructionsLoaded can match on reason: 'session_start', 'nested_traversal', 'path_glob_match', 'include', 'compact'.

items.name: Hook Events: Subagents & Tasks
items.summary: SubagentStart, SubagentStop, TaskCreated, TaskCompleted – fires when subagents spawn/finish or tasks are created/marked done
items.details: SubagentStart fires when a subagent is spawned. Matcher on agent type: 'general-purpose', 'Explore', 'Plan', or custom agent names. SubagentStop fires when a subagent finishes. TaskCreated fires when a task is being created via TaskCreate tool. TaskCompleted fires when a task is marked as completed. TeammateIdle fires when an agent team teammate is about to go idle. These are useful for monitoring, logging, or coordinating multi-agent work.
items.example: Log when Plan subagent starts: {"hooks": {"SubagentStart": [{"matcher": "Plan", "hooks": [{"type": "command", "command": "echo $(date): Plan subagent starting >> ~/.claude/subagent.log"}]}]}}
items.newcomerTip: SubagentStart/Stop don't fire for main-thread agents. TeammateIdle and TaskCreated/Completed do not support matchers. Use these to log, monitor, or integrate with external task tracking systems.

items.name: Hook Events: Compaction & Worktrees
items.summary: PreCompact, PostCompact, WorktreeCreate, WorktreeRemove – fires around context compaction or git worktree lifecycle
items.details: PreCompact fires before context compaction starts. PostCompact fires after compaction completes. Both can match on trigger: 'manual' (/compact) or 'auto' (automatic). WorktreeCreate fires when a worktree is being created via --worktree or isolation: 'worktree'. Can replace default git behavior. WorktreeRemove fires when a worktree is removed (at session exit or subagent finish). Useful for custom cleanup or alternate isolation strategies.
items.example: Alert before auto-compaction: {"hooks": {"PreCompact": [{"matcher": "auto", "hooks": [{"type": "command", "command": "echo 'Auto-compacting context' >&2"}]}]}}
items.newcomerTip: PostCompact is a good place to re-inject critical context (though SessionStart with 'compact' matcher is preferred). WorktreeCreate/Remove are advanced—use them if you need custom isolation instead of git worktrees.

items.name: Hook Events: Messages & Display
items.summary: MessageDisplay – fires while assistant message text is displayed
items.details: MessageDisplay fires while Claude's response text is being displayed in the terminal. Useful for side effects like logging, auditing, or sending notifications. Supports low 10s timeout (shorter than other hooks) because it runs per-line. Does not support matchers.
items.example: Log every message: {"hooks": {"MessageDisplay": [{"hooks": [{"type": "command", "command": "echo $(date): Message displayed >> ~/.claude/messages.log"}]}]}}
items.newcomerTip: MessageDisplay hooks have a 10s timeout (shorter than command hooks' usual 10 minutes). Don't use for heavy computation. Runs per-line/chunk of the message display.

items.name: Hook Configuration: Basic Structure
items.summary: Hooks defined in JSON within settings.json files, organized by event name with matcher/hooks arrays
items.details: Hooks live in a 'hooks' object in settings.json. Each event name is a key (e.g., 'PreToolUse', 'PostToolUse'). Each event contains an array of matcher groups. Each matcher group has a 'matcher' (string, optional) and 'hooks' (array of hook objects). Hook objects specify 'type' (command, http, mcp_tool, prompt, agent), 'command' (for shell), 'url' (for http), etc. Three nesting levels: event -> matcher groups -> individual hooks.
items.example: {"hooks": {"PreToolUse": [{"matcher": "Bash", "hooks": [{"type": "command", "if": "Bash(rm *)", "command": "~/.claude/hooks/block-rm.sh", "timeout": 10}]}, {"matcher": "Edit", "hooks": [{"type": "command", "command": "echo 'Editing file' >&2"}]}]}}
items.newcomerTip: Multiple matcher groups under one event run their hooks in parallel. If you want different logic for different tools, create separate matcher groups. matcher is optional—empty string matches all. Hooks within the same matcher group also run in parallel. Order hooks by criticality, not execution order (since parallel).

items.name: Hook Configuration: Locations (Scope)
items.summary: ~/.claude/settings.json (all projects), .claude/settings.json (single project, shareable), .claude/settings.local.json (single project, local only)
items.details: Global user scope: ~/.claude/settings.json applies to all projects on this machine, local only (not shareable). Project scope: .claude/settings.json is a single project, can be committed to repo and shared. Local project: .claude/settings.local.json overrides project settings, gitignored. Managed settings (admin-deployed) and plugin/skill hooks also available. Precedence: Managed > Local > Project > User.
items.syntax: Global: ~/.claude/settings.json. Project shared: .claude/settings.json. Project local: .claude/settings.local.json (auto-created, gitignored). Managed: /Library/Application Support/ClaudeCode/ (macOS), /etc/claude-code/ (Linux), C:\Program Files\ClaudeCode\ (Windows).
items.example: Add to ~/.claude/settings.json for all projects: {"hooks": {"Notification": [...]}}}. Add to .claude/settings.json to share hooks with team: {"hooks": {"PreToolUse": [...]}}}.
items.newcomerTip: Use ~/.claude/settings.json for personal automation (notifications, local tools). Use .claude/settings.json for team rules (blocking rm -rf, formatting checks). Never commit secrets to .claude/settings.json; use env vars with $VAR syntax (declare in allowedEnvVars). .claude/settings.local.json is auto-created and gitignored if you modify settings during a session.

items.name: Hook Types: Command (Shell)
items.summary: type: 'command' – spawns a shell command, receives JSON on stdin, uses exit code to decide
items.details: Most common hook type. Runs a shell command (/bin/sh on Unix, Git Bash on Windows). Command receives JSON event data on stdin. Can read JSON with jq, parse in any language. Hook logic runs to completion before Claude Code processes results. Supports 'args' field (exec form, no shell quoting needed) or shell form (command string interpolated). Can use placeholders like ${CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR}, ${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}. Exit code controls behavior: 0 = success/no decision, 2 = block, other = non-blocking error.
items.example: Shell form: {"type": "command", "command": "jq -r '.tool_input.command' | xargs npx prettier --write"}. Exec form (safer): {"type": "command", "command": "node", "args": ["/path/to/script.js", "--fix"]}.
items.newcomerTip: Use exec form (with 'args': []) to avoid shell quoting headaches and security issues. Pipe JSON to your script with `cat` or `jq`. Test hooks manually: `echo '{...}' | ./my-hook.sh`. Make scripts executable on Unix: chmod +x script.sh. On Windows, hooks run in Git Bash (if installed) or PowerShell fallback.

items.name: Hook Types: HTTP
items.summary: type: 'http' – POSTs event data to URL, receives JSON response
items.details: Sends hook input as JSON to an HTTP endpoint via POST. Endpoint returns JSON with same format as command hooks. Useful for cloud functions, shared audit services, or external verification. Supports custom headers, authorization tokens, environment variable interpolation ($VAR_NAME). Only variables in 'allowedEnvVars' array are resolved. Timeout default 10min (lower for UserPromptSubmit). HTTP status code alone cannot block; must return JSON decision.
items.example: {"type": "http", "url": "https://audit.example.com/tool-use", "headers": {"Authorization": "Bearer $AUDIT_TOKEN", "X-Session": "$session_id"}, "allowedEnvVars": ["AUDIT_TOKEN"]}
items.newcomerTip: Only list env vars you want exposed in 'allowedEnvVars'—others interpolate as empty. Endpoint should return the same JSON structure as a command hook's stdout. 2xx HTTP status means the hook ran; the JSON body determines the decision (allow, deny, etc.). HTTP hooks are slower than command hooks due to network latency.

items.name: Hook Types: MCP Tool
items.summary: type: 'mcp_tool' – calls a tool on a configured MCP server
items.details: Invokes a tool on an already-connected MCP server. Server must be configured in .mcp.json or settings. Sends hook input as tool arguments. Useful for reusing MCP tools (security scanners, validators, linters) without writing bash/http logic. Can transform input with 'input' field using variables like ${tool_input.file_path}. Tool response becomes the hook decision.
items.example: {"type": "mcp_tool", "server": "security", "tool": "scan", "input": {"path": "${tool_input.file_path}"}}
items.newcomerTip: MCP server must be running and configured before hook fires. Tool response should follow standard hook JSON format (exit 0 with JSON, or error). Useful if you have MCP tools already built (e.g., security-guidance plugin uses MCP tool hooks).

items.name: Hook Types: Prompt (LLM-based)
items.summary: type: 'prompt' – single-turn LLM call to make a yes/no decision
items.details: Uses Claude (Haiku by default, overridable with 'model' field) to evaluate a condition and return {"ok": true} or {"ok": false, "reason": "..."}. Useful when a decision requires judgment, not deterministic rules. Receives hook input + your prompt. Model only job is to answer yes/no. Timeout default 30s. On ok: false, behavior depends on event: Stop/SubagentStop/PostToolBatch return reason to Claude; PreToolUse denies with reason; UserPromptSubmit/Expansion/PostToolUse end turn with warning.
items.syntax: {"type": "prompt", "prompt": "Check if all tests pass. Respond {\"ok\": true} or {\"ok\": false, \"reason\": \"tests failed\"}", "model": "claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022"}
items.example: {"type": "prompt", "prompt": "Is the code change good? Check for security issues, complexity, and best practices. Return {\"ok\": true/false, \"reason\": \"...\"}."}
items.newcomerTip: Prompt hooks use Haiku by default (fast, cheap). Upgrade to Sonnet/Opus with 'model' field if you need better judgment. On 'ok': false, the reason is fed back to Claude or shown to user, so make it actionable. Prompt hooks are slower than command hooks (LLM API call)—use sparingly.

items.name: Hook Types: Agent (Multi-Turn LLM)
items.summary: type: 'agent' – spawns a subagent with tool access to verify complex conditions
items.details: Experimental. Like prompt hooks but with tool use: the subagent can read files, run bash, search code. Default 60s timeout, up to 50 turns. Returns {"ok": true/false, "reason": "..."}. Use when you need to verify state against actual codebase (e.g., 'do tests pass?', 'is the build working?'). Subagent gets tool access; your prompt describes the task.
items.syntax: {"type": "agent", "prompt": "Verify tests pass. Run the test suite and confirm all pass.", "timeout": 120}
items.example: {"type": "agent", "prompt": "Check that all unit tests pass. Run 'npm test' and verify the output shows all tests passing. Return {\"ok\": true} if passing, {\"ok\": false, \"reason\": \"X tests failed\"} if not."}
items.newcomerTip: Agent hooks are experimental—behavior may change. Use prompt hooks for simple decisions, agent hooks when you need to inspect the actual state. Timeout is longer (60s default) because it includes tool execution time. Not recommended for high-frequency events (Stop, PostToolUse) because spawning agents is expensive.

items.name: Hook Input: Common Fields
items.summary: All events include session_id, cwd, hook_event_name, transcript_path, permission_mode, effort; specific events add tool_name, tool_input, prompt, etc.
items.details: Every hook receives JSON on stdin with: session_id (unique per session), cwd (working directory), hook_event_name (e.g., 'PreToolUse'), transcript_path (JSONL log path), permission_mode (current mode), effort level. PreToolUse/PostToolUse events add tool_name and tool_input (tool-specific). UserPromptSubmit adds prompt text. SessionStart adds source. Stop hooks get stop_hook_active (true if already fired 8+ times). Tool events include execution_id. Parse with jq: `jq '.tool_input.command'`.
items.example: Extract bash command: `jq -r '.tool_input.command'`. Check if session resuming: `jq -r '.source' | grep -q resume`. Check effort level: `jq -r '.effort.level'`.
items.newcomerTip: cwd is the working directory when the hook fires—useful for path-relative logic. session_id is stable across the session, good for logging. stop_hook_active tells you if Stop hook is in a loop; check it and exit early to avoid hitting the 8-block cap. transcript_path is a JSONL file; you can read it for context.

items.name: Hook Input: Tool-Specific Fields
items.summary: tool_input contains tool-specific arguments: command (Bash), file_path (Edit/Write), etc.
items.details: tool_input varies by tool. Bash: {"command": "..."}. Edit/Write: {"file_path": "...", "file_text": "..."}. Read: {"file_path": "..."}. MCP tools: {server name, tool name, arguments}. Permission events also include permission_mode_requested, affected_tool, status (pending/allowed/denied). ConfigChange includes source and file_path. CwdChanged includes current_cwd.
items.example: Block edits to .env: `if [[ $(jq -r '.tool_input.file_path') == *'.env'* ]]; then exit 2; fi`. Log read operations: `echo $(jq -r '.tool_input.file_path') >> /tmp/reads.log`.
items.newcomerTip: tool_input structure depends on the tool. Check the documentation or test with sample data to see what fields are available. For MCP tools, the naming is mcp__<server>__<tool>, and input includes server/tool details.

items.name: Hook Output: Exit Codes
items.summary: Exit 0 = no objection (stdout added to context if UserPromptSubmit/SessionStart); exit 2 = block; other = non-blocking error
items.details: Exit 0 means the hook ran successfully and reports no decision. For PreToolUse, exit 0 does NOT approve the tool—normal permission flow still applies. For UserPromptSubmit/SessionStart, anything on stdout is added to Claude's context. Exit 2 blocks the action (for blockable events). Stderr becomes feedback Claude receives. Other exit codes (1, 127, etc.) are non-blocking errors; shows '<hook> error' notice + first stderr line in transcript, full stderr in debug log. Some events cannot be blocked (SessionStart, Setup, Notification)—exit 2 shows stderr to user and continues.
items.example: Block with message: `echo 'Command blocked: rm -rf not allowed' >&2; exit 2`. Inject context: `echo 'Use bun, not npm'; exit 0`. Non-blocking error: `echo 'Hook script not found' >&2; exit 127`.
items.newcomerTip: Exit 0 is silent—use it for side effects (logging, formatting) or context injection. Exit 2 with stderr is how you block + give Claude feedback. Don't confuse: exit 0 does NOT auto-approve tools; it just says 'I have no objection'—permission rules still apply. For structured decisions (allow/deny/ask), use JSON output instead (exit 0 + JSON).

items.name: Hook Output: JSON Structure
items.summary: Exit 0 + JSON output for structured control: hookSpecificOutput with event-specific fields (permissionDecision, decision, additionalContext, etc.)
items.details: Exit 0 and print JSON to stdout for fine-grained control. Top-level can have 'hookSpecificOutput', 'suppressOutput', 'systemMessage', 'terminalSequence'. hookSpecificOutput contains event-specific keys. PreToolUse uses permissionDecision (allow/deny/ask/defer), permissionDecisionReason. PostToolUse/Stop use decision: 'block', additional context. PermissionRequest uses decision.behavior (allow/deny), decision.updatedPermissions (setMode). Output from multiple hooks is merged; most restrictive wins (deny > defer > ask > allow).
items.example: Deny tool + reason: {"hookSpecificOutput": {"hookEventName": "PreToolUse", "permissionDecision": "deny", "permissionDecisionReason": "Use rg instead of grep"}}}. Block turn with context: {"hookSpecificOutput": {"hookEventName": "Stop", "decision": "block"}, "additionalContext": "Tests not passing yet."}}}.
items.newcomerTip: Don't mix exit 2 (simple block) with JSON output—Claude Code ignores JSON when you exit 2. Use exit 0 + JSON for structured decisions. permissionDecision 'allow' skips the prompt but doesn't bypass deny rules in settings. Use 'additionalContext' to feed feedback to Claude when blocking.

items.name: Hook Matchers: Syntax & Patterns
items.summary: Matchers filter by tool name (Bash|Edit), event source (startup|resume), or literal filenames; empty string matches all; regex with special chars
items.details: Matcher is a string that filters which hooks run. Tool events (PreToolUse, PostToolUse): match tool name. Can use pipe | as alternation (Bash|Edit|Write), or ` | ` and `,` are interchangeable in v2.1.191+. Plain text is exact match, case-sensitive. Regex if contains special chars (^, $, ., [, etc.)—e.g., '^Notebook' or 'mcp__.*'. SessionStart/SessionEnd: source or reason. Setup: init|maintenance. Notification: type. ConfigChange: settings source. FileChanged: literal filenames (not regex)—.envrc|.env. Empty string (or omit) matches all.
items.example: Format only file edits: {"matcher": "Edit|Write", "hooks": [{"type": "command", "command": "prettier --write"}]}. GitHub tools: {"matcher": "mcp__github__.*", "hooks": [...]}. Watch env files: {"matcher": ".env|.envrc", "hooks": [...]}.
items.newcomerTip: FileChanged matcher is literal filenames, NOT regex—use | to list multiple. Tool name matchers ARE regex if they contain special chars. Case-sensitive: 'Bash' matches Bash tool, 'bash' does not. MCP tool names are mcp__<server>__<tool>—use regex to match patterns. If you want different logic per tool, create separate matcher groups (not nested hooks).

items.name: Hook Matchers: if Field (Argument Filtering)
items.summary: if field filters by tool name AND arguments using permission rule syntax (e.g., "Bash(git *)"); requires v2.1.85+
items.details: The 'if' field narrows matching beyond tool name. Uses permission rule syntax like Bash(git *), Edit(*.ts), Read(.env). Hook only spawns if the tool call matches. Best-effort: if pattern has args (git push), also matches on $() and backticks. Fails open (runs hook anyway) if command cannot be parsed. Works on tool events: PreToolUse, PostToolUse, PostToolUseFailure, PermissionRequest, PermissionDenied. Not for other event types—if present, prevents hook from running.
items.example: Run hook only on git commands: {"matcher": "Bash", "hooks": [{"type": "command", "if": "Bash(git *)", "command": "./check-git-policy.sh"}]}. Block rm -rf: {"if": "Bash(rm -rf *)", ...}.
items.newcomerTip: Requires v2.1.85+. 'if' is more specific than 'matcher'—use 'matcher' for tool name (faster filter), 'if' for argument patterns. Not a security boundary; use permission rules for hard enforcement. The pattern is best-effort for parsing, so it fails open (runs hook) if parsing fails.

items.name: Hook Configuration: Common Hook Fields
items.summary: type (required), command, timeout, statusMessage, once, if – shared hook options
items.details: 'type' is required (command, http, mcp_tool, prompt, agent). 'command' for shell type. 'timeout' in seconds (command/http/mcp_tool: 10min default, UserPromptSubmit: 30s, MessageDisplay: 10s; prompt: 30s; agent: 60s). Override per hook. 'statusMessage' shows spinner text while hook runs. 'once' boolean—if true, runs once per session (not per event). 'if' filters by tool + arguments (v2.1.85+, tool events only).
items.example: Long-running hook with message: {"type": "command", "command": "npm test", "timeout": 120, "statusMessage": "Running tests..."}. Run once at session start: {"type": "command", "command": "setup.sh", "once": true}.
items.newcomerTip: statusMessage appears during hook execution (spinner). timeout default is 10min for command hooks—lower it for fast checks (10-30s). 'once' is useful for expensive setup scripts. 'if' allows fine-grained filtering without creating separate matcher groups.

items.name: Hook Configuration: Path Placeholders
items.summary: ${CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR}, ${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}, ${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_DATA} – variables resolved in hook paths
items.details: Use placeholders to reference project/plugin paths without hardcoding. ${CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR} expands to project root. ${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT} expands to plugin installation directory. ${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_DATA} expands to plugin persistent data directory (survives uninstall). Useful for portable hook scripts and plugin-bundled hooks. Can use in 'command' field and hook scripts.
items.example: {"type": "command", "command": "\"${CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR}\"/.claude/hooks/block-rm.sh"}. In .claude/hooks/my-hook.sh: `PLUGIN_DIR="${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}"; node "$PLUGIN_DIR/index.js"`
items.newcomerTip: Use placeholders to avoid absolute paths in committed .claude/settings.json. Quote the path if it could have spaces. ${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_DATA} persists even after plugin uninstall—useful for licenses or configs you want to keep.

items.name: Hook Debugging: /hooks Menu
items.summary: Type /hooks to browse all configured hooks, view source, matcher, event, and debug details
items.details: Read-only menu showing every hook organized by event. Select a hook to see details: event name, matcher, type, source file, command, args. Shows count of configured hooks per event. Type /hooks anytime during session. Menu does not support editing; to modify, edit settings files directly.
items.example: Type /hooks, press Enter, navigate with arrow keys, press Enter to select a hook for details, press Esc to return.
items.newcomerTip: Use /hooks to verify hooks are registered and to find which settings file they're in. If hook not appearing, check file watcher (may need session restart). /hooks is read-only—edit settings directly or ask Claude to modify them.

items.name: Hook Debugging: Debug Logs
items.summary: claude --debug-file /tmp/claude.log; or /debug mid-session to enable logging and view hook execution details
items.details: Start with --debug-file flag to write debug logs. Or run /debug during session to enable and find log path. Logs show full hook output, exit codes, stderr, which matchers matched, execution time. Transcript view (Ctrl+O) shows one-line summary per hook. Debug logs show everything.
items.example: Start with debug: claude --debug-file /tmp/claude.log. Enable mid-session: /debug. Tail logs: tail -f /tmp/claude.log.
items.newcomerTip: Transcript (Ctrl+O) shows summary—good for quick check. Debug logs show full details. If hook failing with 'command not found' or 'jq not found', debug logs reveal the exact error. Test hook scripts manually before using in hooks config.

items.name: Hook Testing: Manual Hook Validation
items.summary: Test hook scripts locally by piping JSON and checking exit code: echo '{...}' | ./script.sh; echo $?
items.details: Test hooks before deploying. Create sample JSON input matching event schema, pipe to script, check exit code and output. Verify script is executable (chmod +x). Check for unintended shell startup messages (profile echoing breaks JSON parsing). Use jq to parse JSON in hooks.
items.example: Test bash command validation: `echo '{"tool_name": "Bash", "tool_input": {"command": "rm -rf /"}}' | bash .claude/hooks/block-rm.sh; echo $?` (should print 2 if blocking).
items.newcomerTip: If hook outputs 'command not found' or 'jq: command not found', check script PATH and install jq. If JSON parsing fails with extra output, check shell profile for unconditional echo statements (wrap in `if [[ $- == *i* ]]; then echo ...`. Use exec form (args: []) to avoid shell quoting issues.

items.name: Hook Limitations: Blocking & Non-Blocking Events
items.summary: Some events can be blocked (PreToolUse, UserPromptSubmit, Stop), others cannot (SessionStart, Setup, Notification, PostToolUse, FileChanged)
items.details: Blockable events: PreToolUse (block tool call), PostToolUse (block turn, but tool already ran), UserPromptSubmit/Expansion (block prompt), Stop/SubagentStop (tell Claude to keep working), PermissionRequest (block dialog). Non-blockable: SessionStart, Setup (exit 2 shows stderr to user, execution continues), Notification, FileChanged, ConfigChange (exit 2 shows error, continues), PostToolUseFailure (tool already failed), StopFailure (API error), MessageDisplay, Elicitation.
items.syntax: Blockable: return {"hookSpecificOutput": {"permissionDecision": "deny"}} or exit 2. Non-blockable: exit 2 shows message, continues.
items.example: Block tool (PreToolUse): exit 2 or permissionDecision: deny. Non-blockable (Notification): exit 0 (runs side effect), exit 2 (shows error, continues).
items.newcomerTip: PostToolUse cannot block—tool already ran. Use PreToolUse to prevent execution. Stop hooks can block, but Claude overrides after 8 consecutive blocks; check stop_hook_active field and exit early to prevent. For enforcement that cannot be overridden, use permission rules in settings, not hooks.

items.name: Hook Limitations: Parallel Execution & Deduplication
items.summary: Multiple hooks on the same event run in parallel; identical hooks deduplicated; results merged (most restrictive wins for permission decisions)
items.details: All matching hooks for an event run in parallel, not sequentially. One hook's decision does not stop sibling hooks. Identical hook commands are deduplicated (run once even if registered twice). Results merged: for permission decisions, most restrictive wins (deny > defer > ask > allow). additionalContext from all hooks combined. When multiple hooks modify tool input, last to finish wins (non-deterministic order)—avoid this.
items.syntax: Two Bash hooks on PreToolUse run in parallel: {"PreToolUse": [{"matcher": "Bash", "hooks": [{...}, {...}]}]}
items.example: Log + block: First hook logs command (exit 0), second blocks rm -rf (exit 2). Both run; deny wins. Context: {"PreToolUse": [{"matcher": "Bash", "hooks": [{"type": "command", "command": "jq .tool_input.command >> /tmp/log.txt"}, {"type": "command", "if": "Bash(rm -rf *)", "command": "exit 2"}]}]}
items.newcomerTip: Parallel execution means side effects (logging, notifications) run even if another hook blocks. Don't rely on one hook's deny suppressing side effects in sibling hooks. For deterministic ordering, use separate matcher groups or single hook with multi-step script. Identical commands deduplicated saves redundant execution.

items.name: Hook Limitations: Timeouts & Complexity
items.summary: Command/HTTP/MCP hooks: 10min default; UserPromptSubmit: 30s; MessageDisplay: 10s; prompt: 30s; agent: 60s. Override per hook.
items.details: Each hook type has default timeout. Command/HTTP/MCP: 10 minutes. UserPromptSubmit (and by extension, UserPromptExpansion) lowers to 30s for responsive UX. MessageDisplay even shorter (10s). Prompt hooks 30s. Agent hooks 60s. Override with 'timeout' field in seconds. Heavy hooks on UserPromptSubmit/MessageDisplay will timeout. Cannot trigger tool calls or /commands from hooks—only side effects, context injection, decisions.
items.example: {"type": "command", "command": "npm test", "timeout": 120} (wait up to 2 min). {"hooks": {"UserPromptSubmit": [{"hooks": [{"type": "command", "command": "quick-check.sh", "timeout": 20}]}]}}
items.newcomerTip: Keep UserPromptSubmit hooks fast (< 5s ideally, < 30s max). MessageDisplay hooks must be very fast (< 10s). Use Stop hooks for expensive checks (tests, linting) instead. Cannot call /commands or trigger tools—use hooks for side effects, not orchestration.

items.name: Hook Practical Pattern: Auto-Format Code
items.summary: Format files automatically after edits with PostToolUse + Edit|Write matcher + prettier command
items.details: Run prettier or other formatter after every file edit. Hook runs after tool succeeds, extracts file_path with jq, pipes to formatter. Cannot block (tool already ran), but ensures consistent formatting. Works for any file type with a formatter available.
items.example: Add to .claude/settings.json to share with team. Runs prettier after every edit.
items.newcomerTip: PostToolUse hooks cannot block, but they can transform output or run side effects. For formatting, it's OK that the tool already ran—you're just ensuring future edits are clean. If you want to prevent edits to certain files, use PreToolUse + Edit/Write matcher instead.

items.name: Hook Practical Pattern: Block Dangerous Commands
items.summary: Prevent destructive bash commands (rm -rf, drop table) with PreToolUse + Bash matcher + exit 2
items.details: Use PreToolUse with conditional if field or bash logic to block dangerous commands. Hook runs before command executes. Exit 2 to block, stderr becomes Claude's feedback. Script checks command pattern (grep, jq) and denies if match. Claude receives error and can adjust approach.
items.example: Block rm -rf, drop table, truncate: Script checks for patterns, exits 2 if match.
items.newcomerTip: PreToolUse fires BEFORE permission checks, so it can enforce policy users cannot bypass. Hook output is feedback to Claude (in stderr when exit 2). Use 'if' field to filter by command pattern—only spawns hook process if pattern matches, saving overhead.

items.name: Hook Practical Pattern: Audit Logging
items.summary: Log all tool calls or file edits to audit file with PostToolUse matcher + jq + append to file
items.details: Append command/file data to audit log. Use PostToolUse (runs after tool succeeds) or PreToolUse (before, to catch intent). Extract relevant data with jq, append to log with timestamp. Useful for compliance, debugging, or understanding Claude's behavior.
items.example: Log every bash command: append to /tmp/claude-commands.log. Log every file edit: match Edit|Write, append file path. Parse later for analysis.
items.newcomerTip: PostToolUse logs the actual tool call (command already ran). PreToolUse logs intent (before execution). For all file changes via bash, also match Bash and check git status (because bash can create files). Use jq's `now | todate` for timestamps in ISO format.

items.name: Hook Practical Pattern: Environment Management (direnv)
items.summary: Reload environment vars on cwd change or file change with SessionStart/CwdChanged/FileChanged hooks + direnv export bash
items.details: Pair SessionStart hook with CwdChanged and FileChanged hooks to manage environment per directory. SessionStart on session start, CwdChanged when cd executed, FileChanged when .envrc/.env changes. Each hook writes direnv output to $CLAUDE_ENV_FILE (special file sourced before bash commands). Allows per-directory envs (dev, staging, prod).
items.syntax: {"hooks": {"SessionStart": [{"hooks": [{"type": "command", "command": "direnv export bash > \"$CLAUDE_ENV_FILE\""}]}], "CwdChanged": [{"hooks": [{"type": "command", "command": "direnv export bash > \"$CLAUDE_ENV_FILE\""}]}], "FileChanged": [{"matcher": ".envrc|.env", "hooks": [{"type": "command", "command": "direnv export bash > \"$CLAUDE_ENV_FILE\""}]}]}}
items.example: Run `direnv allow` once in each dir with .envrc. Then SessionStart + CwdChanged + FileChanged hooks keep env in sync. Claude runs commands with the right env vars per directory.
items.newcomerTip: $CLAUDE_ENV_FILE is sourced as a shell preamble before each bash command. Works with direnv, devbox, nix (use devbox shellenv or nix print-dev-env). For .env files, parse with jq or source directly if safe. Not recommended for secrets—use other methods (OAuth, API tokens).

items.name: Hook Practical Pattern: Notifications on Idle
items.summary: Get desktop notification when Claude is waiting for input with Notification hook + platform-specific command
items.details: Use Notification event hook with empty or 'idle_prompt' matcher. Runs osascript (macOS), notify-send (Linux), or PowerShell MessageBox (Windows) to send notification. Useful if Claude finishes work while you're away from terminal.
items.syntax: {"hooks": {"Notification": [{"matcher": "idle_prompt", "hooks": [{"type": "command", "command": "osascript -e 'display notification \"Claude Code needs input\" with title \"Claude Code\"'"}]}]}}
items.example: macOS: osascript. Linux: notify-send 'title' 'message'. Windows: powershell.exe with MessageBox.
items.newcomerTip: Notification event does not fire in headless mode (-p flag). Test notification permission on your OS once. macOS: open System Settings > Notifications, find Script Editor, enable notifications. Linux: ensure notify-daemon running. Windows: PowerShell must have Forms assembly available.

items.name: Hook Practical Pattern: Re-Inject Context After Compaction
items.summary: Use SessionStart + compact matcher to remind Claude of project conventions after context compaction
items.details: When context fills, Claude Code compacts conversation. Re-inject critical context with SessionStart hook matching 'compact' trigger. Output from exit 0 goes to Claude's context. Useful for project rules, sprint status, recent decisions.
items.syntax: {"hooks": {"SessionStart": [{"matcher": "compact", "hooks": [{"type": "command", "command": "echo 'Use TypeScript, run tests before commit. Current sprint: auth refactor.'"}]}]}}
items.example: Or use dynamic command: git log --oneline -5 to show recent commits.
items.newcomerTip: Alternative: use CLAUDE.md for permanent instructions (preferred). Use this hook for session-specific or dynamic context (current sprint, recent decisions). Hook output (exit 0 stdout) is injected as system reminder that Claude reads as plain text.

items.name: Hook Practical Pattern: Auto-Approve Specific Permissions
items.summary: Skip permission prompts for tool calls you always allow with PermissionRequest hook + JSON decision + behavior allow
items.details: Use PermissionRequest event with narrow matcher (e.g., ExitPlanMode). Hook outputs JSON with {"hookSpecificOutput": {"hookEventName": "PermissionRequest", "decision": {"behavior": "allow"}}}. Skips interactive prompt; normal permission rules still apply (deny rules cannot be overridden). Useful for actions you always approve (exiting plan mode, specific MCP tools).
items.example: Auto-approve ExitPlanMode so you're not prompted every time Claude finishes a plan.
items.newcomerTip: Keep matcher very narrow (ExitPlanMode, specific MCP tool). Don't match .* or empty—that would auto-approve everything, including dangerous file/bash operations. 'allow' skips interactive prompt but doesn't override deny rules in settings.

items.name: Hook Practical Pattern: Verify Tests Before Stop
items.summary: Use Stop hook (prompt or agent type) to check tests pass before allowing Claude to stop
items.details: Stop hooks fire when Claude finishes. Use agent type (tool access) or prompt type (LLM only) to verify tests. If ok: false, Claude keeps working with reason as guidance. Ensures code is tested before Claude declares done.
items.syntax: {"hooks": {"Stop": [{"hooks": [{"type": "agent", "prompt": "Run npm test and verify all tests pass. Return {\"ok\": true} if passing, {\"ok\": false, \"reason\": \"X failed\"} if not."}]}]}}
items.example: Prompt type (fast, LLM only): checks if 'all tests passed' from context. Agent type (slower, runs tests): executes npm test and parses output.
items.newcomerTip: Stop hooks fire often—use prompt type (fast) not agent type (slow + expensive) unless you need actual tool execution. Check stop_hook_active field to avoid infinite loop (Claude overrides after 8 blocks). Prefer having Claude just ask to verify instead of forcing verification.

items.name: Hook Security: Principles & Review
items.summary: Hooks have arbitrary command execution – review hooks in shared projects, manage secrets carefully, validate inputs, restrict HTTP endpoints
items.details: Hooks can execute any command with your permissions. In shared projects, review hooks before pulling code. Secrets: never hardcode in JSON; use env vars with $VAR syntax (declare in allowedEnvVars for http hooks). Validate inputs from hook JSON if passing to shell. HTTP hooks: restrict target URLs with allowedHttpHookUrls in settings (managed policy). For managed settings (org-wide), use allowManagedHooksOnly to prevent user hooks.
items.syntax: Use $SECURE_TOKEN in headers, declare in allowedEnvVars. HTTP hooks: limit URLs in managed settings. User hooks: can only run commands they have shell access to.
items.example: Safe: {"headers": {"Authorization": "Bearer $MY_TOKEN"}, "allowedEnvVars": ["MY_TOKEN"]}. Unsafe: {"headers": {"Authorization": "Bearer hardcoded-token"}}.
items.newcomerTip: Hooks run with user's shell permissions—cannot do more than you can do manually. Don't commit secrets to .claude/settings.json. For org-wide safety, admins use managed settings (allowManagedHooksOnly, blockedMarketplaces, etc.). Review hooks before git pull.

items.name: Hook Disable & Overrides
items.summary: Disable all hooks with disableAllHooks: true; managed settings can enforce allowManagedHooksOnly
items.details: Set "disableAllHooks": true in settings to disable all hooks globally. Managed settings hooks still run unless also disabled there. Admin policy: allowManagedHooksOnly (enforce only managed/forced hooks), allowedHttpHookUrls (restrict http targets), httpHookAllowedEnvVars (restrict env var access in http headers).
items.example: Disable for session: {"disableAllHooks": true}. Managed policy: {"allowManagedHooksOnly": true, "allowedHttpHookUrls": ["https://audit.company.com/*"]}
items.newcomerTip: disableAllHooks affects all hooks across all scope levels. Managed policies (org-wide) override user settings. Hooks in skills/agents have their own frontmatter hooks that may not be disabled.

catalogs.domain: Model Context Protocol (MCP) in Claude Code
catalogs.overview: The Model Context Protocol (MCP) is an open-source standard that lets Claude Code connect to hundreds of external tools, databases, and APIs. MCP servers give Claude direct access to your issue trackers, monitoring dashboards, databases, and custom tools without manual copy-paste. Configure servers at local/project/user scope, authenticate with OAuth or tokens, and reference resources via @ mentions or use them as slash commands.

items.name: /mcp
items.summary: Interactive panel to view, authenticate, and manage MCP servers inside a Claude Code session
items.details: Slash command that opens a TUI panel showing all connected servers with detailed status. From here you can authenticate servers requiring OAuth (runs browser sign-in), check tool lists, reconnect failed servers, or clear authentication. Lists servers from all scopes (local, project, user, plugins, claude.ai). Shows which servers need authentication, failed, or are pending approval. The panel also shows tool counts and flags servers with no tools.
items.example: Inside a session, type `/mcp`, select 'sentry' from the list, press Enter, choose 'Authenticate', and your browser opens Sentry's sign-in
items.newcomerTip: Use `/mcp` to authenticate (OAuth) or troubleshoot servers without leaving your session. It's the interactive counterpart to shell `claude mcp` commands.

items.name: claude mcp add
items.summary: Register an MCP server with Claude Code (HTTP, SSE, or local stdio)
items.details: The primary command for connecting MCP servers. Works outside a Claude Code session (in your shell). For HTTP/SSE servers, provide the URL. For local servers, use `--` separator and provide the command. Stores config at local scope by default; use `--scope project` or `--scope user` to change. Returns 'Added MCP server' confirmation. For stdio servers, the `--` (double dash) is critical: everything after it is passed untouched to the server executable.
items.example: HTTP server: `claude mcp add --transport http claude-code-docs https://code.claude.com/docs/mcp`. Stdio server: `claude mcp add playwright -- npx -y @playwright/mcp@latest`. With token: `claude mcp add --transport http github https://api.githubcopilot.com/mcp/ --header "Authorization: Bearer YOUR_GITHUB_PAT"`
items.newcomerTip: Run `claude mcp add` from your shell, not inside a Claude session. It configures the server before you start chatting.

items.name: claude mcp list
items.summary: Show all configured MCP servers and their connection status
items.details: Displays all servers from local, project, and user scopes with status indicators. Statuses: '✓ Connected' (ready), '! Connected · tools fetch failed' (connected but tools unavailable), '! Needs authentication' (requires OAuth/token), '✗ Failed to connect' (unreachable), '✗ Connection error' (threw error), '⏸ Pending approval' (project-scoped, awaiting approval). Run from shell, not inside a session. Shows tool count per server and flags servers advertising tools but exposing none.
items.example: `claude mcp list` outputs something like: `✓ Connected claude-code-docs (3 tools)` / `! Needs authentication sentry`
items.newcomerTip: Check status here before troubleshooting. 'Needs authentication' means the server works but requires a sign-in via `/mcp` inside a session.

items.name: What is MCP
items.summary: Open-source standard for AI-tool integrations that lets Claude Code connect to external services and data sources
items.details: MCP (Model Context Protocol) is the foundation for extending Claude Code beyond its built-in tools. Instead of copying data from Jira, Sentry, or your database into chat, an MCP server lets Claude read and act on those systems directly. Servers run locally (stdio), over HTTP/SSE (remote), or via WebSocket. MCP handles tool discovery, authentication, and resource management automatically. Common use cases: query databases, implement features from issue trackers, analyze monitoring data, integrate designs, automate workflows, react to external events.
items.example: Connect Sentry for monitoring: `claude mcp add --transport http sentry https://mcp.sentry.dev/mcp`, then ask 'What are the most common errors in the last 24 hours?'
items.newcomerTip: Think of MCP as 'bringing your tools into Claude's conversation' rather than copying data out. Once connected, Claude sees them like built-in tools.

items.name: claude mcp get
items.summary: Show detailed configuration for a specific MCP server
items.details: Prints the full server definition including type, URL/command, environment variables, OAuth settings, and connection status. Helps debug configuration issues. Shows which scope (local, project, user) holds the server definition. Run from shell.
items.example: `claude mcp get github` shows the GitHub server's URL, authentication headers, and current status
items.newcomerTip: Use this when a server isn't connecting or you forgot what URL it points to.

items.name: claude mcp remove
items.summary: Unregister an MCP server from Claude Code
items.details: Deletes a server's configuration. If a server exists in multiple scopes, omit `--scope` to remove from all or specify which one. Each connected server takes space in Claude's context window (their tool names and instructions load every session), so remove servers you no longer use. Run from shell.
items.example: `claude mcp remove claude-code-docs` / `claude mcp remove github --scope local`
items.newcomerTip: Removing saves context window space. If a server exists in multiple scopes and you remove it without `--scope`, it removes from all of them.

items.name: claude mcp add --transport http
items.summary: Add a remote HTTP server (hosted at a URL, recommended for cloud services)
items.details: HTTP is the recommended transport for cloud-based MCP servers. Claude Code makes HTTP requests to the server's endpoint. Supports OAuth 2.0 authentication automatically: if the server responds with 401/403, Claude prompts you to sign in via browser. Can pass authentication headers with `--header 'Authorization: Bearer TOKEN'` for services like GitHub. Works with `--callback-port` for custom OAuth ports. Handles reconnection with exponential backoff if the connection drops mid-session.
items.example: Basic: `claude mcp add --transport http notion https://mcp.notion.com/mcp` / With token: `claude mcp add --transport http github https://api.githubcopilot.com/mcp/ --header "Authorization: Bearer ghp_xxx"`
items.newcomerTip: HTTP is best for cloud services (Notion, Sentry, Linear). They usually just need a URL, though some require a token or OAuth sign-in.

items.name: claude mcp add --transport sse
items.summary: Add a Server-Sent Events (SSE) server for real-time event streaming (deprecated in favor of HTTP)
items.details: SSE transport is deprecated; use HTTP when available. SSE holds a persistent connection for servers that push events to Claude unprompted. Supports same authentication as HTTP: static headers or OAuth. Some services may still use SSE (check their docs). HTTP now handles most cloud services; SSE mainly for legacy integrations.
items.example: `claude mcp add --transport sse asana https://mcp.asana.com/sse --header "X-API-Key: your-key"`
items.newcomerTip: If the service's docs recommend SSE, use it. But if they say 'HTTP endpoint', prefer HTTP. SSE is being phased out in favor of HTTP.

items.name: claude mcp add -- (stdio transport)
items.summary: Add a local stdio server (a process Claude Code starts on your machine)
items.details: Use for tools that need local filesystem access, a browser, or a database socket. Claude Code starts the command as a subprocess; the `--` (double dash) separates Claude's options from the server command. Everything after `--` is passed untouched. Server inherits environment variables including `CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR` (the project root). For `npx` commands, add `-y` to skip prompts. Use `--env KEY=value` for additional variables. No `--transport` flag needed (stdio is default).
items.example: Playwright (browser): `claude mcp add playwright -- npx -y @playwright/mcp@latest` / With env vars: `claude mcp add --env AIRTABLE_API_KEY=YOUR_KEY airtable -- npx -y airtable-mcp-server`
items.newcomerTip: The `--` is crucial: it tells Claude Code 'stop parsing my flags and pass the rest to the server.' Without it, Claude tries to parse the server's flags and fails.

items.name: claude mcp add --transport ws (WebSocket)
items.summary: Add a WebSocket server for persistent bidirectional connections (configured via JSON)
items.details: WebSocket holds a persistent connection, useful for servers that push events unprompted. Must be configured via `claude mcp add-json` (no `--transport ws` flag exists). Accepts same auth patterns as HTTP (static headers or `headersHelper` for dynamic auth). Requires JSON config. Use HTTP instead when server only responds to requests (HTTP supports more auth patterns and CLI configuration).
items.example: `claude mcp add-json events-server '{"type":"ws","url":"wss://mcp.example.com/socket","headers":{"Authorization":"Bearer TOKEN"}}'`
items.newcomerTip: WebSocket is rare; use HTTP for most cloud services and stdio for local tools. You'll only need WebSocket if a server's docs explicitly recommend it.

items.name: MCP local scope
items.summary: Server configuration stored in ~/.claude.json, private to you, active only in the current project
items.details: Default scope when you run `claude mcp add`. Stored under the project path in ~/.claude.json. Only you see it; not shared with teammates. Use for personal development servers, experimental configs, or servers with credentials you don't want in version control. Same server name in different projects creates separate entries. Run `claude mcp add --scope local <name>` to explicitly set local scope. Local scope is 'local to you in this project,' not 'local computer' — don't confuse with stdio servers (which run locally but can have any scope).
items.example: `claude mcp add --transport http stripe --scope local https://mcp.stripe.com` stores in ~/.claude.json under current project path
items.newcomerTip: Local is the safe default: only you see it, it's private to this project. Move to project scope when your team needs the same server.

items.name: MCP project scope
items.summary: Server configuration in .mcp.json at project root, shared with team via version control
items.details: Stored in `.mcp.json` at your project root, designed to be checked into git. All team members see it when they clone and run Claude Code. First use prompts approval (so cloning a repo can't auto-launch processes). After approval, the server connects. Use for team tools like shared GitHub, Notion, or custom databases. Environment variables can use `${VAR}` or `${VAR:-default}` syntax so secrets stay in local env, not git. Run `claude mcp reset-project-choices` if you previously rejected a server and want to re-approve.
items.example: .mcp.json with two servers: `{"mcpServers": {"github": {"type": "http", "url": "https://..."}, "db": {"command": "npx", "args": [...]}}}`. Commit to git; teammates see approval prompt on first run.
items.newcomerTip: Project scope = team collaboration. Use it for shared tools your teammates all need. It asks for approval on first run for security.

items.name: MCP user scope
items.summary: Server configuration in ~/.claude.json, available across all your projects, private to your account
items.details: Stored at top level of ~/.claude.json (not under a project path). Works in every project you open. Only you see it (not shared with team). Use for personal utility servers, dev tools, or services you use across many projects. Run `claude mcp add --scope user <name> <url>` to add at user scope. If a server is defined in multiple scopes, local takes precedence, then project, then user.
items.example: `claude mcp add --transport http hubspot --scope user https://mcp.hubspot.com/anthropic` makes it available in every project, just for you
items.newcomerTip: User scope = 'I use this tool everywhere, but it's personal.' Good for your favorite dev tools that every project needs.

items.name: MCP scope precedence
items.summary: When the same server is defined at multiple scopes, Claude uses this order: local > project > user > plugins > claude.ai connectors
items.details: If 'github' exists in local, project, and user scopes, Claude connects to the local definition and ignores the others. The entire entry from the highest-priority scope is used; fields are not merged. Scopes match by name; plugins and claude.ai connectors match by endpoint (URL or command), so duplicates are detected across different transport types. Use this to override a team server for personal testing.
items.example: Team adds github at project scope. You add github at local scope for testing. Your local version takes precedence only in this project.
items.newcomerTip: Higher specificity wins: your local project setup beats team config. Useful for testing or personal overrides.

items.name: claude mcp add --header
items.summary: Pass a static authentication header (like Bearer token) when adding an HTTP server
items.details: For servers that use static tokens instead of OAuth. Header is stored in config (not secret). If the server rejects the header with 401/403, Claude fails the connection instead of falling back to OAuth — check token validity. Useful for GitHub PATs, API keys, and custom auth. Can pass multiple headers by repeating the flag. For dynamic tokens (that change at connection time), use `headersHelper` instead.
items.example: GitHub with PAT: `claude mcp add --transport http github https://api.githubcopilot.com/mcp/ --header "Authorization: Bearer ghp_xxxx"`
items.newcomerTip: Use `--header` for static tokens (API keys that never change). If your token expires and rotates, use `headersHelper` to regenerate it dynamically.

items.name: claude mcp add --env
items.summary: Pass environment variables to an MCP server at startup
items.details: Sets variables in the server's process environment. Use for API keys, config paths, or any server-specific settings. Can pass multiple `--env` flags. Values are stored in config (not secret). For stdio servers, the server inherits environment from Claude Code's shell plus your `--env` additions. Important: if `--env` is directly followed by the server name, Claude reads the name as another KEY=value pair and fails. Place at least one other option between `--env` and the name (like `--scope` or `--transport`).
items.example: `claude mcp add --env AIRTABLE_API_KEY=YOUR_KEY --transport stdio airtable -- npx -y airtable-mcp-server` / Multiple: `--env KEY1=val1 --env KEY2=val2`
items.newcomerTip: Pass secrets via `--env` for stdio servers so they don't go in config files. For HTTP servers, use `--header` for auth tokens or `headersHelper` for dynamic ones.

items.name: Environment variable expansion in .mcp.json
items.summary: Use ${VAR} or ${VAR:-default} syntax in .mcp.json to expand env vars at runtime
items.details: Allows teams to share .mcp.json configs without hardcoding secrets or machine-specific paths. Expansion happens at session start, so different machines can use different values. Supported in: command, args, env, url, headers. Syntax: `${VAR}` expands to the value, `${VAR:-default}` uses 'default' if VAR is unset. If a required var is unset and has no default, Claude fails to parse config. Useful for API keys, paths, and database URLs.
items.example: .mcp.json: `{"mcpServers": {"api-server": {"type": "http", "url": "${API_BASE_URL:-https://api.example.com}/mcp", "headers": {"Authorization": "Bearer ${API_KEY}"}}}`. Your .env has API_KEY=token123; Claude expands it at runtime.
items.newcomerTip: Use `${VAR:-default}` (with a default) in shared .mcp.json so missing vars don't crash. Use `${VAR}` (no default) for required secrets you always set locally.

items.name: .mcp.json configuration file
items.summary: Project-scope MCP configuration file: edit by hand to define servers for your team
items.details: JSON file at project root defining all project-scoped servers. Format: `{"mcpServers": {"server-name": {...}}}`. Each server object has fields like `type` (http/stdio/sse/ws), `url`/`command`, `args`, `env`, `headers`, `timeout`, `alwaysLoad`, etc. Supports environment variable expansion. Check into git so all team members get the same tools. Claude Code asks for approval on first run. Also used by Claude Code on the web. Alternative: use `claude mcp add --scope project` CLI commands to auto-generate .mcp.json.
items.example: Project-scoped .mcp.json: `{"mcpServers": {"claude-code-docs": {"type": "http", "url": "https://code.claude.com/docs/mcp"}, "playwright": {"type": "stdio", "command": "npx", "args": ["-y", "@playwright/mcp@latest"]}}}` — commit to git.
items.newcomerTip: If you're comfortable with JSON, edit .mcp.json by hand and commit it. Otherwise, use `claude mcp add --scope project` CLI commands and let Claude handle the JSON.

items.name: OAuth 2.0 authentication for MCP servers
items.summary: Secure, browser-based sign-in for cloud services like Sentry, Linear, Notion (automatic via /mcp or claude mcp login)
items.details: When a server requires OAuth, Claude Code shows '! Needs authentication' in `claude mcp list`. Inside a session, use `/mcp`, select the server, and choose 'Authenticate' — your browser opens for sign-in. Tokens are stored securely (system keychain on macOS, creds file on others) and auto-refresh. From CLI (v2.1.186+), use `claude mcp login <name>` to OAuth from shell. For servers needing pre-configured credentials (e.g., client ID/secret), use `--client-id` and `--client-secret` flags on `claude mcp add`. Can pin OAuth scopes with `oauth.scopes` in .mcp.json. If browser doesn't auto-open, copy the URL and open manually, then paste the callback URL back.
items.example: `claude mcp add --transport http sentry https://mcp.sentry.dev/mcp`, then inside session type `/mcp`, select sentry, choose Authenticate, sign in in browser. Or from CLI: `claude mcp login sentry`.
items.newcomerTip: OAuth is seamless: add the server, authenticate once, and Claude refreshes the token automatically. If you see 'Needs authentication,' use `/mcp` or `claude mcp login` to complete the flow.

items.name: claude mcp login
items.summary: Run OAuth authentication from the shell, without opening a Claude Code session
items.details: Available from v2.1.186+. Starts the OAuth flow for a server directly from your terminal. Useful for headless or SSH sessions. If no local browser is detected, prints the auth URL for you to open on another machine, then paste the callback URL back. Pass `--no-browser` to force URL printing even if a browser is available. Clears auth with `claude mcp logout <name>`.
items.example: `claude mcp login sentry` / SSH without browser: `claude mcp login sentry --no-browser` (prints URL, you open it locally, paste the callback URL back)
items.newcomerTip: Use this when you can't open a `/mcp` panel (e.g., in CI or remote SSH). It brings OAuth to the CLI.

items.name: headersHelper for dynamic authentication
items.summary: Custom command that generates authentication headers at connection time (for Kerberos, SSO, rotating tokens)
items.details: For MCP servers using non-OAuth auth (like Kerberos, LDAP, or short-lived token generation). `headersHelper` is a shell command that Claude runs at session start and on reconnect; it outputs JSON headers that merge into connection headers. Must output valid JSON object of string key-value pairs. Times out after 10 seconds. Command receives environment: `CLAUDE_CODE_MCP_SERVER_NAME` and `CLAUDE_CODE_MCP_SERVER_URL`. Dynamic headers override static ones with the same name. No caching — your script handles token reuse. Must be configured in .mcp.json (project or user scope); project-scoped helpers require workspace trust approval.
items.example: .mcp.json: `{"mcpServers": {"internal-api": {"type": "http", "url": "https://mcp.internal.example.com", "headersHelper": "/opt/bin/get-mcp-auth-headers.sh"}}}`. Script outputs: `{"Authorization": "Bearer <dynamic-token>"}`
items.newcomerTip: Use `headersHelper` only if your auth doesn't fit OAuth or static headers. It's for edge cases like internal SSO or rotating tokens.

items.name: claude mcp add-json
items.summary: Add an MCP server from a raw JSON configuration (for complex configs or copy-paste)
items.details: Alternative to `claude mcp add` for servers with complex configurations (WebSocket, pre-configured OAuth, etc.) or when copying config from server docs. JSON must be valid and conform to MCP schema. Supports all transport types (http, stdio, sse, ws). Can use `--client-secret` flag for OAuth (prompts for secret input). Useful when JSON config is already available in server docs.
items.example: `claude mcp add-json my-server '{"type":"http","url":"https://mcp.example.com/mcp","headers":{"Authorization":"Bearer token"}}'` / WebSocket: `claude mcp add-json events '{"type":"ws","url":"wss://events.com/socket"}'`
items.newcomerTip: Use this when docs give you JSON config or when you need features not covered by `claude mcp add` flags.

items.name: claude mcp add-from-claude-desktop
items.summary: Import MCP servers from Claude Desktop's configuration (macOS, WSL, Windows with proper setup)
items.details: One-way import: reads Claude Desktop's `claude_desktop_config.json`, shows interactive dialog to pick which servers to import, adds them to Claude Code. Only works on macOS and WSL. Imported servers keep their original names; if a name conflict exists, gets a suffix like `server_1`. Use `--scope user` to add to user scope instead of local scope.
items.example: `claude mcp add-from-claude-desktop` (interactive dialog) / `claude mcp add-from-claude-desktop --scope user` (adds to ~/.claude.json globally)
items.newcomerTip: One-time migration from Claude Desktop. After importing, use `claude mcp list` and `claude mcp get` to manage them normally.

items.name: MCP resources via @ mentions
items.summary: Reference MCP server resources in prompts using @server:protocol://resource/path syntax
items.details: Some MCP servers expose resources (like GitHub issues, database schemas, docs) that you can reference directly in prompts. Type `@` to see autocomplete of resources from all connected servers. Use format `@servername:protocol://path` to reference one. Resources are fuzzy-searchable. When you reference a resource, Claude fetches and includes it as context. Common resource types: `issue://123` (GitHub issues), `file://path` (file systems), `schema://table` (databases).
items.example: `Can you analyze @github:issue://123 and suggest a fix?` / `Please review the API docs at @docs:file://api/authentication` / Compare: `@postgres:schema://users with @docs:file://database/user-model`
items.newcomerTip: Resources are like smart @ mentions: Claude fetches them automatically and includes them in context. Use them to bring data into the conversation without manual copy-paste.

items.name: MCP prompts as slash commands
items.summary: MCP servers can expose prompts that become /mcp__servername__promptname commands in Claude Code
items.details: Some MCP servers define prompts (reusable prompt templates). These show up in `/` autocomplete as `/mcp__servername__promptname`. Run them like any slash command, with or without arguments. Server and prompt names are normalized (spaces become underscores). Claude substitutes your arguments into the prompt. Useful for common workflows like 'review this PR' or 'list my open issues.'
items.example: GitHub server offers prompt `list_prs`. Run `/mcp__github__list_prs` to see your open PRs. Or with args: `/mcp__github__pr_review 456`
items.newcomerTip: After connecting a server, type `/` to see its prompts. They're quick shortcuts for common server tasks.

items.name: MCP tool search (deferred tool loading)
items.summary: Defer MCP tool definitions to save context: only tool names and server instructions load upfront; full schemas load on demand
items.details: Enabled by default (unless on Vertex AI). Keeps MCP context usage low by deferring tool definitions: only tool names and server instructions fit in context at start. When Claude needs a tool, tool search fetches its full schema. Allows unlimited servers without context bloat. Disabled on Vertex AI, non-first-party proxies, or when `ENABLE_TOOL_SEARCH=false`. Some tools declare `anthropic/alwaysLoad` to always load upfront (use sparingly). Requires a model supporting `tool_reference` blocks (not Haiku).
items.example: With 50 MCP servers connected, only tool names load at start. Claude searches for 'list_issues' when you ask to list issues; the GitHub tool schema then loads. Tool search happens in background; you don't see it.
items.newcomerTip: Tool search is invisible: MCP tools work the same, but you can connect many more servers without running out of context. It's a performance win.

items.name: ENABLE_TOOL_SEARCH environment variable
items.summary: Control how MCP tools load: deferred (on demand), upfront, or threshold-based
items.details: Unset (default): All MCP tools deferred, loads on demand. Falls back to upfront on Vertex AI or non-first-party proxy. `true`: All deferred (ignores Vertex AI). `false`: All upfront (loads all schemas at start). `auto`: Threshold mode — loads upfront if tools fit in 10% of context, otherwise defers. `auto:N` (e.g., `auto:5`): Custom threshold (5%). Also set in settings.json `env` field. Haiku doesn't support tool search (no `tool_reference` blocks).
items.example: 5% threshold: `ENABLE_TOOL_SEARCH=auto:5 claude` / Disable: `ENABLE_TOOL_SEARCH=false claude` / Set in settings.json: `{"env": {"ENABLE_TOOL_SEARCH": "auto:5"}}`
items.newcomerTip: Leave it unset for default behavior. Use `auto:5` if you have a few large tool sets and want more upfront visibility.

items.name: MCP server alwaysLoad flag
items.summary: Exempt a specific MCP server from tool search deferred loading (load tools upfront)
items.details: Set `"alwaysLoad": true` in a server's .mcp.json entry to always load that server's tools into context at session start, even if tool search is enabled. Every tool from that server consumes context upfront. Use sparingly — only for critical servers with few tools. Blocks startup until the server connects (5-second timeout). Applies to all transport types (http, stdio, sse, ws). Available from v2.1.121+. Tools can also declare `anthropic/alwaysLoad: true` in their `_meta` to load individually.
items.example: .mcp.json: `{"mcpServers": {"core-tools": {"type": "http", "url": "https://mcp.example.com/mcp", "alwaysLoad": true}}}` — this server's tools always available upfront.
items.newcomerTip: Use `alwaysLoad` for tools you need on every turn. For everything else, let tool search handle it on demand.

items.name: MCP_TIMEOUT environment variable
items.summary: Set server startup timeout (default 30 seconds, useful for slow npx downloads)
items.details: If an MCP server takes longer than the timeout to start, Claude fails to connect. Default is 30 seconds (30000 ms). Stdio servers' first run can be slow while `npx` downloads packages. Increase timeout if you see 'Connection timed out at startup' errors. Example: `MCP_TIMEOUT=60000` for 60 seconds.
items.example: `MCP_TIMEOUT=60000 claude` (60-second timeout) / PowerShell: `$env:MCP_TIMEOUT = '60000'; claude`
items.newcomerTip: If `claude mcp list` shows 'Failed to connect' for a stdio server on first run, try increasing `MCP_TIMEOUT` to give `npx` time to download.

items.name: Per-server timeout in .mcp.json
items.summary: Set tool execution timeout for a specific MCP server (overrides MCP_TOOL_TIMEOUT)
items.details: Add `"timeout": 600000` (10 minutes, in ms) to a server's .mcp.json entry to set a hard wall-clock limit per tool call. Only that server uses this limit; other servers fall back to `MCP_TOOL_TIMEOUT` env var or default (~28 hours). Minimum 1000 ms (below that, falls through to MCP_TOOL_TIMEOUT). Progress notifications don't extend the wall clock. Available from v2.1.162+.
items.example: .mcp.json: `{"mcpServers": {"slow-api": {..., "timeout": 600000}}}` — tools from this server timeout after 10 minutes.
items.newcomerTip: Useful for servers that run long tasks. Set per-server limits for slow services without affecting fast ones.

items.name: MAX_MCP_OUTPUT_TOKENS environment variable
items.summary: Set maximum tokens allowed in MCP tool output (default 25,000; warning at 10,000)
items.details: Claude warns when any MCP tool output exceeds 10,000 tokens. Default max is 25,000 tokens. Use this env var to raise the limit for tools producing large outputs (database queries, reports, logs). Individual tools can declare `anthropic/maxResultSizeChars` (character limit) to override this for text; tools returning images still subject to token limit. Example: `MAX_MCP_OUTPUT_TOKENS=50000` for 50,000 tokens.
items.example: `MAX_MCP_OUTPUT_TOKENS=50000 claude` (50,000 token limit) / In settings.json: `{"env": {"MAX_MCP_OUTPUT_TOKENS": "50000"}}`
items.newcomerTip: If you see token warnings from large query results, increase this. But 25,000 is usually plenty; large outputs are often truncated to disk anyway.

items.name: CLAUDE_CODE_MCP_TOOL_IDLE_TIMEOUT environment variable
items.summary: Abort tool calls to remote servers if no response/progress for 5 minutes (v2.1.187+, stdio excluded)
items.details: If a remote HTTP, SSE, WebSocket, or claude.ai connector server sends no response and no progress notification for 5 minutes, the tool call aborts with error instead of waiting for the wall-clock timeout.
