Prevents hanging on stuck servers. Stdio servers (local processes) not affected. Set to `0` to disable idle timeout. Default: 5 minutes (300000 ms).
items.example: `CLAUDE_CODE_MCP_TOOL_IDLE_TIMEOUT=600000 claude` (10 minutes) / Disable: `CLAUDE_CODE_MCP_TOOL_IDLE_TIMEOUT=0 claude`
items.newcomerTip: Rarely needed. Useful if you have flaky remote servers that hang. Default 5-minute idle timeout is usually fine.

items.name: CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR in MCP servers
items.summary: Environment variable passed to stdio servers containing the project root path
items.details: Claude Code sets `CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR` in every stdio server's environment, pointing to the project root (where `.mcp.json` lives or where you ran `claude`). Servers can read it to resolve project-relative paths without depending on working directory. Same directory hooks receive. Also available via MCP `roots/list` request. For `.mcp.json` variable expansion, use `${CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR:-default}` (with default) since the var is set in the server's env, not Claude Code's.
items.example: Server reads `process.env.CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR` (Node) or `os.environ['CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR']` (Python) to resolve project config files.
items.newcomerTip: If building an MCP server, use this to find project-specific config without guessing working directory.

items.name: Plugin-provided MCP servers
items.summary: Plugins can bundle MCP servers that auto-activate when the plugin is enabled
items.details: Plugins define MCP servers in .mcp.json or inline in plugin.json. When you enable a plugin, its servers start automatically. Plugin servers work identically to manually configured ones. Tool names include plugin and server name: `mcp__plugin_<plugin-name>_<server-name>__<tool-name>`. Use `${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}` and `${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_DATA}` for bundled files and persistent state. If you enable/disable a plugin mid-session, run `/reload-plugins` to connect/disconnect its servers. Plugin MCP servers appear in `/mcp` with indicators.
items.example: Plugin defines: `{"mcpServers": {"db-tools": {...}}}`. Tool name becomes `mcp__plugin_my-plugin_db-tools__query`.
items.newcomerTip: Plugins can bring MCP servers with them. Install a plugin, and its servers are ready to use — no separate `claude mcp add` needed.

items.name: claude.ai connectors in Claude Code
items.summary: MCP servers configured in claude.ai/customize/connectors auto-load in Claude Code if signed in with that account
items.details: If you sign into Claude Code with your claude.ai account, connectors you've set up at claude.ai/customize/connectors appear automatically in Claude Code without manual `claude mcp add`. On Team/Enterprise plans, admins add connectors centrally. Connectors take lower precedence than locally configured servers (local > project > user > plugins > claude.ai). Some Anthropic-hosted connectors (Gmail, Google Calendar, MS 365) can't use local OAuth from Claude Code; authenticate them on claude.ai instead. Disable all claude.ai connectors with `disableClaudeAiConnectors: true` in settings or `ENABLE_CLAUDEAI_MCP_SERVERS=false` env var.
items.example: Add Slack connector at claude.ai/customize/connectors. Sign into Claude Code with the same account. `/mcp` shows Slack connector automatically.
items.newcomerTip: If your organization has pre-configured connectors, they appear in Claude Code when you sign in with your work account. No setup needed.

items.name: disableClaudeAiConnectors setting
items.summary: Block all claude.ai MCP connectors from loading in Claude Code
items.details: Set to `true` in any settings scope (project, user, policy) to disable all claude.ai connectors for that scope. Uses any-source-true semantics: if any scope sets `true`, connectors are disabled. A project-level `false` can't re-enable connectors disabled at user/policy level. Also set via env var `ENABLE_CLAUDEAI_MCP_SERVERS=false`. Servers passed via `--mcp-config` CLI flag unaffected.
items.example: settings.json: `{"disableClaudeAiConnectors": true}` / Env: `ENABLE_CLAUDEAI_MCP_SERVERS=false claude`
items.newcomerTip: Use this if your org policy requires no cloud connectors. Set in project `.claude/settings.json` to opt out for the whole team.

items.name: claude mcp serve (Claude Code as MCP server)
items.summary: Run Claude Code itself as an stdio MCP server for other apps (like Claude Desktop)
items.details: Starts Claude Code as an MCP server exposing its tools (View, Edit, LS, etc.) via stdio. Other apps can connect to Claude Code using standard MCP protocol. Useful for integrating Claude Code's file and shell tools into Claude Desktop or other MCP clients. The calling app is responsible for permission prompts (Claude Code doesn't add extra safeguards). Requires the full path to `claude` executable in config if `claude` isn't in PATH.
items.example: Claude Desktop config: `{"mcpServers": {"claude-code": {"type": "stdio", "command": "/path/to/claude", "args": ["mcp", "serve"]}}}`
items.newcomerTip: Rare use case: lets other apps (Claude Desktop) use Claude Code's tools. Most users won't need this.

items.name: Managed MCP configuration
items.summary: Enterprise feature: admins control which MCP servers users can connect to via managed-mcp.json, allowedMcpServers, deniedMcpServers
items.details: Organizations can deploy a fixed set of MCP servers and restrict user access. Admins create `managed-mcp.json` with `allowedMcpServers` (whitelist) or `deniedMcpServers` (blacklist). Users see blocked servers as unavailable in `/mcp`. Policies can be name-based ('github') or URL/command-based patterns. Overrides user's local config but user-added local servers bypass it. See [Managed MCP configuration](/en/managed-mcp) docs for full details.
items.example: Org blocks 'sentry': admin sets `deniedMcpServers: [{"serverName": "sentry"}]`. Users see '✗ Denied by organization policy' in `/mcp`.
items.newcomerTip: Enterprise feature. If your org enforces MCP restrictions, ask your admin for the allowlist. Personal local servers still work.

items.name: Troubleshooting: Failed to connect or Connection error
items.summary: Server didn't start or URL didn't respond. Check URL, network, command, and credentials.
items.details: Run `claude mcp list` or select server in `/mcp` for error details. For HTTP servers, test with `curl -I https://...` (use `curl.exe` in PowerShell). 404 = URL is wrong. 401/403 = auth needed. 5xx = server down. No response = network issue. For stdio servers, run the command directly in terminal to see startup errors (e.g., missing Node.js). As of v2.1.191, 404 errors show the URL Claude tried. Check server docs for correct endpoint URL.
items.example: `curl -I https://mcp.sentry.dev/mcp` (test HTTP) / `npx -y @playwright/mcp@latest` (test stdio) / `claude mcp get sentry` (see config and error)
items.newcomerTip: Start with `claude mcp list` to see which servers failed. Then match the error to the troubleshooting guide in the docs.

items.name: Troubleshooting: Server connected but no tools appear
items.summary: Server started but didn't register tools. Usually missing required environment variables (API key).
items.details: Run `/mcp`, select the server, and check its tool list. If empty, the server started but couldn't discover tools. Cause: missing required env var (API key, token, etc.). Check server docs for required vars. Solution: pass them with `--env KEY=value` on `claude mcp add` or in server's .mcp.json entry. Example: `claude mcp add --env API_KEY=xxx --transport stdio db -- ...`
items.example: Airtable server needs AIRTABLE_API_KEY. Pass it: `claude mcp add --env AIRTABLE_API_KEY=YOUR_KEY --transport stdio airtable -- npx -y airtable-mcp-server`
items.newcomerTip: No tools showing = check server docs for required env vars. Add them with `--env` and reconnect.

items.name: Troubleshooting: No MCP servers configured (/mcp shows empty)
items.summary: Claude Code didn't find any servers for this project. Likely scope mismatch or wrong file path.
items.details: Cause 1: You ran `claude mcp add` from a different project. Local servers are project-specific. Re-add from current project or use `--scope user`. Cause 2: Edited config at wrong path. Correct paths: `~/.claude.json` (local/user scope) or `.mcp.json` (project scope). Claude Code doesn't read `~/.claude/mcp.json` or similar. Cause 3: Project-scoped server but you haven't approved it yet. Run `claude` interactively to see approval prompt.
items.example: Ran `claude mcp add` in /path/a, now in /path/b? Re-add from /path/b or use `--scope user`. Edited wrong file? Check `~/.claude.json` or `.mcp.json` at project root.
items.newcomerTip: Check `claude mcp list` first. No output = likely you added in a different project. Re-add with `--scope user` to make it global.

items.name: Troubleshooting: Connection timed out at startup
items.summary: MCP server took too long to start (default 30 seconds). Increase MCP_TIMEOUT.
items.details: Stdio servers' first run slow due to `npx` downloading packages. Increase timeout: `MCP_TIMEOUT=60000 claude` (60 seconds). Or set in settings.json `env` field. Also check `/mcp` for pending status — wait and retry after download finishes. As of v2.1.121+, timeout also applies to capability discovery (tools/list, resources/list).
items.example: `MCP_TIMEOUT=60000 claude` / PowerShell: `$env:MCP_TIMEOUT = '60000'; claude` / Settings: `{"env": {"MCP_TIMEOUT": "60000"}}`
items.newcomerTip: First `npx` run is slow. Increase `MCP_TIMEOUT` and wait for it to cache. Future runs will be faster.

items.name: Troubleshooting: Server already exists
items.summary: Server with that name is already configured at the same scope.
items.details: Remove the existing entry first: `claude mcp remove <name>` (or add `--scope` if it exists in multiple scopes). Then re-add with new config. Or choose a different name for the new server.
items.example: `claude mcp remove github` then `claude mcp add --transport http github <new-url>` / Or: `claude mcp add --transport http github-dev <new-url>`
items.newcomerTip: Each server name must be unique per scope. Use `claude mcp list` to see existing names.

items.name: Troubleshooting: Changes to .mcp.json don't take effect
items.summary: Claude Code reads .mcp.json at session start, not mid-session. Restart Claude.
items.details: Exit the current session and run `claude` again. Also run `/mcp` and look for parse warnings if servers still don't appear. If you previously rejected a server, reset project approvals: `claude mcp reset-project-choices`. Verify .mcp.json syntax (valid JSON).
items.example: Edited .mcp.json. Exit session (`Ctrl+D` or `exit`). Run `claude` again. Servers should now appear.
items.newcomerTip: .mcp.json only loads at session start. Always restart Claude after editing it.

items.name: Troubleshooting: OAuth sign-in fails or browser doesn't open
items.summary: Run /mcp, select server, Authenticate again. If browser doesn't open, copy URL and open manually.
items.details: Inside session, `/mcp`, select the server, choose `Authenticate`. If browser doesn't auto-open, Claude prints the URL. Copy it, open in browser manually, sign in, and paste the full callback URL back at the prompt. Also ensure no firewall blocking localhost. For SSH sessions, use `--no-browser` flag on `claude mcp login` to get the URL printed.
items.example: Sentry auth fails. Type `/mcp`, select sentry, Authenticate. Browser doesn't open? Copy the printed URL, open manually, sign in, paste callback URL back.
items.newcomerTip: OAuth setup is usually automatic, but if browser fails, manual copy-paste works. For headless/SSH, use `claude mcp login --no-browser`.

catalogs.overview: Subagents are specialized AI assistants in Claude Code that run in isolated context windows with custom system prompts, specific tool access, and independent permissions. They help preserve context by handling side tasks (research, analysis, testing) in parallel while the main conversation stays focused. You can create custom subagents as Markdown files in .claude/agents/ (project) or ~/.claude/agents/ (user), or manage them through the /agents command interface.

items.name: /agents command
items.summary: Interactive interface to view, create, edit, and manage subagents with tabbed UI for Running and Library views
items.details: Opens a two-tabbed interface: Running tab shows live and recently finished subagents with status; Library tab lets you view all available subagents (built-in, user, project, plugin), create new ones with guided setup or Claude generation, edit existing configurations, delete custom subagents, and see which are active when duplicates exist. Recommended way to manage subagents. Created subagents take effect immediately without session restart, unlike manual file edits.
items.example: In Claude Code, run `/agents` then switch to Library tab and click 'Create new agent' to generate a new subagent
items.newcomerTip: Start with /agents to create your first subagent—the guided interface is easier than writing YAML by hand. Choose 'Generate with Claude' to have Claude write the system prompt for you.

items.name: Built-in Explore subagent
items.summary: Fast, read-only agent optimized for codebase exploration using Haiku model with search and analysis capabilities
items.details: Automatically delegates to Explore when Claude needs to search or understand code without making changes. Uses Haiku for speed and low latency. Supports three thoroughness levels: quick (targeted lookups), medium (balanced exploration), or very thorough (comprehensive analysis). Skips CLAUDE.md and git status to keep exploration fast and inexpensive. Read-only tools only—denied access to Write and Edit tools. Output stays in subagent context to preserve main conversation space.
items.example: Claude automatically uses Explore when you ask 'find all uses of the UserService class' without explicitly requesting a subagent
items.newcomerTip: Explore is great for digging into large codebases—your main conversation won't get flooded with search results.

items.name: Built-in Plan subagent
items.summary: Research agent used in plan mode to gather context before presenting a plan, with read-only tool access
items.details: Automatically delegates during plan mode to research the codebase before Claude presents a plan. Has read-only tools only (denied Write and Edit). Exploration output stays in separate context window while main conversation remains read-only. Skips CLAUDE.md and git status like Explore. Model inherits from main conversation.
items.example: When you enter plan mode and Claude needs codebase context, it automatically delegates to Plan subagent for research
items.newcomerTip: Plan works automatically in plan mode—no action needed. It keeps the main conversation clean during the planning phase.

items.name: Built-in general-purpose subagent
items.summary: Capable agent for complex multi-step tasks requiring both exploration and code modifications
items.details: Delegates to general-purpose when task requires both exploration and modification, complex reasoning to interpret results, or multiple dependent steps. Has access to all tools. Inherits model from main conversation. Can be resumed and used as a named subagent.
items.example: Claude delegates to general-purpose when fixing a bug that requires understanding the codebase and modifying multiple files
items.newcomerTip: General-purpose is the all-rounder—use it when you need a subagent that can both read and write code.

items.name: Project subagent file location
items.summary: Store project-specific subagents in .claude/agents/ directory, checked into version control for team sharing
items.details: Project subagents are ideal for subagents specific to a codebase. Files are discovered by walking up from current working directory, so every .claude/agents/ between there and repository root is scanned. As of v2.1.178, when nested directories define the same name, Claude Code uses the definition closest to working directory. Check into version control so team can use and improve collaboratively. Subdirectories like agents/review/ are supported; subdirectory path doesn't affect identity (only name frontmatter field matters).
items.example: Create ~/.claude/projects/my-app/.claude/agents/code-reviewer.md for a project-specific code review subagent
items.newcomerTip: Use .claude/agents/ for subagents your whole team will use; this keeps them with your codebase.

items.name: User subagent file location
items.summary: Store personal subagents in ~/.claude/agents/ directory, available across all projects on your machine
items.details: User subagents are personal subagents available in all your projects. Scanned recursively, so subdirectories like agents/review/ or agents/research/ organize them but don't affect identity. Unique name values required across whole tree. Priority is lower than project subagents (priority 4) but higher than plugin subagents (priority 5). These are your personal tools you can reuse everywhere.
items.example: Create ~/.claude/agents/security-auditor.md to have a security review subagent in every project
items.newcomerTip: Create your go-to subagents in ~/.claude/agents/ once, then use them everywhere.

items.name: Plugin subagent files
items.summary: Subagents distributed through plugins, appearing in /agents alongside custom subagents with scoped identifiers
items.details: Plugin agents/ directories are scanned recursively. Unlike project and user scopes, subfolder inside plugin's agents/ directory becomes part of scoped identifier: file at agents/review/security.md in plugin my-plugin registers as my-plugin:review:security. For security reasons, plugin subagents do NOT support hooks, mcpServers, or permissionMode frontmatter fields—these are ignored when loading from plugins. If needed, copy agent file into .claude/agents/ or ~/.claude/agents/. Can reference in @-mention with full scoped name.
items.example: Reference @agent-my-plugin:code-reviewer or @agent-my-plugin:review:security in prompt, or pass claude --agent my-plugin:review:security
items.newcomerTip: Plugin subagents work out of the box if installed, but to customize them, copy them to your own .claude/agents/ directory.

items.name: Subagent frontmatter: name
items.summary: Required unique identifier using lowercase letters and hyphens; receives this value as agent_type in hooks
items.details: Must be unique across the scope (project or user). Filename doesn't have to match. This is the identity used for delegation, @-mentions, and hook matching. Hooks receive this value as agent_type field.
items.example: name: security-auditor
items.newcomerTip: Use descriptive names with hyphens: code-reviewer, bug-debugger, data-analyst.

items.name: Subagent frontmatter: description
items.summary: Required field explaining when Claude should delegate to this subagent; guides automatic delegation decisions
items.syntax: description: Expert code reviewer. Use proactively after code changes.
items.details: Claude uses this description to decide when to delegate tasks. Write clear, specific descriptions so Claude knows when to use it. Include phrases like 'use proactively' to encourage automatic delegation. Descriptions should explain the subagent's domain and when it's needed.
items.example: description: Debugging specialist for errors and test failures. Use proactively when encountering any issues.
items.newcomerTip: The better you describe what the subagent does, the smarter Claude's automatic delegation becomes.

items.name: Subagent frontmatter: tools
items.summary: List of allowed tools (allowlist); if omitted, subagent inherits all tools from main conversation
items.details: Controls what a subagent can do via allowlist. To use disallowlist instead, use disallowedTools field. Accepts tool names (Read, Write, Edit, Bash, etc.), MCP server-level patterns (mcp__<server> or mcp__<server>__*), and Agent(type) for controlling which subagents can be spawned. Both fields accept MCP patterns: mcp__github removes all tools from github server, mcp__* removes all MCP tools. Skill tool should not be listed here; use skills field instead to preload skills.
items.example: tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Bash  # read-only analysis subagent
items.newcomerTip: Restrict tools to what the subagent actually needs—a code reviewer doesn't need Write, a researcher doesn't need Bash.

items.name: Subagent frontmatter: disallowedTools
items.summary: List of tools to deny (denylist), removed from inherited or specified list; applied before tools field
items.details: Useful when subagent inherits most tools but you want to block specific ones. Applied first, then tools field is resolved against remaining pool. If tool listed in both fields, it's removed. Supports same patterns as tools field: exact names, MCP server patterns, and Agent(type) restrictions. mcp__* removes all MCP tools while keeping built-in tools.
items.example: disallowedTools: mcp__github  # removes all github MCP tools but keeps others
items.newcomerTip: Use disallowedTools when you want 'all tools except X'—it's cleaner than listing 20 allowed tools.

items.name: Subagent frontmatter: model
items.summary: Which AI model the subagent uses: alias (sonnet, opus, haiku, fable), full ID, inherit, or omitted
items.details: Options: model alias (sonnet, opus, haiku, fable), full model ID (claude-opus-4-8), inherit (use main conversation's model), or omitted (defaults to inherit). When Claude invokes subagent, can pass per-invocation model parameter. Resolution order: CLAUDE_CODE_SUBAGENT_MODEL env var > per-invocation parameter > frontmatter > main conversation model. All values checked against availableModels allowlist.
items.example: model: haiku  # fast, cheap subagent for basic analysis
items.newcomerTip: Use haiku for fast exploratory work, sonnet for analysis, opus for complex reasoning. Inherit for consistency with main session.

items.name: Subagent frontmatter: permissionMode
items.summary: How subagent handles permission prompts: default, acceptEdits, auto, dontAsk, bypassPermissions, or plan
items.details: Controls permission behavior. Modes: default (standard checking with prompts), acceptEdits (auto-accept file edits for working directory), auto (background classifier reviews), dontAsk (auto-deny prompts, explicitly allowed tools still work), bypassPermissions (skip prompts—use carefully), plan (read-only exploration). Parent mode takes precedence: if parent uses bypassPermissions or acceptEdits, subagent inherits it and cannot override. If parent uses auto mode, subagent inherits auto and any permissionMode frontmatter is ignored. Ignored for plugin subagents.
items.example: permissionMode: auto
items.newcomerTip: Use auto for subagents that don't need babysitting. Use plan for read-only research.

items.name: Subagent frontmatter: maxTurns
items.summary: Maximum number of agentic turns before subagent stops; prevents runaway execution
items.details: Sets an upper bound on how many thinking/action cycles the subagent can perform before it must stop. Useful for preventing infinite loops or runaway execution.
items.example: maxTurns: 5  # subagent can take at most 5 turns
items.newcomerTip: Optional, but useful for quick tasks that shouldn't exceed a certain complexity.

items.name: Subagent frontmatter: skills
items.summary: List of skill names to preload into subagent context at startup; full content injected, not discovery
items.details: Injects full skill content into subagent's context at startup, giving domain knowledge without discovery during execution. Controls which skills are preloaded, not which skills subagent can access—without this, subagent can still invoke project, user, and plugin skills through Skill tool. To prevent skill access entirely, omit Skill from tools or add to disallowedTools. Cannot preload skills with disable-model-invocation: true. Missing or disabled skills are skipped with warning.
items.example:
skills:
  - security-patterns
  - performance-optimization
items.newcomerTip: Preload domain skills to give your subagent instant expertise without discovery overhead.

items.name: Subagent frontmatter: mcpServers
items.summary: MCP servers available to subagent: either string references to existing servers or inline definitions scoped to subagent
items.syntax:
mcpServers:
  - playwright:
      type: stdio
      command: npx
      args: ["-y", "@playwright/mcp@latest"]
  - github
items.details: Each entry either references an already-configured MCP server by name (reuses parent's connection) or defines inline server that connects when subagent starts and disconnects when finishes. Uses same schema as .mcp.json (stdio, http, sse, ws). Keeps MCP server out of main conversation entirely, avoiding tool descriptions consuming context there. Inline definitions scoped to subagent only. Same restrictions apply as main session: --strict-mcp-config, enterprise managed MCP config, and allowedMcpServers/deniedMcpServers policies. Ignored for plugin subagents.
items.example:
mcpServers:
  - browser-tools:  # inline def
      type: stdio
      command: npx
      args: [@playwright/mcp]
  - slack  # reference existing
items.newcomerTip: Use inline mcpServers to give a subagent specialized tool access without cluttering the main session.

items.name: Subagent frontmatter: hooks
items.summary: Lifecycle hooks scoped to subagent; only run while subagent active and cleaned up when finishes
items.syntax:
hooks:
  PreToolUse:
    - matcher: "Bash"
      hooks:
        - type: command
          command: "./scripts/validate-command.sh"
items.details: Defines lifecycle hooks that run only during subagent execution. All hook events supported, but common ones for subagents are PreToolUse (before tool use), PostToolUse (after tool use), and Stop (when finished—converted to SubagentStop at runtime). Hooks fire when agent spawned as subagent via Agent tool or @-mention, and when agent runs as main session via --agent or agent setting. Ignored for plugin subagents.
items.example:
hooks:
  PostToolUse:
    - matcher: "Edit|Write"
      hooks:
        - type: command
          command: "./scripts/run-linter.sh"
items.newcomerTip: Use hooks for auto-validation or cleanup—e.g., lint after every file edit.

items.name: Subagent frontmatter: memory
items.summary: Persistent memory scope: user (global), project (shareable), or local (not versioned); survives across conversations
items.details: Gives subagent persistent directory that survives conversations. Scopes: user (~/.claude/agent-memory/<name>/)—remembers across all projects, project (.claude/agent-memory/<name>/)—project-specific and shareable via version control, local (.claude/agent-memory-local/<name>/)—project-specific but not versioned. When enabled: system prompt includes memory management instructions, first 200 lines or 25KB of MEMORY.md included in prompt, Read/Write/Edit tools auto-enabled. Project scope recommended; use user for broadly applicable knowledge, local for knowledge not checked in.
items.example: memory: user  # this subagent learns across all projects
items.newcomerTip: Enable memory to give your subagent institutional knowledge that grows over time. Project scope is the safe default.

items.name: Subagent frontmatter: background
items.summary: Set to true to always run subagent as background task, or let Claude decide based on task
items.details: Controls foreground vs background execution. Foreground blocks main conversation until complete; background runs concurrently. As of v2.1.186, background subagent permission prompts surface in main session naming the subagent. Can ask Claude to 'run this in the background' or press Ctrl+B to background running task. When CLAUDE_CODE_FORK_SUBAGENT=1, every subagent spawn runs background regardless. Set CLAUDE_CODE_DISABLE_BACKGROUND_TASKS=1 to disable all background functionality.
items.example: background: true  # always run in background
items.newcomerTip: Use background: true for long-running tasks like testing or analysis that don't need your input.

items.name: Subagent frontmatter: effort
items.summary: Effort level for this subagent: low, medium, high, xhigh, max; overrides session effort level
items.details: When subagent active, overrides session effort setting. Useful for making certain subagents more thorough (e.g., security review at max effort) or faster (e.g., exploration at low effort). Available levels depend on model. If omitted, inherits from session.
items.example: effort: xhigh  # security reviews get extra thorough analysis
items.newcomerTip: Use high/xhigh effort for critical subagents like security review; low effort for quick exploration.

items.name: Subagent frontmatter: isolation
items.summary: Set to worktree to run subagent in temporary git worktree, giving isolated copy of repository
items.details: When set to worktree, subagent gets isolated git worktree branched from default branch (not parent's HEAD). Worktree auto-cleaned up if subagent makes no changes. Useful when subagent edits might conflict with main session work. Subagent starts in worktree's main directory.
items.example: isolation: worktree  # experimental branch, isolated from main work
items.newcomerTip: Use isolation: worktree when a subagent does exploratory coding that shouldn't touch your main checkout.

items.name: Subagent frontmatter: color
items.summary: Display color in task list and transcript: red, blue, green, yellow, purple, orange, pink, or cyan
items.details: Visual identifier for subagent in UI. Helps distinguish multiple running subagents at a glance.
items.example: color: green
items.newcomerTip: Pick a memorable color so you can spot your subagent in the task list.

items.name: Subagent frontmatter: initialPrompt
items.summary: Auto-submitted as first user turn when agent runs as main session via --agent or agent setting
items.syntax: initialPrompt: "Start by reviewing the recent git changes"
items.details: Commands and skills are processed. Prepended to any user-provided prompt. Only applies when agent runs as main session, not when spawned as subagent.
items.example: initialPrompt: "Read CLAUDE.md and review the recent changes"
items.newcomerTip: Use initialPrompt to set a default first action when this agent is the main session.

items.name: CLI subagent definition via --agents flag
items.summary: Define subagents as JSON when launching Claude Code for single-session use without saving to disk
items.details: Pass JSON when launching Claude. Useful for automation scripts or quick testing. Accepts all frontmatter fields: description, prompt (= markdown body), tools, disallowedTools, model, permissionMode, mcpServers, hooks, maxTurns, skills, initialPrompt, memory, effort, background, isolation, color. On Windows PowerShell use @' ... '@ syntax for multiline. Subagents exist only for that session.
items.newcomerTip: Use --agents for testing new subagents before committing them to .claude/agents/.

items.name: @-mention subagent
items.summary: Type @ and pick subagent from typeahead to guarantee that specific subagent runs for one task
items.details: Ensures specific subagent runs rather than leaving choice to Claude. Full message still goes to Claude, which writes subagent's task prompt. @-mention controls which subagent runs, not what prompt it receives. Plugin subagents appear as scoped names (e.g., my-plugin:code-reviewer or my-plugin:review:security). Named background subagents show status. Can mention manually without picker: @agent-<name> or @agent-my-plugin:review:security.
items.example: @"code-reviewer (agent)" review the authentication changes
items.newcomerTip: Use @-mention when you want a specific subagent, not Claude's automatic choice.

items.name: --agent flag for main-session subagent
items.summary: Start entire session where main thread takes subagent's system prompt, tool restrictions, and model
items.details: Subagent's system prompt replaces default Claude Code system prompt. CLAUDE.md and project memory still load. Agent name appears as @<name> in startup header. Works with built-in and custom subagents. Choice persists when session resumes. For plugin subagent, pass name and Claude finds it. If multiple plugins provide same name, use scoped name to disambiguate: my-plugin:security-reviewer. If plugin places agent in subfolder, include path: my-plugin:review:security.
items.example: claude --agent code-reviewer
items.newcomerTip: Use --agent when you want to become a subagent for an entire session, not just one task.

items.name: agent setting in .claude/settings.json
items.summary: Set default main-session subagent for every session in a project
items.details: Makes subagent default for project. CLI flag --agent overrides if both present. Persists across sessions.
items.example: { "agent": "security-auditor" }
items.newcomerTip: Use this when a project always starts with a specialized agent view.

items.name: Automatic subagent delegation
items.summary: Claude automatically delegates tasks to subagents based on task description and subagent's description field
items.details: Claude uses subagent's description field to decide when to delegate. To encourage proactive delegation, include phrases like 'use proactively' in description. No explicit invocation needed—Claude just delegates when task matches. Automatic delegation based on description quality and task fit.
items.example: Subagent with description 'Code reviewer. Use proactively after code changes.' automatically invoked when changes are made
items.newcomerTip: Write clear, specific descriptions like 'use proactively' to trigger automatic delegation.

items.name: Subagent context isolation
items.summary: Each subagent runs with fresh context window, not seeing conversation history, previous skills, or files already read
items.details: Exception: forked subagents inherit full parent conversation. Subagent initial context contains: system prompt (agent's own, not full Claude Code prompt), task delegation message, CLAUDE.md and memory, git status snapshot (except Explore/Plan), preloaded skills from skills field. Non-fork subagent does NOT see previous conversation history. This preserves main conversation space and keeps output separate.
items.example: You research module A, then delegate research of module B to a subagent—module B research doesn't flood your conversation
items.newcomerTip: Isolation is why subagents are great for big analysis tasks—verbose output stays separate from main work.

items.name: Forked subagent
items.summary: Subagent that inherits entire parent conversation instead of starting fresh; runs in background by default
items.details: Fork inherits full conversation history, system prompt, tools, model, and message context. Different from named subagent which starts fresh. Only fork's final result returns to main conversation, keeping context clean. Runs in background. Fork cannot spawn another fork but can spawn other subagent types. Enable with CLAUDE_CODE_FORK_SUBAGENT=1 environment variable (or use /fork command). When fork mode enabled, every subagent spawn runs background. Set CLAUDE_CODE_DISABLE_BACKGROUND_TASKS=1 to keep spawns synchronous. First invocation reuses parent's prompt cache, making fork cheaper than fresh subagent.
items.example: /fork draft unit tests for the parser changes so far
items.newcomerTip: Forks are great when a named subagent would need too much background—fork gives it your full context without re-explaining.
items.platformNotes: Available in terminal CLI, VS Code, desktop app, and web. /fork command enabled by default as of v2.1.161; earlier versions need CLAUDE_CODE_FORK_SUBAGENT=1.

items.name: Fork vs named subagent differences
items.summary: Fork inherits parent's context and system prompt; named subagent starts fresh with its own definition
items.details: Fork: full conversation history, same system prompt/tools/model, shared prompt cache. Named: fresh context, own definition (prompt, tools, model), separate cache. Both run in background (as of v2.1.186). Fork sees parent's full context but returns only summary. Named subagent explores independently.
items.example: Fork for 'draft tests while I continue implementation'. Named subagent for 'analyze the codebase security'.
items.newcomerTip: Fork when task needs context. Named subagent when it needs isolation.

items.name: Nested subagents (subagent depth limit)
items.summary: Subagents can spawn their own subagents up to depth limit; only top-level summary returns to main conversation
items.details: As of v2.1.172, subagent can spawn subagents. Useful when delegated task splits into parallel subtasks—intermediate output never reaches main. Panel below prompt shows full tree with (+N) descendant counts. Depth counted as subagent levels below main conversation, regardless of foreground/background. Subagent at depth 5 cannot spawn further (limit fixed, not configurable). As of v2.1.187, background subagent depth fixed when spawned; resuming later doesn't change. Prevent subagent from spawning others by omitting Agent from tools or adding to disallowedTools.
items.example: Main task delegated to reviewer subagent → reviewer spawns verify subagents for each finding → only reviewer's summary returns
items.newcomerTip: Nested subagents let you hide implementation details—only the top-level summary reaches you.

items.name: Resume subagent
items.summary: Continue an existing subagent's work instead of starting fresh; retains full conversation history and tool calls
items.syntax: Continue that code review and now analyze the authorization logic
items.details: Each subagent invocation creates new instance. To continue existing work, ask Claude to resume subagent. Resumed subagents retain full history including previous tool calls and reasoning. Claude uses SendMessage tool with agent's ID to resume. Built-in Explore and Plan are one-shot and return no ID, so cannot be resumed; use general-purpose or custom subagent when continuation needed. Stopped subagent auto-resumes in background if receives SendMessage. Find agent ID in transcript files at ~/.claude/projects/{project}/{sessionId}/subagents/agent-{agentId}.jsonl.
items.example: Use code-reviewer subagent to review auth module, then continue that reviewer and analyze authorization
items.newcomerTip: Resuming is cheaper than spawning fresh—subagent picks up where it left off.

items.name: Subagent memory directory locations
items.summary: Persistent memory survives conversations: user (~/.claude/agent-memory/), project (.claude/agent-memory/), or local (.claude/agent-memory-local/)
items.details: User scope: global learnings across projects. Project scope: team-shareable via version control. Local scope: project-specific, not versioned. System prompt includes memory instructions. First 200 lines or 25KB of MEMORY.md included in prompt. Subagent should maintain MEMORY.md as it learns.
items.example: ~/.claude/agent-memory/code-reviewer/MEMORY.md for user scope, .claude/agent-memory/code-reviewer/MEMORY.md for project
items.newcomerTip: Start with project scope—it's shareable but doesn't clutter the working tree.

items.name: Subagent vs main conversation decision tree
items.summary: Use main conversation for back-and-forth iteration; use subagents for self-contained tasks with verbose output
items.details: Main conversation when: task needs frequent back-and-forth, multiple phases share context, quick targeted change, latency matters. Subagents when: task produces verbose output, need to enforce tool restrictions, work is self-contained, can return summary. Consider skills for reusable prompts in main context. Use /btw for quick questions with no tool access.
items.example: Implement feature in main conversation (iterative). Run full test suite in subagent (verbose). Code review in subagent (specialized).
items.newcomerTip: Think 'verbose, one-time, specialized' for subagents. Think 'iterative, shared context, quick' for main.

items.name: Subagent foreground vs background
items.summary: Foreground blocks until complete; background runs concurrently with permission prompts surfacing in main session
items.details: Claude decides based on task, but can request 'run in background' or press Ctrl+B. As of v2.1.186, background permission prompts surface in main session naming subagent. Approve to continue, Esc to deny one call without stopping subagent. CLAUDE_CODE_DISABLE_BACKGROUND_TASKS=1 disables all background. When CLAUDE_CODE_FORK_SUBAGENT=1, every spawn runs background regardless of background field.
items.example: Long test run: background: true so main session stays responsive
items.newcomerTip: Use background for long-running tasks (tests, analysis). Use foreground for interactive work.

items.name: Disable specific built-in subagents
items.summary: Add subagent names to permissions.deny in settings to prevent Claude from using specific subagents
items.details: Works for built-in and custom subagents. Format: Agent(subagent-name) where name matches subagent's name field. Also available via CLI flag --disallowedTools "Agent(Explore)". To prevent all subagent delegation, deny the Agent tool itself.
items.example: "deny": ["Agent(Explore)", "Agent(general-purpose)"]
items.newcomerTip: Use this if a subagent is interfering with your workflow.

items.name: Subagent tool access: Agent(type) allowlist
items.summary: Restrict which subagent types a main-session agent can spawn using Agent(worker, researcher) syntax
items.details: Only applies to agent running as main thread with --agent. Allowlist of allowed subagent types. If agent tries to spawn other types, request fails and agent sees only allowed types in prompt. To block specific agents while allowing others, use permissions.deny instead. Agent without parentheses allows any subagent. If Agent omitted entirely, agent cannot spawn any subagents. In subagent definition, Agent in tools allows nested spawning but parentheses list ignored.
items.example: tools: Agent(security-auditor, code-reviewer)  # only these can be spawned
items.newcomerTip: Use Agent(type1, type2) when you want tight control over which subagents can be invoked.

items.name: Subagent scope priority
items.summary: Managed settings (1) > CLI flag (2) > project .claude/agents/ (3) > user ~/.claude/agents/ (4) > plugin (5)
items.details: When multiple scopes define same subagent name, higher priority wins. Project subagents checked into version control; user subagents personal across projects; CLI subagents single-session only.
items.example: If you have my-agent in both .claude/agents/ and ~/.claude/agents/, project version (priority 3) wins
items.newcomerTip: Higher priority doesn't mean 'better'—it just means local overrides user-wide which overrides plugins.

items.name: Subagent YAML frontmatter structure
items.summary: Markdown file with YAML frontmatter defining configuration, followed by markdown body as system prompt
items.syntax:
---
name: code-reviewer
description: Reviews code
tools: Read, Grep
model: sonnet
---

You are a code reviewer...
items.details: Only name and description required. Body becomes system prompt (not full Claude Code prompt). Subagent starts in main conversation's working directory. cd commands don't persist between tool calls and don't affect main session. For isolation, set isolation: worktree.
items.example: See full example in Create custom subagents > Write subagent files section
items.newcomerTip: The body is the subagent's personality and instructions—make it clear and detailed.

items.name: Subagent file location: restarting to load changes
items.summary: File edits to subagent .md files require session restart; /agents interface changes apply immediately
items.details: Subagents loaded at session start. If you edit .md file on disk, restart session to load it. /agents command creation/edit takes effect immediately without restart.
items.example: Edit ~/.claude/agents/my-agent.md → must restart session. Use /agents interface instead for live changes.
items.newcomerTip: Always use /agents UI to create/edit—it's live. Manual file edits require restart.

items.name: Subagent Hook events: PreToolUse, PostToolUse, Stop
items.summary: Lifecycle hooks for subagent: PreToolUse (before tool), PostToolUse (after tool), Stop (when done)
items.details: All hook events supported, but these common for subagents. Hooks in frontmatter run only while subagent active, cleaned up when finished. When agent spawned as subagent, Stop hooks auto-converted to SubagentStop. PreToolUse useful for validation, PostToolUse for linting.
items.example: PreToolUse with matcher "Bash" to validate commands; PostToolUse with matcher "Edit|Write" to auto-lint
items.newcomerTip: Use PreToolUse to validate before action, PostToolUse to clean up after.

items.name: SubagentStart and SubagentStop hooks in settings.json
items.summary: Project-level hooks in settings.json responding to subagent lifecycle in main session
items.details: SubagentStart fires when subagent begins, SubagentStop when completes. Matchers target specific agent types by name. Useful for setup/cleanup in main session when subagent starts/stops.
items.example: SubagentStart db-agent runs setup-db.sh; SubagentStop runs cleanup-db.sh
items.newcomerTip: Use SubagentStart/Stop for environment setup that the main session needs to manage.

items.name: Subagent example: code-reviewer
items.summary: Read-only subagent that reviews code without modifying; demonstrates tool restriction and detailed prompt
items.details: Tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Bash (no Write/Edit). Model: inherit. Prompt guides review checklist: readability, naming, duplication, error handling, secrets, validation, testing, performance. Output organized by priority (critical/warning/suggestion).
items.example: See example in sub-agents.md under Example subagents > Code reviewer

items.name: Subagent example: debugger
items.summary: Subagent that analyzes and fixes bugs; includes Edit for modifications and structured debugging workflow
items.details: Tools: Read, Edit, Bash, Grep, Glob. Prompt guides root cause analysis, hypothesis testing, minimal fix, verification. Output includes explanation, evidence, fix, testing approach, prevention.
items.example: See example in sub-agents.md under Example subagents > Debugger

items.name: Subagent example: data-scientist
items.summary: Domain-specific subagent for data analysis; SQL queries, BigQuery, analysis. Model: sonnet.
items.details: Tools: Bash, Read, Write. Demonstrates specialized workflows outside typical coding. Prompt guides SQL optimization, aggregations, joins, comments, formatting, recommendations.
items.example: See example in sub-agents.md under Example subagents > Data scientist

items.name: Subagent example: db-reader with validation hook
items.summary: Read-only database subagent using PreToolUse hook to block write operations; demonstrates conditional tool control
items.details: Tools: Bash. Hook validates queries, blocks INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE/etc. Script exits code 2 to block write ops. Shows how to allow tool while restricting specific operations.
items.example: See example in sub-agents.md under Example subagents > Database query validator

items.name: Subagent isolation with worktree
items.summary: Set isolation: worktree to run subagent in temporary git worktree, isolated from main checkout
items.details: Subagent gets separate worktree branched from default branch (not parent HEAD). Auto-cleaned if no changes. Useful for exploratory coding that shouldn't touch main work. Subagent starts in worktree directory.
items.example: isolation: worktree in subagent definition for experimental branch work
items.newcomerTip: Use worktree isolation when subagent is trying risky code changes.

items.name: Subagent parallelism and context
items.summary: Running many parallel subagents multiplies token usage; each has fresh context. Results return to main conversation.
items.details: Subagents work within single session. For many independent parallel sessions monitored from one place, use agent view. For sessions that communicate, use agent teams. Worktrees give parallel sessions separate git checkouts so they never edit same files.
items.example: Spawn research-auth, research-db, research-api subagents in parallel; each explores independently
items.newcomerTip: Parallel subagents are powerful but expensive—use when task truly benefits from parallelism.

items.name: Subagent cost management via model choice
items.summary: Control costs by routing tasks to faster, cheaper models like Haiku for exploration subagents
items.details: Built-in Explore uses Haiku for speed/cost. Custom subagents can specify model: haiku for fast/cheap work, sonnet for balanced, opus for complex reasoning.
items.example: Exploration subagent uses model: haiku; security review uses model: opus
items.newcomerTip: Use haiku for exploratory/repetitive work, opus for high-stakes analysis. Save money and latency.

items.name: Agent SDK subagent methods
items.summary: Claude Agent SDK (Python/TypeScript) supports programmatic subagent definition and invocation for custom agents
items.details: Build custom agents using Agent SDK with full control over orchestration, tool access, permissions. SDK supports spawning subagents. See Agent SDK docs for details.
items.example: Use SDK to build multi-agent workflows with custom logic

catalogs.overview: Claude Code uses a permission system to control what the agent can do—reading files, editing files, running shell commands, and making network requests. Permission modes control how often Claude pauses to ask for approval: from conservative default mode (asks for everything) to auto mode (runs with background safety checks) to bypassPermissions (no checks, for isolated environments only). You control permissions through modes, rules, and the /permissions command, with settings that can be checked into version control for team consistency.

items.name: Permission Modes Overview
items.summary: Six different modes control whether Claude prompts for approval when editing files, running shell commands, or making network requests
items.details: Claude Code offers a tiered system of permission modes, each making different tradeoffs between convenience and oversight. The mode determines what Claude can do without a permission prompt. All modes respect explicit ask and deny rules, and all except bypassPermissions protect against writes to critical paths like .claude/, .git/, and shell config files. Modes can be set as a default in settings.json, passed at startup with --permission-mode, or cycled mid-session with Shift+Tab (in CLI/JetBrains) or the mode indicator (VS Code/Desktop/web).

items.name: default Mode
items.summary: Standard mode: prompts for approval the first time Claude wants to edit a file or run a shell command
items.details: In default mode, Claude can read files without prompting, but pauses to ask before editing, running commands, or making network requests. After you approve once, approval is remembered for that command in that project directory for the rest of the session. This mode is best when getting started or working with sensitive code. Protected paths (like .claude/ and .git/) always prompt even after approval.
items.example: Start a session in default mode: `claude --permission-mode default` or just `claude` (default is the default). Press Shift+Tab once in an existing session to switch to default.
items.newcomerTip: Start here if you're new to Claude Code or working with sensitive projects. You get to review each action as it happens, but won't be interrupted repeatedly for the same command.

items.name: acceptEdits Mode
items.summary: Auto-approves file edits and common filesystem commands without prompting
items.details: acceptEdits mode lets Claude create and edit files, run mkdir/touch/rm/mv/cp/sed, and on Windows run Set-Content/Add-Content/Remove-Item—all without asking. The status bar shows '⏵⏵ accept edits on'. Commands still prompt if they target paths outside the working directory, are write operations on protected paths, or are more complex operations like git push. This mode is ideal for iterating on code you trust the direction of, then reviewing all changes in git diff afterwards.
items.example: Start in acceptEdits: `claude --permission-mode acceptEdits`. Or press Shift+Tab once from default mode. Set as default: `{"permissions": {"defaultMode": "acceptEdits"}}` in settings.json.
items.newcomerTip: Use this when you want Claude to move quickly without asking about every file edit, but you still want prompts for risky operations. Great for fixing bugs or implementing features once you've approved the plan.

items.name: plan Mode
items.summary: Claude explores and proposes changes without editing source files
items.details: Plan mode lets Claude read files and run read-only shell commands to explore your codebase, but it does not edit your source files. Claude writes a plan of what it would do, then waits for you to review and approve it before proceeding. Once you approve the plan, Claude switches to the permission mode you chose (auto, acceptEdits, or manual review) and starts making changes. This is excellent for understanding a codebase before making changes, or for complex refactorings where you want to see the strategy first.
items.example: Start in plan mode: `claude --permission-mode plan`. Or press Shift+Tab from default to enter plan mode (press Shift+Tab again to exit without approving). Prefix a single prompt with `/plan` to plan that request only: `claude "add a dark mode" /plan`.
items.platformNotes: In VS Code, plan mode appears as 'Plan mode' in the mode indicator. On the web/mobile, it appears as 'Plan mode' in the mode dropdown.
items.newcomerTip: Use plan mode when you're uncertain about the impact of changes or want Claude to explain its approach before executing. It's a safe way to explore without risk.

items.name: auto Mode
items.summary: Auto-approves actions with background safety checks that verify alignment with your request
items.details: Auto mode eliminates routine permission prompts by using a classifier model that reviews actions before execution and blocks anything that seems to escalate beyond your request, targets unrecognized infrastructure, or appears driven by hostile content. Explicit ask rules still force a prompt. The classifier trusts your working directory and repo remotes by default and blocks dangerous actions like curl | bash, production deploys, force push to main, and mass deletion. If actions are blocked repeatedly, auto mode falls back to prompting. This is a research preview and requires a recent enough account and Claude model (Opus 4.6+, Sonnet 4.6+, or provider-specific later versions). On Bedrock/Vertex AI/Foundry, set CLAUDE_CODE_ENABLE_AUTO_MODE=1 to enable.
items.example: Start in auto mode: `claude --permission-mode auto`. Or press Shift+Tab to cycle through modes until auto appears. Set as default in ~/.claude/settings.json: `{"permissions": {"defaultMode": "auto"}}`. On Bedrock: set CLAUDE_CODE_ENABLE_AUTO_MODE=1 first, then set defaultMode to auto.
items.newcomerTip: Use auto mode for long tasks where you trust the general direction and want fewer interruptions. It's not a replacement for review on sensitive operations, but it reduces prompt fatigue. You can still add explicit ask rules or boundaries in your prompt to block specific actions.
items.platformNotes: Available on Anthropic API by default. On Bedrock, Vertex AI, and Foundry, requires CLAUDE_CODE_ENABLE_AUTO_MODE=1. Not available in cloud sessions on claude.ai/code (use it for local Remote Control instead).

items.name: dontAsk Mode
items.summary: Auto-denies all tool calls that aren't pre-approved; only reads and allow-listed tools run
items.details: dontAsk mode auto-denies every action that would normally prompt. Only actions matching your permissions.allow rules and read-only Bash commands (like ls, cat, grep) can execute. Explicit ask rules are denied rather than prompting. This mode is fully non-interactive—perfect for CI pipelines, restricted environments, or batch operations where you pre-define exactly what Claude may do. If Claude tries to use a denied tool, the action fails silently.
items.example: Start in dontAsk mode: `claude --permission-mode dontAsk`. Typically combined with pre-approved permissions: `{"permissions": {"allow": ["Bash(npm test)", "Bash(npm run build)"]}}`. Use in CI with: `claude -p "deploy to staging" --permission-mode dontAsk`.
items.newcomerTip: This mode is for automation and scripts, not interactive sessions. Set up your allow rules first, then lock Claude down with dontAsk for safe, repeatable execution.

items.name: bypassPermissions Mode
items.summary: Skips all permission prompts and safety checks; use only in isolated containers/VMs
items.details: bypassPermissions mode disables permission prompts and safety checks so tool calls execute immediately, with no confirmation. Explicit ask rules still force a prompt, and removals targeting root or home (rm -rf /, rm -rf ~) still prompt as a circuit breaker. Protected-path writes are allowed. This mode offers no protection against prompt injection or unintended actions and should only be used in fully isolated environments (containers, VMs, dev containers) where Claude Code cannot damage your host system. Claude Code refuses to start in this mode if running as root/sudo (unless inside a recognized sandbox).
items.example: Start with bypass: `claude --permission-mode bypassPermissions` or `claude --dangerously-skip-permissions` (equivalent). To add it to the Shift+Tab cycle without activating: `claude --allow-dangerously-skip-permissions`, then press Shift+Tab to cycle to it.
items.newcomerTip: Avoid this mode unless you're in a container or VM. For most use cases where you want fewer prompts, auto mode is safer and more appropriate.

items.name: Shift+Tab: Cycling Permission Modes (CLI)
items.summary: Press Shift+Tab in the CLI or JetBrains IDE to cycle through permission modes mid-session
items.details: Shift+Tab cycles through available modes: default → acceptEdits → plan → (optional: bypassPermissions) → (optional: auto) → back to default. The current mode appears in the status bar. Not all modes appear in the cycle by default: auto mode requires an opt-in prompt the first time it appears; bypassPermissions only appears after you start with --permission-mode bypassPermissions or --dangerously-skip-permissions (or --allow-dangerously-skip-permissions to add it without activating). dontAsk never appears in the cycle; set it with --permission-mode dontAsk at startup.
items.example: In a CLI session, press Shift+Tab to switch from default to acceptEdits. Press again to switch to plan. Press again to cycle back to default. Auto mode prompts for opt-in the first time you cycle to it; select 'No, don't ask again' if you want to skip it in future cycles.
items.platformNotes: Works in the CLI terminal and JetBrains IDE terminal. VS Code uses a visual mode selector instead. Desktop and web use dropdown/button selectors.
items.newcomerTip: You don't have to restart to change modes. Shift+Tab is the fastest way to switch gears mid-session—flip to acceptEdits to move faster, or back to default for careful review.

items.name: /permissions Command
items.summary: View, manage, and audit all permission rules and where they come from
items.details: /permissions opens an interactive UI that lists all permission rules organized by tool, showing whether each is Allow, Ask, or Deny. It displays which settings file each rule comes from (user settings, project settings, local project settings, or managed settings). You can navigate with arrow keys, press 'a' to add a rule, 'd' to delete one, and view detailed information about rules. The UI also shows Recently denied tab with actions auto-denied by auto mode. Press 'r' on a recent denial to retry it with manual approval.
items.example: In a session, type `/permissions` and press Enter. You'll see a table of all rules. Use arrow keys to highlight a rule, press Enter for details, 'd' to delete, 'a' to add. The display shows whether each rule comes from user settings (~/.claude/settings.json), project settings (.claude/settings.json), local project settings (.claude/settings.local.json), or managed settings.
items.newcomerTip: If you're confused about why Claude is prompting or not prompting, run /permissions to see exactly what rules are in effect and where they come from. It's your permission audit trail.

items.name: Permission Rules: Allow/Ask/Deny
items.summary: Three rule types control whether Claude can use a tool: allow (auto-approve), ask (prompt), deny (block)
items.details: Permission rules follow precedence: deny first, then ask, then allow. The first match wins. A deny rule blocks the call completely. An ask rule prompts you for approval. An allow rule auto-approves. Rules are evaluated by Claude Code before the model sees them—prompt text and CLAUDE.md don't override rules. Rules can target entire tools (Bash, Read, Edit, WebFetch, etc.) or specific uses with specifiers like Bash(npm run test), Read(./.env), or WebFetch(domain:example.com). Deny and ask rules can match on input parameters with syntax like Agent(model:opus). Settings precedence is: managed settings (highest, cannot be overridden) → command-line flags → local project settings → shared project settings → user settings (lowest). If a tool is denied at any level, no other level can allow it.
items.example: "allow": ["Bash(npm test)"] auto-approves npm test. "deny": ["Bash(rm *)"] blocks all rm commands. "ask": ["WebFetch(domain:*.example.com)"] prompts for requests to *.example.com. Within one settings file: "deny": ["Bash(curl *)"], "allow": ["Bash(npm *)"]—deny is checked first, so curl is blocked even though Bash is broadly allowed.
items.newcomerTip: Think of rules as a security boundary. Deny is your firewall, ask is your approval gate, allow is your trusted list. Use deny sparingly and as high as possible in precedence.

items.name: Bash Permission Rules: Exact Match
items.summary: Match exact Bash commands with Bash(command) syntax
items.details: Bash rules match commands using exact-match or wildcard patterns. Exact match with no wildcards: Bash(npm run build) matches only that exact command. The space before a wildcard matters: Bash(ls *) enforces a word boundary and matches ls -la but not lsof, while Bash(ls*) without a space matches both. Compound commands with &&, ||, ;, |, &, and newlines are evaluated per-subcommand, so you need rules for each part. When you approve a compound command like git status && npm test, Claude Code saves separate rules for each subcommand that needs approval. Process wrappers like timeout, time, nice, nohup, and xargs (without flags) are stripped before matching, so Bash(npm test *) matches timeout 30 npm test. Exec wrappers like watch, setsid, or find -exec always prompt and cannot be auto-approved.
items.example: Bash(npm run build) matches only npm run build. Bash(npm run *) matches npm run build, npm run test, npm run lint, etc. Bash(npm *) matches any npm command. Bash(git * main) matches git checkout main, git log main, git push origin main. Approve git status && npm test once, and Claude Code saves Bash(npm test) to allow it later independently.
items.newcomerTip: Use wildcards (*) to match command families without enumerating every variant. But be specific—Bash(npm *) is safer than Bash(*) because it narrows the scope.

items.name: Bash Permission Rules: Wildcards and Patterns
items.summary: Use * wildcards to match command families at any position in the command string
items.details: Bash rules support glob patterns with * at the beginning, middle, or end. A single * matches any sequence of characters including spaces, so Bash(git *) matches multi-word commands like git log --oneline --all. The :* suffix is equivalent to a trailing space-* (Bash(ls:*) matches Bash(ls *)). Unquoted glob patterns are permitted for fully read-only commands, so ls *.ts runs without a prompt. Commands with write or exec flags like find, sed, and git still prompt when an unquoted glob is present. Compound commands like cd packages/api && npm test are each checked independently; the compound runs without prompting only if each part qualifies on its own.
items.example: Bash(npm run test *) matches npm run test:unit, npm run test:integration, npm run test:e2e. Bash(* --version) matches any command with --version at the end: git --version, npm --version, etc. Bash(git * main) matches git checkout main, git push origin main, git log --oneline main.
items.newcomerTip: A single * in Bash rules is powerful—one pattern can cover many commands. But be conservative: Bash(docker *) is safer than Bash(*) because it restricts to docker instead of allowing everything.

items.name: Read/Edit Permission Rules: Path Patterns
items.summary: Control file access with path patterns using /path (project), ~/path (home), //path (absolute), and path (relative to cwd)
items.details: Read and Edit rules follow gitignore specification with four anchors: //path (absolute from filesystem root), ~/path (from home directory), /path (relative to project root), and path or ./path (relative to current directory). * matches within one path segment, ** matches across directories. Example: Edit(/src/**/*.ts) allows editing TypeScript files in project/src/. Read(~/.zshrc) allows reading home/.zshrc. Edit(//tmp/scratch.txt) allows editing /tmp/scratch.txt absolutely. Read(.env) matches any .env at or under current directory. On Windows, paths normalize to POSIX: C:\Users\alice becomes /c/Users/alice. A deny rule like Read(.env) blocks any .env at or under cwd but not .env in parent directories. Symlinks are checked both at the symlink path and target: allow rules require both to match; deny rules block if either matches.
items.example: Edit(/docs/**) allows editing anything in <project>/docs/. Read(~/.ssh/**) blocks reading anything in ~/.ssh/. Edit(//c/Users/alice/file) on Windows allows editing C:\Users\alice\file. Read(*.env) allows reading .env in current directory only, not subdirectories (use Read(**/.env) to allow all depths).
items.newcomerTip: The anchor matters: /path is project-relative, ~/path is home-relative, //path is filesystem-absolute. Most of the time you'll use /path for project files and deny sensitive areas like ~/.ssh.

items.name: WebFetch Permission Rules: Domain Filtering
items.summary: Control web access with domain: specifiers that match hostnames
items.details: WebFetch rules use domain: prefix and match hostnames case-insensitively. WebFetch(domain:example.com) matches example.com. WebFetch(domain:*.example.com) matches api.example.com and a.b.example.com but not example.com itself. WebFetch(domain:example.*) matches example.org or example.com but not example.evil.com (the wildcard spans only to the next dot, preventing attackers from registering example.bad.com). WebFetch(domain:*) or bare WebFetch matches every domain. A trailing dot is stripped from both rule and hostname, so example.com. is the same as example.com.
items.example: WebFetch(domain:github.com) allows fetches to github.com. WebFetch(domain:*.github.com) allows any GitHub subdomain. WebFetch(domain:*.anthropic.com) allows Anthropic and all subdomains. Deny with: "deny": ["WebFetch(domain:*.internal.company.com)"] blocks internal company domains.
items.newcomerTip: Use domain rules to restrict web access to known-safe domains. Combined with a deny rule on Bash(curl *), you can force Claude to use WebFetch instead, giving you domain-level control.

items.name: Tool-Specific Permission Rules: MCP Servers
items.summary: Control MCP server tool access with mcp__servername syntax
items.details: MCP server tools are named with the format mcp__servername__toolname. Permission rules can target entire servers or specific tools within them. mcp__puppeteer matches any tool from the puppeteer server. mcp__puppeteer__* also matches all puppeteer tools. mcp__puppeteer__puppeteer_navigate matches the specific puppeteer_navigate tool. Deny rules with bare server names (mcp__puppeteer) remove that entire server from Claude's context. To deny every MCP tool, use "deny": ["mcp__*"]. Allow rules accept tool-name globs only after a literal mcp__<server>__ prefix; unanchored globs like mcp__* in allow rules are skipped with a warning.
items.example: "deny": ["mcp__github__*"] blocks all GitHub MCP tools. "allow": ["mcp__slack__send_message"] pre-approves Slack message sends. To block one GitHub tool while allowing others, you'd need to use an ask rule or hook; deny is all-or-nothing per rule.
items.newcomerTip: MCP tool names can be long. Run /permissions to see the exact canonical names of MCP tools in your session, then use those in your rules.

items.name: Tool-Specific Permission Rules: Subagents
items.summary: Control subagent use with Agent(agentname) rules
items.details: Subagents (mini agents spawned by Claude) are controlled with Agent() rules. Agent(Explore) controls the Explore subagent, Agent(Plan) controls the Plan subagent, Agent(my-custom-agent) controls custom subagents by name. Use deny rules to disable specific agents: "deny": ["Agent(Explore)"] blocks spawning Explore. Allow rules cannot grant access to subagents (this is intentional for safety), but you can deny them.
items.example: "deny": ["Agent(Explore)"] prevents Claude from spawning the Explore subagent. Set in .claude/settings.json or with CLI flag --disallowedTools Agent(Explore).
items.newcomerTip: Subagents are advanced. If you want to prevent Claude from splitting work across agents, add a deny rule for the agents you want to block.

items.name: Permission Rule Syntax: Parameter Matching
items.summary: Deny and ask rules can match on input parameters with Tool(param:value) syntax
items.details: Deny and ask rules can target specific parameter values with Tool(param:value) syntax. Agent(model:opus) matches Agent calls that request the Opus model. Agent(isolation:worktree) matches Agent calls that request git worktree isolation. Bash(run_in_background:true) matches Bash calls with run_in_background=true. Values support * wildcard: Agent(isolation:*) matches any explicit isolation value. Parameters must be direct fields (not nested), the value is compared against the literal input Claude sends (before normalization), and a parameter the model omits is never matched. For fields already matched by their tool's own specifier (like command for Bash), parameter matching is not available—use tool-specific syntax instead. A warning is emitted at startup if a rule is invalid.
items.example: "ask": ["Agent(model:*)"] prompts before spawning any subagent that names a model. "deny": ["Bash(run_in_background:true)"] blocks background bash execution. Run with --verbose to see exact parameter names and values in each tool call.
items.newcomerTip: Parameter matching is advanced. Use it when you want to gate on something besides what the tool naturally matches, like model selection or isolation mode.

items.name: Protected Paths: Automatic Safeguards
items.summary: Writes to .claude/, .git/, .vscode/, and other critical paths never auto-approve in default/acceptEdits/plan/auto modes
items.details: Claude Code protects a fixed set of paths from accidental corruption in every mode except bypassPermissions. Protected directories include .git, .claude (except .claude/worktrees), .vscode, .idea, .husky, .cargo, .devcontainer, .yarn, .mvn, and .config/git. Protected files include .gitconfig, .bashrc, .npmrc, .bazelrc, .pre-commit-config.yaml, and many others. In default/acceptEdits/plan modes, protected-path writes prompt. In auto mode, they route to the classifier. In dontAsk mode, they're denied. In bypassPermissions, they're allowed. Permission allow rules in settings.json do not override protected-path checks; a rule like Edit(.claude/**) still prompts in default mode. When Claude tries to edit a .claude/ file, the prompt offers "Yes, and allow Claude to edit its own settings for this session", which approves later .claude/ edits in that session.
items.example: Claude wants to edit .claude/settings.json in default mode: prompted. Claude wants to edit src/app.ts in default mode: prompted only once per command, remembered for the session. Claude wants to rm .git/hooks/pre-commit in dontAsk mode: denied. Approve a .claude/ write in default mode: future .claude/ edits that session skip the prompt.
items.newcomerTip: Protected paths are automatic. You don't configure them. They're there to prevent accidents—if Claude tries to corrupt .git or your shell config, you'll always be asked to confirm first (except in bypassPermissions).

items.name: Settings Files and Configuration
items.summary: Configure permissions in settings.json or settings.local.json checked into version control, or ~/.claude/settings.json for user-level defaults
items.details: Permission rules are defined in settings files as allow/ask/deny arrays. .claude/settings.json (in project root) is checked into version control and shared across the team. .claude/settings.local.json is local-only (in .gitignore). ~/.claude/settings.json is user-level (applies to all projects). Managed settings files (e.g., deployed via MDM) cannot be overridden. Settings precedence: managed > command-line flags > local project > shared project > user. Set the default permission mode with {"permissions": {"defaultMode": "acceptEdits"}} in any file. Set additional directories with {"permissions": {"additionalDirectories": ["/path/to/dir"]}} to extend file access beyond the working directory.
items.example: In .claude/settings.json: {"permissions": {"allow": ["Bash(npm run *)", "Bash(git commit *)"], "deny": ["Bash(rm *)"], "defaultMode": "acceptEdits"}}. User-level in ~/.claude/settings.json: {"permissions": {"defaultMode": "plan"}} makes plan the default for all projects, until overridden by a project setting.
items.platformNotes: Windows: use forward slashes in paths. WSL can inherit Windows managed settings if wslInheritsWindowsSettings is set in Windows registry or C:\Program Files\ClaudeCode\managed-settings.json.
items.newcomerTip: Commit .claude/settings.json to your repo so the whole team has consistent rules. Use .claude/settings.local.json for personal overrides (add to .gitignore).

items.name: Disable Dangerous Modes: Managed Settings
items.summary: Administrators can prevent auto mode or bypassPermissions by setting disableAutoMode or disableBypassPermissionsMode in managed settings
items.details: The managed settings permissions.disableAutoMode and permissions.disableBypassPermissionsMode can be set to "disable" to prevent users from using those modes. Managed settings cannot be overridden by user or project settings, making this an organizational policy control. Set CLAUDE_CODE_ENABLE_AUTO_MODE=1 to allow auto mode on Bedrock/Vertex AI/Foundry; disableAutoMode still applies and can lock it off.
items.example: In managed settings (e.g., /etc/claude-code/settings.json on Linux or via MDM): {"permissions": {"disableBypassPermissionsMode": "disable"}} blocks --dangerously-skip-permissions. {"permissions": {"disableAutoMode": "disable"}} blocks auto mode.
items.newcomerTip: This is for teams and organizations. If you're an admin and want to lock down your org's Claude Code, use managed settings to enforce policy.

items.name: PreToolUse Hook: Custom Permission Logic
items.summary: Register a shell command in settings.json hooks to evaluate permissions at runtime before the permission prompt
items.details: PreToolUse hooks run before the permission prompt and can return "allow", "ask", or a blocking exit code 2. A hook returning "allow" skips the prompt (unless an ask rule matches). A hook returning "ask" prompts. Exit code 2 blocks the tool call before permission rules are evaluated. Deny and ask rules always take precedence: a deny rule still blocks even if the hook returns "allow", and an ask rule still prompts even if the hook returns "allow". This lets you implement custom logic like blocking edits to protected files or validating URLs. See /en/hooks-guide for hook script templates.
items.example: Hook that blocks edits to CHANGELOG.md: in settings.json, register a PreToolUse hook script that checks if the call is Edit(CHANGELOG.md) and exits with code 2 if so. The hook runs before the permission prompt, so the edit is blocked immediately.

items.name: Add Directory Access: --add-dir and /add-dir
items.summary: Extend file access to directories outside the working directory with --add-dir flag or /add-dir command
items.details: By default, Claude can read and edit files only in the directory where it was launched. Use --add-dir /path or /add-dir /path to extend access to additional directories. Files in additional directories follow the same permission rules as the primary working directory. Directories added with --add-dir also load skills (.claude/skills/), subagents (.claude/agents/), and some settings from their .claude/ folders (not full configuration). Use /cd to change the session's primary working directory instead, which loads the new directory's full CLAUDE.md and configuration.
items.example: Start with: claude --add-dir ~/shared-lib --add-dir /opt/tools. In a running session: /add-dir ~/another-project to grant access.
items.newcomerTip: Use --add-dir when you need to work across multiple project directories in one session. Use /cd when you want to fully switch to a new working directory with its own configuration.

items.name: Non-Interactive Mode: -p Flag and dontAsk
items.summary: Run Claude Code without prompts using -p flag for one-off commands or --permission-mode dontAsk for scripts
items.details: The -p flag runs a single prompt in headless mode (no interactive prompt, output to stdout). Combined with --permission-mode dontAsk, Claude only executes pre-approved actions. Example: claude -p "deploy to staging" --permission-mode dontAsk --allowedTools Bash(npm run deploy). In dontAsk, if Claude tries an unapproved action, the session aborts (in non-interactive mode with -p). This is perfect for CI/CD pipelines and automation where you pre-define exactly what Claude may do.
items.example: claude -p "write a README" --permission-mode dontAsk (runs once, exits). In CI: claude -p "run tests" --permission-mode dontAsk --allowedTools Bash(npm test)

items.name: Permissions and Sandboxing: Defense in Depth
items.summary: Permissions and sandboxing are complementary layers: permissions control which tools Claude can use, sandboxing restricts what Bash commands can do at the OS level
items.details: Permissions are application-level: they control which tools Claude Code can invoke and what file paths or domains it can access. Sandboxing is OS-level: it restricts Bash commands' filesystem and network access via container/VM boundaries. Both layers apply independently. A permission deny rule blocks Claude from even attempting to access a resource. A sandbox restriction prevents Bash from reaching resources outside defined boundaries, even if a prompt injection bypassed Claude's decision-making. When sandboxing is enabled with autoAllowBashIfSandboxed: true (the default), sandboxed Bash runs without prompting even on a bare Bash ask rule, because the sandbox boundary substitutes for the whole-tool prompt. Content-scoped ask rules like Bash(git push *) still force a prompt. Read and Edit deny rules combine with sandbox filesystem restrictions; Network denials combine with allowedDomains/deniedDomains.
