items.example: Permissions: deny: ["Bash(curl *)"] blocks curl at the tool level. Sandboxing: deniedDomains: ["internal.company.com"] blocks network access at the OS level. Together: curl is blocked by permission, and even if a subprocess bypassed that, the sandbox would block the network connection.

items.name: Settings Precedence: Which Rules Win
items.summary: Rules are evaluated in order: managed settings > CLI flags > local project > shared project > user settings. Deny rules from any level override allow rules from lower levels.
items.details: Permission settings follow a precedence hierarchy. If a tool is denied at any level, higher precedence levels cannot override it (fail-secure). Managed settings are highest and cannot be overridden. Command-line flags override all files. .claude/settings.local.json (local project) overrides .claude/settings.json (shared project), which overrides ~/.claude/settings.json (user). If user settings allow a tool and project settings deny it, the deny wins. This prevents a team's security policy from being bypassed by user settings.
items.example: User settings: {"permissions": {"allow": ["Bash(*)"]}}, Project settings: {"permissions": {"deny": ["Bash(rm *)"]}}. Result: Bash is broadly allowed except rm is denied. CLI flag: claude --disallowedTools Bash(aws *) blocks AWS commands even if all settings allow them.
items.newcomerTip: Managed and deny rules are strongest. If you're locked out of something, check /permissions to see which level is blocking you.

items.name: --dangerously-skip-permissions Flag
items.summary: Shorthand for --permission-mode bypassPermissions; starts Claude without any permission checks
items.details: --dangerously-skip-permissions is equivalent to --permission-mode bypassPermissions. It disables all permission prompts and safety checks (except explicit ask rules and root/home removals). Only use in isolated containers, VMs, or dev containers without internet access where Claude Code cannot damage your host. On Linux/macOS, Claude Code refuses to start in this mode if running as root or sudo, unless inside a recognized sandbox.
items.example: claude --dangerously-skip-permissions (same as claude --permission-mode bypassPermissions). Inside a Docker container: allowed and safe. On your local machine: dangerous.
items.platformNotes: Windows with Git for Bash: supported. Windows without Git for Bash (PowerShell only): supported. Linux/macOS: blocked if running as root/sudo (use /sandbox instead for safe isolation).
items.newcomerTip: Don't use this flag unless you're certain you're in an isolated environment. Auto mode is the safer alternative for reducing prompts.

items.name: --allow-dangerously-skip-permissions Flag
items.summary: Adds bypassPermissions to the Shift+Tab cycle without activating it, so you can toggle it on mid-session
items.details: --allow-dangerously-skip-permissions enables bypassPermissions mode in the Shift+Tab cycling menu but starts the session in default mode. This lets you toggle bypass permissions on and off during the session without restarting. The bypassPermissions mode only appears in the Shift+Tab cycle after you use this flag.
items.example: claude --allow-dangerously-skip-permissions starts in default mode, but you can press Shift+Tab to cycle to bypassPermissions. Useful for long CI/automation sessions where you want the option to toggle it.
items.newcomerTip: This is useful if you might need to toggle bypass mode mid-session but want to start cautiously.

items.name: View Recently Denied Actions: /permissions
items.summary: The /permissions command shows a 'Recently denied' tab listing actions blocked by auto mode, where you can retry them
items.details: In auto mode, denied actions are logged in the /permissions UI under the 'Recently denied' tab. You can navigate to an action and press 'r' to retry it with manual approval. This is useful for discovering why the classifier blocked something and deciding whether to add it to your allow rules or adjust your prompt.
items.example: Auto mode denies a curl command because it targets an unknown domain. Run /permissions, navigate to 'Recently denied', highlight that curl action, press 'r' to retry with a manual prompt. Then you can decide whether to add a WebFetch allow rule for that domain.
items.newcomerTip: Auto mode denials aren't permanent failures. Use /permissions to review and retry them.

items.name: Allowlist Safe Commands: Compound Commands
items.summary: When you approve a compound command like 'git status && npm test' once, Claude Code saves separate rules for each subcommand that needs approval, so future similar commands don't re-prompt
items.details: Compound commands separated by &&, ||, ;, |, |&, &, or newlines are each checked independently. When you approve a compound command and select "Yes, don't ask again", Claude Code saves separate rules for each subcommand that required approval (up to 5 rules). This means if you approve git status && npm test, Claude Code saves a rule for npm test, so future npm test invocations are recognized regardless of what precedes it. Subcommands like cd into a subdirectory also generate their own Read rule.
items.example: Approve git status && npm test once. Claude Code saves Bash(npm test) internally. Later, npm test alone runs without prompting, and npm test && npm run build also runs without prompting for npm test (though npm run build might prompt if not yet approved).
items.newcomerTip: Approval is smart—it extracts the actual commands from compound statements, so you don't have to re-approve each one separately.

items.name: Process Wrappers: Automatic Stripping
items.summary: Bash rules automatically strip process wrappers like timeout, time, nice, nohup, stdbuf, and bare xargs, so rules for the inner command apply
items.details: Claude Code recognizes a fixed set of process wrappers and strips them before matching Bash rules. timeout, time, nice, nohup, and stdbuf are built-in. Bare xargs (with no flags) is also stripped. This means Bash(npm test *) matches timeout 30 npm test. Development environment runners like direnv exec, devbox run, mise exec, npx, and docker exec are NOT stripped, so a rule like Bash(devbox run *) matches the entire string including what comes after run. To approve work inside an environment runner, write a specific rule like Bash(devbox run npm test). Exec wrappers like watch, setsid, ionice, and flock always prompt and cannot be auto-approved; same with find -exec or -delete.
items.example: Bash(npm test) matches npm test and also matches time npm test (time is stripped). Bash(devbox run npm test) matches devbox run npm test exactly (devbox run is not stripped, so you must include it). Bash(watch npm test) always prompts because watch is an exec wrapper.
items.newcomerTip: Wrappers are usually safe to auto-approve alongside their inner commands. If you want to allow npm test, the same rule covers it with or without a timeout wrapper.

items.name: Read-Only Bash Commands: No Prompt Needed
items.summary: A built-in set of read-only Bash commands (ls, cat, echo, pwd, head, tail, grep, find, wc, which, diff, stat, du, cd, git status, etc.) run without permission prompts in every mode
items.details: Claude Code recognizes certain Bash commands as read-only and auto-approves them without a prompt in every mode except when explicitly blocked by an ask/deny rule. These include ls, cat, echo, pwd, head, tail, grep, find, wc, which, diff, stat, du, cd, and read-only forms of git (like git status, git log, git show). Unquoted globs are permitted for fully read-only commands (ls *.ts runs without prompt), but commands with write or exec flags (find, sed, git with write operations) still prompt when unquoted globs appear. The set is not configurable; to require a prompt for one of these, add an ask or deny rule.
items.example: ls, cat file.txt, pwd, git status, head -20 app.py, grep pattern src/*.ts all run without prompting. ls with a glob ls *.ts also runs without prompting. But sed -i s/old/new/ *.ts prompts even though sed is partially read-only, because -i is a write flag.
items.newcomerTip: You don't need to approve every ls or cat. Read-only commands are fast and safe. If you want stricter control, add an ask rule.

catalogs.overview: Claude Code uses a multi-level memory hierarchy consisting of CLAUDE.md files at different scopes (organization, user, project, local) plus auto-memory that Claude writes automatically. These files persist instructions, learnings, and configurations across sessions without rewriting them every time. The system includes plain text markdown files you maintain, optional path-scoped rules for file-type-specific guidance, and automatic learning that Claude captures from corrections and debugging patterns.

items.name: CLAUDE.md (Project Root)
items.summary: Team-shared instructions committed to version control at ./CLAUDE.md or ./.claude/CLAUDE.md
items.details: The primary memory file for a project, readable by all team members through git. Should contain project-wide conventions, build commands, architecture decisions, coding standards, naming conventions, and common workflows. Limit to under 200 lines to preserve context tokens. Loaded at the start of every session in that project.
items.example:
Create ./CLAUDE.md with: '# Build Commands
npm run build && npm test

# Code Standards
- Use TypeScript
- 2-space indentation
- ESLint before commit'
items.newcomerTip: Start with /init to auto-generate a CLAUDE.md from your codebase, then refine it with your team's specific guidelines.

items.name: CLAUDE.local.md (Personal Project)
items.summary: Personal project-specific preferences that stay local to your machine and aren't committed to git
items.details: Add to .gitignore so it's never committed. Use for sandbox-specific URLs, local test data, personal workflow tweaks, or machine-specific paths. Loads alongside CLAUDE.md at session start. /init with the personal option auto-adds it to .gitignore. In git worktrees, a gitignored CLAUDE.local.md only exists in the specific worktree where you created it.
items.example:
~/.claude/CLAUDE.local.md:
local-api-key=xxx\ntest-db-url=http://localhost:5432
items.newcomerTip: If you have sandbox URLs or test credentials, CLAUDE.local.md keeps them out of version control while still available every session.

items.name: ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md (User Instructions)
items.summary: Your personal coding preferences that apply across all projects on this machine
items.details: Loaded before project CLAUDE.md, so project-specific instructions override user preferences. Use for personal coding style, favorite libraries, workflow shortcuts, or conventions you want everywhere. Shared only with you across all your projects. Loaded in full at session start.
items.example:
$HOME/.claude/CLAUDE.md:
# Personal Preferences
- Always use pnpm, not npm
- Prefer async/await over .then()
- Run prettier before commit
items.newcomerTip: This is your personal playbook for coding style and tooling choices—it's a great place for 'I always...' rules.

items.name: Managed CLAUDE.md (Organization-wide)
items.summary: Organization policy file deployed by IT/DevOps to enforce company-wide coding standards and compliance
items.details: Deployed to a managed policy location that all users on a machine read. Cannot be excluded. Locations vary: macOS `/Library/Application Support/ClaudeCode/CLAUDE.md`, Linux/WSL `/etc/claude-code/CLAUDE.md`, Windows `C:\Program Files\ClaudeCode\CLAUDE.md`. Can also be set inline in managed-settings.json with a `claudeMd` key. Loads before user and project CLAUDE.md, establishing baseline compliance.
items.example: Managed org policy: 'Never commit to main branch. Always run security-audit.sh before deploy.'
items.newcomerTip: If your company manages Claude Code centrally, this is where company-wide rules come from—you can't override it.
items.platformNotes: macOS: `/Library/Application Support/ClaudeCode/CLAUDE.md`. Windows: `C:\Program Files\ClaudeCode\CLAUDE.md`. Linux/WSL: `/etc/claude-code/CLAUDE.md`

items.name: Load Order and Precedence
items.summary: CLAUDE.md files are loaded from filesystem root down to working directory, with later entries overriding earlier ones
items.details: Claude Code walks up the directory tree from your working directory, loading all CLAUDE.md and CLAUDE.local.md files. Content is ordered from filesystem root down, so instructions in your working directory appear last and take highest priority. Within each directory, CLAUDE.local.md is appended after CLAUDE.md. Subdirectories load on demand when Claude reads files in them. Managed policy CLAUDE.md cannot be excluded and always applies.
items.example: Running 'claude' in /projects/myapp loads: (1) managed CLAUDE.md, (2) ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md, (3) /projects/CLAUDE.md, (4) /projects/myapp/CLAUDE.md, (5) /projects/myapp/CLAUDE.local.md. Each later file's rules appear in context after earlier ones, so myapp CLAUDE.md overrides /projects/ if they conflict.
items.newcomerTip: More specific (closer to your working directory) CLAUDE.md files override broader ones. Local beats project beats user.

items.name: @path imports
items.summary: Import external files into CLAUDE.md using @relative/path syntax for modular organization
items.details: In CLAUDE.md, use @path/to/file to import another file's content inline. Supports relative paths (resolved from the CLAUDE.md file's directory) and absolute paths. Imported files expand at launch, not on demand, so they consume context tokens like inline content. Skip code spans and code blocks during parsing. Supports recursive imports up to 4 hops deep. Wrap paths in backticks (like `@README`) to mention them literally without importing.
items.newcomerTip: Use @imports to keep CLAUDE.md organized when you have lots of shared docs, but remember imported files still load into context.

items.name: .claude/rules/ (Path-Scoped Rules)
items.summary: Organize instructions into topic files in .claude/rules/ with optional YAML frontmatter to scope them to specific file patterns
items.details:
Place markdown files in .claude/rules/ to organize large projects. Each file is one topic (e.g., testing.md, api-design.md, security.md). Rules without a `paths` frontmatter load unconditionally like CLAUDE.md. Rules with YAML frontmatter like '---
paths:
  - "src/api/**/*.ts"
---' only load when Claude reads matching files. Glob patterns are supported (e.g., **/*.ts, src/**/*.{ts,tsx}). Recursive discovery allows subdirectories like frontend/ and backend/. Load into context when matched, saving space by not loading unrelated rules.
items.example:
.claude/rules/api-design.md:
---
paths:
  - "src/api/**/*.ts"
---
# API Development Rules
- All endpoints require input validation
- Use standard error response format
- Include OpenAPI comments
items.newcomerTip: Path-scoped rules are perfect for large monorepos: only load backend rules when Claude touches backend files, frontend rules for frontend.

items.name: ~/.claude/rules/ (User-Level Rules)
items.summary: Personal rules in ~/.claude/rules/ apply to every project on your machine
items.details: Create topic-specific markdown files in ~/.claude/rules/ for personal preferences that aren't project-specific. Load before project rules, so project rules override them. Useful for your coding style, preferred workflows, and personal automation rules. Unlike CLAUDE.md, these are modular and only load when matching paths are encountered.
items.example:
~/.claude/rules/preferences.md:
# Personal Coding Style
- Use const over let
- Always run lint before committing
- Prefer explicit error handling
items.newcomerTip: Your personal rules directory is like personal CLAUDE.md but modular—good for coding standards you want everywhere but don't need in every session.

items.name: Auto Memory (MEMORY.md)
items.summary: Claude writes notes automatically in ~/.claude/projects/<project>/memory/MEMORY.md as it discovers build commands, debugging patterns, and your preferences
items.details: Auto memory is on by default. Claude decides what's worth remembering: build commands, debugging insights, architecture notes, code style preferences discovered through your corrections. Stored per git repository (all worktrees share one memory directory). First 200 lines or 25KB of MEMORY.md loads at session start; topic files load on demand. Plain markdown you can edit or delete anytime. Disable with /memory toggle or set `autoMemoryEnabled: false` in settings.json, or use `CLAUDE_CODE_DISABLE_AUTO_MEMORY=1` environment variable.
items.example:
MEMORY.md after debugging a build issue:
# Learnings
## Build Command
`npm run build` requires NODE_ENV=production
## Jest Config
Tests in src/ must have .test.ts extension
items.newcomerTip: Auto memory is Claude learning from your corrections—you don't write it, Claude does. Run /memory to see what it learned.

items.name: /memory (command)
items.summary: View, edit, and manage all CLAUDE.md, CLAUDE.local.md, rules files, and auto-memory from within a session
items.details: Opens a menu listing all loaded memory files. Select any file to open it in your editor. Toggle auto memory on/off from here. View the auto-memory folder. When you ask Claude to remember something (e.g., 'remember that the API tests need Redis'), Claude saves it to auto memory. To add instructions to CLAUDE.md instead, ask Claude directly or edit the file yourself via /memory.
items.example: Type /memory → see all CLAUDE.md files, CLAUDE.local.md, .claude/rules/ files, and auto memory index. Select one to edit.
items.newcomerTip: When you want to update project memory, use /memory to open the files. If you ask Claude to 'remember X', it goes to auto memory; to update CLAUDE.md, ask 'add X to CLAUDE.md'.
items.syntax: In a session, type `/memory` at the start of a line

items.name: /init (command)
items.summary: Auto-generate a starter CLAUDE.md by analyzing your codebase for build commands, test instructions, and conventions
items.details: Analyzes the codebase and creates a CLAUDE.md with discovered build commands, test instructions, and project conventions. If CLAUDE.md exists, /init suggests improvements instead of overwriting. Refine the generated file from there. Set `CLAUDE_CODE_NEW_INIT=1` to enable interactive multi-phase setup that walks through CLAUDE.md files, skills, and hooks, explores the codebase with a subagent, fills gaps with follow-up questions, and presents a reviewable proposal before writing.
items.example: /init → Claude analyzes your project and generates CLAUDE.md with 'npm run build', 'npm test', naming conventions, etc.
items.newcomerTip: Start every project with /init to jumpstart CLAUDE.md, then refine it with team-specific rules.
items.syntax: In a session, type `/init` at the start of a line

items.name: claudeMdExcludes (setting)
items.summary: Exclude specific CLAUDE.md files by path or glob pattern to skip irrelevant instructions in large monorepos
items.details: In large monorepos, ancestor CLAUDE.md files may be irrelevant to your work. Set `claudeMdExcludes` in .claude/settings.local.json to skip specific files by absolute path or glob pattern. Patterns use glob syntax and match against absolute file paths. Arrays merge across settings layers. Managed policy CLAUDE.md cannot be excluded—organization policies always apply. Common use case: exclude other teams' CLAUDE.md files in a shared monorepo.
items.newcomerTip: If you're in a large monorepo and see irrelevant team rules in /memory, add them to claudeMdExcludes to clean it up.

items.name: Writing Effective Instructions
items.summary: Keep CLAUDE.md specific, concise, and well-structured to maximize Claude's adherence and reduce context waste
items.details: CLAUDE.md content is delivered as a user message, not enforced by the system, so how you write matters. (1) Size: target under 200 lines per file. Longer files consume more context and reduce adherence. Use path-scoped rules to load instructions only when needed. (2) Structure: use markdown headers and bullets to group related instructions. Organized sections are easier to follow. (3) Specificity: write concrete rules, not vague ones. 'Use 2-space indentation' works better than 'Format code properly'. 'API handlers live in src/api/handlers/' works better than 'Keep files organized'. (4) Consistency: review CLAUDE.md files periodically to remove outdated or conflicting rules. If two files contradict, Claude may pick one arbitrarily.
items.example:
Bad: 'Format code nicely and test your changes.'
Good: 'Run npm test before commit. Use 2-space indentation. Format with prettier on save.'
items.newcomerTip: Think of CLAUDE.md like a code review checklist—be specific about what you want checked, and Claude follows the checklist better.

items.name: AGENTS.md Compatibility
items.summary: If your repo uses AGENTS.md for other coding agents, create a CLAUDE.md that imports it so both tools read the same instructions
items.details: Some repos already use AGENTS.md for Cursor, Devin, or other agents. Instead of duplicating instructions, create CLAUDE.md with @AGENTS.md import. Claude loads the imported file, then you can add Claude-specific instructions below. On Windows, symlinks may require Administrator or Developer Mode, so use the @AGENTS.md import instead. Running /init in a repo with AGENTS.md reads it and incorporates relevant parts into the generated CLAUDE.md. Also reads .cursorrules, .devin/rules/, and .windsurfrules automatically.
items.example:
CLAUDE.md:
@AGENTS.md

## Claude Code
Use plan mode for changes under src/billing/
items.newcomerTip: If you already have AGENTS.md, just import it into CLAUDE.md to avoid duplicating effort.

items.name: Subdirectory CLAUDE.md
items.summary: Create CLAUDE.md files in subdirectories of large projects to scope instructions to that subtree
items.details: Subdirectory CLAUDE.md files don't load at launch like root CLAUDE.md. Instead, they load on demand when Claude reads files in those subdirectories. Useful for large projects where different team areas have different conventions. Example: src/api/CLAUDE.md for API-specific rules loads when Claude touches src/api/ files, but doesn't bloat the context for frontend work in src/ui/.
items.example:
Project structure:
project/
  CLAUDE.md                (loads at session start)
  src/
    api/
      CLAUDE.md          (loads when Claude reads src/api/**)
    ui/
      CLAUDE.md          (loads when Claude reads src/ui/**)
items.newcomerTip: Subdirectory CLAUDE.md files let large projects keep separate rules for separate teams, loaded only when relevant.

items.name: What Survives /compact
items.summary: After /compact (context summarization), project-root CLAUDE.md is re-read, but nested subdirectory CLAUDE.md files need another file read to reload
items.details: /compact summarizes conversation history to free context. After compaction, Claude re-reads and re-injects project-root CLAUDE.md from disk. Nested CLAUDE.md files in subdirectories don't auto-reload; they reload the next time Claude reads a file in that subdirectory. If an instruction disappeared after /compact, it was either conversation-only or lived in a nested file that hasn't reloaded yet. Add persistent instructions to project CLAUDE.md to survive compaction.
items.example: After /compact, root ./CLAUDE.md reloads but ./src/api/CLAUDE.md doesn't until Claude reads a file in src/api/.
items.newcomerTip: If you need an instruction to survive /compact, put it in the root CLAUDE.md, not a conversation message or nested file.

items.name: Auto Memory Storage Location
items.summary: Auto memory files are stored in ~/.claude/projects/<project>/memory/, indexed by git repository or project root
items.details: Each project gets a machine-local memory directory. The <project> path is derived from the git repository name, so all worktrees and subdirectories within the same repo share one auto memory directory. Outside a git repo, the project root is used instead. To customize the location, set `autoMemoryDirectory` in settings.json (user, project, local, or policy scope). Must be an absolute path or start with ~/. When set in .claude/settings.json or .claude/settings.local.json, honored only after you trust the workspace.
items.example:
Default: ~/.claude/projects/my-project/memory/MEMORY.md
Custom: In settings.json, set "autoMemoryDirectory": "~/my-custom-memory-dir"
items.newcomerTip: Auto memory lives on your local machine per project—it's not synced across machines or to cloud.

items.name: Troubleshooting: CLAUDE.md Not Followed
items.summary: If Claude ignores your CLAUDE.md, check that it's loaded, make instructions more specific, and look for conflicts
items.details: CLAUDE.md is delivered as a user message, not a system constraint, so there's no guarantee Claude strictly follows vague instructions. To debug: (1) Run /memory to verify your CLAUDE.md is listed and loaded. If not listed, Claude can't see it. (2) Check the file is in a location that gets loaded for your session (root directory or parent, or managed policy location). (3) Make instructions more specific: 'Use 2-space indentation' works better than 'Format code nicely'. (4) Look for conflicting instructions across multiple CLAUDE.md files—if two say different things, Claude may pick one arbitrarily. (5) If the instruction must run at a specific point (e.g., before every commit), use a hook instead—hooks execute as shell commands regardless of Claude's decision.
items.example: Run /memory → verify CLAUDE.md is listed. If missing, check: (1) Is it at ./CLAUDE.md or ./.claude/CLAUDE.md? (2) Are you in the right directory? (3) Does managed policy exclude it?
items.newcomerTip: CLAUDE.md shapes Claude's behavior but doesn't force it—use hooks for hard enforcement, CLAUDE.md for guidance.

items.name: Manage CLAUDE.md for Teams
items.summary: For organizations, deploy centrally managed CLAUDE.md via MDM or Group Policy to enforce company standards
items.details: Organizations can deploy a managed CLAUDE.md to enforce company-wide coding standards and compliance. Deploy to managed policy locations (macOS, Linux, Windows vary). Cannot be excluded by individual settings. Use managed settings file's `claudeMd` key to put content directly in managed-settings.json instead of a separate file. Managed CLAUDE.md serves different purposes than managed settings: use CLAUDE.md for behavioral guidance and code style, use settings for technical enforcement like tool permissions and environment variables.
items.example: Deploy via MDM: Create /Library/Application Support/ClaudeCode/CLAUDE.md (macOS) with company standards. All users read it automatically. Or set in managed-settings.json: "claudeMd": "Always run make lint before commit."
items.newcomerTip: If your company manages Claude Code, compliance rules come from the managed CLAUDE.md—you can't override it, and that's by design.

items.name: #-based Memory Shortcut
items.summary: In conversation, ask Claude to remember something with natural language, and it saves to auto memory
items.details: You don't need to edit files to add to auto memory. Just ask: 'Remember that the API tests require a local Redis instance' or 'Always use pnpm, not npm'. Claude saves these learnings to auto-memory MEMORY.md. To add to CLAUDE.md instead of auto memory, ask directly: 'Add this to CLAUDE.md' or edit via /memory manually.
items.example:
You: 'Remember that the build fails without NODE_ENV=production'
Claude saves to ~/claude/projects/<project>/memory/MEMORY.md automatically next time it does that check.
items.newcomerTip: Use natural language 'remember' requests for quick notes; use /memory to formally document things in CLAUDE.md.

items.name: InstructionsLoaded Hook
items.summary: Use the InstructionsLoaded hook to debug which CLAUDE.md and rules files are loaded and in what order
items.details: For debugging path-specific rules or lazy-loaded subdirectory files, the InstructionsLoaded hook logs exactly which instruction files loaded, when, and why. Useful for understanding why a rule didn't apply or to trace the order of multi-layer CLAUDE.md files.
items.example: In hooks.json, set a PreToolUse hook to log instruction loads, or run a session with --debug to see InstructionsLoaded events.
items.newcomerTip: If a rule disappeared or didn't load, enable InstructionsLoaded hook to see the exact load trace.

items.name: CLAUDE_CODE_ADDITIONAL_DIRECTORIES_CLAUDE_MD
items.summary: Environment variable to load CLAUDE.md files from additional directories specified via --add-dir
items.details: By default, CLAUDE.md files from --add-dir directories are not loaded. Set `CLAUDE_CODE_ADDITIONAL_DIRECTORIES_CLAUDE_MD=1` to also load memory files from those directories. Loads CLAUDE.md, .claude/CLAUDE.md, .claude/rules/*.md, and CLAUDE.local.md from the additional directory. CLAUDE.local.md is skipped if you exclude `local` from --setting-sources.
items.example: CLAUDE_CODE_ADDITIONAL_DIRECTORIES_CLAUDE_MD=1 claude --add-dir ../shared-config
items.newcomerTip: If you use --add-dir for shared config, set this env var to also load shared CLAUDE.md rules.

catalogs.overview: Claude Code plugins are self-contained directories that extend Claude Code with reusable functionality including skills, agents, hooks, MCP servers, and LSP servers. Plugins can be installed from marketplaces, distributed across teams, and managed through the /plugin command or CLI. They enable sharing custom workflows, integrations, and automations either personally, across teams, or with the community.

items.name: What is a plugin
items.summary: A self-contained directory that bundles Claude Code extensions including skills, agents, hooks, MCP/LSP servers, monitors, and themes
items.details: A plugin is the primary way to share Claude Code customizations beyond a single project. Unlike standalone configuration in .claude/, plugins are versioned, discoverable through marketplaces, can be installed at user/project/local scope, and support namespaced skills like /plugin-name:skill-name. Plugins can include: skills (model-invoked or user-invoked), subagents (specialized AI agents), hooks (event handlers for automation), MCP servers (external tool integrations), LSP servers (code intelligence), background monitors (log watchers), color themes, and settings defaults.
items.example: A plugin called 'code-formatter' would provide /code-formatter:format-file skill, hooks to auto-format on save, and MCP servers to integrate with external linting tools
items.newcomerTip: Start with standalone .claude/ skills for a single project, then convert to a plugin once you want to share it across projects or teams. Use /plugin marketplace add to discover plugins, then /plugin install to add them.

items.name: Plugin components: Skills
items.summary: Reusable workflows that Claude can invoke automatically based on context or users can trigger manually
items.details: Skills are the core component of a plugin. Each skill is a folder in the skills/ directory with a SKILL.md file containing YAML frontmatter (description, disable-model-invocation flag) and markdown instructions. Skills can include supporting files and scripts alongside SKILL.md. Claude auto-invokes skills based on task context if disable-model-invocation is false (default). Plugins namespace skills with /plugin-name:skill-name format. A single-skill plugin can place SKILL.md at the plugin root instead of creating skills/.
items.example: A plugin's skills/ directory might contain code-review/SKILL.md with metadata about reviewing code for bugs, and pdf-processor/SKILL.md with subdirectories for supporting scripts
items.newcomerTip: Include a clear description field so Claude understands when to auto-invoke your skill. Use disable-model-invocation: true only if you want manual invocation only.

items.name: Plugin components: Subagents
items.summary: Specialized AI agents that Claude can spawn for specific tasks, with custom system prompts and tool access
items.details: Agents are Markdown files in the agents/ directory with YAML frontmatter defining agent behavior: name, description, model, effort level, maxTurns, allowed/disallowed tools, and isolation mode. Plugin agents support frontmatter fields like skills (restrict to specific plugin skills), memory, background (context), and isolation: "worktree" for isolated file edits. Agents appear in /agents and Claude can invoke them automatically when appropriate. Plugin agents work alongside built-in Claude agents but security-sensitive fields (hooks, mcpServers, permissionMode) are not supported for plugin agents.
items.example: An agents/security-reviewer.md file defining an agent specialized in finding security vulnerabilities, with disallowedTools: ["Execute"] to prevent risky commands
items.newcomerTip: Plugin agents are similar to custom agents you'd create in .claude/agents/ but scoped to a plugin. Use them to package specialized expertise that Claude can use automatically or on demand.

items.name: Plugin components: Hooks
items.summary: Event handlers that respond to Claude Code lifecycle events (pre/post tool use, file changes, session start/end) automatically
items.details: Hooks are defined in hooks/hooks.json (or inline in plugin.json) and react to 23+ events like PostToolUse, UserPromptSubmit, SessionStart, SessionEnd, FileChanged, WorktreeCreate, etc. Each hook entry specifies an event, a matcher pattern (optional, for filtering tools/files), and an action (command, http POST, MCP tool call, prompt evaluation, or agentic verification). Hooks can use ${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT} to reference plugin files. Unlike user hooks, plugin hooks run at the same trust level and cannot be disabled by project workspace trust.
items.example: A PostToolUse hook with matcher: "Write|Edit" that runs a linting script after file writes: hooks: {PostToolUse: [{matcher: "Write|Edit", hooks: [{type: "command", command: "\"${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}\"/scripts/format.sh"}]}]}
items.newcomerTip: Event names are case-sensitive (PostToolUse not posttooluse). Use matcher patterns to trigger only on relevant tools. Test with --debug to verify hooks are firing.

items.name: Plugin components: MCP servers
items.summary: Model Context Protocol servers bundled in plugins to expose external tools and services as Claude Code capabilities
items.details: MCP server configurations in .mcp.json (or inline in plugin.json) define how to start server processes and expose their tools. Each entry specifies command, args, env vars, cwd, and startup options. Servers start automatically when the plugin is enabled and integrate seamlessly with Claude's toolkit. Unlike user MCP servers, plugin MCP servers don't require per-server approval for project-scope plugins. Use ${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT} for plugin paths and ${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_DATA} for persistent state across updates.
items.example: An .mcp.json entry: {"plugin-database": {"command": "${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/servers/db-server", "args": ["--config", "${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/config.json"]}}
items.newcomerTip: MCP servers let you package integrations with external APIs (databases, APIs, services). Always use ${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT} for paths since plugins are cached in ~/.claude/plugins/cache.

items.name: Plugin components: LSP servers
items.summary: Language Server Protocol servers that provide real-time code intelligence (diagnostics, go-to-definition, type information)
items.details: LSP configurations in .lsp.json map language identifiers to server binaries and config. Each entry specifies command, args, extensionToLanguage mapping, optional transport (stdio/socket), env, initializationOptions, settings, startup timeout, maxRestarts, and diagnostics flag. When installed, LSP plugins give Claude automatic diagnostics after every edit and code navigation abilities. The language server binary must be installed separately on the user's system (e.g., npm install -g typescript-language-server). If binary is missing, /plugin Errors tab shows 'Executable not found in $PATH'.
items.example: A TypeScript LSP entry: {"typescript": {"command": "typescript-language-server", "args": ["--stdio"], "extensionToLanguage": {".ts": "typescript", ".tsx": "typescriptreact"}}}
items.newcomerTip: Install prebuilt LSP plugins from the official marketplace (pyright-lsp, typescript-lsp, rust-analyzer-lsp, etc.) rather than creating custom ones. Create custom LSP plugins only for unsupported languages.
items.platformNotes: Language servers are cross-platform but binaries must be installed for your OS (macOS, Windows, Linux)

items.name: Plugin components: Background monitors
items.summary: Persistent processes that watch logs, status endpoints, or files and deliver updates to Claude as notifications
items.details: Monitors in monitors/monitors.json (or monitors.json at plugin root) are shell commands that run for the session lifetime, with every stdout line sent to Claude as a notification. Each entry specifies: name (unique identifier), command (shell command to run), description, and optional 'when' trigger (always, or on-skill-invoke:skill-name). Monitors support variable substitution: ${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}, ${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_DATA}, ${CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR}, ${user_config.*}, and shell ENV vars. Monitors are experimental; they require Claude Code v2.1.105+.
items.example: A monitor polling deployment status: {"name": "deploy-status", "command": "\"${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}\"/scripts/poll-deploy.sh", "description": "Deployment status changes", "when": "always"}
items.newcomerTip: Monitors let Claude react to events without being asked. Use for watching logs, polling APIs, or tracking build status. Disable/re-enable via /plugin or restart the session.

items.name: Plugin manifest: plugin.json
items.summary: YAML/JSON metadata file at .claude-plugin/plugin.json that describes the plugin and configures its components
items.details: The plugin.json manifest is optional but recommended for distribution. Required field: name (kebab-case identifier used for namespacing, e.g., 'code-formatter'). Optional metadata: displayName (human-readable, can have spaces), version (semantic version string for pinning; omit to use git commit SHA), description, author {name, email, url}, homepage, repository, license, keywords. Component paths: skills, commands, agents, hooks, mcpServers, lspServers, outputStyles, experimental.themes, experimental.monitors. Advanced: dependencies (other plugins this requires), userConfig (prompt user for settings), channels (message injection), defaultEnabled (install disabled by default).
items.example: {"name": "code-formatter", "displayName": "Code Formatter", "version": "2.1.0", "description": "Auto-format code on save", "author": {"name": "Dev Team"}, "skills": "./custom/skills/", "hooks": "./hooks.json"}
items.newcomerTip: Minimize required fields: just name and description are enough to start. Add other fields as your plugin grows. Use kebab-case for the name (my-plugin, not myPlugin or my_plugin).

items.name: Plugin directory structure
items.summary: Standard layout for organizing plugin files and directories
items.details: Standard plugin structure places .claude-plugin/plugin.json at the root for metadata only. Parallel directories at root level: skills/ (subdirectories with SKILL.md), commands/ (flat .md files, legacy), agents/ (subagent markdown files), hooks/ (hooks.json), .mcp.json (MCP config), .lsp.json (LSP config), output-styles/ (output style defs), themes/ (color theme files), monitors/ (monitors.json), bin/ (executables added to PATH), settings.json (default config), scripts/ (helper scripts), and documentation files. Do NOT put skills/, commands/, agents/, or hooks/ inside .claude-plugin/ - only plugin.json goes there.
items.example:
my-plugin/
  .claude-plugin/plugin.json
  skills/code-review/SKILL.md
  agents/reviewer.md
  hooks/hooks.json
  .mcp.json
  scripts/format.sh
  README.md
items.newcomerTip: Common mistake: putting skills/ inside .claude-plugin/. Skills must be at the plugin root level alongside .claude-plugin/.

items.name: /plugin command
items.summary: Interactive UI for discovering, installing, managing, and configuring plugins
items.details: /plugin opens a four-tab interface: Discover (browse available plugins from all marketplaces with details on context cost, last updated, components), Installed (view, enable, disable, uninstall plugins grouped by scope with 'Not used recently' filter), Marketplaces (add, remove, update marketplaces), Errors (view plugin loading errors). Tab through with Tab/Shift+Tab, filter by typing, press Enter to view details or install. Shows components the plugin will install before confirming. Installation scopes: user (personal across projects), project (shared via .claude/settings.json), local (gitignored, personal to repo).
items.example: /plugin → Tab to Discover → type 'formatter' → Enter on result → 'User scope' → confirms install with skill list
items.newcomerTip: Run /plugin without arguments to open the manager. Use /plugin list to see installed plugins from the command line. /plugin install name@marketplace-name to install directly without the UI.

items.name: /plugin install
items.summary: Install a plugin from a marketplace by name
items.details: /plugin install <name>@<marketplace> installs a single plugin. If no marketplace specified, Claude searches all added marketplaces. Default scope is user (personal). Use /plugin install <name>@<marketplace> --scope project to share with team via .claude/settings.json, or --scope local for gitignored local install. If marketplace is not found, run /plugin marketplace update <marketplace-name> to refresh, or /plugin marketplace add anthropics/claude-plugins-official if you haven't added the official marketplace.
items.example: /plugin install formatter@claude-plugins-official --scope project
items.newcomerTip: Install to project scope (--scope project) if you want to share the plugin with your entire team via version control. Otherwise defaults to user scope which persists across projects.

items.name: /plugin marketplace add
items.summary: Register a plugin marketplace catalog so plugins from it become installable
items.details: /plugin marketplace add <source> registers the marketplace without installing any plugins. Source can be: GitHub owner/repo (anthropics/claude-code), git URL (https://gitlab.com/team/plugins.git), local path (./my-marketplace), or direct URL to marketplace.json (https://example.com/marketplace.json). Optional: append @ref to GitHub shorthand or #ref to git URLs to pin a branch/tag. After adding, plugins from that marketplace appear in /plugin Discover tab and are installable with /plugin install <name>@<marketplace-name>.
items.example: /plugin marketplace add anthropics/claude-plugins-community
items.newcomerTip: The official marketplace (claude-plugins-official) is pre-added. Add community plugins with /plugin marketplace add anthropics/claude-plugins-community to access third-party submissions.

items.name: Plugin marketplace: marketplace.json
items.summary: Catalog file at .claude-plugin/marketplace.json that lists available plugins and their sources
items.details: marketplace.json defines a catalog of plugins to distribute. Required fields: name (kebab-case marketplace ID), owner {name, email optional}, plugins array. Each plugin entry needs: name (kebab-case), source (string or object pointing to plugin location), description. Optional plugin fields: version, author, homepage, repository, license, keywords, category, tags, displayName, strict (true=plugin.json is authority, false=marketplace entry is entire definition), relevance (signals for admin-approved suggestions), defaultEnabled. Plugin sources: relative path (./plugins/my-plugin), GitHub (source: github, repo: owner/repo), git URL (source: url, url: https://...), git-subdir (sparse clone from monorepo subdirectory), npm (source: npm, package: @org/pkg). Can pin with ref (branch/tag) or sha (exact commit).
items.example: {"name": "my-plugins", "owner": {"name": "DevTools Team"}, "plugins": [{"name": "formatter", "source": "./plugins/formatter", "description": "Code formatting", "version": "1.0.0"}]}
items.newcomerTip: Create one marketplace.json per repository to distribute multiple plugins. Each plugin entry can point to the same repo (relative path) or different repos (GitHub/URL sources). Validate with claude plugin validate .

items.name: Create a plugin: Quick start
items.summary: Step-by-step walkthrough to create your first plugin with a skill
items.details: 1) mkdir my-first-plugin && mkdir my-first-plugin/.claude-plugin 2) Create plugin.json with {name, description, version, author} 3) mkdir -p my-first-plugin/skills/hello 4) Create SKILL.md with YAML frontmatter (description, disable-model-invocation) and instructions 5) Test with claude --plugin-dir ./my-first-plugin 6) Try /my-first-plugin:hello 7) For dynamic input use $ARGUMENTS placeholder in SKILL.md. When ready to share, create marketplace.json, host on GitHub, users add with /plugin marketplace add owner/repo.
items.example: See /plugin quickstart: create plugin folder, add plugin.json, create skills/hello/SKILL.md with frontmatter and greeting text, test with --plugin-dir
items.newcomerTip: Don't put skills/ inside .claude-plugin/. Only plugin.json goes there. Skills go at the plugin root. Use /reload-plugins during development to pick up SKILL.md changes immediately.

items.name: Convert standalone .claude/ to plugin
items.summary: Migrate existing skills, commands, agents, hooks from .claude/ directory into a shareable plugin
items.details: 1) Create plugin directory with .claude-plugin/plugin.json (name, description, version) 2) Copy .claude/commands to plugin/commands/ (or convert to plugin/skills/) 3) Copy .claude/agents to plugin/agents/ 4) Create plugin/hooks/hooks.json with hooks from settings.json 5) Copy .claude/skills to plugin/skills/ if present 6) Test with --plugin-dir flag 7) Create marketplace.json to distribute 8) Remove originals from .claude/ to avoid duplicates. Plugin skills are namespaced (/plugin-name:skill-name). Project and user .claude/agents override plugin agents with same name.
items.example: From .claude/commands/deploy.md → move to my-plugin/commands/deploy.md or refactor to my-plugin/skills/deploy/SKILL.md
items.newcomerTip: Migration is one-way essentially - once converted, the plugin provides the functionality via marketplace. Keep both until you're sure everything works.

items.name: Develop plugin locally: --plugin-dir
items.summary: Load a plugin from a directory during a session for testing and development without installing it
items.details: --plugin-dir <path> loads a plugin from a local directory for that session only. Useful for development and testing before publishing. Can be repeated to load multiple plugins: claude --plugin-dir ./plugin-one --plugin-dir ./plugin-two. When a --plugin-dir plugin has same name as installed marketplace plugin, the local version takes precedence. As you edit plugin files, run /reload-plugins to pick up changes (SKILL.md changes take effect immediately; other components need reload). Accepts .zip archives of plugin directories for Claude Code v2.1.128+.
items.example: claude --plugin-dir ./my-plugin → test skills with /my-plugin:skill-name → edit SKILL.md → /reload-plugins to see changes
items.newcomerTip: Use --plugin-dir extensively during development. Run /reload-plugins frequently to test changes without restarting. When satisfied, move to --plugin-url for remote testing or publish to marketplace.

items.name: Test plugin locally: --plugin-url
items.summary: Load a plugin from a remote .zip URL for testing without installation
items.details: --plugin-url <url> fetches a .zip archive from a URL and loads the plugin for that session only. Useful for testing packaged plugins from CI artifacts or CDN before distributing through marketplaces. Can be repeated for multiple URLs: claude --plugin-url url1 url2 url3 or pass space-separated quoted: claude --plugin-url "url1 url2". If fetch fails or zip is invalid, Claude Code reports error and starts without plugin. Same trust considerations apply as marketplace plugins: only use URLs you control or trust.
items.example: claude --plugin-url https://releases.example.com/build-123/my-plugin.zip
items.newcomerTip: Test packaged plugins from CI before releasing to marketplace. If the URL is unreachable, Claude Code will start without the plugin but show a clear error.

items.name: Reload plugins in session: /reload-plugins
items.summary: Apply plugin changes (install, enable, disable, update) without restarting Claude Code
items.details: /reload-plugins picks up changes to plugins without restarting. Needed after: /plugin install, /plugin enable, /plugin disable, /plugin update, or manual edits to .claude-plugin/plugin.json. SKILL.md changes take effect immediately without reload. Other components (agents, hooks, MCP servers, LSP servers, monitors) require reload. Reloading has token cost: newly loaded components announce themselves in appended chat content while existing history uses prompt cache. If plugin provides MCP servers without tool search deferral, reload invalidates cache and next request re-reads entire conversation (v2.1.163+).
items.example: /plugin install formatter@my-marketplace → /reload-plugins → formatter skills now available
items.newcomerTip: SKILL.md edits take effect immediately; everything else needs /reload-plugins. Use this frequently during plugin development.

items.name: Validate plugin: claude plugin validate
items.summary: Check plugin syntax, schema, and component definitions for errors before publishing
items.details: claude plugin validate <path> checks plugin.json JSON syntax, schema compliance, marketplace.json structure, skill/agent/command YAML frontmatter, and hooks/hooks.json validity. Reports errors (blocking) and warnings (non-blocking, like unrecognized fields). Use from marketplace root to validate marketplace.json only; use from plugin directory to validate plugin.json and all components. Pass --strict to treat warnings as errors (useful for CI). Checks: duplicate plugin names, source path traversal (..), version mismatches between manifest and marketplace entry, YAML parse failures, malformed JSON.
items.example: claude plugin validate ./my-plugin → checks plugin.json and all components\nclaude plugin validate . --strict → strict mode for CI/CD
items.newcomerTip: Always validate before submitting to community marketplace or distributing. --strict mode catches typos in field names before publishing.

items.name: List installed plugins: /plugin list
items.summary: Display all installed plugins with version, source, and enable status
items.details: /plugin list shows installed plugins in tabular format: name, version, marketplace source, scope, and enabled/disabled state. Use /plugin ls as shorthand. Interactive form accepts --enabled or --disabled to filter. CLI form: claude plugin list [--json] [--available] shows JSON output with optional --available flag to include all available plugins from marketplaces (requires --json). Interactive /plugin interface shows unused plugins in a collapsed group (v2.1.187+) - plugins not invoked in 2+ weeks across 10+ sessions (marketplace plugins only).
items.example: /plugin list → shows: formatter 2.1.0 @my-marketplace project enabled\nclaude plugin list --json → JSON with full details
items.newcomerTip: Use /plugin list to audit what's installed. The Installed tab shows unused plugins you can disable to reduce context cost.

items.name: Plugin uninstall: /plugin uninstall
items.summary: Remove a plugin completely or from a specific scope
items.details: /plugin uninstall <plugin> removes plugin from specified scope (default user). Removes from all scopes if --scope not given. When removing from last remaining scope, also deletes the plugin's ${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_DATA} directory (persistent state) by default. Use --keep-data to preserve it (e.g., for reinstalling after testing). Use --prune to also remove auto-installed dependencies no longer needed. Aliases: remove, rm. CLI: claude plugin uninstall <plugin> [--scope scope] [--keep-data] [--prune] [-y]. Note: uninstalling removes the plugin definition from settings.json but may leave cache files for 7 days.
items.example: /plugin uninstall formatter@my-marketplace --keep-data
items.newcomerTip: Use --keep-data if you might reinstall the plugin. It preserves node_modules, virtual environments, and caches. Use --prune to clean up dependencies that were auto-installed with this plugin.

items.name: Plugin disable/enable
items.summary: Temporarily turn off a plugin without uninstalling, or re-enable it
items.details: /plugin disable <plugin> turns off a plugin without removing it (persists until re-enabled). Fails if another enabled plugin depends on it; shows chained command to disable dependents first. /plugin enable <plugin> turns it back on. CLI: claude plugin disable <plugin> [--scope scope], claude plugin enable <plugin> [--scope scope]. Disabled plugins don't load their components and add zero token cost. Useful for reducing context without losing the plugin.
items.example: /plugin disable expensive-plugin → reduces token cost\n/plugin enable expensive-plugin → re-enable
items.newcomerTip: Disable costly plugins (LSP servers, MCP servers) when not needed. Use /plugin details to see projected token cost of each plugin.

items.name: Plugin init: Create skills-directory plugin
items.summary: Scaffold a plugin at ~/.claude/skills/<name>/ that loads automatically in next session
items.details: claude plugin init <name> creates a plugin skeleton in ~/.claude/skills/<name>/ with .claude-plugin/plugin.json manifest and starter SKILL.md. Loads automatically as <name>@skills-dir in next session (no marketplace, no install). Use --with skills|agents|hooks|mcp|lsp|output-style|channel to scaffold those component directories. Use -f/--force to overwrite existing. Personal-scope plugins load without trust gate. Project-scope plugins (in repo .claude/skills/) need workspace trust gate to load.
items.example: claude plugin init my-helper --with skills hooks → creates ~/.claude/skills/my-helper/ with skills/ and hooks/ folders
items.newcomerTip: Use plugin init for quick local plugins you don't intend to share. For shareable plugins, use --plugin-dir with a standalone directory instead.

items.name: Plugin settings.json defaults
items.summary: Default Claude Code configuration applied when a plugin is enabled
items.details: A plugin can ship settings.json at its root to apply default settings when enabled. Currently supported: 'agent' (activates one of the plugin's custom agents as the main thread), 'subagentStatusLine' (status line format for subagents). Settings from plugin-level settings.json override manifest-level settings. Unknown keys are silently ignored. Takes lower priority than user's own settings declared in their .claude/settings.json.
items.example: {"agent": "security-reviewer"} in settings.json activates the plugin's security-reviewer agent by default when enabled
items.newcomerTip: Use plugin settings.json to apply smart defaults (e.g., auto-activate a security agent) but don't force settings users might want to override.

items.name: Plugin environment variables
items.summary: Special variables available in plugin config for referencing paths and user settings
items.details: ${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}: absolute path to plugin's installation directory in ~/.claude/plugins/cache. Changes on updates. Use for scripts, binaries, config. ${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_DATA}: persistent directory for plugin state (node_modules, venvs, caches, generated files) at ~/.claude/plugins/data/{id}/, survives updates. ${CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR}: project root where Claude Code was launched. All three substituted in skill content, agent content, hook commands, monitor commands, MCP/LSP configs, and exported as env vars to subprocess. Also available: ${user_config.*} for plugin user config values, and any ${ENV_VAR} from environment.
items.example: Hook: {"command": "\"${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}\"/scripts/format.sh"} → ${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_DATA}/node_modules for persisted dependencies → ${CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR}/scripts/local-tool.sh for project scripts
items.newcomerTip: Always use ${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT} for plugin paths since plugins are cached and their path changes on updates. Use quotes around path variables in shell commands to handle spaces.

items.name: Plugin scopes: user vs project vs local
items.summary: Where plugins are installed determines who can use them and how they're shared
items.details: User scope (~/.claude/settings.json): plugin available personally across all projects, persists across machines via sync. Project scope (.claude/settings.json): plugin shared with all collaborators on repository, checked into version control, available to whole team. Local scope (.claude/settings.local.json): gitignored, personal to you in this repo only. Managed scope: organization-wide plugins set by admins (read-only, update only). Use /plugin install <name>@<marketplace> --scope <scope> to choose at install time, or use /plugin interface to select before confirming.
items.example: --scope project installs formatter plugin for entire team via .claude/settings.json checkin; --scope local keeps it personal/gitignored
items.newcomerTip: Install plugins at project scope if the whole team should use them. Use user scope for personal tools. Local scope for experimental/testing.

items.name: Marketplace sources: relative paths vs GitHub vs npm
items.summary: Different ways to specify where plugins are located in a marketplace catalog
items.details: Relative path ("source": "./plugins/my-plugin"): plugin in same repository as marketplace.json, resolved relative to marketplace root. GitHub ("source": {"source": "github", "repo": "owner/repo"}): plugin from GitHub repo. Git URL ("source": {"source": "url", "url": "https://gitlab.com/team/plugin.git"}): plugin from any git host. Git-subdir ("source": {"source": "git-subdir", "url": "...", "path": "tools/plugin"}): sparse clone subdirectory from monorepo. npm ("source": {"source": "npm", "package": "@org/plugin"}): installed via npm. All git sources support optional ref (branch/tag) and sha (exact commit) pins. npm supports version ranges.
items.example: Relative in same repo: {"source": "./plugins/formatter"}\nGitHub: {"source": "github", "repo": "acme/tools"}\nnpm: {"source": "npm", "package": "@company/plugin", "version": "^2.0.0"}
items.newcomerTip: Use relative paths for monorepo plugins, GitHub for distributed plugins, npm for npm-published plugins. Relative paths only work when marketplace is added via git, not direct URL.

items.name: Submit plugin to community marketplace
items.summary: Process for getting your plugin reviewed and listed in the public anthropics/claude-plugins-community catalog
items.details: 1) Validate with claude plugin validate --strict 2) Use submission form: claude.ai/admin-settings/directory/submissions/plugins/new (requires Team/Enterprise org) or platform.claude.com/plugins/submit (any user) 3) Passes automated validation and safety screening 4) Approved plugins pinned to specific commit SHA in public catalog 5) CI bumps pin automatically on new commits 6) Syncs nightly, so delay between approval and appearance 7) Check anthropics/claude-plugins-community main branch .claude-plugin/marketplace.json for live list 8) No application process for official marketplace (claude-plugins-official) - Anthropic curates separately.
items.example: Run claude plugin validate --strict locally, then use form at platform.claude.com/plugins/submit
items.newcomerTip: Validate strictly before submitting. Make sure your plugin.json has all required fields and your components load correctly with --plugin-dir.

items.name: Plugin version management: explicit vs git SHA
items.summary: How to control when users receive plugin updates
items.details: Two strategies: 1) Explicit version: set "version": "2.1.0" in plugin.json. Users get updates only when you bump this field. Pushing commits without bumping does nothing. Recommended for published plugins with stable release cycles. 2) Git SHA version: omit version field. Every new commit is treated as new version. Users get updates on every commit. Recommended for internal/team plugins under active development. Resolution order: plugin.json version > marketplace entry version > git commit SHA > "unknown" (npm/non-git). Warning: don't set version in both places (plugin.json wins, silently hiding marketplace version). If using explicit versions, follow semantic versioning (MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH).
items.example: Development plugin: omit version, every commit is new → Stable plugin: bump version: "2.1.0" field in plugin.json for each release
items.newcomerTip: Omit version for active development (git SHA per commit). Set version for published plugins (requires manual bump per release). Don't maintain version in both places.

items.name: Plugin caching and file resolution
items.summary: How Claude Code caches plugins and limitations on file references
items.details: Marketplace plugins are copied to ~/.claude/plugins/cache/<marketplace>/<plugin>/<version>/ rather than used in-place. Each version is separate directory. Old versions orphaned after 7 days. Cannot reference files outside plugin directory (../shared-utils won't work - not copied). Within-plugin symlinks are preserved. Symlinks to siblings in same marketplace are dereferenced (content copied). Symlinks outside marketplace are skipped for security. --plugin-dir and --plugin-url plugins are used in-place, not cached, but same path restrictions apply.
items.example: Plugin tries to load ../shared-lib.js → fails after install because that file not copied to cache. Solution: use symlink or reorganize to shared-plugin approach.
items.newcomerTip: Don't use relative paths outside your plugin directory. If you need shared files, either: (1) put them in same plugin, (2) use symlinks within same marketplace, or (3) create a meta-plugin that symlinks to other plugins' skills.

items.name: Plugin marketplace: strict mode
items.summary: Control whether plugin.json or marketplace entry is the authority for component definitions
items.details: The "strict" field in marketplace plugin entries controls component source of truth. strict: true (default): plugin.json is authority, marketplace can supplement with additional components, both merged. strict: false: marketplace entry is entire definition, if plugin.json also declares components, conflict causes load failure. Use strict: true when plugin manages its own components (normal case). Use strict: false when marketplace operator wants full control over what's exposed, useful for restructuring or curating a plugin's components differently than plugin author intended.
items.example: strict: true (normal) - plugin.json declares skills, marketplace adds extra hooks\nstrict: false - marketplace entry completely replaces plugin.json component definitions
items.newcomerTip: Leave strict as default (true) unless you have a specific reason to control components from the marketplace entry.

items.name: Plugin marketplace distribution
items.summary: How to host and share your marketplace catalog publicly or privately
items.details: Host on GitHub (recommended): create repo with .claude-plugin/marketplace.json, users add with /plugin marketplace add owner/repo. Other git hosts: users add with full URL like /plugin marketplace add https://gitlab.com/team/plugins.git. Local testing: /plugin marketplace add ./path/to/marketplace. Remote URL: /plugin marketplace add https://example.com/marketplace.json (relative plugin paths won't work this way). Private repos: use git credentials (gh auth login, keychain) for manual install/updates. For auto-updates, set GITHUB_TOKEN, GITLAB_TOKEN, or BITBUCKET_TOKEN environment variables. Admin can require specific marketplaces via strictKnownMarketplaces in managed settings.
items.example: Create acme-corp/plugins repo with .claude-plugin/marketplace.json → users: /plugin marketplace add acme-corp/plugins
items.newcomerTip: GitHub is easiest. Create a public or private repo, add marketplace.json at root, and share the owner/repo shorthand with your team.

items.name: Plugin user configuration: userConfig field
items.summary: Prompt users for settings when they enable a plugin instead of requiring hand-editing settings.json
items.details: "userConfig" in plugin.json defines values prompted at enable time. Each config key has: type (string, number, boolean, directory, file), title, description, optional sensitive flag (masks input, stores in keychain), required, default, and for string type: multiple (array of strings), for number: min/max. Non-sensitive values stored in settings.json under pluginConfigs[id].options. Sensitive values in system keychain or ~/.claude/.credentials.json (2KB limit). Available for substitution as ${user_config.KEY} in MCP/LSP configs, hook commands, monitor commands, and skill/agent content (non-sensitive only). Exported as CLAUDE_PLUGIN_OPTION_<KEY> env vars to subprocesses.
items.example: {"userConfig": {"api_endpoint": {"type": "string", "title": "API endpoint", "description": "Your team's API", "required": true}, "api_token": {"type": "string", "title": "Token", "sensitive": true}}}
items.newcomerTip: Use userConfig to avoid requiring users to edit JSON. Sensitive flag stores passwords securely instead of in settings.json. Users see a dialog when they enable the plugin.

items.name: Plugin dependencies
items.summary: Declare that a plugin requires other plugins to function
items.details: "dependencies" array in plugin.json lists required plugins. Each entry: {"name": "other-plugin"} or {"name": "other-plugin", "version": "~2.0.0"} for semver constraints. When installing a plugin with dependencies, Claude Code auto-installs unmet dependencies at same scope. When enabling a plugin with dependencies, Claude Code enables them transitively. Version constraints use semver ranges (^2.0.0, ~2.1.0, etc.). If multiple plugins constrain same dependency, all constraints must be satisfiable. Install fails if versions conflict. Plugins tagged with version git tags: owner/plugin-name--v1.0.0 for dependency resolution.
items.example: {"dependencies": [{"name": "base-tools", "version": "^2.0.0"}]} → auto-installs base-tools when this plugin installs
items.newcomerTip: Use dependencies when your plugin builds on another. Declare compatible version ranges to avoid breaking when dependencies update.

items.name: Managed plugin restrictions: strictKnownMarketplaces
items.summary: Admin setting to restrict which marketplaces users can add to their organization
items.details: Admins set strictKnownMarketplaces in managed-settings.json to control plugin sources: undefined (default) = no restrictions, empty array [] = complete lockdown (no new marketplaces), list of sources = whitelist only those sources. Supports exact matching for GitHub/URL sources, regex patterns for hostPattern (internal git servers) and pathPattern (local paths). Examples: {"source": "hostPattern", "hostPattern": "^github\.example\.com$"} for internal servers, {"source": "pathPattern", "pathPattern": "^/opt/approved/"} for local paths. Combined with extraKnownMarketplaces to auto-register approved marketplaces and optionally set initial plugins.
items.example: Restrict to one internal marketplace: {"strictKnownMarketplaces": [{"source": "github", "repo": "acme-corp/approved-plugins"}]}
items.newcomerTip: Admins use this for compliance/security. Users can't add other marketplaces. Pair with extraKnownMarketplaces to auto-setup approved marketplaces.

catalogs.overview: Claude Code offers extensive customization for appearance, behavior, and workflow through settings, keybindings, output styles, and terminal configuration. Control everything from the visual theme and status line to model selection, fast mode, and rendering behavior. Most changes are instant with live reload, and preferences persist across sessions through settings files.

items.name: Ctrl+L
items.summary: Redraw terminal or clear conversation (fullscreen mode: press twice within 2 seconds)
items.example: Screen garbled? Ctrl+L. In fullscreen mode, press Ctrl+L twice within 2 seconds to run /clear and start fresh.
items.details: Single press: full screen redraw, input and history preserved. Fullscreen mode: first press shows hint, second press runs /clear. Also available as Cmd+K on macOS in fullscreen mode.

items.name: Ctrl+O
items.summary: Toggle verbose transcript viewer (fullscreen: shows less-style search and navigation)
items.example: Press Ctrl+O to expand all tool calls and show raw output; press again to collapse.
items.details: Toggles between condensed view (one-line tool summaries) and verbose transcript (full output). In fullscreen mode, opens a full-featured viewer with `less`-style navigation (/search, n/N for next/prev, q/Esc to exit, [ to write to scrollback, v to open in $EDITOR).

items.name: Shift+Tab
items.summary: Cycle through permission modes: default → plan → acceptEdits → auto (if enabled)
items.example: Start in default mode (asks before edits), press Shift+Tab to switch to plan mode (view plan, approve edits), press again for acceptEdits mode (auto-applies edits).
items.details: Quickly switch between permission modes without `/config`. Cycles through available modes in order. The current mode is shown in a colored border around the input box (prompt, plan, or autoAccept colors). Can be rebound via chat:cycleMode.
items.newcomerTip: Use default mode for safety (asks before each edit), plan mode for complex changes (review first), and acceptEdits mode for rapid prototyping (auto-apply).

items.name: /theme
items.summary: Select or customize visual themes (built-in presets or create custom themes with color overrides)
items.example:
/theme
# Then select 'dark', 'light', 'auto', or 'New custom theme...'
items.details: Opens a theme picker showing built-in presets (dark, light, auto-detect, daltonized, ANSI variants) and any custom themes you've created. Select 'New custom theme...' to create a custom theme interactively with color token overrides. Custom themes are stored as JSON in ~/.claude/themes/ and reload automatically when edited. The `auto` option detects your terminal's light/dark background and follows OS appearance changes.
items.newcomerTip: Start with 'auto' to match your system's appearance; create a custom theme only if you need specific colors that the presets don't provide.

items.name: /terminal-setup
items.summary: Auto-configure your terminal: enables Shift+Enter, fixes GPU acceleration in VS Code, configures mouse options
items.example: /terminal-setup (detects terminal, runs once, modifies config files or IDE settings)
items.details: One-time setup that configures: Shift+Enter for newlines (VS Code, Cursor, Alacritty, Zed only), terminal GPU acceleration toggle, mouse wheel sensitivity. Detects your terminal and applies appropriate changes. Some terminals (iTerm2, Ghostty, Kitty) work without setup. For tmux/screen, add configuration lines manually. On macOS, can also enable Option as Meta key.
items.newcomerTip: Run once per terminal type. Safe to re-run; won't duplicate existing settings. Shows messages if binding already exists.

items.name: /keybindings
items.summary: Create or open the keybindings configuration file to customize keyboard shortcuts
items.example: /keybindings
items.details: Opens ~/.claude/keybindings.json in your default editor. The file uses a bindings array with contexts and action maps. Changes are automatically detected and applied without restart. You can bind multiple modifiers (ctrl, shift, meta, cmd), use chords (sequences of keys), and unbind defaults by setting to null.
items.newcomerTip: Start by copying an example from the docs and changing just the keys you use most; most defaults work well out of the box.

items.name: /model
items.summary: Open model picker to switch models mid-session (press 's' to apply this session only, Enter to save as default)
items.example:
/model
/model opus
/model sonnet[1m]
items.details: Opens interactive picker showing model families, effort level (slider), and context window. Press Enter to switch and save as new default. Press 's' to apply to current session only (does not change settings). Using /model directly with a model name (e.g., /model sonnet) acts like Enter. Shows warning if model is retiring or mapped to newer version.
items.newcomerTip: Use /model frequently to experiment. Opus is the strongest but most expensive; Sonnet balances speed/cost; Haiku is fastest/cheapest for simple tasks.

items.name: /effort
items.summary: Adjust adaptive reasoning effort level: low, medium, high (default), xhigh, max (session-only), or ultracode (dynamic workflows)
items.example:
/effort high
/effort xhigh
/effort (opens interactive slider)
/effort auto (resets to model default)
items.details: Controls how much the model thinks on each step. low/medium/high/xhigh persist across sessions; max and ultracode apply to current session only. Fable 5 supports max; Opus 4.8/4.7 support max; Opus/Sonnet 4.6 do not. Model defaults vary: Fable/Opus 4.8/Sonnet 4.6 default high; Opus 4.7 defaults xhigh. ultracode plans dynamic workflows at xhigh reasoning per turn. ultrathink keyword in prompt does one-off deep reasoning without changing session effort.
items.newcomerTip: Use high for typical work, xhigh for complex problems, low for cost-sensitive/latency-sensitive tasks. Try max only if xhigh isn't enough.

items.name: /fast
items.summary: Toggle fast mode: 2.5x faster Opus at higher per-token cost (research preview)
items.example: /fast (toggles on/off, shows confirmation 'Fast mode ON' or 'Fast mode OFF' with ↯ icon)
items.details: Switches Opus to a high-speed configuration. Up to 2.5x faster at higher cost per token. Requires Anthropic API or subscription with usage credits. Available on Opus 4.8, 4.7, 4.6 only; not on Sonnet, Haiku, or third-party providers. Enabling auto-switches to Opus if on another model. Can also be set in settings as fastMode: true.
items.newcomerTip: Use fast mode for interactive debugging or rapid iteration where speed is critical. Turn off for long-running tasks or batch processing.

items.name: Color token customization
items.summary: Override individual colors in themes by setting token names to hex, RGB, ANSI, or named values
items.details: Claude Code supports 40+ color tokens organized by category: text accents (claude, text, inactive, subtle), status colors (success, error, warning, merged), input/mode colors (promptBorder, planMode, autoAccept), diff colors (diffAdded, diffRemoved, diffAddedWord), and fullscreen-mode colors. Tokens not overridden fall back to the base preset. Invalid colors are silently ignored.
items.newcomerTip: Use hex colors (#rrggbb) for exact control, or ansi256(n) for terminal colors. The interactive `/theme` editor shows live preview as you customize.

items.name: Keybinding contexts
items.summary: Define which parts of Claude Code a keybinding applies to: Chat, Autocomplete, Settings, Confirmation, Transcript, etc.
items.details: Contexts isolate bindings to specific UI sections so the same key can do different things in different modes. Available contexts: Global, Chat, Autocomplete, Settings, Confirmation, Tabs, Help, Transcript, HistorySearch, Task, ThemePicker, Footer, MessageSelector, DiffDialog, ModelPicker, Select, Plugin, Scroll, Doctor, and more.

items.name: Keybinding syntax
items.summary: Syntax rules for keystroke notation: modifiers, chords, special keys, uppercase letters
items.syntax: ctrl+k | shift+tab | cmd+p | meta+shift+c | ctrl+k ctrl+s | escape | enter | space
items.example:
ctrl+shift+k
ctrl+x ctrl+e
meta+p
shift+enter
K (for uppercase implies Shift)
items.details: Use +  to combine modifiers (ctrl, shift, alt/meta/opt, cmd/super/win). Space-separated sequences create chords. Uppercase letters imply Shift (K = shift+k). Special keys: escape/esc, enter/return, tab, space, up/down/left/right, backspace/delete. Reserved shortcuts (Ctrl+C, Ctrl+D) cannot be rebound. On macOS, cmd sends if the terminal supports Kitty protocol; otherwise use ctrl.
items.platformNotes: macOS uses Cmd; Windows/Linux use Ctrl for most bindings. Alt is Option on macOS, Alt elsewhere. Reserved keys may conflict with tmux (Ctrl+B) or screen (Ctrl+A).

items.name: chat:submit
items.summary: Submit a prompt message (default: Enter)
items.example: Rebind to Ctrl+J: { "context": "Chat", "bindings": { "ctrl+j": "chat:submit", "enter": null } }
items.details: Sends the current prompt to Claude. Default binding is Enter. Can be rebound to any key or chord. Related: chat:newline (Ctrl+J) inserts a newline without submitting.

items.name: chat:newline
items.summary: Insert a newline in the prompt without submitting (default: Ctrl+J)
items.example: Rebind to Shift+Enter: { "context": "Chat", "bindings": { "shift+enter": "chat:newline" } }
items.details: Adds a line break to multiline input. Works in any terminal. Shift+Enter also works natively in iTerm2, WezTerm, Ghostty, Kitty, and Windows Terminal without configuration; use /terminal-setup for VS Code, Cursor, or Alacritty.
items.platformNotes: Shift+Enter works natively on iTerm2, Terminal.app, WezTerm, Ghostty, Kitty, Warp, Windows Terminal. Requires /terminal-setup on VS Code, Cursor, Alacritty, Zed. gnome-terminal and JetBrains IDEs: use Ctrl+J or backslash+Enter instead.

items.name: chat:externalEditor
items.summary: Open current prompt in external editor ($EDITOR or system default)
items.example: Type a prompt, press Ctrl+G, edit in vim/nano, save and exit. If configured, Claude's previous response appears as comments above for context.
items.details: Launches your default text editor with the current prompt. When you save and exit, the edited text is loaded back into Claude Code. Can be configured in /config to prepend Claude's last response as comments for context.

items.name: Option+P (macOS) / Alt+P (Windows/Linux)
items.summary: Open model picker without clearing input, instantly switch models mid-session
items.example: Type a prompt, press Alt+P, select 'sonnet', the prompt stays and the model changes. Press Tab or Enter to apply for current session only, or press 's' to save as default.
items.details: Switches models without clearing your prompt or conversation history. The picker shows current effort level, model family, and context window. Select a model with arrow keys and press Enter to apply and save as default for future sessions, or 's' to apply to current session only. Use this to upgrade to a more capable model mid-conversation or downgrade for cost.
items.newcomerTip: Use Alt+P frequently to experiment with different models. If you like a model, pressing Enter saves it as your default.

items.name: Option+T (macOS) / Alt+T (Windows/Linux)
items.summary: Toggle extended thinking (Claude's reasoning output) on/off for current session
items.example: Press Alt+T to enable thinking, Claude shows reasoning before the response; press again to hide thinking.
items.details: Toggles whether Claude's internal reasoning is displayed. Thinking is shown as gray italic text and is collapsed by default; press Ctrl+O to expand. Thinking output is included in token counts and billed. Has no effect on Fable 5, which always uses thinking. Can also be configured in /config as alwaysThinkingEnabled.
