items.platformNotes: On macOS, requires Option as Meta in terminal (iTerm2: Settings → Profiles → Keys; Terminal.app: Settings → Profiles → Keyboard → 'Use Option as Meta Key'). As of v2.1.132, this shortcut works on macOS without the Option as Meta setup.

items.name: Option+O (macOS) / Alt+O (Windows/Linux)
items.summary: Toggle fast mode (2.5x faster Opus responses at higher cost per token)
items.example: Press Alt+O to enable fast mode (↯ icon shows in status). Press again to disable and revert to standard speed.
items.details: Switches Opus to a high-speed configuration, up to 2.5x faster at higher per-token cost. Fast mode requires Anthropic API or subscription with usage credits enabled. Not available on Sonnet, Haiku, Bedrock, Vertex AI, or Foundry. Enabling fast mode auto-switches to Opus if on a different model. Can also be toggled with /fast or set in settings as fastMode: true.
items.newcomerTip: Use fast mode for interactive debugging or rapid iteration; turn it off for long-running tasks where cost matters more than latency.

items.name: Ctrl+R
items.summary: Reverse search through command history across current session, project, or all projects
items.example: Press Ctrl+R, type 'test', press Ctrl+R again to cycle through matches, press Tab or Enter to accept.
items.details: Interactive history search supporting regex. Scope cycles between session, project, and all projects via Ctrl+S. Loads the 100 most recent unique prompts. Press Enter to execute, Tab/Esc to accept and edit, Ctrl+C to cancel. Backspace on empty search cancels without restoring input.

items.name: Ctrl+V (or Alt+V on Windows/WSL)
items.summary: Paste image from clipboard and insert [Image #N] chip at cursor
items.example: Screenshot to clipboard, Ctrl+V, reference the image in your prompt (e.g., 'Fix the error on [Image 1]').
items.details: Inserts an [Image #N] reference at the cursor so you can discuss the pasted image in your prompt. Works in all terminals. WSL users can use both Ctrl+V and Alt+V; use Alt+V if your terminal intercepts Ctrl+V.
items.platformNotes: Windows/WSL: use Alt+V if Ctrl+V conflicts with terminal. Mac: Cmd+V also works in iTerm2.

items.name: Ctrl+B (or Ctrl+X Ctrl+B)
items.summary: Background a running command or subagent task, continue working immediately
items.example: Claude runs `npm build`. Press Ctrl+B to move to background. Continue with new prompts while build runs. Retrieve output later with the Read tool.
items.details: Moves bash commands or agents to background, returning a task ID. Output is written to a file. Tmux users must press Ctrl+B twice due to prefix conflict. Can be rebound via task:background.

items.name: Esc
items.summary: Interrupt Claude (stop mid-response) or clear input (when not running)
items.example: Claude is responding. Press Esc to stop, keeping work done so far. Press Esc again to enter rewind menu to restore/summarize from checkpoints.
items.details: Single press while Claude is working: pauses the response. In normal mode: clears input and saves draft to history. Double Esc: opens rewind menu to restore or summarize from previous checkpoints.

items.name: /output-style
items.summary: Change Claude's response style: Default, Proactive, Explanatory, Learning, or custom
items.syntax: /config and select 'Output style' OR edit outputStyle in settings.json
items.example:
/config
Select 'Output style' > 'Explanatory'
OR { "outputStyle": "Explanatory" } in settings.json
items.details: Output styles modify the system prompt to change tone and response format without changing Claude's coding knowledge. Default: standard engineering assistant. Proactive: executes immediately, makes reasonable assumptions. Explanatory: adds insights between helping. Learning: collaborative, asks you to implement small pieces. Custom: create Markdown files in ~/.claude/output-styles or .claude/output-styles with frontmatter and instructions. Custom styles can keep or drop Claude's built-in coding instructions via keep-coding-instructions: true/false.
items.newcomerTip: Start with Explanatory to understand implementation choices. Create custom styles for specialized workflows like documentation writing or domain-specific analysis.

items.name: Built-in output styles
items.summary: Four shipped response styles that modify system prompt behavior
items.example: outputStyle: "Explanatory" or "Learning" or "Proactive"
items.details: Default: standard software engineering assistant, concise and action-focused. Proactive: executes immediately, makes reasonable assumptions without pausing. Explanatory: provides educational insights between code changes, helps you understand decisions. Learning: collaborative learn-by-doing, adds TODO(human) markers for you to implement pieces. Each style modifies the system prompt; changes take effect after /clear.
items.newcomerTip: Explanatory is great for learning codebases. Proactive is great for rapid iteration. Learning is great for team onboarding.

items.name: Custom output styles
items.summary: Create Markdown files with instructions to customize Claude's responses beyond built-in styles
items.example:
~/.claude/output-styles/diagrams-first.md:
---
name: Diagrams First
description: Lead every explanation with a Mermaid diagram
keep-coding-instructions: true
---

When explaining code or architecture, start with a Mermaid diagram showing structure.
items.details: Output style files are Markdown with YAML frontmatter. Name, description, and keep-coding-instructions are frontmatter keys. keep-coding-instructions: true keeps Claude's built-in coding behavior; leave it out or set to false to replace it entirely. Styles load from every .claude/output-styles/ between CWD and repo root; closest to CWD wins if multiple define same name. Plugins can ship output styles in output-styles/ directories.
items.newcomerTip: Start by copying a built-in style concept and adding specific instructions. Set keep-coding-instructions: true to modify tone while preserving engineering behavior.

items.name: /tui fullscreen
items.summary: Switch to fullscreen rendering mode (eliminates flicker, flat memory, mouse support, less-style search)
items.example: /tui fullscreen (to enable mid-session)
items.details: Switches to alternate-screen rendering (like vim), eliminating flicker in VS Code terminal, tmux, SSH. Keeps memory flat in long sessions. Adds mouse support: click to position cursor, click to expand tool output, select text, wheel scroll. Transcript mode (Ctrl+O) offers less-style search (/) and navigation. Can be enabled at startup with CLAUDE_CODE_NO_FLICKER=1 environment variable.
items.newcomerTip: Enable fullscreen if you see flickering or scroll position jumping. It's especially helpful in slow terminal connections or tmux.

items.name: Fullscreen rendering features
items.summary: Mouse support, less-style search, scroll-to-position, click-to-expand, flat memory usage
items.syntax: /tui fullscreen to enable; Ctrl+O for transcript mode; / for search; [ to write to scrollback; v to open in $EDITOR
items.example: Enable fullscreen. Click in conversation to expand collapsed tool output. Ctrl+O to enter transcript mode. Type / and search for 'error'. Press [ to write conversation to terminal scrollback so Cmd+F works.
items.details: Click-expand tool output, click-to-position cursor, select text with mouse (double-click word, triple-click line), wheel scroll (adjust speed with /scroll-speed or CLAUDE_CODE_SCROLL_SPEED env var), Cmd/Ctrl-click to open URLs/files. Transcript mode: / for regex search (n/N for next), less navigation (j/k, g/G, Space, b, Ctrl+B/F/D/U), [ to dump to scrollback, v to open in editor.
items.newcomerTip: Mouse wheel scrolling feels slow? Run /scroll-speed to adjust multiplier (try 3 for vim-like speed). Hold Shift while clicking to force native terminal selection.

items.name: /statusline
items.summary: Configure a custom status bar that runs any shell script to display context (model, git, costs, etc.)
items.example: /statusline (opens picker)
items.details: Status line is a customizable bar at the bottom showing whatever your shell script outputs. Receives session data as JSON on stdin (current model, context usage, costs, token counts, working directory, git status, etc.). Script output is live-updated. Useful for tracking context window usage, session costs, git branch, or custom metrics. Status line is separate from footer badges (which show PR status, usage meter, etc.).
items.newcomerTip: Start with a simple example: print the model name and remaining context percentage. Customize as you learn what metrics matter for your workflow.

items.name: Settings files
items.summary: JSON files controlling Claude Code behavior across five scopes: managed, CLI args, local, project, user
items.example:
~/.claude/settings.json:
{
  "model": "opus",
  "permissions": { "allow": ["Bash(npm *)"] },
  "env": { "CLAUDE_CODE_NO_FLICKER": "1" },
  "outputStyle": "Explanatory"
}
items.details: Precedence (highest first): managed → CLI args → local → project → user. Project settings commit to git (team-shared); user settings personal to one machine. Local settings gitignored for testing. Changes hot-reload except model (use /model to switch), outputStyle (use /clear), and managed settings (require restart). Use $schema for IDE autocomplete.
items.newcomerTip: Put personal preferences in user settings (~/.claude/settings.json). Put team preferences and shared rules in project settings (.claude/settings.json). Use local only for temporary testing.

items.name: model setting
items.summary: Default model for new sessions (aliases: opus, sonnet, haiku, fable, opusplan; or full model names)
items.details: Set the initial model when Claude Code starts. Alias options: default (recommended for your account), best (Fable 5 if available, else latest Opus), fable, opus, sonnet, haiku, opusplan (Opus in plan mode, Sonnet in execute mode), sonnet[1m], opus[1m] (with 1M token context). Can override at startup with --model flag or ANTHROPIC_MODEL env var. Use /model to switch mid-session.
items.newcomerTip: Start with opus for complex tasks, sonnet for everyday coding, haiku for simple tasks. Try fable for tough problems that need deep reasoning.

items.name: effortLevel setting
items.summary: Set persistent default effort level in settings: low, medium, high, xhigh (max and ultracode are session-only)
items.example: { "effortLevel": "xhigh" }
items.details: Saved to user, project, or local settings and applies to all sessions with that model unless overridden by /effort, --effort flag, or CLAUDE_CODE_EFFORT_LEVEL env var. When switching models, Claude Code applies the model's default effort (Fable/Opus 4.8 default high; Opus 4.7 defaults xhigh) even if you previously set a different level, so run /effort again if needed.

items.name: Keyboard Vim mode
items.summary: Enable vim-style editing (hjkl navigation, v for select, d/c/y operators, text objects) in prompt input
items.example:
/config, select Editor mode, choose vim
OR { "editorMode": "vim" } in settings.json
Then: press Esc to enter normal mode, hjkl to move, v to select, d/c/y to edit, etc.
items.details: Full NORMAL, INSERT, and VISUAL mode support with motions (hjkl, w/e/b, 0/$), operators (d/c/y with text objects), and text objects (iw/aw/i"/a" etc.). Escape switches INSERT to NORMAL; Enter in INSERT still submits (use o/O or Ctrl+J for newline in NORMAL). Vim motions are not rebindable; other actions (submit, newline, etc.) can be rebound in keybindings.json.
items.newcomerTip: Start with /config → Editor mode → vim. Press Esc to see normal-mode keys. Most vim muscle memory works; some advanced features (block select, regex search) are not supported.

items.name: Extended thinking
items.summary: Claude's internal reasoning before responding, displayed as gray italic text (toggle with Alt+T or settings)
items.syntax: Toggle: Alt+T or Option+T (macOS) | Setting: { "alwaysThinkingEnabled": true } | Env: MAX_THINKING_TOKENS
items.example: Press Alt+T to enable; Claude shows reasoning. Ctrl+O to toggle verbose mode and expand thinking. Press Alt+T again to hide.
items.details: On models with adaptive reasoning (Fable 5, Opus 4.7+, Sonnet 4.6), effort level controls how much thinking happens per step. Thinking is collapsed by default; press Ctrl+O for verbose mode. All thinking tokens are billed. Cannot be turned off on Fable 5. Set showThinkingSummaries: true to see full summaries instead of redacted summaries on Anthropic API.
items.newcomerTip: Enable thinking (Alt+T) when debugging complex problems or verifying solutions. Thinking tokens cost the same as output tokens, so disable for cost-sensitive work.

items.name: alwaysThinkingEnabled
items.summary: Global default for extended thinking: always on, always off, or per-session toggle
items.example: { "alwaysThinkingEnabled": true } in ~/.claude/settings.json to enable thinking in every session by default
items.details: Set in user or project settings. Session toggle (Alt+T) overrides this. Cannot be turned off on Fable 5.

items.name: preferredNotifChannel
items.summary: Choose how Claude notifies you when a task completes: desktop notification, terminal bell, or Notification hook
items.example: { "preferredNotifChannel": "terminal_bell" }
items.details: Options: 'desktop' (default on Ghostty, Kitty, iTerm2; requires notification permission), 'terminal_bell' (rings terminal bell). Or use Notification hooks in ~/.claude/settings.json to play a sound or run a command (e.g., afplay /System/Library/Sounds/Glass.aiff on macOS).
items.newcomerTip: On slow/remote terminals, terminal_bell is more reliable than desktop notifications. Terminal bells work over SSH.

items.name: tui setting
items.summary: Rendering mode: fullscreen (alternate screen, flicker-free, mouse support) or default (classic scrollback mode)
items.example:
{ "tui": "fullscreen" } in ~/.claude/settings.json to enable by default
OR /tui fullscreen to switch mid-session
items.details: fullscreen: uses alternate screen buffer like vim, no flicker, flat memory, mouse support (click to expand, select text, wheel scroll). default: traditional inline rendering, stays in scrollback, slower in long sessions. Can toggle mid-session with /tui or switch permanently in settings.
items.newcomerTip: Enable fullscreen if flickering or memory usage bothers you, especially in VS Code terminal or tmux. Disable if native scrollback searching is critical.

items.name: Effort level and fast mode tradeoff
items.summary: Effort controls reasoning depth; fast mode controls latency at higher cost. Can combine for max speed.
items.example: For fastest response: set /effort low (less thinking) and /fast (2.5x speed). For best quality: set /effort xhigh (deep reasoning).
items.details: Fast mode: same model quality, lower latency, higher cost. Lower effort: less thinking, faster response, potentially lower quality. You can combine both: low effort + fast mode for maximum speed on simple tasks. Standard mode + xhigh effort for deepest reasoning at normal speed.
items.newcomerTip: Use effort to control quality/cost tradeoff. Use fast mode only when you're waiting interactively and speed matters more than cost.

items.name: editorMode setting
items.summary: Choose prompt input editing style: normal (default readline) or vim (hjkl, v, d/c/y operators)
items.example: { "editorMode": "vim" }
items.details: normal: standard readline (Ctrl+A/E, Ctrl+K/U, Ctrl+W). vim: full normal/insert/visual modes. Changes take effect immediately.

items.name: outputStyle setting
items.summary: Response style: Default, Proactive, Explanatory, Learning, or name of custom output style
items.example:
{ "outputStyle": "Learning" }
{ "outputStyle": "my-custom-style" }
items.details: Changes system prompt. Takes effect after /clear or next session. Can be set in user, project, or local settings.

items.name: permissions setting
items.summary: Fine-grained allow/deny rules for file reads, bash commands, and tool access (auto/ask mode)
items.example: { "permissions": { "allow": ["Bash(npm test)", "Read(src/**)"], "deny": ["Bash(curl *)", "Read(.env)"] } }
items.details: Supports glob patterns and regex. defaultMode can be 'auto' (permit all), 'ask' (ask before each tool), 'plan' (show plan first), 'acceptEdits' (auto-apply edits). Can be overridden per-session with Shift+Tab.

items.name: env setting
items.summary: Set environment variables for Claude Code and all subprocesses
items.example: { "env": { "ANTHROPIC_MODEL": "opus", "DEBUG": "true" } }
items.details: Variables in user settings apply to all sessions; project/local override. User .claude/settings.json env is merged with system env and passed to bash, git, npm, and other tools.

items.name: CLAUDE_CODE_NO_FLICKER environment variable
items.summary: Enable fullscreen rendering at startup (flicker-free, flat memory, mouse support)
items.example:
CLAUDE_CODE_NO_FLICKER=1 claude
OR in ~/.claude/settings.json: { "env": { "CLAUDE_CODE_NO_FLICKER": "1" } }
items.details: Enables fullscreen rendering mode on startup. Equivalent to /tui fullscreen. Can be set in settings.json or as environment variable.
items.newcomerTip: Add to settings.json if fullscreen is your default, or export in shell if you toggle per-session with /tui.

items.name: CLAUDE_CODE_SCROLL_SPEED environment variable
items.summary: Multiply mouse wheel scroll distance in fullscreen mode (1-20, default auto-detected)
items.example:
CLAUDE_CODE_SCROLL_SPEED=3 claude
OR in settings.json: { "env": { "CLAUDE_CODE_SCROLL_SPEED": "3" } }
items.details: Adjust if scroll feels too fast or too slow. Value of 3 matches vim defaults. Fractional values like 0.5 slow down accelerated trackpads. Use /scroll-speed command for interactive adjustment with live preview.
items.platformNotes: Some terminals (Ghostty, iTerm2) already amplify wheel events; others (VS Code terminal) send exact wheel notches. CLAUDE_CODE_SCROLL_SPEED multiplies the base distance.

items.name: ANTHROPIC_MODEL environment variable
items.summary: Set the model for current session (overrides settings, but not --model flag)
items.example:
ANTHROPIC_MODEL=fable claude
ANTHROPIC_MODEL=sonnet claude
items.details: Applies to single session only. Precedence: --model flag > ANTHROPIC_MODEL > settings file model. Use --model flag or ANTHROPIC_MODEL to compare models without changing persistent settings.

items.name: ANTHROPIC_DEFAULT_OPUS_MODEL, SONNET_MODEL, HAIKU_MODEL, FABLE_MODEL
items.summary: Pin model versions for your provider (Bedrock, Vertex AI, Foundry, Claude Platform on AWS)
items.example:
On Bedrock: export ANTHROPIC_DEFAULT_OPUS_MODEL='us.anthropic.claude-opus-4-8'
On Vertex AI: export ANTHROPIC_DEFAULT_OPUS_MODEL='claude-opus-4-8'
items.details: Use full model IDs or ARNs for your provider. Append [1m] for 1M context window: ANTHROPIC_DEFAULT_OPUS_MODEL='claude-opus-4-8[1m]'. Required on third-party providers to control which model versions users access.
items.platformNotes: Bedrock uses ARN format (arn:aws:bedrock:...). Vertex AI and Foundry use model IDs. Claude Platform on AWS uses full model names.

items.name: MAX_THINKING_TOKENS environment variable
items.summary: Control extended thinking budget: 0 to disable, or a token count limit (Opus 4.6/Sonnet 4.6 only)
items.example:
MAX_THINKING_TOKENS=0 claude (disables thinking)
MAX_THINKING_TOKENS=10000 claude (limits thinking to 10k tokens)
items.details: On Opus 4.6 and Sonnet 4.6 with fixed thinking budgets, controls how much Claude can think. On Opus 4.7+ and Fable 5 (adaptive reasoning), setting to 0 disables thinking output display but the model may still think. No effect when setting another value; effort level controls adaptive reasoning instead.
items.newcomerTip: On modern models (Opus 4.7+, Fable 5), use effort level to control reasoning. MAX_THINKING_TOKENS=0 can disable thinking display if you want to save on token costs.

items.name: DISABLE_PROMPT_CACHING, DISABLE_PROMPT_CACHING_HAIKU, _SONNET, _OPUS, _FABLE
items.summary: Disable prompt caching globally or per-model tier to reduce costs or debug
items.example:
DISABLE_PROMPT_CACHING=1 claude (disables caching entirely)
DISABLE_PROMPT_CACHING_SONNET=1 claude (disables cache for Sonnet only)
items.details: DISABLE_PROMPT_CACHING takes precedence over per-tier settings. Useful for testing cache behavior or avoiding cold-cache costs when switching models. Normally caching should remain enabled for cost savings.

items.name: /statusline configuration
items.summary: Custom status bar script showing model, costs, context usage, git status, or any metrics
items.details: Executable script at ~/.claude/statusline receives session JSON on stdin with fields: model, contextUsedPercent, totalTokens, totalCost, elapsedSeconds, workingDir, gitBranch, gitStatus. Output is displayed on status line and updates live. Can be Bash, Python, or any executable.
items.newcomerTip: Start simple: print just the model name and remaining context. Add git branch and costs as needed.

catalogs.domain: Claude Code: Sessions, Context, and Workflow Automation
catalogs.overview: Claude Code organizes work into persistent sessions that resume across devices, supports context management to keep conversations efficient, and provides multiple ways to automate recurring tasks—from session-scoped loops to cloud routines and IDE integrations. Understanding these features helps you maintain long-running projects, parallelize work across branches, and offload repetitive tasks.

items.name: /resume
items.summary: Switch to a different conversation from inside the current session
items.example:
/resume
/resume feature-auth
items.details: Opens the session picker from within a session. Searching and keyboard shortcuts work the same as `claude --resume`. If a name is provided, it looks for exact matches across the current worktree and repository.
items.newcomerTip: This keeps you in the CLI without needing to exit and restart.

items.name: /context
items.summary: Display what is consuming context window space
items.example: /context
items.details: Shows a breakdown of what's in your context: system prompt, auto memory, conversation history, file contents, MCP tool definitions (deferred by default), and more. Helps you identify which features are using the most tokens.
items.newcomerTip: Run /context when your prompts slow down—it often reveals that compaction or /clear will help.

items.name: /compact
items.summary: Replace conversation history with an AI-generated summary to free context space
items.example: /compact focus on code changes and test results
items.details: Compresses the conversation history into a summary while keeping CLAUDE.md, recent messages, and code intact. You can provide optional focus instructions to preserve certain topics. Similar to /rewind summarize, but targets the entire conversation. Claude automatically compacts when approaching context limits.
items.newcomerTip: Use /compact when early setup discussions are taking up space but you still need recent work intact.

items.name: /branch
items.summary: Branch the current conversation to try a different approach
items.example: /branch try-streaming-approach
items.details: Creates a copy of the conversation so far and switches you into it, leaving the original session unchanged. The command prints two session IDs: the new branch and the original. You can return to the original by passing its ID to /resume.
items.newcomerTip: Use /branch when you want to explore a different direction but keep your current progress safe.

items.name: /export
items.summary: Export the current session conversation as readable text
items.example:
/export
/export session-notes.txt
items.details: Copies the conversation to your clipboard or saves to a file. Messages and tool outputs are rendered as readable text, not raw JSON. Useful for sharing or archiving.
items.newcomerTip: Use /export to create a shareable record of your work for team review or documentation.

items.name: /rename
items.summary: Rename the current session
items.example: /rename auth-refactor-final
items.details: Changes the name of the active session. The name appears in the prompt bar and in the session picker. You can also rename from the picker with Ctrl+R.
items.newcomerTip: Rename descriptively before using /clear so you can find the session later with /resume <name>.

items.name: claude --continue
items.summary: Resume the most recent session in the current directory
items.example: cd ~/my-project && claude --continue
items.details: Re-opens your last conversation and appends new messages to the existing transcript. All context from the previous session is available.
items.newcomerTip: This is the fastest way to pick up where you left off. Your conversation history and learnings persist.

items.name: claude --resume
items.summary: Open an interactive session picker to resume any past conversation
items.example: claude --resume auth-refactor
items.details: Opens a searchable picker showing all sessions in the current project. Press up/down to navigate, / to search, Ctrl+A for all projects, Ctrl+W for all worktrees. Press Space to preview session content. If you supply a name, it attempts an exact match and resumes directly, or shows the picker with the name pre-filled for ambiguous matches.
items.newcomerTip: Use /resume inside an active session to switch conversations without exiting.

items.name: Session picker keyboard shortcuts
items.summary: Navigate and filter sessions with keyboard commands
items.syntax: ↑/↓ | Space | Ctrl+R | Ctrl+W | Ctrl+A | Ctrl+B | / | Esc
items.example: ↑↓ to browse, Space to preview, / to search, Ctrl+W to show all worktrees, Ctrl+A to show all projects
items.details: Up/Down navigate, Space previews, Ctrl+R renames the highlighted session, Ctrl+W widens to all worktrees of the current repo, Ctrl+A widens to every project on the machine, Ctrl+B filters by current git branch. / starts search mode. Esc exits.
items.newcomerTip: Sessions are grouped by root session when forked. Press → to expand a group. Paste a PR/MR URL in search to find the session that created it.

items.name: claude -n <name>
items.summary: Start a new session with a specific name
items.example: claude -n auth-refactor
items.details: Launches Claude Code with a descriptive session name that appears in the session picker and in `/resume <name>` lookups. Names make it easy to find and resume work on parallel tasks.
items.newcomerTip: Always name sessions when working on multiple parallel tasks. It saves time later.

items.name: claude --continue --fork-session
items.summary: Branch a session to try a different approach without losing the original
items.example: claude --continue --fork-session
items.details: Creates a copy of the current session with a new ID and switches you into it. The original session remains unchanged and available in the picker. This is useful when you want to explore an alternative direction without losing your current path. Permissions you approved ('allow for this session') do not carry over to the fork.
items.newcomerTip: Forking and resuming the original lets you safely experiment without commitment.

items.name: Session ID lookup
items.summary: Sessions are identified by unique IDs stored in ~/.claude/projects/<project>/
items.example: claude --resume abc12345def
items.details: Each session is saved to a .jsonl file under ~/.claude/projects/<project>/. Sessions created via 'claude -p' (headless) or the Agent SDK don't appear in the picker but can still be resumed by ID. ID lookup is scoped to the directory where the session was started, so you must run the command from that directory.
items.newcomerTip: If a session doesn't show in /resume, try `claude --resume <session-id>` with the exact ID from earlier output.

items.name: Esc Esc (rewind menu)
items.summary: Open the rewind menu to undo changes or summarize parts of the conversation
items.example: Press Esc twice when the prompt box is empty
items.details: Opens a menu showing every prompt in the session. Select a point and choose 'Restore code and conversation', 'Restore conversation', 'Restore code', 'Summarize from here', or 'Summarize up to here'. If the prompt input is not empty, Esc clears it instead (the text is saved to history).
items.newcomerTip: Rewind is your safety net—always use it before asking Claude to try something risky.

items.name: Checkpoint safety mechanism
items.summary: Every file edit is automatically saved and reversible within a session
items.syntax: File snapshots are taken before each edit
items.example: After Claude edits a file, press Esc Esc and select 'Restore code' to undo
items.details: Claude Code snapshots files before editing, so checkpoints let you rewind to any prior state. Checkpoints are local to the session and separate from git. They only cover direct file edits, not bash command modifications. Checkpoints persist across session resume with --continue or --resume. They're automatically cleaned up after 30 days (configurable via cleanupPeriodDays in settings).
items.newcomerTip: Checkpoints are separate from git—they're for quick session-level recovery, not permanent history.

items.name: /loop <interval> <prompt>
items.summary: Run a prompt repeatedly on a fixed interval
items.example: /loop 5m check if deployment finished
items.details: Schedules a prompt to run every N seconds/minutes/hours/days. Intervals are converted to cron expressions. Supported units: s, m, h, d. Non-standard intervals (like 7m) are rounded to the nearest clean cron step. The prompt fires between your turns, not mid-response. Times are in your local timezone. Tasks expire after 7 days and resume with --resume if unexpired.
items.newcomerTip: Use /loop for quick polling during a session. For durable automation, use Routines or Desktop tasks.

items.name: /loop <prompt>
items.summary: Run a prompt on a dynamically chosen interval
items.example: /loop check CI status and review comments
items.details: Omit the interval and Claude picks one dynamically between 1 minute and 1 hour based on what it observes. Short waits while builds are active, longer waits when idle. The chosen delay is printed at the end of each iteration.
items.newcomerTip: Dynamic loops are great for babysitting PRs or deployments—Claude adapts the frequency as things progress.

items.name: /loop (built-in maintenance)
items.summary: Run a built-in maintenance prompt: continue unfinished work, tend to PRs, run cleanup
items.example:
/loop
/loop 15m
items.details: A bare /loop runs the built-in prompt at a dynamically chosen interval (or fixed if you supply one). The prompt continues unfinished work, reviews PR comments, checks CI, and runs cleanup tasks. Does not start new initiatives. Override the default with .claude/loop.md or ~/.claude/loop.md.
items.newcomerTip: Bare /loop is a great way to babysit a release branch—it handles routine maintenance automatically.

items.name: loop.md customization
items.summary: Replace the built-in /loop prompt with custom instructions
items.example: Check the release/next PR. If CI is red, pull logs and fix. If comments arrived, address them.
items.details: Place a .claude/loop.md (project-level, takes precedence) or ~/.claude/loop.md (user-level) file to define a custom default prompt for bare /loop. Write it as you would type the prompt directly. Project-level takes precedence. Edits take effect on the next iteration. Ignored when you supply a prompt on the command line. Keep under 25,000 bytes.
items.newcomerTip: Use loop.md to codify your team's standard maintenance workflow for release branches or production branches.

items.name: Esc (stop /loop)
items.summary: Stop a /loop while it is waiting for the next iteration
items.example: Press Esc to cancel the pending wakeup
items.details: Clears the pending scheduled task so the loop does not fire again. Applies only to /loop-style tasks, not to tasks created with CronCreate or via natural language.
items.newcomerTip: Press Esc to stop a loop, but use /schedule to delete a cron task created another way.

items.name: One-time reminder (natural language)
items.summary: Schedule a single-fire task using plain language
items.syntax: Type a reminder naturally, e.g., 'remind me at 3pm to push the release'
items.example:
in 45 minutes, check integration tests
remind me at 3pm to deploy
items.details: Claude interprets your reminder and creates a one-shot cron task that fires once and then deletes itself. Times are in your local timezone.
items.newcomerTip: Use natural language—Claude is good at understanding casual time descriptions.

items.name: CronCreate / CronList / CronDelete tools
items.summary: Programmatically manage scheduled tasks with cron expressions
items.syntax: Ask Claude to create/list/delete tasks, or use /schedule
items.example:
what scheduled tasks do I have?
cancel the deploy check
schedule a task to run npm test every day at 9am
items.details: CronCreate schedules a new task with a 5-field cron expression. CronList shows all tasks with IDs. CronDelete removes a task by ID. A session can hold up to 50 tasks. Use the natural language approach for simplicity; use these tools directly if needed for automation.
items.newcomerTip: Ask Claude directly—'what tasks do I have?' or 'cancel the deploy check'—instead of using tool names.

items.name: Cron expression reference
items.summary: 5-field format for scheduling: minute hour day-of-month month day-of-week
items.example:
*/5 * * * * (every 5 min)
0 9 * * * (daily at 9am)
0 9 * * 1-5 (weekdays at 9am)
items.details: Supports wildcards (*), single values, steps (*/15), ranges (1-5), and lists (1,15,30). Day-of-week: 0/7=Sunday, 1=Monday...6=Saturday. When both day-of-month and day-of-week are set, a date matches if either field matches (vixie-cron semantics). No L, W, ?, or name aliases.
items.newcomerTip: Avoid :00 and :30 for one-shot tasks—they get jitter up to 90 seconds early. Use :03 or :23 for precise timing.

items.name: Routines (cloud scheduling)
items.summary: Run tasks on Anthropic-managed infrastructure even when your computer is off
items.syntax: Configured via /schedule in CLI or Routines page in Desktop app
items.example: Create a routine to run daily code reviews every morning at 9am
items.details: Routines run on cloud infrastructure and support schedule triggers, API calls, and GitHub event triggers. Unlike /loop, they don't require an open session. Minimum interval is 1 hour. More details at: https://code.claude.com/docs/en/routines.md
items.newcomerTip: Use Routines for important recurring work that should run even when you're offline.

items.name: Desktop scheduled tasks (local scheduling)
items.summary: Run tasks on your machine at a recurring interval
items.syntax: Click Routines in Desktop app, select Local, configure schedule
items.example: Schedule a daily task to review changes: 'review today's commits'
items.details: Local tasks run on your machine with full access to files and tools. Only fire when the app is open and the computer is awake. Minimum interval is 1 minute. Each task gets its own isolated worktree if enabled. Catch up for missed runs in the last 7 days (one catch-up max). More details at: https://code.claude.com/docs/en/desktop-scheduled-tasks.md
items.newcomerTip: Use Desktop tasks when you need access to local files. Use Routines for reliability when offline.

items.name: Session persistence across resume
items.summary: Sessions are saved to disk in ~/.claude/projects/<project>/<session-id>.jsonl
items.syntax: JSONL format, one JSON object per line
items.example: ~/.claude/projects/my-project/abc12345.jsonl
items.details: Each session is a continuous JSONL transcript. Messages, tool uses, and metadata are written as you work. Sessions expire after 30 days by default (configurable via cleanupPeriodDays). To suppress transcript writes, set CLAUDE_CODE_SKIP_PROMPT_HISTORY. To store elsewhere, set CLAUDE_CONFIG_DIR.
items.newcomerTip: Sessions are plaintext—you can review or audit them directly. Transcripts survive session exit.

items.name: /cost and /usage
items.summary: View token usage and estimated cost for the current session
items.example: /usage
items.details: Shows total cost, API duration, wall-clock duration, lines added/removed, and context usage breakdown. On Pro/Max/Team, shows usage attributed to skills, subagents, plugins, and MCP servers. The dollar figure is estimated locally and may differ from actual billing. For authoritative billing, check the Claude Console.
items.newcomerTip: Run /usage when you notice prompts are taking longer—high costs often mean context is bloated. Use /context to see what's consuming space.

items.name: /usage --day and /usage --week
items.summary: Switch between 24-hour and 7-day usage breakdown
items.example: Press 'd' for last 24 hours, 'w' for last 7 days
items.details: In the /usage dialog, press 'd' to switch to last-24-hours view or 'w' for last-7-days. Figures are approximate, computed from local session history on this machine, so multi-device or claude.ai usage is not included.
items.newcomerTip: Check weekly usage to spot trends—increasing costs often point to features (like subagents) that need optimization.

items.name: VS Code extension @-mentions
items.summary: Reference specific files or line ranges in VS Code with @file#start-end syntax
items.example: @auth.ts#12-30 (lines 12-30 of auth.ts)
items.details: Type @, start typing a filename, and Claude shows fuzzy-matched suggestions. Include a trailing slash for folders. Add line numbers with #. For large PDFs, use @file.pdf#1-10 (pages 1-10) or @file.pdf#3+ (page 3 onward). Press Alt+K / Option+K to insert a reference to your current selection.
items.newcomerTip: Use @-mentions to give Claude precise context without reading entire files. This saves tokens and time.

items.name: VS Code Alt+K / Option+K
items.summary: Insert @-mention reference to current file and selection
items.example: Select lines 5-10, press Option+K, Claude gets @file.ts#5-10
items.details: In VS Code, when your cursor is in a file, pressing this shortcut inserts the file path and selected line numbers into the prompt box. The selected text is automatically visible to Claude.
items.newcomerTip: Use this shortcut to quickly reference the exact code you're asking about.

items.name: VS Code Cmd/Ctrl+Esc (focus toggle)
items.summary: Toggle focus between code editor and Claude prompt box
items.example: Type some code, press Ctrl+Esc to jump to the prompt, ask Claude about it
items.details: When the editor is focused, your cursor is in code. When Claude is focused, you can type prompts. This shortcut toggles between them without using the mouse.
items.newcomerTip: Keep your hands on the keyboard—use this shortcut for fast navigation.

items.name: VS Code Cmd/Ctrl+N (new conversation)
items.summary: Start a new Claude conversation as an editor tab
items.example: Press Ctrl+N to open a new conversation tab
items.details: Requires Claude to be focused and the setting enableNewConversationShortcut set to true. Opens a fresh conversation tab alongside your current work.
items.newcomerTip: Use this to parallelize work without leaving VS Code.

items.name: VS Code Cmd/Ctrl+Shift+Esc (new tab)
items.summary: Open a new Claude conversation in a separate editor tab
items.example: Press Ctrl+Shift+Esc to open a new conversation as a tab
items.details: Equivalent to the 'Open in New Tab' command. Each tab maintains its own conversation history and context.
items.newcomerTip: Keep multiple conversations open for parallel work—each tab is independent.

items.name: VS Code Session history (local/remote tabs)
items.summary: Browse and resume past conversations from Local and Remote tabs
items.syntax: Click Session history button at top of Claude panel, switch tabs
items.example: Click Session history, browse yesterday's work, click to resume
items.details: The Local tab shows conversations from your machine. The Remote tab shows sessions from claude.ai if you're signed in with Claude.ai Subscription. Requires v2.1.174 or later. Search by keyword or browse by time. Remote sessions are downloaded and continued locally; changes don't sync back.
items.newcomerTip: The Remote tab is useful for picking up web work in VS Code—one unified history.

items.name: VS Code /mcp (MCP management)
items.summary: Manage MCP servers from the VS Code extension chat panel
items.example: /mcp (opens MCP management dialog)
items.details: Shows configured MCP servers and lets you enable/disable, reconnect, or manage OAuth from within the extension. This is the graphical alternative to CLI-based MCP config.
items.newcomerTip: Use /mcp to see what servers are configured and their per-server token costs.

items.name: VS Code diff viewer (inline)
items.summary: Review Claude's proposed code changes side-by-side in the editor
items.syntax: Automatic when Claude proposes a file edit
items.example: Claude edits a file, a diff panel opens showing before/after side-by-side
items.details: The VS Code extension automatically opens a diff viewer for file edits. You can accept, reject, or edit the proposed changes directly in the diff before accepting. Editing the diff tells Claude you modified it, so it doesn't assume the file matches its proposal.
items.newcomerTip: Review diffs carefully before accepting—you can catch issues without committing them.

items.name: VS Code plan mode (Shift+Tab -> Plan)
items.summary: Review Claude's plan before implementation starts
items.syntax: Click permission mode indicator at bottom of prompt box, select 'Plan'
items.example: Switch to Plan mode, describe the task, Claude proposes an approach as markdown
items.details: In plan mode, Claude describes what it will do before making changes. The plan opens as a markdown document where you can add inline comments. Claude reads your feedback before implementing.
items.newcomerTip: Use plan mode for complex tasks to avoid expensive rework. Review the plan carefully before approval.

items.name: VS Code auto-accept edits mode
items.summary: Claude makes file edits without asking for permission each time
items.syntax: Click permission mode indicator, select 'Auto-accept edits'
items.example: Switch to auto-accept mode for fast iteration
items.details: Claude edits files freely without prompts but still asks for shell commands with external side effects. Useful during rapid iteration.
items.newcomerTip: Use auto-accept mode only for trusted tasks. Switch back to default mode if something unexpected happens.

items.name: JetBrains IDE integration
items.summary: Claude Code plugin for IntelliJ IDEA, PyCharm, WebStorm, and other JetBrains IDEs
items.syntax: Install from JetBrains Marketplace, restart IDE
items.example: Search 'Claude Code' in IDE Extensions, install, restart, open Sessions from the side panel
items.details: The JetBrains plugin requires the standalone CLI installed separately. It provides interactive diff viewing and selection context sharing. More details at: https://code.claude.com/docs/en/jetbrains.md
items.newcomerTip: The JetBrains plugin works much like VS Code—use it if you prefer your IDE's ecosystem.

items.name: IDE MCP server (built-in)
items.summary: Local MCP server that connects VS Code CLI to the extension for diffs, selections, and Jupyter execution
items.syntax: Automatic; appears as 'ide' in tool logs
items.example: Named 'ide', hidden from /mcp, runs on 127.0.0.1 with auth token
items.details: When the extension is active, it runs a local MCP server that the CLI connects to automatically. It exposes mcp__ide__getDiagnostics (language-server errors/warnings) and mcp__ide__executeCode (Jupyter execution, always prompts first). The server is not reachable from other machines and uses per-session auth tokens.
items.newcomerTip: This server is automatic—you don't need to configure it. It's just there to make IDE integration seamless.

items.name: GitHub Actions integration (/install-github-app)
items.summary: Set up Claude GitHub App and Actions workflows for @claude mentions in issues and PRs
items.example:
@claude implement this feature
@claude how should I authenticate?
items.details: Run /install-github-app to install the GitHub app and set up workflows. Responds to @claude mentions (not /claude). Requires repository admin access and an API key in GitHub Secrets. Supports custom automation with prompts and skills. More details at: https://code.claude.com/docs/en/github-actions.md
items.newcomerTip: This is the fastest way to enable AI-driven PR creation and issue automation in your repo.

items.name: Worktrees (parallel sessions on separate branches)
items.summary: Use git worktrees to run independent Claude sessions on different branches simultaneously
items.example: claude --worktree feature-auth
items.details: Each worktree gets its own directory and independent file state, preventing Claude instances from interfering. Sessions are tied to directories, so each worktree maintains separate conversation history. Useful for parallel work on different features.
items.newcomerTip: Use worktrees when working on multiple features in parallel—it keeps file state and sessions completely isolated.

items.name: /teleport (web to CLI)
items.summary: Pull a long-running cloud session into the terminal for continued work
items.example: Kick off a long task on claude.ai/code, then pull it down with --teleport
items.details: Requires a claude.ai subscription. Brings a cloud session from the web into your local CLI without losing conversation history.
items.newcomerTip: Use teleport when you've started work on the web but want to switch to local tools or the terminal.

items.name: /desktop (CLI to desktop app)
items.summary: Hand off a terminal session to the Desktop app for visual diff review
items.example: /desktop
items.details: Switches the current session from the CLI to the Desktop app so you can use the graphical diff viewer.
items.newcomerTip: Use /desktop when a diff gets complex and you want to review it visually.

items.name: Shift+Tab (permission mode cycling)
items.summary: Cycle through permission modes: Default -> Auto-accept -> Plan -> Auto (if available)
items.example: Press Shift+Tab multiple times to switch modes
items.details: Quick toggle between permission modes without opening a menu. In the CLI, cycles through available modes for the current session.
items.newcomerTip: Use Shift+Tab to rapidly switch between Plan mode (for vetting approaches) and Auto-accept (for fast iteration).

items.name: Esc (interrupt Claude's work)
items.summary: Stop Claude immediately and wait for the next instruction
items.example: Claude is running a long test suite, press Esc to stop
items.details: Cancels the current tool call and returns control to you. Use this if Claude is heading in the wrong direction or taking too long.
items.newcomerTip: Press Esc early if you see Claude making a mistake—it's faster than waiting for a wrong result.

items.name: Type during tool execution (course correction)
items.summary: Send a correction while Claude is running without stopping mid-action
items.syntax: Type your message and press Enter while Claude is working
items.example: Claude is reading files, you type 'wait, that's the wrong directory' and press Enter
items.details: Claude reads your correction as soon as the current tool call completes and adjusts before deciding the next step. You don't need to wait or stop execution.
items.newcomerTip: Type corrections as Claude works—they're processed at the next checkpoint, making iteration faster.

items.name: Token usage metrics (prompt/completion tokens)
items.summary: API usage is measured in prompt tokens (input) and completion tokens (output)
items.example: /usage shows: prompt_tokens: 12500, completion_tokens: 3200
items.details: Claude Code shows detailed token breakdowns per session. Output (completion) tokens are more expensive than input (prompt) tokens. Context management strategies (compact, /clear, skill offloading) reduce overall token consumption.
items.newcomerTip: High prompt tokens mean you're sending a lot of context. High completion tokens mean Claude is generating long responses. Both are addressable.

items.name: Effort level (model reasoning budget)
items.summary: Control how much time Claude spends reasoning: low, medium, high, xhigh, or max
items.example: /effort medium (for simple tasks)
items.details: Higher effort levels increase extended thinking budget, improving reasoning but raising costs and latency. Defaults vary by model. Use low effort for straightforward tasks; high/xhigh for complex architectural decisions.
items.newcomerTip: Start with medium effort and raise it only if Claude seems unsure. Low effort saves tokens on simple tasks.

items.name: Auto-compaction (automatic context management)
items.summary: Claude Code automatically compresses conversation history when approaching context limits
items.syntax: Automatic; add 'Compact instructions' to CLAUDE.md to guide it
items.example:
Add to CLAUDE.md:
# Compact instructions
Focus on test failures and code changes
items.details: When context fills, Claude clears older tool outputs first, then summarizes conversation if needed. Requests and key code snippets are preserved. If a single file is too large, auto-compaction may stop after a few attempts; run /clear and /compact manually instead.
items.newcomerTip: Put 'Compact instructions' in CLAUDE.md to tell Claude what to preserve during auto-summaries.

items.name: CLAUDE.md (persistent instructions)
items.summary: A project-level markdown file that Claude reads at the start of every session
items.syntax: Create CLAUDE.md at your project root
items.example:
# Project Style

Use Tailwind CSS and shadcn/ui for components.

# Test Requirements

All new functions must have unit tests.
items.details: CLAUDE.md is loaded into context at session start and survives /clear commands. Use it for coding standards, architecture decisions, testing requirements, and project conventions. First 200 lines or 25KB are loaded (whichever comes first). Keep it concise—move specialized instructions into skills.
items.newcomerTip: Think of CLAUDE.md as your coding constitution. Update it as team standards evolve.

items.name: Auto memory (MEMORY.md)
items.summary: Claude's automatic learnings saved across sessions in MEMORY.md
items.syntax: Claude writes to MEMORY.md; you can edit it manually
items.example: MEMORY.md contains: 'Build command: npm run build. Takes ~30s. Fails if deps are stale.'
items.details: Claude writes learnings after each session: build commands, patterns it noticed, mistakes to avoid. The first 200 lines or 25KB load at session start. You can prune stale entries manually or ask Claude to with /learn.
items.newcomerTip: Auto memory saves you from re-explaining how to build the project. Review it occasionally to prune outdated learnings.

items.name: Extended thinking (reasoning budget)
items.summary: Enable or disable Claude's ability to spend extra time thinking through complex problems
items.syntax: Toggle in /config or /model, or set MAX_THINKING_TOKENS env var
items.example:
/model -> toggle 'Extended thinking'
MAX_THINKING_TOKENS=8000
items.details: Extended thinking is enabled by default on most models. Thinking tokens are billed as output. Disable for simple tasks to save costs. On adaptive models, use /effort to control reasoning. On fixed-budget models, set MAX_THINKING_TOKENS.
items.newcomerTip: Leave extended thinking on for complex architecture work. Disable it for routine tasks like formatting or simple edits.

items.name: MCP tool search (deferred loading)
items.summary: MCP tool definitions are deferred by default and loaded on-demand
items.syntax: Automatic; run /mcp to see per-server costs
items.example: You have 500 tools across 10 MCP servers, but only ~50 tool names are in context
items.details: Only tool names load upfront (small cost). Full schemas load when Claude uses a specific tool, reducing base context overhead. Set ENABLE_TOOL_SEARCH=false to load all schemas upfront, or ENABLE_TOOL_SEARCH=auto to load them if they fit within 10% of context.
items.newcomerTip: Deferred loading means you can safely configure many MCP servers without bloating context.

items.name: Hooks (PreToolUse, PostToolUse, PreEdit, PostEdit)
items.summary: Automate behaviors before/after tool use or file edits
items.example: PreToolUse to filter test output, PostEdit to auto-format
items.details: Hooks run shell commands or arbitrary JSON transformations before/after actions. Use them to preprocess data (like grepping logs), auto-format, or validate changes. More details at: https://code.claude.com/docs/en/hooks.md
items.newcomerTip: Hooks are powerful for reducing tokens—use them to filter noise before Claude sees it.

items.name: Skills (.claude/skills/)
items.summary: Reusable workflows packaged as markdown files for teams to share
items.example: /review-pr (if review-pr.md exists in .claude/skills/)
items.details: Skills load on-demand, keeping base context small. Use them for domain-specific workflows like 'review-pr', 'deploy-staging', or 'audit-security'. Teams share skills via plugins or git repos.
items.newcomerTip: Package complex workflows into skills to keep CLAUDE.md focused and allow reuse.

items.name: Plugins (skill marketplaces)
items.summary: Bundled collections of skills and tools distributed via Git repos
items.example: Install 'code-review' plugin for /code-review:code-review skill
items.details: Plugins package multiple skills, MCP servers, and hooks. Install globally (user), per-project, or locally. Manage with /plugins command in VS Code or CLI.
items.newcomerTip: Plugins are like app stores for Claude Code—browse available ones before building your own skill.

items.name: Subagents (delegated tasks)
items.summary: Spawn separate Claude instances with their own context to parallelize work
items.syntax: Ask Claude to 'use subagents' or configure via settings
items.example: Delegate verbose operations (tests, log parsing) to subagents to keep main context clean
items.details: Each subagent runs independently with its own context window. Loads CLAUDE.md, MCP servers, and skills automatically. Token usage scales with team size. Best for isolating high-volume operations.
items.newcomerTip: Use subagents when a subtask generates lots of verbose output—it prevents context bloat.

items.name: Settings scopes (organization/global/user/local/project)
items.summary: Control permissions and configuration at multiple levels
items.example:
Global settings: npm test allowed for all projects
Project settings: override for specific repo
items.details: Permissions cascade from organization (most restrictive) down to local (least restrictive). Each level can add rules without overriding. Useful for team governance.
items.newcomerTip: Use global settings for trusted commands that apply everywhere, project settings for repo-specific rules.

items.name: Environment variables (CLAUDE_*)
items.summary: Configure Claude Code behavior via environment variables
items.syntax: Set in shell or .env, or add to settings.json environmentVariables
items.example:
CLAUDE_CONFIG_DIR=/custom/path
CLAUDE_CODE_SKIP_PROMPT_HISTORY=1
items.details: Common variables: CLAUDE_CONFIG_DIR (alternate config location), CLAUDE_CODE_SKIP_PROMPT_HISTORY (disable transcripts), ENABLE_TOOL_SEARCH (defer/load MCP tools). Full list at: https://code.claude.com/docs/en/env-vars.md
items.newcomerTip: Most users don't need to set env vars. Use them only for non-standard setups.

items.name: Session storage location (~/.claude/projects/)
items.summary: Sessions are stored in ~/.claude/projects/<project>/ as .jsonl files
items.syntax: CLAUDE_CONFIG_DIR env var changes the base path
items.example: ~/.claude/projects/my-project/abc12345.jsonl
items.details: You can customize the config directory with CLAUDE_CONFIG_DIR. Sessions are plaintext and survive session exit. Expires after 30 days by default (configurable via cleanupPeriodDays).
items.newcomerTip: Sessions are stored locally—your conversations never reach Anthropic's servers unless you run cloud tasks.

items.name: Transcript export (plaintext, JSON, markdown)
items.summary: Export session conversations in multiple formats via /export
items.example: /export my-session.txt
items.details: Exports the full transcript with messages and tool outputs rendered readably. Useful for team reviews, audits, or archival.
items.newcomerTip: Export sessions for documentation or team handoff—transcripts are comprehensive records of work.

items.name: Token counting (/cost, /usage)
items.summary: Monitor API token consumption and estimate costs in real-time
items.example: /usage shows per-skill, per-subagent, per-MCP-server costs
items.details: Displays prompt tokens, completion tokens, estimated cost, and attribution. Helps you identify expensive operations and optimize.
items.newcomerTip: Check /usage when costs spike—it usually points to a feature (like subagents) that needs tuning.

items.name: Third-party provider integration (Bedrock, Vertex AI, Foundry)
items.summary: Route Claude Code through Amazon Bedrock, Google Vertex AI, or Microsoft Foundry
items.syntax: Configure in settings.json or environment variables
items.example: Set ANTHROPIC_BEDROCK_REGION=us-west-2 for Bedrock
items.details: Allows organizations to use Claude through their preferred cloud provider. Requires separate authentication setup. More details at: https://code.claude.com/docs/en/amazon-bedrock.md
items.newcomerTip: Ask your org's platform team how to set up third-party providers—they handle setup.

items.name: Remote Control (browser UI with local execution)
items.summary: Use Claude Code in a browser while keeping everything local
items.syntax: Start with /remote-control or from Desktop app
items.example: Access Claude from your phone while code runs on your home machine
items.details: Execution stays local, UI moves to the browser. Useful for monitoring long-running tasks from another device.
items.newcomerTip: Use Remote Control when you want the web UI convenience but need local file access.

missingItems.name: Reverse search keyboard shortcut Ctrl+S
missingItems.summary: In reverse history search (initiated with Ctrl+R), press Ctrl+S to cycle scope through this session, this project, and all projects

missingItems.name: Jump to previous/next user prompt
missingItems.summary: In fullscreen rendering with transcript viewer open, press { to jump to previous user prompt or } to jump to next user prompt, like vim paragraph motion
missingItems.newcomerTip: Only available in fullscreen mode; use Ctrl+O to toggle transcript viewer first

missingItems.name: Write scrollback to native terminal buffer
missingItems.summary: In fullscreen rendering, press [ to write full conversation to terminal's native scrollback so Cmd+F, tmux copy mode, and other native search tools can find it

missingItems.name: Open conversation in editor
missingItems.summary: In fullscreen rendering, press v to write conversation to temp file and open in $VISUAL or $EDITOR

missingItems.name: /fewer-permission-prompts
missingItems.summary: Scan transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts during development
missingItems.newcomerTip: Useful after you've been working in a project and want to reduce permission prompt fatigue

missingItems.name: /scroll-speed
missingItems.summary: Adjust mouse wheel scroll speed interactively in fullscreen rendering mode with a ruler preview
missingItems.newcomerTip: Only available in fullscreen mode (/tui fullscreen)

missingItems.name: /remote-env
missingItems.summary: Choose the default environment for cloud agents running on Claude Code on the web

missingItems.name: /workflows
missingItems.summary: Open the workflow progress view to watch, pause, resume, or save running and completed workflows

missingItems.name: /bashes
missingItems.summary: Alias for /tasks. View and manage everything running in the background

missingItems.name: --disable-slash-commands
missingItems.summary: Disable all skills and commands for this session

missingItems.name: --input-format
missingItems.summary: Specify input format for print mode (options: text, stream-json)

missingItems.name: Setup hook event
missingItems.summary: Hook event that runs setup tasks before the session, triggered by --init or --maintenance flags or @matcher. Can modify the working directory or install tools

missingItems.name: InstructionsLoaded hook event
missingItems.summary: Hook event fired after CLAUDE.md files and project memory load. Useful for validating or augmenting project instructions

missingItems.name: UserPromptExpansion hook event
missingItems.summary: Hook event that fires when user submits text starting with @ or / but before command/file expansion. Can modify or reject the expansion

missingItems.name: PostToolBatch hook event
missingItems.summary: Hook event fired after all tools in a batch complete. Receives results from all tools in the batch for aggregate handling

missingItems.name: PostToolUseFailure hook event
missingItems.summary: Hook event fired when a tool call fails. Can be used to log failures, retry, or provide alternate data

missingItems.name: TeammateIdle hook event
missingItems.summary: Hook event fired when an agent team teammate becomes idle. Can be used to reassign work or check on progress

missingItems.name: ConfigChange hook event
missingItems.summary: Hook event fired when settings or configuration change during a session. Can be used to validate, log, or react to config changes

missingItems.name: CwdChanged hook event
missingItems.summary: Hook event fired when the current working directory changes (via /cd). Input includes old and new paths

missingItems.name: FileChanged hook event
missingItems.summary: Hook event fired when files change on disk outside of Claude's edits. Can be used to reload context or re-read files

missingItems.name: WorktreeCreate hook event
missingItems.summary: Hook event fired when a new git worktree is created. Includes worktree path and branch information

missingItems.name: WorktreeRemove hook event
missingItems.summary: Hook event fired when a git worktree is removed. Includes the worktree path being removed

missingItems.name: PreCompact hook event
missingItems.summary: Hook event fired before conversation compaction starts. Can be used to save state or reject compaction

missingItems.name: PostCompact hook event
missingItems.summary: Hook event fired after conversation compaction completes. Can be used to log compaction or verify results

missingItems.name: SessionEnd hook event
missingItems.summary: Hook event fired when a session ends. Can be used to clean up resources, save artifacts, or log metrics

missingItems.name: Elicitation hook event
missingItems.summary: Hook event fired when an MCP server requests information from Claude. Can provide data or reject the request

missingItems.name: ElicitationResult hook event
missingItems.summary: Hook event fired after Claude responds to an MCP elicitation request. Can be used to log or validate the response

missingItems.name: respondToBashCommands setting
missingItems.summary: Boolean setting that controls whether Claude automatically responds to shell mode (!) commands. Default true. Set to false to restore older behavior where output is added to context without response

missingItems.name: viewMode setting
missingItems.summary: Set default view mode for sessions: focus (show only last prompt/response) or normal (show full history). Can be overridden per-session with /focus

missingItems.name: fallbackModel setting
missingItems.summary: Comma-separated list of model fallback chain for when primary model is overloaded. Overridable with --fallback-model flag

missingItems.name: advisorModel setting
missingItems.summary: Set default advisor model (opus, sonnet, fable, or full model ID). Can be overridden with /advisor command or --advisor flag

missingItems.name: axScreenReader setting
missingItems.summary: Boolean setting for screen-reader friendly output (flat text without decorative borders). Can be overridden with --ax-screen-reader flag

missingItems.name: teammateMode setting
missingItems.summary: Default display mode for agent team teammates: in-process, auto, tmux, or iterm2. Can be overridden with --teammate-mode flag

missingItems.name: CLAUDE_CODE_SKIP_PROMPT_HISTORY
missingItems.summary: Environment variable that disables session persistence so sessions are not saved to disk and cannot be resumed. Works across all modes (equivalent to --no-session-persistence)

missingItems.name: CLAUDE_REMOTE_CONTROL_SESSION_NAME_PREFIX
missingItems.summary: Prefix for auto-generated Remote Control session names when no explicit name is set. Defaults to machine hostname

missingItems.name: CLAUDE_CODE_ENABLE_PROMPT_SUGGESTION
missingItems.summary: Environment variable to disable prompt suggestions globally. Set to 0 or false to turn off suggestions

missingItems.name: CLAUDE_CODE_DISABLE_BACKGROUND_TASKS
missingItems.summary: Environment variable to disable all background task functionality. Set to 1 to turn off background commands with Ctrl+B

missingItems.name: CLAUDE_CODE_TASK_LIST_ID
missingItems.summary: Environment variable to share a task list across sessions. Set to named directory in ~/.claude/tasks/ to use persistent task tracking

missingItems.name: CLAUDE_CODE_NEW_INIT
missingItems.summary: Environment variable that enables interactive flow for /init command, walking through skills, hooks, and personal memory setup

gapAssessment:
The current catalog is quite comprehensive but has several notable gaps:

**What's Missing:**
1. **Lesser-known keyboard shortcuts** from Interactive Mode not in the catalog: `{` / `}` (jump to prev/next user prompt), `Ctrl+S` in reverse search, cycling paste history with `Alt+Y`
2. **Slash commands** that are present in docs but missing from catalog: `/pr-comments` (removed in v2.1.91), `/audio` mode options, `/vim` (removed), `/simplify`, `/fewer-permission-prompts`, `/scroll-speed`, `/remote-env`, `/bashes` (alias), `/tp` (alias for /teleport), `/settings` (alias for /config), `/reset` and `/new` (aliases for /clear), `/bg` (alias), `/quit`/`/exit` variants, `/stats`, `/workflows`
3. **CLI flags and options** missing from catalog: `--disable-slash-commands`, `--strict-mcp-config`, `--input-format`, `--output-format` (stream-json, json modes), `--json-schema`, `--max-budget-usd`, `--maintenance`, `--init`, `--init-only`, `--ax-screen-reader`, `--replay-user-messages`, `--exclude-dynamic-system-prompt-sections`, `--fallback-model`, `--from-pr`, `--no-chrome`, `--permission-prompt-tool`, many more
4. **Hook events** missing from catalog: `Elicitation`, `ElicitationResult`, `TeammateIdle`, `ConfigChange`, `CwdChanged`, `FileChanged`, `WorktreeCreate`, `WorktreeRemove`, `PreCompact`, `PostCompact`, `SessionEnd`, `InstructionsLoaded`, `Setup`, `UserPromptExpansion`, `PostToolBatch`, `PostToolUseFailure`
5. **Settings keys** not fully catalogued: `respondToBashCommands`, `viewMode`, `alwaysThinkingEnabled`, `fallbackModel`, `advisorModel`, `axScreenReader`, `teamateMode`, `enabledMcpServers`, `strictKnownMarketplaces`, `extraKnownMarketplaces`
6. **Environment variables** missing: `CLAUDE_CODE_SKIP_PROMPT_HISTORY`, `CLAUDE_REMOTE_CONTROL_SESSION_NAME_PREFIX`, `CLAUDE_CODE_ENABLE_PROMPT_SUGGESTION`, `CLAUDE_CODE_DISABLE_BACKGROUND_TASKS`, `CLAUDE_CODE_TASK_LIST_ID`, various provider-specific variables
7. **Interactive features** not in catalog: prompt suggestions, side questions advanced key navigation with `/btw`, task list lifecycle, session recap customization

**Categories that need expansion:**
- Keyboard shortcuts (some lesser-known ones missing)
- Slash commands (many bundled skills and workflow commands missing)
- CLI flags (extensive list of flags only partially covered)
- Hook events (many specific event types not listed)
- Settings keys (many configuration options missing)
- Environment variables (incomplete list)

**Overall assessment:** The catalog covers ~60-70% of Claude Code's documented features. The core features and most common workflows are well-represented, but niche features, advanced configuration options, newer additions, and less-frequently-used commands are underrepresented. The catalog would benefit from more complete coverage of all hook event types, CLI flags, settings keys, and environment variables.
